CuratorialText:
In the early 1940s, decades into his artistic career, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) turned his focus to a new medium: cut paper. 

He had become bedridden and unable to paint following an excruciating abdominal surgery, and cut paper allowed him to continue to create in his relatively sedentary state. Encouraged by his friend, the book publisher Tériade (the pseudonym of Stratis Eleftheriades), Matisse furthered his exploration of this new technique. He mined his memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and mythology, and produced a series of 20 cut-paper maquettes.
persona1_adapted:
In the early 1940s, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) had been making art for decades. But then he had a serious surgery on his stomach. After that, he was stuck in bed and couldn't paint anymore.

So he tried something new: cutting shapes out of colored paper. This let him keep making art even while lying down. His friend Tériade, a book publisher, encouraged him to keep going with this new style.

Matisse looked back on his memories—Paris music halls, the circus, a trip to Tahiti, old folktales, and myths. From these ideas, he created 20 models made from cut paper.

CuratorialText: These vibrant original compositions were then faithfully reproduced using pochoir (stencil) and combined with an original text written by Matisse to form an unbound book, Jazz, that was published in 1947—when Matisse was 77 years old. Jazz caused an immediate international sensation and reinvigorated the aging artist, setting him on a new course of artistic discovery.
persona1_adapted: Matisse made these bright, colorful pictures using a special stencil technique called *pochoir*. He also wrote the words that go with them. Together, the pictures and text became a book called *Jazz*, published in 1947—when Matisse was 77 years old. *Jazz* was a huge hit around the world right away. It gave the older artist new energy and inspired him to try new things in his art.

CuratorialText: Matisse’s extraordinary images of Jazz are not only captivating and provocative in their own right but also reflect a practice devoted to the exploration of color and line. Throughout the artist’s decades-long career, he pushed color from the purely visual to the nearly transcendental and progressively simplified his line to the most elemental. But until his cut-outs, his color and line existed separately, one modifying the other but never fully unified—at least not to Matisse’s own satisfaction.
persona1_adapted: Matisse’s *Jazz* images are exciting and thought-provoking. They also show how much he loved experimenting with color and line. Over his long career, Matisse made color feel almost magical, not just something you see. He also made his lines simpler and simpler. But before he started making his cut-outs, his color and line didn’t work together perfectly. They affected each other, but never fully came together—at least not in a way that made Matisse happy.

CuratorialText: This exhibition marks the first time Matisse’s Jazz will be shown in its entirety at the Art Institute; the museum acquired it in 1948. One of the most important livres d’artiste (artist books) of the 20th century, Jazz joins over 50 works from the museum’s renowned collection of paintings, sculpture, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles to showcase the famed artist’s commitment across his 50-year career to continual innovation and the expressive power of color and line.
persona1_adapted: This is the first time the Art Institute has shown all of Matisse’s *Jazz* at once. The museum got it back in 1948. *Jazz* is one of the most famous artist books of the 1900s. It’s joined by more than 50 other works from the museum’s collection—paintings, sculptures, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and fabrics. Together, they show how Matisse spent his 50-year career always trying new things and using color and line in powerful ways.

CuratorialText: From 6th-century gilt bronze Buddhist sculpture through Joseon dynasty painting and white porcelain and contemporary paintings of the late 20th century, the works of art in this exhibition demonstrate the artistic legacy produced on the Korean peninsula over millennia.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition shows art made on the Korean peninsula over more than 1,500 years. You'll see a shiny gold Buddhist statue from the 500s, paintings and white porcelain from the Joseon dynasty, and modern paintings from the late 1900s. Together, these works tell the story of Korea's long artistic tradition.

CuratorialText: Once privately held, these artworks now belong to the Korean people thanks to a single groundbreaking gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021 the family donated over 23,000 works to the Korean government for public audiences to study and appreciate in perpetuity. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected exceptional and storied objects from throughout Korean history as a means of preserving and celebrating the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition marks the first time the works are on public display.
persona1_adapted:
These artworks used to belong to one family. Now, they belong to all the people of Korea. That’s thanks to a huge, one-of-a-kind gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, the late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021, his family gave more than 23,000 works of art to the Korean government. Now, everyone can study and enjoy them forever.

Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected amazing and important objects from all across Korean history. They wanted to save and celebrate their country’s culture. This exhibition is the very first time these works have been shown to the public.

CuratorialText: Among the 140 artworks on view—including modern and historical painting, ceramics, and objects made for Buddhist worship and scholarly study—22 are officially recognized as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These objects are recognized as remarkable examples of their type and distinguished for their exceptional historic, artistic, and academic value.
persona1_adapted: Out of the 140 artworks on display, 22 are officially called **National Treasures** or **Treasures** by the Korean government. These special pieces are considered some of the best examples of their kind—whether they are paintings, ceramics, or objects used for Buddhist worship and study. They stand out because of their amazing history, beauty, and importance to learning.

CuratorialText: The largest Art Institute exhibition devoted to Korean art in four decades, Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art charts the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped the country’s creative production, from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) through the 1900s, offering insights into these objects’ rich meanings across centuries.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is the biggest show of Korean art at the Art Institute in 40 years. It’s called *Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art*. The show explores the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped Korean art over time. You’ll see objects from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) all the way to the 1900s. Each piece tells a story about what it meant to people back then—and what it can mean to us today.

CuratorialText: Epic and intricate, monumental and meticulous—the paintings of Kashmir-raised, London-based artist Raqib Shaw offer fantastical meditations on identity, transformation, and the redemptive power of beauty.
persona1_adapted: Raqib Shaw grew up in Kashmir and now lives in London. His paintings are huge and full of tiny, careful details. They show dreamlike worlds where he thinks about big ideas like who we are, how people change, and how beauty can make things better.

CuratorialText: Born in Calcutta and raised in the verdant Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws deeply on the landscapes and memories of his early life, many of which were fractured by political upheaval. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he relocated first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design. His work is influenced by a broad range of sources—Mughal and Persian miniatures, Renaissance altarpieces, Old Masters paintings, Japanese arts of the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and Hindu and Western mythology—and yet his visual vocabulary and technique are all his own. His signature method involves applying an acrylic liner on gesso to create a golden line almost like the leading of a stained glass window. He then applies automobile enamel paints with needle-fine syringes and manipulates those with a porcupine quill, often adding glittering inset stones and baubles that further enhance the magical and dreamlike quality of the depicted scenes.
persona1_adapted:
Shaw was born in Calcutta and grew up in the Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, a place full of green landscapes. But his early life was broken apart by political conflict. When he was a teenager, he had to leave Kashmir. He moved first to New Delhi, then to London, where he studied art at Central Saint Martins College.

His art is inspired by many different styles—like paintings from Mughal and Persian cultures, religious art from the Renaissance, old master paintings, Japanese art from the Momoyama period, poetry from Kashmir and Urdu, and stories from Hindu and Western myths. Even with all these influences, his own style is completely unique.

Shaw has a special way of making his art. He starts by drawing a golden line on a surface using a material called acrylic liner on gesso. This line looks like the metal that holds stained glass windows together. Then he uses tiny syringes to apply car paint, and he moves the paint around with a porcupine quill. He often adds shiny stones and jewels to make the scenes look magical and dreamlike.

CuratorialText: At the Art Institute, Shaw debuts his more-than 100-feet-wide, 21-panel Paradise Lost (2009–25), the artist’s most ambitious and personal project to date. This magnificent allegorical painting takes viewers on a spellbinding journey, from the nocturnal solitude of the artist’s childhood in Kashmir to the frenzied daylight of the art world and the West to finally a fragile, renewed dawn. Each panel is dense with symbolism: mythical beasts, anthropomorphic hybrids, collapsing kingdoms, and natural beauty in various states of transformation. Throughout, the work is dotted with images of the artist, sometimes as a humanoid creature with different animal heads, at another time as a monkey looking with awe at the gleaming edifices and the wealth of the West, and sometimes unambiguously in full human form seated on a bed of saffron under a blossoming cherry tree, lost deep in his thoughts
persona1_adapted:
At the Art Institute, Shaw is showing off his biggest and most personal project yet: *Paradise Lost* (2009–25). It’s over 100 feet wide and made up of 21 panels. This giant, story-filled painting takes you on an amazing journey. It starts in the quiet, dark nights of Shaw’s childhood in Kashmir, moves into the bright, busy world of art and Western culture, and ends with a fragile, hopeful new dawn.

Each panel is packed with symbols and hidden meanings. You’ll see mythical beasts, creatures that are part human and part animal, falling kingdoms, and beautiful nature scenes that are always changing. Throughout the painting, you can spot the artist himself. Sometimes he’s a human-like creature with different animal heads. Other times, he’s a monkey staring in wonder at the shiny buildings and wealth of the West. And sometimes, he’s clearly a human, sitting on a bed of saffron under a blooming cherry tree, lost in his own thoughts.

CuratorialText: The work is not a direct retelling of Milton’s 17th-century poem Paradise Lost, but rather a reflection on the many paradises lost across a lifetime: childhood innocence, creative freedom, mental tranquility, cultural belonging. “This is not just my story,” Shaw explained. “It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”
persona1_adapted: This artwork isn't a straight retelling of John Milton's old poem *Paradise Lost*. Instead, it makes you think about all the different "paradises" we lose as we grow up—things like the innocence of childhood, the freedom to be creative, peace of mind, or a sense of belonging to a culture. "This isn't just my story," the artist Shaw said. "It's the story of every one of us, and the story of the world we live in today."

CuratorialText: While Shaw has previously shown the first chapter of Paradise Lost, this exhibition marks the first time all four chapters will be displayed, showcasing the fullest extent yet of this epic masterpiece. “This painting was made not only to document a life,” Shaw shared, “but to offer something to those who come after, to offer a space of reflection for emotional recognition and perhaps for quiet rebellion against forgetfulness. I do hope that this work invites the viewer to slow down and to look carefully and to feel without haste.”
persona1_adapted: Shaw has shown the first part of *Paradise Lost* before, but this is the first time all four parts are on display. That means you get to see the full story of this huge, amazing artwork. Shaw said, “This painting wasn’t just made to record a life. It was made to give something to people in the future—a place to think, to recognize your own feelings, and maybe to quietly push back against being forgotten. I hope it makes you slow down, look closely, and feel without rushing.”

CuratorialText: Exquisitely crafted and shrouded in mystery, Saint-Porchaire ceramics are among the rarest treasures of the French Renaissance.
persona1_adapted: These ceramics from the French Renaissance are incredibly rare and beautiful. They are so carefully made that they seem almost magical, and no one knows for sure how they were created.

CuratorialText: Only around 70 examples are known to exist worldwide, and each one dazzles and intrigues with its refined and delicate modeling and astonishingly intricate decoration. Created between about 1520 and 1570 and closely associated with the royal courts of François I and Henri II, these extraordinary works bear the emblems of power, prestige, and desire—including the interlaced cipher of Henri II and his famed mistress, Diane de Poitiers.
persona1_adapted: Only about 70 of these objects are known to exist in the whole world. Each one amazes viewers with its fine, delicate design and incredibly detailed decoration. They were made between about 1520 and 1570 and are closely linked to the royal courts of King François I and King Henri II of France. These special works show symbols of power, status, and desire—including the linked initials of Henri II and his famous mistress, Diane de Poitiers.

CuratorialText: Saint-Porchaire ceramics embody the sophistication and inventive spirit of Renaissance court culture. Fashioned from lead-glazed earthenware, their sculptural forms echo precious metalwork, while their ornamental patterns draw inspiration from textiles, bookbinding, and other luxury arts. Together, they express a unique and daring artistry, playful elegance, and refined taste at the height of French royal patronage.
persona1_adapted: Saint-Porchaire ceramics show off the creativity and elegance of Renaissance royal life. Made from lead-glazed clay, these fancy pots and dishes look like they could be made of gold or silver. Their designs borrow ideas from fancy fabrics, book covers, and other luxury items. Together, they show a bold and playful style that was all about good taste during the peak of French royal support for the arts.

CuratorialText: Beginning in March, the Art Institute will present an exceptional group of Saint-Porchaire ceramics from a private collection—most on public view for the first time since the late 1800s—in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor. These rare works will be shown alongside other notable examples of French Renaissance ceramics drawn from the Art Institute’s collection, including several recent acquisitions.
persona1_adapted:
**Starting in March**, the Art Institute will show a special collection of Saint-Porchaire ceramics. These are fancy pottery pieces from a private collection. Most of them haven’t been seen by the public since the late 1800s. You’ll find them in the Deering Family Galleries, which focus on Medieval and Renaissance art, arms, and armor.

The rare ceramics will be displayed next to other famous French Renaissance pottery from the Art Institute’s own collection. That includes several pieces the museum just got recently.

CuratorialText: Installed in the intimate James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery, this focused presentation invites close encounters with these remarkable jewel-like objects, while its placement within the broader context of the Deering Family Galleries highlights the rich dialogue between ceramics and the metalwork, textiles, and decorative arts that flourished during the Renaissance.
persona1_adapted: This small, cozy gallery is the perfect place to get up close to these tiny, sparkling treasures. They look like jewels! And because this room is part of the larger Deering Family Galleries, you can see how pottery, metalwork, and fabric designs all influenced each other during the Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Over a long and distinguished career, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has focused on making mezzotint prints, perhaps the most demanding of all print techniques.
persona1_adapted: Hamanishi Katsunori is a Japanese artist born in 1949. He has spent his long and impressive career focused on making mezzotint prints. Mezzotint is one of the hardest printmaking techniques to master.

CuratorialText: Mezzotints are known for their dark and atmospheric appearance. This is because the starting point for any mezzotint is the creation of a roughened surface, which produces a solid black background when printed. To draw the image that emerges from this dark background, the artist uses a series of burnishers and scrapers; the deepest gouges print as white areas on the finished print.
persona1_adapted: Mezzotints are a type of print that looks dark and moody. That’s because the artist starts by making the whole surface rough, which prints as a solid black background. To create the image, the artist uses tools to smooth or scrape away parts of the rough surface. The deeper the scrape, the lighter that area will be in the final print—so the deepest marks show up as white.

CuratorialText: This presentation includes earlier, smaller-format works done without color, as well as more recent larger work that boast many hues. Among the earlier monochromatic prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 Viva Chicago series, which focuses on the city’s beloved public monuments like the Picasso and Miró sculptures. As the artist recalled, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”
persona1_adapted: This show has both early black-and-white prints and newer, bigger, colorful works. One of the early black-and-white pieces is Hamanishi’s 1997 *Viva Chicago* series. It focuses on famous public monuments in the city, like the Picasso and Miró sculptures. The artist said, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”

CuratorialText: The exhibition also celebrates several important gifts to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series—shown here for the first time—is a recent gift from the artist. Each work in the series is reminiscent of the panels of a folding screen and features seasonal floral imagery. Many works in the show come from the 2013 gift to the museum from the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection, which gave the Art Institute the largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints in the world.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition also highlights some special gifts to the museum. Hamanishi’s *Four Seasons* series from 2022 is shown here for the first time—it was a recent gift from the artist himself. Each piece in the series looks like a panel from a folding screen and shows flowers from different seasons. Many other artworks in the show come from a 2013 gift by the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection. That gift gave the Art Institute the biggest collection of Hamanishi’s prints anywhere in the world.

CuratorialText:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham. Over the course of five decades, Dunham has engaged in wide-ranging formal and thematic experimentation across various media, yet his drawings represent a distinct, interconnected body of work. Dunham’s mature artistic career began in 1970s New York amid a scene dominated by Minimalist aesthetics. Using simple elements such as line, shape, and color, he made a tremendous impact with work that nevertheless signaled a return to subject matter. In the intervening decades, Dunham has continued to produce drawings that test, and ultimately collapse, the porous boundaries between abstraction and figuration: bodies shapeshift into buildings, trees pose like models, the cellular resembles the cosmic, and internal organs or private parts are made very public.
persona1_adapted:
Carroll Dunham once said, “There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.” For over fifty years, Dunham has tried out all kinds of different styles and ideas in his art. But no matter what he makes—paintings, prints, or sculptures—drawing is always at the center.

Dunham started his career as an artist in New York City in the 1970s. Back then, many artists were into Minimalism, which means making art that is very simple and stripped down. Dunham used simple things like lines, shapes, and colors, but his work was different because it actually showed real subjects—things you could recognize.

Over the years, Dunham kept making drawings that blur the line between abstract (not looking like anything real) and figurative (looking like real things). In his drawings, bodies turn into buildings, trees stand like models, tiny cells look like outer space, and private body parts become very public.

CuratorialText: Working in series, Dunham methodically tests out every possible outcome of a drawing’s composition or content. This approach seems to spontaneously yield new imagery and ideas that link one body of work to the next. His influences are likewise wide-ranging—art history, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and evolutionary science as well as science fiction and comic books—all of which inform his explorations of the tension between male and female, nature and culture, self and other.
persona1_adapted: Dunham works in series, meaning he carefully tries out every possible version of a drawing’s layout or subject. This way of working helps him come up with new images and ideas that connect one group of artworks to the next. He gets ideas from many different places—art history, philosophy, psychology, and science about evolution, as well as science fiction and comic books. All of these influences help him explore the conflicts between male and female, nature and culture, and self and others.

CuratorialText: Dunham foregrounds the development of his work by precisely dating each drawing. This practice is evidenced in the thousands of drawings he has organized into an archive, from which this show is derived. Developed in conversation with the artist, this comprehensive survey is the first museum presentation focused exclusively on Dunham’s drawings. Encompassing a 50-year period and featuring many drawings making their public debut, it celebrates the medium central to Dunham’s expansive practice.
persona1_adapted: Dunham carefully dates each drawing to show how his work has changed over time. He has made thousands of drawings and keeps them all in an organized collection, called an archive. This exhibition comes from that archive. Created with the artist’s help, this is the first museum show ever to focus only on Dunham’s drawings. It covers 50 years of his work, and many of these drawings are being shown to the public for the first time. The exhibition celebrates drawing, which is the most important part of Dunham’s art.

CuratorialText: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute unveiled a newly imagined and installed gallery for the arts of Korea—our first space fully dedicated to this cultural region.
persona1_adapted: In fall 2024, the Art Institute opened a brand-new gallery for Korean art. It's the first space in the museum that is completely dedicated to this culture.

CuratorialText: The new installation presents a wider range of objects than previous displays—extending from ceramics to textiles and painting and spanning 2,000 years from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE–676 CE) until today. Intentionally located between the Chinese and Japanese art galleries, the thoughtful display offers insight into how these artworks both reflect the religious and material culture of Korea and how they relate to the arts of Korea’s influential neighbors, China and Japan.
persona1_adapted:
This new exhibit shows a bigger variety of Korean art than ever before. You’ll see everything from pottery and fabric to paintings, covering 2,000 years of history—from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE to 676 CE) all the way to today.

The gallery is placed right between the Chinese and Japanese art rooms on purpose. This setup helps you see how Korean art was shaped by religion and daily life, and also how it connects to the art of its powerful neighbors, China and Japan.

CuratorialText: Six themes introduce Korea’s long and rich history as well as its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section introduces objects that were made to help understand and spread the teachings of the Buddha. The centerpiece, an 18th-century Buddha statue, is on view for the first time since its acquisition after extensive conservation treatment. A section devoted to celadon and tea culture provides a glimpse into the practice of tea ceremonies during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392), and objects grouped around the idea of symbolism illustrate how beautiful embellishments represent desired outcomes, such as a blissful marriage, career advancement, and long life.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibit has six parts that show Korea’s long and fascinating history, along with its religion, politics, and daily life.

The first part features objects made to help people understand and share the teachings of the Buddha. The main attraction is a statue of the Buddha from the 1700s. It’s on display for the first time since the museum got it, after a lot of careful cleaning and repair work.

Another part focuses on celadon pottery and tea culture. It gives you a peek into how people held tea ceremonies during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392).

There’s also a section about symbolism. It shows how beautiful decorations on objects stand for things people hoped for, like a happy marriage, getting a better job, or living a long life.

CuratorialText: The remaining three sections focus on how material culture responded to a major ideological, political, and cultural shift during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897) as evidenced in the creation of the earthy grey stoneware known as buncheong, elegant white porcelain, and scholars’ objects. The new installation also includes select contemporary works that meaningfully engage with traditional forms and materials.
persona1_adapted: The last three parts of the exhibit show how art and everyday objects changed during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897). Back then, Korea went through big shifts in ideas, politics, and culture. You can see this change in three types of objects: rough, gray stoneware called buncheong; beautiful white porcelain; and items used by scholars. The new display also includes a few modern artworks that connect with these old styles and materials in interesting ways.

CuratorialText: The transformed space explores aspects of life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley with the first new installation of works from the museum’s historic collection of ancient Egyptian art in a quarter-century. Striking artifacts—displayed along one wall of the gallery in a series of innovative cases that promote viewing from multiple vantage points—provide insight into the beliefs and practices of this illustrious North African culture.
persona1_adapted:
This new gallery takes you on a journey through life and the afterlife in ancient Egypt. It’s the first time in 25 years that the museum has completely reimagined how it shows its collection of Egyptian art.

Along one wall, you’ll find amazing artifacts displayed in special cases. These cases let you see the objects from different angles, so you can get a closer look at the details. Each piece helps tell the story of what people in this famous North African culture believed and how they lived.

CuratorialText: Recurring themes throughout this fresh presentation of the collection consider the impact of Egypt’s natural environment, including the Nile River, on its visual culture; reveal the processes of ancient Egyptian artists; and explore the centrality of gods and goddesses to life (and death) along the Nile. Arresting sculptures, such as the statue of Shebenhor, reveal how ancient Egyptians chose to present themselves so that they would be remembered for eternity, while funerary works—such as a gilded funerary mask—unveil practices for preparing for and protecting oneself in the afterlife.
persona1_adapted:
This new exhibit shows how Egypt’s natural world—especially the Nile River—shaped its art. You’ll see how ancient Egyptian artists worked, and learn why gods and goddesses were so important to life (and death) along the Nile.

Amazing sculptures, like the statue of Shebenhor, show how Egyptians wanted to be remembered forever. Other objects, like a shiny gold funeral mask, reveal how they prepared for and protected themselves in the afterlife.

CuratorialText: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago unveils an elegant and thought-provoking new display of architectural fragments from buildings in and around Chicago. Since the 19th century, Chicago’s urban landscape has experienced continuous change. Over time, many  transformations of the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become invisible, preserved only in collective memory and archival photographs. Architectural fragments provide another avenue to understanding our urban past, including historical building practices and technologies, our dynamic communities, and evolving ways of life.
persona1_adapted: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago is showing a new, elegant, and thought-provoking exhibit. It features pieces of buildings from Chicago and the surrounding area. Since the 1800s, Chicago’s cityscape has been constantly changing. Over time, many of these changes to streets and neighborhoods have become invisible. They only survive in people’s memories and old photos. These architectural fragments offer another way to learn about our city’s past—including how buildings were made, the technology used, our lively communities, and how people’s ways of life have evolved.

CuratorialText: In this new installation, sculptural elements from building facades and interiors are displayed in groups that allow visitors to learn about many architecturally or culturally significant structures and moments in Chicago. These range from celebrated buildings by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan to important local histories related to the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, labor, and preservation.
persona1_adapted: In this new exhibit, you'll see pieces of buildings—like parts of walls and rooms—grouped together to tell the story of Chicago's most important architecture and history. You can explore famous buildings by top architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan, as well as learn about local stories from the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, the history of workers, and efforts to save old buildings.

CuratorialText: Featuring well-loved works from the permanent collection alongside important new acquisitions—such as a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey—this installation, designed by the Chicago-based practice Norman Kelley, offers dynamic encounters with the museum’s rich holdings of architectural fragments, an integral part of our shared material history.
persona1_adapted: This installation mixes old favorites from the museum’s collection with exciting new pieces. You’ll see a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey. The display was designed by the Chicago-based team Norman Kelley. It gives you a fresh look at the museum’s amazing collection of architectural fragments—pieces of buildings that tell the story of our shared history.

CuratorialText: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa, embroidery is more than surface decoration. In this region, known as SWANA, artists have used, and continue to use, centuries-old embellishment techniques to transmit cultural expressions of identity, status, and long-held belief systems. Cultural groups throughout the region wear layers of dress and adornment to protect their bodies physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are ornamented with valuable materials that function as wearable wealth, including symbolic motifs and designs constructed with coins, thread, beads, amulets, ornaments, and jewelry.
persona1_adapted:
In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa (a region often called SWANA), embroidery is much more than just decoration. For hundreds of years, artists have used—and still use—old stitching techniques to share ideas about who they are, their social status, and their long-held beliefs.

People in this region wear layers of clothing and accessories to protect themselves, both physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are decorated with valuable materials that act like "wearable wealth." These include symbolic patterns and designs made with coins, thread, beads, amulets, ornaments, and jewelry.

CuratorialText: The vast area that spans from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east is connected by millennia-old trade networks. The works presented reveal pathways of deep cultural exchange shaped by diverse landscapes—from mountains and deserts to seas—along with the movement of nomadic peoples and the spread and interweaving of multiple religious traditions and urban fashions.
persona1_adapted: A huge region stretching from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east has been linked by trade routes for thousands of years. The artworks in this exhibit show how different cultures shared ideas and styles as they traveled across mountains, deserts, and seas. Nomadic people, religious traditions, and city fashions all mixed together along these paths.

CuratorialText: Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago devoted exclusively to the artistic and cultural traditions of SWANA and spectacularly showcases the region’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual expressions. Drawn primarily from the Art Institute’s rich collection of textiles, jewelry, and adornment, this display presents over 70 works from the 18th through 20th century—most of which have never been exhibited publicly—including four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
persona1_adapted:
**Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan** is the first show at the Art Institute of Chicago that focuses only on the art and culture of the SWANA region (Southwest Asia and North Africa). It shows off the area’s unique beauty and famous visual styles.

The display features over 70 artworks from the 1700s to the 1900s. Most of these have never been shown to the public before. They come mostly from the museum’s own collection of textiles, jewelry, and accessories. There are also four rare face veils on loan from a private collector in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

CuratorialText: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.
persona1_adapted:
Here's the adapted text for an 8th-grade reader:

**Adapted Text:**

Other cool highlights include fancy decorated clothes from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral "treasure necklaces" from the Anti-Atlas Mountains in Morocco; beautifully embroidered linen pieces from the Art Institute's amazing collection of Ottoman Turkish fabrics; and a wedding blanket stitched by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) people of Iraq.

CuratorialText: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film Sanctus depicts human bodies at risk. It features archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982) of people making simple gestures and engaging in everyday activities: applying lipstick, drinking liquids, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer rephotographed the original film and spliced the images together in a visceral montage set to overwhelming requiems by Neil B. Rolnick. She further transformed the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects and by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. In presenting images captured by medical technology that uses large amounts of radiation, Hammer underscored her idea that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging, and even treatment, can cause harm.
persona1_adapted: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film *Sanctus* shows human bodies in danger. It uses old X-ray footage taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982). In the X-rays, people are doing simple, everyday things: putting on lipstick, drinking, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer re-filmed the original footage and cut the images together in a powerful, fast-paced sequence set to intense music by Neil B. Rolnick. She changed the X-rays even more by adding flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects. She also sped up, looped, and reversed the movements. By showing images made with medical technology that uses a lot of radiation, Hammer wanted to make a point: “Science advances through the death of animal, including human, subjects in experiments.” She also wanted to show that medical imaging—and even treatment—can sometimes cause harm.

CuratorialText: Sanctus emerged from Hammer’s initial engagement with medical themes, an interest incited in the early 1980s by the AIDS epidemic. Over the course of her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video with an unflinching focus on queer communities and on her encounters within the American medical system.
persona1_adapted: Sanctus grew out of Hammer’s first look at medical topics. She became interested in these themes in the early 1980s because of the AIDS crisis. Over her 50-year career, Hammer used photography, 16mm film, and video to focus honestly on queer communities and her own experiences with the American healthcare system.

CuratorialText: Step back in time and immerse yourself in the spiritual, domestic, and chivalric worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor present nearly 700 objects from the museum’s rich holdings of art from 1200 to 1600: monumental altarpieces, exquisite jewelry, and the beloved arms and armor collection.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Travel back in time and explore what life was like during the Middle Ages and Renaissance. In the Deering Family Galleries, you'll find almost 700 objects from the museum's collection, all made between the years 1200 and 1600. See giant altarpieces, beautiful jewelry, and the popular collection of weapons and armor.

CuratorialText: The sumptuous display begins with a large room featuring architectural sculpture, treasury objects, and early painting that convey the austere sanctity of earlier medieval art. The centerpiece of the gallery is the meticulously conserved Ayala Altarpiece, commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s funerary chapel in northern Spain. Spanning nearly 24 feet across and eight feet high, the painted wood altarpiece has undergone extensive treatment over the last three years and makes its restored debut in brilliant form. Two linked vaulted galleries showcase altarpieces and altarpiece fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute favorite Saint George and the Dragon by Bernat Martorell. Each is displayed to suggest its original religious space, highlighting the church as a center of public life.
persona1_adapted:
This gallery shows how art looked in churches hundreds of years ago. You’ll see stone carvings, fancy objects, and early paintings that feel serious and holy.

The main artwork here is the **Ayala Altarpiece**. It was made in 1396 for a Spanish church. A powerful man named the Chancellor of Castile paid for it to go in his family’s private chapel. It’s huge—almost 24 feet wide and 8 feet tall. Experts spent three years carefully cleaning and fixing it. Now it’s on display, looking bright and beautiful for the first time in a long time.

In the next two rooms, you’ll find more altarpieces and parts of altarpieces from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe. One famous piece is *Saint George and the Dragon* by Bernat Martorell. Each artwork is set up to remind you of the church where it was first seen. Back then, the church was the center of town life—a place where people gathered, prayed, and celebrated.

CuratorialText: The spaces that follow are more intimate, focusing on the texture of late Gothic and Renaissance life. One gallery presents luxury goods and the accessories of feasting, while another displays works of art for the bedchambers of Tuscany’s merchant elite. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and items of personal display, complete the domestic picture of the period.
persona1_adapted: The next rooms feel smaller and more personal. They show what life was like during the late Gothic and Renaissance periods. One room has fancy goods and items used for feasts. Another room shows artwork made for the bedrooms of rich merchants in Tuscany. You’ll also see everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and other things people wore to show off their style. Together, these items give you a fuller picture of what home life was like back then.

CuratorialText: An elegant rotunda introduces the collection of arms and armor with equipment for the bodyguards of four rulers. From here, the space opens to a gallery filled with weaponry and armor dominated by two armored figures on horseback—one dressed for battle, the other for sport—and two armed and costumed figures engaged in foot combat. Finally, a gallery lined with cabinetry features the art of the sword, luxury firearms, and equipment for the hunt extending through the Baroque era.
persona1_adapted: A fancy round room starts the tour of weapons and armor. It shows gear used by the bodyguards of four different rulers. From there, the space opens into a big gallery full of weapons and armor. The main attractions are two armored riders on horses—one ready for battle, the other for sport—and two costumed figures fighting on foot. At the end, a gallery with cabinets displays the art of sword-making, fancy guns, and hunting gear from the Baroque period.

CuratorialText: The new installation expands the display of art of this fascinating period sixfold, premiering several recently conserved works and many significant new acquisitions—an elegant late Gothic breastplate and backplate, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the architecture of the galleries propel visitors through time and from religious to secular works, while digital labels provide an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations serve as the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering, Sienese and Florentine gold-ground paintings and early Northern works given by Martin Ryerson, a terracotta altarpiece by Florentine Benedetto Buglioni and a South German carved and painted Saint Catherine from Kate Buckingham, and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to enchant.
persona1_adapted:
**The New Deering Family Galleries: A Journey Through Time**

The new Deering Family Galleries show six times more art than before. You’ll see recently cleaned and repaired works, plus many new pieces the museum just got. Highlights include:

- A fancy late Gothic chest and back plate (armor)
- A rare 1400s German portrait
- An amazing set of swords from the 1600s and 1700s

The galleries have been redesigned to guide you through different time periods and from religious art to everyday objects. Digital labels let you explore with videos and interactive features.

Many of the artworks come from generous collectors whose gifts built the museum’s collection. You’ll see:

- The Ayala Altarpiece and *Saint George and the Dragon* from Charles Deering
- Gold-backed paintings from Siena and Florence, plus early Northern European works from Martin Ryerson
- A clay altarpiece by Florentine artist Benedetto Buglioni and a carved and painted wooden statue of Saint Catherine from South Germany, given by Kate Buckingham
- George F. Harding’s impressive collection of weapons and armor

All these treasures come together in the new Deering Family Galleries. Step inside and feel like you’ve traveled back to a time that still fascinates us today.

CuratorialText: Jill Mulleady paints by breathing new life into unseen histories. Drawing from art-historical, cinematic, and literary sources, her memorable images can haunt the viewer long after encountering them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together approximately 20 paintings by the artist.
persona1_adapted: Jill Mulleady makes paintings that feel like stories you’ve never heard before. She gets ideas from old paintings, movies, and books. Her images can stick in your mind long after you’ve seen them. This show is Mulleady’s first solo exhibition at a big museum, and it brings together about 20 of her paintings.

CuratorialText: Mulleady uses various methods including staining, and at times incorporating materials such as copper, to achieve fragile, luminous surfaces that feel impossibly thin yet visually deep, holding tension between flatness and depth. Not limited to canvas or linen, she employs a range of supports, including glass and velvet. The resultant works balance different degrees of transparency and opacity and can be looked at and, sometimes, seen through.
persona1_adapted: Mulleady uses different techniques, like staining fabric or adding materials such as copper, to create surfaces that look delicate and glow. Her work feels incredibly thin but also deep, balancing between flatness and depth. She doesn’t just paint on canvas or linen—she also uses glass and velvet. The finished pieces mix see-through and solid parts, so you can look at them and, sometimes, look right through them.

CuratorialText: Mulleady’s paintings often depict scenes that are enigmatic and dangerous, showing figures at risk or under threat—from appetite, predation, or compulsion. While these ambiguous scenes may imply narratives, they likewise refuse tidy resolutions. Her imagery draws actively on painting lineages—allegory and the Baroque, Symbolist unease, the grotesque—recasting genres and canonical images for the present. In contrast to the purity and rationality of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady prioritizes a tradition in which chaos, instinct, and history drive art making.
persona1_adapted: Mulleady’s paintings often show strange and risky scenes. People in her art might be in danger—from hunger, being hunted, or feeling like they have to do something. These confusing scenes seem to tell a story, but they don’t give you a clear ending. Her work is inspired by older styles of painting, like dramatic Baroque art, unsettling Symbolist images, and weird, grotesque figures. She takes old ideas and makes them feel new again. Unlike the clean, simple, and logical art of the 20th century, Mulleady focuses on chaos, instincts, and history to create her work.

CuratorialText: As Mulleady has said, she works with existing languages in order to invent a new one, and her staged tableaux are particularly resonant within the Art Institute galleries, as they rhyme with many works in the collection that have informed the artist.
persona1_adapted: Mulleady takes languages that already exist and uses them to invent a brand new one. Her staged scenes feel especially at home in the Art Institute galleries because they echo many artworks in the collection that have inspired her.

CuratorialText: After decades as a representational painter, in her seventies Alma Thomas turned to abstraction, creating shimmering, mosaic-like fields of color with rhythmic dabs of paint that were often inspired by forms from nature. The artist had been fascinated with space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings often referenced America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Although she had never flown, Thomas began to paint as if she were in an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”
persona1_adapted:
Alma Thomas spent most of her life painting realistic pictures—things like people, places, and objects you could recognize. But in her seventies, she switched to abstract art. Instead of painting real things, she created colorful, shimmering patterns made of tiny dabs of paint. These patterns often looked like mosaics and were inspired by shapes in nature.

Thomas was also really into space exploration. Starting in the late 1960s, she got excited about NASA’s Apollo missions that sent astronauts to the moon. Even though she never flew in a plane or a spaceship, she started painting as if she were looking down from high above. She wanted to capture the way light and color shift when you’re moving fast. “You look down on things,” she said. “You streak through the clouds so fast… You see only streaks of color.”

CuratorialText: Starry Night and the Astronauts evokes the open expanse and celestial patterns of a night sky, but despite its narrative title, the work could also be read as an aerial view of a watery surface, playing with our sense of immersion within an otherwise flat picture plane. The viewer is immersed not only in the sense of organic expanse that this painting achieves, however, but also in an encounter with Thomas’s process: the surface here is clearly constructed stroke by stroke. Meanwhile, the glimpses of raw canvas between each primary-colored mark seem as vivid as the applied paint itself—almost as if the composition were backlit. Thomas relied on the enlivening properties of color throughout her late-blooming career. “Color is life,” she once proclaimed, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was created in 1972, when the artist was eighty. In the same year, she became the first African American woman to receive a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.
persona1_adapted:
**Starry Night and the Astronauts**  
*By Alma Thomas, 1972*

At first glance, this painting looks like a sparkling night sky full of stars and patterns. But look again—it could also be a view of water from above, with ripples and waves. The artist, Alma Thomas, plays with what we see, making us feel like we’re floating in the scene, even though the painting is flat.

If you look closely, you can see how Thomas built the painting one small brushstroke at a time. Between the bright colors, you’ll notice tiny bits of bare canvas. These gaps make the painting seem to glow from behind, almost like it has its own light.

Thomas believed that color was full of life. She once said, “Color is life, and light is the mother of color.” She spent her whole career exploring how colors can make us feel.

This painting was made in 1972, when Thomas was 80 years old. That same year, she made history: she became the first African American woman to have a solo show at a major art museum—the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.

CuratorialText: Perhaps the most famous depiction of a bedroom in Western art history, this vibrant painting documents Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping quarters in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The composition exists in three versions, the first of which Van Gogh conceived in October 1888, a month after he moved into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its bold colors, thick and broken brushwork, and sharply receding lines, the picture might suggest a nervous energy. But the artist understood it as a calming and restful image.
persona1_adapted:
This painting is one of the most famous bedrooms in art history. It shows Vincent van Gogh’s own room in his “Yellow House” in Arles, France. Van Gogh made three versions of this painting. He painted the first one in October 1888, just a month after moving in.

In a letter to his brother Theo, Van Gogh described the colors he used: pale lilac walls, faded red floor, chrome yellow chairs and bed, pale lemon green pillows and sheet, blood-red bedspread, orange dressing table, blue washbasin, and green window.

The painting uses bright colors, thick brushstrokes, and lines that seem to pull back sharply. This might make the room feel busy or nervous. But Van Gogh saw it as a calm and restful scene.

CuratorialText: The painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of the scene, made nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. He produced a third, smaller version at the same time as a gift for his mother and sister.
persona1_adapted: This painting is Van Gogh’s second version of the same scene. He made it in September 1889, almost a year after the first one. Around the same time, he also made a smaller, third version as a gift for his mother and sister.

CuratorialText: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India, which was, as a result of settlers from Srivijaya (Indonesia), one of the few places where Buddhism was still flourishing in the twelfth century. The Buddha—with his elongated earlobes, the wheel marks on his palms, the urna between his brows, and the cranial protuberance covered with snail-shell curls—is seated in the posture of meditation, with his hands resting on his lap (dhyanamudra), wearing a seemingly diaphanous monastic garment. As in other images from Nagapattinam, a flame emerges out of the Buddha’s cranial protuberance, probably signifying wisdom. This monumental granite sculpture originally would have graced a monastic site at Nagapattinam, which is also well known for its Buddhist bronzes. The Tamil inscription covering its back is no longer legible.
persona1_adapted:
This Buddha statue shows a person meditating. It comes from a town called Nagapattinam, on the coast of southern India. In the 1100s, Buddhism was still strong there because people from Indonesia (the Srivijaya kingdom) had settled in the area.

The Buddha has several special features. His earlobes are long. There are wheel marks on his palms. He has a small dot (called an *urna*) between his eyebrows. On top of his head is a bump covered with curls that look like snail shells. He sits in a meditation pose, with his hands resting in his lap. He wears a thin, almost see-through monk’s robe.

Like other statues from Nagapattinam, a flame rises from the bump on his head. This flame probably stands for wisdom. The statue is made of granite and is very large. It would have stood in a monastery in Nagapattinam, a place famous for its bronze Buddhist statues. There is a Tamil inscription carved on the back, but it is too worn to read anymore.

CuratorialText: Many Mansions is the first in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large-scale paintings depicting public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles such as Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in Many Mansions, Stateway Gardens. Struck by the absurdity of the term “garden” to describe these failed solutions to low-income housing, Marshall was inspired to represent the profound contradictions of living in such an environment. Many Mansions is filled with ironic and startling juxtapositions of the real and artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting, ebony-skinned figures dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title of the painting, visible on the red ribbon above, is a variation on Christ’s oft-quoted remark found in John [14:2]: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”
persona1_adapted:
**Many Mansions** is the first painting in a series of five big artworks by Kerry James Marshall. These paintings show public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles—places like Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, and Stateway Gardens (which is the one in *Many Mansions*).

Marshall thought it was strange and kind of ridiculous to call these places “gardens.” After all, they were supposed to be solutions for low-income housing, but they didn’t really work out that well. So he decided to paint the weird mix of real life and fake happiness you might find there.

The painting is full of surprising contrasts. For example, the landscape looks unnaturally cheerful, but then you see three dark-skinned figures dressed in their fancy Sunday best—like they’re going to church. It’s a little haunting and a lot ironic.

The title *Many Mansions* comes from a famous line in the Bible (John 14:2), where Jesus says, “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.” You can see the title written on the red ribbon at the top of the painting.

CuratorialText: In Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye intensely studies this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. Featuring people of every age and social class on the banks of the River Seine, A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884 has captivated visitors ever since its arrival at the museum in 1924.
persona1_adapted: In *Ferris Bueller’s Day Off*, Ferris’s best friend Cameron stares closely at this giant painting—it’s almost ten feet wide! The scene takes place at the Art Institute. The painting shows people of all ages and social classes hanging out by the River Seine. Ever since it arrived at the museum in 1924, *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884* has amazed visitors.

CuratorialText: If we, like Cameron, come closer to the painting, figures and forms dissolve into dots and dashes of complementary colors laid side by side, characteristic of Seurat’s pointillist technique. Many smaller painted and drawn sketches and several larger canvases, in which Seurat laid out the parameters for the landscape and figures, led up to this majestically composed scene. Seurat returned to the work two years after its start date, amplifying the silhouettes of some figures and adding others. Some of these, like the monkey on the leash, seem so integral to the final composition that it is hard to imagine them as add-ons, but others, like the man carrying a rolled newspaper in the furthest distance, are barely noticeable. To the artist, however, every decision was essential to his aim of making a painting of modern life equivalent to a classical Greek frieze.
persona1_adapted:
If you step closer to the painting—just like Cameron does—the people and shapes start to break apart into tiny dots and dashes of color placed side by side. This is a special technique called pointillism, and it’s what makes Seurat’s work so unique.

Before he painted this big, beautiful scene, Seurat made many smaller sketches and drawings to plan out the landscape and the people. He even came back to the painting two years after he started it, adding darker outlines to some figures and putting in new ones. Some of these additions, like the monkey on a leash, feel so important to the final picture that it’s hard to believe they weren’t there from the beginning. Others, like the man carrying a rolled-up newspaper way in the background, are so small you might not even notice them.

But for Seurat, every single detail mattered. He wanted this painting to show modern life in a way that felt as timeless and important as an ancient Greek frieze.

CuratorialText: Laboring at a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and Nahua, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With expertise and dexterity, Jiménez threads weft through warp, slowly building her intricately patterned textile, the completed portion resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who came to prominence in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk cultures, distinct from the legacies of Spanish colonialism. By centering Jiménez in Weaving, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.
persona1_adapted:
In this painting, a woman named Luz Jiménez is working at a backstrap loom. She is a master weaver and a member of the Nahua people, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With skill and care, she threads the weft (the horizontal yarn) through the warp (the vertical yarn), slowly creating a detailed, patterned cloth. The part she has already finished rests on her lap.

The artist, Diego Rivera, was a famous modernist painter who became well-known after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20). He wanted to create a new vision of Mexican identity—one that celebrated Indigenous and folk traditions, instead of the influence of Spanish colonialism. By putting Luz Jiménez at the center of this painting, Rivera was saying that her culture and traditions were also part of his own.

CuratorialText: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest, most ambitious work and has been a visitor favorite since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964, well before the artist’s contributions to the Impressionist movement were widely recognized. Caillebotte captured a busy intersection of Paris that was only a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely depict areas beyond his familiar neighborhoods.
persona1_adapted: This giant painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s biggest and most important work. It has been a favorite of visitors ever since the Art Institute got it in 1964—long before people realized how much Caillebotte helped shape the Impressionist movement. He painted a busy street corner in Paris, just a short walk from his home. In fact, most of his paintings of Paris show places he knew well.

CuratorialText: Caillebotte demonstrated his affinity with the Impressionists through his subject of modern urban life but approached these settings with a more Realist technique. The dramatic cropping of the scene may have been inspired by the newly popularized medium of photography; the two central figures seem to be walking straight into the viewer as something captures their attention outside of the frame. At the time, artists rarely attempted to depict people in motion; Caillebotte carefully placed each of the background figures to evoke the feeling of bustling city streets. When he started exhibiting with the Impressionists, Caillebotte also focused on portraying the atmosphere of his scenes; here, instead of explicitly painting raindrops to indicate the weather, he suggests it through the incorporation of newly available retractable umbrellas and the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.
persona1_adapted: Caillebotte shared the Impressionists' interest in painting modern city life, but he used a more realistic style. The way he cuts off the scene—almost like a photograph—was probably inspired by the new invention of photography. The two main figures seem to be walking right toward us, looking at something outside the picture. Back then, artists rarely tried to show people in motion. Caillebotte carefully placed every person in the background to make the street feel busy and alive. When he started showing his work with the Impressionists, he also focused on capturing the mood of the scene. Instead of painting raindrops directly, he shows it's raining by including new retractable umbrellas and the soft reflections on the wet cobblestones.

CuratorialText: Richard Hunt created Hero Construction in 1958, one year after graduating from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is composed of found objects—old pipes, bits of metal, and automobile parts—that the artist discovered in junkyards and on the street. Using a torch like a paintbrush, Hunt welded these elements into a form that is abstract yet recognizable.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Richard Hunt made *Hero Construction* in 1958, just one year after finishing art school in Chicago. To build it, he used old pipes, metal scraps, and car parts he found in junkyards and on the street. Hunt used a welding torch like a paintbrush, fusing these pieces together into a shape that is abstract but still looks like something you can recognize.

CuratorialText: Hunt was fascinated with mythology, and Hero Construction can be related to other sculptures past and present, from ancient statues of Greek heroes to modern monuments of statesmen. The modest but monumental stance of the figure suggests a contemporary hero who maintains strength in the face of the uncertainties and dangers of the present age.
persona1_adapted: Hunt loved stories about gods and heroes. This sculpture, *Hero Construction*, connects to other statues from long ago and today—from ancient Greek heroes to modern monuments of leaders. The figure stands in a simple but powerful way, showing a present-day hero who stays strong even when facing the unknowns and dangers of our world.

CuratorialText: The Mexicas, or Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This monument uses glyphs to record the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, 1 Rabbit represents the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are inscribed in the corners, while the current cosmic era 4 Movement appears in the center. 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) indicate the date July 15, 1503, when the ninth emperor Moctezuma Xocoyotzin was coronated. Just as these glyphs recurred throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago: 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.
persona1_adapted:
**The Aztec Sun Stone: A Giant History Book in Stone**

The Mexica people (also called the Aztecs) ruled central Mexico from 1428 to 1521. This huge stone monument is like a history book carved in rock. It uses picture symbols called **glyphs** to tell the story of the Mexica universe up to the year 1503.

- **On the back** of the stone, the glyph **1 Rabbit** marks the very beginning of time.
- **On the front**, the four corners show the four worlds that existed before ours. In the center is the current world, called **4 Movement**.
- The glyphs **1 Crocodile** (at the top) and **11 Reed** (in the square frame) together stand for the date **July 15, 1503**. That’s the day the ninth emperor, Moctezuma Xocoyotzin, was crowned.

These same symbols weren’t just used in the Aztec capital, Tenochtitlan. You can still find them today in Chicago! Look for **4 Movement** in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s lively Mexican community.

CuratorialText: A palpable energy and sense of movement enliven Nightlife, Archibald Motley’s portrayal of a crowded cabaret in the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville in Chicago. With stylized figures, an array of diagonal lines, and heightened colors keyed to shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the exuberance of city dwellers out on the town. Motley created a network of gestures and glances among the people, drawing attention to the various social interactions that animate the scene. The composition is an exploration of artificial lighting. Motley was inspired, in part, to paint Nightlife after having seen Edward Hopper’s Nighthawks (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the prior year.
persona1_adapted: **Nightlife** by Archibald Motley shows a busy cabaret in Chicago's Bronzeville neighborhood. The painting feels full of energy and movement. Motley used bright colors like magenta and violet, along with diagonal lines and stylized figures, to capture the excitement of people enjoying a night out. He also created a web of looks and gestures between the people, highlighting all the different social interactions happening at once. The painting is also a study of artificial light. Motley was partly inspired to make *Nightlife* after seeing Edward Hopper’s painting *Nighthawks*, which had just been added to the Art Institute’s collection the year before.

CuratorialText: Although influenced by Abstract Expressionist artists in New York in the early 1950s, Joan Mitchell did not prioritize self-expression: her often exuberant abstractions were “about landscape, not about me,” she once explained. Mitchell painted large, light-filled canvases animated by loosely applied skeins of bright color—here infused with the energy of a large metropolis. The title suggests a relationship between the painting’s network of pigments and the nerves or arteries of an urban space. The sense of spontaneity conveyed in City Landscape, however, belies Mitchell’s methods. Unlike many of her contemporaries, who were dubbed “action painters,” Mitchell worked slowly and deliberately. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”
persona1_adapted:
Joan Mitchell was inspired by artists in New York in the early 1950s, but she didn't use her art just to express her own feelings. Instead, she said her bright, energetic paintings were "about landscape, not about me." Her large canvases are filled with light and loose, colorful lines that feel alive—like the energy of a big city. The title *City Landscape* hints that the painting's web of colors might remind you of the nerves or streets of a city.

Even though the painting looks spontaneous, Mitchell didn't work quickly. Unlike other artists of her time who were called "action painters," she worked slowly and carefully. She once explained, "I paint a little. Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do."

CuratorialText: Around 1920 Georgia O’Keeffe painted a number of oils exploring, as she later recalled, “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In Blue and Green Music, O’Keeffe’s colors and forms simultaneously suggest the natural world and evoke the experience of sound. She was drawn to the theories of the Russian Expressionist painter Vasily Kandinsky, who, in his 1912 text Concerning the Spiritual in Art, argued that visual artists should emulate music in order to achieve pure expression free of literary references.
persona1_adapted: In the 1920s, artist Georgia O’Keeffe painted a series of works that explored a cool idea: “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In her painting *Blue and Green Music*, she used colors and shapes that remind us of nature, but also make us feel like we’re hearing music. O’Keeffe was inspired by the ideas of Russian artist Vasily Kandinsky. In his 1912 book, *Concerning the Spiritual in Art*, Kandinsky said that visual artists should try to be like musicians—creating pure feelings without needing to tell a specific story or use obvious symbols.

CuratorialText: Henri Matisse considered Bathers by a River to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it facilitated the evolution of the artist’s style over the course of nearly a decade. Originally, the work was related to a 1909 commission by the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three pastoral images, though Shchukin decided to purchase only two works, Dance II and Music (both State Hermitage Museum, Saint Petersburg).
persona1_adapted: Henri Matisse thought *Bathers by a River* was one of the five most important works he ever made. And he had a good reason: it helped change his style over almost ten years. At first, the painting was part of a project from 1909. A Russian collector named Sergei Shchukin asked Matisse to make two large paintings for the staircase of his home in Moscow. Matisse came up with three nature scenes, but Shchukin only bought two of them: *Dance II* and *Music* (both now in the State Hermitage Museum in Saint Petersburg).

CuratorialText: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas, the rejected third image, altering the idyllic scene and changing the pastel palette to reflect his new interest in Cubism. He reordered the composition, making the figures more columnar, with faceless, ovoid heads. Over the next years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical bands and turned the formerly blue river into a thick black vertical band. With its restricted palette and severely abstracted forms, Bathers by a River is far removed from Dance II and Music, which convey a graceful lyricism. The sobriety and hint of danger in Bathers by a River may in part reflect the artist’s concerns during the terrible, war-torn period during which he completed it.
persona1_adapted:
Four years later, Matisse went back to this painting—the one that had been rejected. He changed the peaceful scene and swapped the soft pastel colors for something new, inspired by a style called Cubism. He rearranged the picture, making the figures look more like tall columns, with blank, egg-shaped heads. Over the next few years, he turned the background into four simple vertical stripes and changed the blue river into a thick black stripe.

With its limited colors and highly simplified shapes, *Bathers by a River* feels very different from *Dance II* and *Music*, which are graceful and flowing. The serious, slightly dangerous mood of *Bathers by a River* may partly reflect how Matisse felt during the terrible war years when he finished it.

CuratorialText: One of four posts sculpted for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this evocative image of the Yoruba concept of a divine ruler is a masterwork of Olowe of Ise. The renowned artist’s dynamic style can be distinguished by the interrelationships between figures, their exaggerated proportions, and the use of open space. The sculptor’s diminutive portrayal of the king suggests his dependence on others, and his crown has been emphasized to evoke the spiritual power residing within it. The towering image of the king’s senior wife standing behind him underscores her superior status as a guardian of her husband’s interests, endowed with keen political understanding and supernatural knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his enthronement ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.
persona1_adapted:
This carved post is one of four that once stood in the palace of the king (called the *ogoga*) of Ikere, a city in Nigeria. It shows the Yoruba people’s idea of a divine ruler. The artist, Olowe of Ise, was famous for his skill. You can spot his style in how the figures relate to each other, their oversized features, and the empty spaces he left around them.

In this carving, the king is shown smaller than you might expect. This isn’t meant to make him look weak—it shows that he depends on the people around him. His crown is extra large and detailed to remind us of the spiritual power it holds.

The tall woman standing behind the king is his senior wife. She looks powerful because she was. She guarded her husband’s interests and was known for her political wisdom and spiritual knowledge. In fact, she would have placed the crown on his head during his coronation ceremony.

At the king’s feet are two smaller figures: one of his junior wives and Eshu, the trickster god who plays the flute.

CuratorialText: About Nighthawks Edward Hopper recollected, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In an all-night diner, three customers sit at the counter opposite a server, each appear to be lost in thought and disengaged from one another. The composition is tightly organized and spare in details: there is no entrance to the establishment, no debris on the streets. Through harmonious geometric forms and the glow of the diner’s electric lighting, Hopper created a serene, beautiful, yet enigmatic scene. Although inspired by a restaurant Hopper had seen on Greenwich Avenue in New York, the painting is not a realistic transcription of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the figures, their relationships, and this imagined world.
persona1_adapted:
**About *Nighthawks***  
Edward Hopper once said, “I think I was painting the loneliness of a big city without really meaning to.” The painting shows an all-night diner. Inside, three customers sit at the counter across from a worker. Everyone seems lost in their own thoughts, not talking to each other.  

The scene is simple and carefully arranged. There’s no door to the diner, and the streets are clean and empty. Hopper used smooth shapes and the warm glow of electric lights to create a calm, beautiful, but mysterious mood.  

Even though Hopper got the idea from a real restaurant in New York City, the painting isn’t a perfect copy of that place. Instead, it’s an imagined world that makes us wonder: Who are these people? What are they thinking? What’s their story?

CuratorialText: The Assumption of the Virgin was the central image of El Greco’s first major commission, an altarpiece for the Church of Santo Domingo el Antiguo, a convent in Toledo, Spain.
