The painting’s subject, the raising of the Virgin Mary’s body into heaven, relates to the function of the church as the funerary monument of a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva; the iconography of the entire commission focuses on death and salvation.
persona1_adapted: This painting, *The Assumption of the Virgin*, was the centerpiece of El Greco’s first big job. He made it for a church in Toledo, Spain, that was also a convent. The painting shows the Virgin Mary being lifted up to heaven. This idea connects to the church’s purpose: it was built as a memorial for a rich noblewoman named Doña Maria de Silva. The whole artwork is about death and being saved.

CuratorialText: El Greco used broad, free brushwork, flickering hues, rich color harmonies, and bold figural arrangements to arouse devotional fervor in the viewer and impart a deep sense of faith. The composition is divided into two zones: the earthly sphere of the apostles and the celestial realm of the angels. El Greco masterfully compressed many figures into a tall and narrow format: In the earthly zone, the apostles’ faces and gestures express a range of emotions through their dynamic, agitated gestures and poses. They are so individually considered that it seems El Greco studied actual studio models. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, graceful, and direct in their focus toward the looming figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.
persona1_adapted: El Greco used bold, quick brushstrokes, bright colors, and dramatic poses to make viewers feel strong religious emotions. The painting is split into two parts: the earthly world of the apostles below and the heavenly world of the angels above. He packed many figures into a tall, narrow space. In the lower part, the apostles show different feelings through their lively, excited gestures and expressions. Each one looks so real that it seems El Greco used live models. In contrast, the angels above are calm and graceful, all looking up at the Virgin Mary as she rises into heaven.

CuratorialText: Katsushika Hokusai’s much celebrated series, Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (Fugaku sanjûrokkei), was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This tour-de-force series established the popularity of landscape prints, which continues to this day. Perhaps most striking about the series is Hokusai’s copious use of the newly affordable Berlin blue pigment, featured in many of the compositions in the color for the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the protagonist in each scene, viewed from afar or up close, during various weather conditions and seasons, and from all directions.
persona1_adapted: Katsushika Hokusai was 70 years old when he started his famous series, *Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji*, in 1830. This amazing set of prints helped make landscape art popular, and people still love it today. One of the coolest things about the series is how Hokusai used a new, cheaper blue pigment called Berlin blue. You can see it in the sky and water of many prints. In every scene, Mount Fuji is the main focus—sometimes far away, sometimes close up, and in all kinds of weather and seasons.

CuratorialText: The most famous image from the set is the “Great Wave” (Kanagawa oki nami ura), in which a diminutive Mount Fuji can be seen in the distance under the crest of a giant wave. The three impressions of Hokusai’s Great Wave in the Art Institute are all later impressions than the first state of the design.
persona1_adapted: The most famous picture in the series is the "Great Wave." In it, you can see a tiny Mount Fuji far away, beneath the top of a giant wave. The Art Institute has three copies of Hokusai's Great Wave, but all of them were printed after the very first version of the design.

CuratorialText: Pablo Picasso made The Old Guitarist while working in Barcelona. In the paintings of his Blue Period (1901-04), the artist restricted himself to a cold, monochromatic blue palette, flattened forms, and emotional, psychological themes of human misery and alienation related to the work of such artists as Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The elongated, angular figure of the blind musician also relates to Picasso’s interest in Spanish art and, in particular, the great 16th-century artist El Greco. The image reﬂects the twenty-two-year-old Picasso’s personal struggle and sympathy for the plight of the downtrodden; he knew what it was like to be poor, having been nearly penniless during all of 1902.
persona1_adapted:
Pablo Picasso painted *The Old Guitarist* when he was living in Barcelona. During his "Blue Period" (1901–1904), he mostly used cold blue colors, made his figures look flat, and focused on sad, lonely themes about human suffering. This style was influenced by artists like Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin.

The old, blind musician in the painting has a long, skinny body. That style shows Picasso’s interest in Spanish art, especially the work of a famous 16th-century artist named El Greco.

This painting also reflects Picasso’s own life. He was only 22 years old when he made it, and he knew what it was like to be poor. He had almost no money during 1902, so he understood the struggles of people who were down on their luck.

CuratorialText: Illustrator Norman Rockwell was one of the most popular artists of the 20th century, beloved in particular for the hundreds of cover images he created for the Saturday Evening Post. The Dugout is the largest of several studies for one such cover, published on September 4, 1948. It was inspired by a springtime doubleheader that he attended during which the hometown Boston Braves swept the visiting Chicago Cubs. The artist created an image contrasting dejected Cubs players and elated Braves fans.
persona1_adapted:
Norman Rockwell was one of the most famous artists of the 1900s. He’s best known for the many cover pictures he made for a magazine called the *Saturday Evening Post*. 

*The Dugout* is the biggest version of several practice drawings he did for one of those covers. The cover came out on September 4, 1948. Rockwell got the idea for this picture after watching a spring baseball doubleheader. In those games, the hometown team, the Boston Braves, beat the visiting Chicago Cubs both times. 

In the painting, Rockwell shows the sad, disappointed Cubs players in the dugout next to the happy, excited Braves fans.

CuratorialText: Rockwell staged the entire scene, which enabled him to take photographs for later reference. He recruited several family members of Braves players to act as fans. In the dugout, he positioned (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The figure of the forlorn batboy, however, was not a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to don the Cubs uniform and pose.
persona1_adapted: Rockwell set up the whole scene so he could take photos to work from later. He asked some family members of Braves players to pretend to be fans. In the dugout, he placed (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. But the sad-looking batboy wasn’t actually a Cubs player. Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to put on a Cubs uniform and pose for the picture.

CuratorialText: By the time the issue was published, the Cubs had fallen to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped bolster their growing reputation as the “loveable losers,” and it became one of the most recognized sports images of the 20th century.
persona1_adapted: By the time the magazine came out, the Cubs had dropped to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped build their image as the “loveable losers,” and it became one of the most famous sports pictures of the 1900s.

CuratorialText: The monumental stacks that Claude Monet depicted in his series Stacks of Wheat rose fifteen to twenty feet and stood just outside the artist’s farmhouse at Giverny. Through 1890 and 1891, he worked on this series both in the field, painting simultaneously at several easels, and in the studio, refining pictorial harmonies. In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these canvases next to each other in one small room in the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. An unprecedented critical and financial success, the exhibition marked a breakthrough in Monet’s career, as well as in the history of French art. In this view, and in nearly all of the autumn views in the series, the conical tops of the stacks break the horizon and push into the sky. But in most of the winter views, which constitute the core of the series, the stacks seem wrapped by bands of hill and field, as if bedded down for the season. For Monet, the stack was a resonant symbol of sustenance and survival. He followed this group with further series depicting poplars, the facade of Rouen Cathedral, and, later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute has the largest group of Monet’s Stacks of Wheat in the world.
persona1_adapted:
Claude Monet painted a famous series of pictures called *Stacks of Wheat*. The real wheat stacks were huge—about 15 to 20 feet tall—and stood right outside his farmhouse in Giverny, France.

From 1890 to 1891, Monet worked on this series both outdoors and in his studio. Outside, he would paint at several easels at the same time to catch different light and weather. Inside, he worked on making the colors and shapes fit together just right.

In May 1891, Monet hung 15 of these paintings side by side in a small room at a Paris gallery. The show was a huge hit—both with critics and with buyers. It was a big moment for Monet’s career and for French art.

In this painting, and in most of the autumn ones, the pointed tops of the wheat stacks rise above the horizon and reach into the sky. But in the winter paintings—which are the heart of the series—the stacks look wrapped by hills and fields, as if they are tucked in for the cold season.

For Monet, the wheat stack was a powerful symbol of food and survival. After this series, he painted more groups of pictures showing poplars, the front of Rouen Cathedral, and later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute of Chicago has the largest collection of Monet’s *Stacks of Wheat* in the world.

CuratorialText: In 1886 Vincent van Gogh left his native Holland and settled in Paris, where his beloved brother Theo was a dealer in paintings. Van Gogh was apparently inspired by the energy of the city and by his introduction to Impressionism, and he created at least twenty-four self-portraits during his two-year stay in the French capital.
persona1_adapted: In 1886, Vincent van Gogh moved from his home country of the Netherlands to Paris, France. His brother Theo, whom he loved very much, worked there as an art dealer. Van Gogh was excited by the busy energy of the city and by a new style of painting called Impressionism. During the two years he lived in Paris, he painted at least twenty-four pictures of himself.

CuratorialText: This early example is modest in size and was painted on prepared artist’s board rather than canvas. Its densely dabbed brushwork, which became a hallmark of Van Gogh’s style, reﬂects the artist’s response to Georges Seurat’s revolutionary pointillist technique in A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884. What was for Seurat a method based on the cool objectivity of science became in Van Gogh’s hands an intense emotional language. The surface of the painting dances with particles of color—intense greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this dazzling array of staccato dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.
persona1_adapted: This early painting is small and was made on a special artist’s board, not on canvas. Van Gogh used thick, dabbed brushstrokes—a style he became famous for. He was inspired by Georges Seurat’s new pointillist technique, which used tiny dots of color in a painting called *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884*. But while Seurat’s method was cool and scientific, Van Gogh turned it into something full of strong emotion. The surface of the painting seems to dance with specks of bright greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Standing out among all these quick dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and their intense stare. Van Gogh once wrote to his brother Theo, “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals. No matter how grand a cathedral is, a human soul—even a poor streetwalker’s—is more interesting to me.” After living in Paris, Van Gogh moved to the small town of Arles in the south of France for fifteen months. By the time he died in 1890, he had been making art for only five years.

CuratorialText: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very brief. From Paris, he traveled to the southern town of Arles for ﬁ ft een months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for just ﬁve years.
persona1_adapted: Van Gogh didn't paint for very long. After leaving Paris, he spent about fifteen months in a small town in southern France called Arles. By the time he died in 1890, he had only been seriously making art for five years.

CuratorialText: In American Gothic, Grant Wood directly evoked images of an earlier generation by featuring a farmer and his daughter posed stiffly and dressed as if they were, as the artist put it, “tintypes from my old family album.” They stand outside of their home, built in an 1880s style known as Carpenter Gothic. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When it was exhibited at the Art Institute in 1930, the painting became an instant sensation, its ambiguity prompting viewers to speculate about the figures and their story. Many understood the work to be a satirical comment on midwesterners out of step with a modernizing world. Yet Wood intended it to convey a positive image of rural American values, offering a vision of reassurance at the beginning of the Great Depression.
persona1_adapted:
In *American Gothic*, Grant Wood painted a farmer and his daughter standing stiffly in front of their house. They look like old-fashioned photos from a family album. The house is built in a style called Carpenter Gothic, which was popular in the 1880s. Wood got the idea for the house when he visited Eldon, Iowa.

When the painting was shown at the Art Institute in 1930, it became an instant hit. People couldn't agree on what it meant. Some thought it was making fun of midwesterners who seemed stuck in the past. But Wood actually wanted to show a positive view of rural American values. He painted it at the start of the Great Depression, when people needed something reassuring to look at.

CuratorialText: Little did Claude Monet know that the water garden he created three years after buying his Giverny, France, property in 1890 would become his primary inspiration over the next two and a half decades. These paintings, numbering around 250, mark Monet’s artistic journey from more straightforward depictions of the pond spanned by the wooden Japanese bridge to the monumental and near-abstract series on the water lily theme he made in preparation for his murals at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris between 1914 and 1926.
persona1_adapted: When Claude Monet bought his house in Giverny, France, in 1890, he had no idea that the water garden he built three years later would become his biggest source of inspiration for the next 25 years. He went on to paint about 250 pictures of that garden. At first, his paintings showed the pond and the wooden Japanese bridge in a simple, clear way. But over time, his style changed. His later paintings of water lilies became huge and almost abstract. He made these final works as studies for giant murals that now hang in the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris, which he worked on from 1914 until his death in 1926.

CuratorialText: Water Lilies, one of a group of paintings on the subject made between 1903 and 1908, comes at the midpoint of Monet’s developing style and spatial experimentations. The nearly square format underscores his move away from painting the conventional zones of land, sky, and water to focus solely on the water’s surface. Clusters of water lilies at bottom left and top right frame a watery path, while the water’s surface reflects trees and clouds. Although the dreamlike quality of floating forms might seem to be a natural development for the artist, he considered these works “an obsession,” a sentiment borne out by technical examinations on the Art Institute’s canvas, revealing many significant changes made to the painting in progress.
persona1_adapted:
**Water Lilies**  
This painting is part of a series Claude Monet worked on between 1903 and 1908. It shows a key moment in his career when he was trying new ideas and playing with space in his art.

The painting is almost square, which was unusual at the time. Instead of painting the usual land, sky, and water separately, Monet focused only on the water’s surface. You can see clusters of water lilies in the bottom left and top right corners, creating a kind of watery path in the middle. The water also reflects trees and clouds above.

Even though the painting looks calm and dreamy, Monet didn’t find it easy to make. He called these works “an obsession.” Scientists who studied this painting at the Art Institute of Chicago found that Monet made many big changes to it while he was working on it.

CuratorialText: Pierre-Auguste Renoir considered the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou (a village along the Seine River) the perfect spot to enjoy nature and good company away from the urban distractions of Paris. His painting Two Sisters (On the Terrace), which he exhibited at the Impressionist exhibition of 1882, is among the last of his paintings set there.
persona1_adapted: Pierre-Auguste Renoir loved the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou, a small village by the Seine River. For him, it was the perfect place to enjoy nature and hang out with friends, far from the busy city life of Paris. His painting *Two Sisters (On the Terrace)* shows this spot. It was shown at the Impressionist exhibition in 1882 and is one of the last paintings he made there.

CuratorialText: Sometimes, as in his most famous work on this subject, Luncheon of the Boating Party (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For Two Sisters, however, despite the title of the painting, he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captivates. Her oversize headdress composed of a floral bouquet is echoed in the brightly colored skeins of yarn in the basket that she gently touches. As was typical for the artist, Renoir painted the faces of his models with controlled and precise brushwork creating a porcelain-like surface that contrasts brilliantly with the diaphanous, impressionist backdrop of river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the railing of the terrace.
persona1_adapted:
In his most famous painting about this topic, *Luncheon of the Boating Party* (1882), Renoir used his close friends as models. But for *Two Sisters*, even though the title suggests a family bond, he paid two unrelated girls to pose. He wanted to create a calm, balanced scene that connects youth and nature.

The older girl wears a blue flannel boating outfit and a fancy red hat. But it’s the younger girl who really catches your eye. Her oversized headdress is made of flowers, and the bright yarn in the basket beside her matches the colors of the bouquet. She gently touches the yarn, adding a soft, playful feel.

As he often did, Renoir painted the girls’ faces with careful, smooth brushstrokes, making their skin look like porcelain. This contrasts sharply with the blurry, dreamy background of the river, boats, and blooming trees beyond the terrace railing.

CuratorialText: Willem de Kooning was a central figure in Abstract Expressionism, an art movement that espoused the painterly actions of the artist as a sign of his or her emotions. De Kooning completed Excavation in June 1950, just in time for it to be exhibited in the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale. His largest painting up to that date, the work exemplifies the Dutch-born innovator’s style, with its expressive brushwork and distinctive organization of space into sliding planes with open contours. According to the artist, the point of departure for the painting was an image of women working in a rice field in Bitter Rice, a 1949 Neorealist film by Italian director Giuseppe de Santis. The mobile structure of hooked calligraphic lines defines anatomical parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to dance across the painted surface, revealing the particular tension between abstraction and figuration that is inherent in de Kooning’s work. The original white pigment has yellowed over the years, diluting somewhat the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that punctuate the composition. Aptly titled, the painting reflects de Kooning’s technically masterful painting process: an intensive building up of the surface and scraping down of its paint layers, often for months, until the desired effect was achieved.
persona1_adapted:
Willem de Kooning was a key artist in a movement called Abstract Expressionism. In this style, artists used bold, energetic brushstrokes to show their emotions. De Kooning finished his painting *Excavation* in June 1950, just in time to show it at the famous Venice Biennale art exhibition. It was the biggest painting he had ever made.

The painting shows de Kooning’s unique style: expressive brushwork and a space that feels like sliding, open shapes. The artist said he got the idea from a movie called *Bitter Rice*, which shows women working in a rice field. In the painting, you can see curving, hook-like lines that suggest body parts—like noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—along with bird and fish shapes. These forms seem to dance across the canvas, creating a tension between abstract shapes and recognizable figures.

Over time, the white paint has yellowed, softening the bright reds, blues, yellows, and pinks. The title *Excavation* fits because de Kooning built up layers of paint and then scraped them down again, working for months until he got the effect he wanted.

CuratorialText: White Crucifixion is the first in Marc Chagall’s series of compositions that feature Jesus as a Jewish martyr and dramatically call attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany at the hands of the National Socialist Party. Chagall stressed Jesus’s religious identity by depicting him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish garments. The surrounding images show the devastation of pogroms, violent attacks against Jewish communities often organized or sanctioned by local governments. Combining the Crucifixion with contemporary events, Chagall’s painting links the martyred Jesus with the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe and implicitly compares the Nazis with Jesus’s tormentors.
persona1_adapted:
**White Crucifixion** is the first painting in a series by Marc Chagall that shows Jesus as a Jewish martyr. The artwork draws attention to the persecution and suffering of Jewish people in 1930s Germany under the Nazi Party.

Chagall highlights Jesus’s Jewish identity by dressing him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish clothing. Around them, the painting shows scenes of destruction from pogroms—violent attacks on Jewish communities that were often organized or approved by local governments.

By mixing the story of the Crucifixion with real events from his time, Chagall connects the suffering of Jesus with the suffering of Jewish people across Europe. He also suggests a comparison between the Nazis and those who tortured Jesus.

CuratorialText: The Child’s Bath is a tender portrayal of familial closeness, a subject that Mary Cassatt explored throughout her career. The caregiver’s cheek brushing the child’s shoulder, her encircling embrace, and the child’s pudgy hand on her knee suggest an emotional bond between the two. Captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris in 1890, Cassatt set out to produce a series of color prints influenced by Japanese aesthetics. She then continued her investigation across media, culminating in this bold composition, with its dramatically flattened picture plane, decorative patterning, and bright palette.
persona1_adapted:
**The Child’s Bath** shows a quiet, tender moment between a caregiver and a child. The caregiver’s cheek gently touches the child’s shoulder, her arms wrap around the child, and the child’s chubby hand rests on her knee. These small details show how close and loving their bond is.

Mary Cassatt, the artist, was inspired by a big exhibition of Japanese prints she saw in Paris in 1890. She loved their bold colors, flat shapes, and decorative patterns. After that, she created her own series of colorful prints using similar ideas. She kept exploring this style in different art forms, and *The Child’s Bath* is one of her most striking results. The painting has a flat look, bright colors, and lots of pattern—just like the Japanese art that inspired her.

CuratorialText: René Magritte painted Time Transfixed at a moment when he was attempting to elicit “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to juxtapose two images in such a way that they would suggest in the viewer’s mind a third un-pictured thing. Here he connected two highly disparate objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Although the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that travel up chimneys, uncovering the painting’s poetic secret.
persona1_adapted:
René Magritte painted *Time Transfixed* at a time when he was trying to uncover “poetic secrets” in his art. He was thinking about both philosophy and psychology, and he challenged himself to put two very different images together in a way that would make viewers imagine a third thing that wasn’t actually in the painting.

In this work, he connected two very different objects—a train and a fireplace. Even though a fireplace belongs in a home and a train is a machine, they are linked by smoke. Magritte knew that people would see the train’s puffing steam and think of the smoke rising from a chimney. That connection is the painting’s poetic secret.

CuratorialText: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter, which frequently included scenes derived from modern, industrial Paris, from iron bridges to exhibition halls to train sheds. The train station at Saint-Lazare would have been a familiar, meaningful sight to Claude Monet in the 1870s. The terminal linked Paris to Normandy, where the artist developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the point of departure for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition, in 1877, probably displaying them in the same gallery.
persona1_adapted:
The Impressionists were known for doing things differently, and one of their most daring choices was what they painted. Instead of the usual landscapes or historical scenes, they focused on modern, busy Paris—things like iron bridges, exhibition halls, and train stations.

For Claude Monet, the Saint-Lazare train station was a familiar and important place in the 1870s. This station connected Paris to Normandy, where Monet had learned to paint outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the starting point for many towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists loved to visit.

Monet painted the Gare Saint-Lazare at least twelve times. He finished eight of those paintings in time for the third Impressionist exhibition in 1877, and he probably hung them all in the same room.

CuratorialText: Monet chose to focus his attention here on the glass-and-iron train shed, where he found an appealing combination of artiﬁ cial and natural effects—the rising steam of locomotives trapped within the structure and the daylight penetrating large, glazed sections of the roof, for instance. Monet’s depictions of the station inaugurated what was to become for him an established pattern of painting a speciﬁc motif repeatedly in order to capture subtle and temporal atmospheric changes, as in his famous series of stacks of wheat. But the Saint-Lazare paintings also represented his last attempt to capture urban life: from this point on in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.
persona1_adapted: Monet focused on the train shed made of glass and iron. He liked how it mixed man-made and natural things—like steam from trains rising inside the building while sunlight came through the glass roof. Painting the train station started a habit for Monet: he painted the same scene over and over to show small changes in light and weather, just like he did later with his famous paintings of haystacks. But the Saint-Lazare paintings were also his last try at capturing city life. After that, Monet mostly painted landscapes.

CuratorialText: Salvador Dalí, Surrealism’s most publicized practitioner, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out, which he made even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired Inventions of the Monsters in 1943, the artist wrote his congratulations and explained.
persona1_adapted: Salvador Dalí was one of the most famous artists of a movement called Surrealism. Surrealist artists created strange, dreamlike images that often felt like the world was twisted or turned upside down. Dalí was especially good at making these weird scenes look incredibly real, thanks to his amazing painting skills. In 1943, the Art Institute bought one of his paintings called *Inventions of the Monsters*. Dalí was so happy that he wrote a letter to congratulate them and explain what the painting meant.

CuratorialText: “According to Nostradamus the apparition of monsters presages the outbreak of war. The canvas was painted in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany] and has a prophetic character. Horse women equal maternal river monsters. Flaming giraffe equals masculine apocalyptic monster. Cat angel equals divine heterosexual monster. Hourglass equals metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí equal sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster.”
persona1_adapted:
Here's the adapted version for an 8th-grade reader:

---

Nostradamus, a famous fortune-teller from the past, once said that seeing monsters is a sign that war is coming. This painting was made in the mountains near Vienna, Austria, just a few months before Germany took over Austria in 1938. So it feels like a warning or a prediction.

In the painting, different things stand for different kinds of "monsters":
- The horse-women = scary river monsters (like a mother's worst fear)
- The flaming giraffe = a huge, destructive monster (like the end of the world)
- The cat angel = a strange, holy monster (mixing good and evil)
- The hourglass = a monster of time and death
- Gala and Dalí (the artist and his wife) = a monster of love and emotion

The little blue dog? That's not a real monster at all.

CuratorialText: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. It is rife with threats of danger, from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground with an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his native Catalonia embroiled in the Spanish Civil War, the artist surely felt great anxiety over a world without a safe haven, a world that indeed had allowed for the invention of monsters.
persona1_adapted: The painting *Inventions of the Monsters* feels dark and scary. It is full of warnings about danger. In the background, a fire burns, looking threatening. In the front, a mysterious figure holds an hourglass and a butterfly—both symbols that remind us that death is unavoidable. Next to this figure are Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. At the time, Dalí’s home region of Catalonia was caught in the Spanish Civil War. The artist was probably very worried about a world where nowhere felt safe—a world that, he believed, had allowed monsters to be created.

CuratorialText: Shiva, one of the most important Hindu divinities, is here depicted as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), an iconic image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death; it pervades the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire that is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair. One pair of his arms balances the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life while another performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta) pointing down toward his raised left foot signifies release from the ignorance that hinders realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right leg planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught up in his matted, flowing locks. Shiva was believed to break the fall of the great Ganges River as it descends from the Himalayas by standing beneath the waters, which divide over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.
persona1_adapted:
This statue shows Shiva, one of the most important gods in Hinduism, as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja). It’s a famous image in Indian art. Shiva’s dance isn’t just for fun—it controls the rhythm of life and death. The ring of fire around him represents the universe, and his wild, snake-like hair fills that ring.

Shiva has four arms. One pair holds a flame (which stands for destruction) and a small drum (which beats the rhythm of life). Another pair makes hand gestures: his raised right hand says “don’t be afraid,” and his left hand points down toward his raised left foot. That pointing means he can free you from ignorance—the confusion that stops you from understanding the ultimate truth.

Shiva is perfectly balanced. His right foot is planted on a little demon named Apasmara, who represents darkness and ignorance. By stepping on him, Shiva is crushing ignorance. You can also see a tiny figure of the river goddess Ganga caught in his messy, flowing hair. According to legend, Shiva broke the fall of the great Ganges River as it came down from the Himalayas. He stood under the water, and the river split over his hair into seven holy rivers of India.

This bronze statue was made during the Chola period in southern India. Back then, people would carry statues like this in parades during religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Albert Bierstadt is best remembered for his paintings of the American West, but during his early career, the ambitious artist also created New England landscapes, particularly of the White Mountains, as seen here. When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics declared it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised the artist’s deft contrast of “light and shade,” which brought a heightened realism to the image. Bierstadt’s affinity for the White Mountains mirrored a growing interest in the region as one of America’s premier tourist attractions. Photographs of the area from the 19th century suggest that Bierstadt utilized landscape elements from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, in this imaginary composition.
persona1_adapted:
Albert Bierstadt is famous for his paintings of the American West. But early in his career, he also painted landscapes of New England, especially the White Mountains, like the one you see here.

When this painting was first shown in 1863, critics called it Bierstadt’s “best work.” They praised how he used light and shadow to make the scene look very real.

Bierstadt loved the White Mountains, and many other people did too. The area became one of America’s top tourist spots. Photos from the 1800s show that Bierstadt used real features from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire attraction, to create this imaginary scene.

CuratorialText: In this large-scale canvas, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of varying ages appear to go about daily life within a colorful, stylized outdoor environment. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed detail with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene whose narrative resists simple interpretation. The work’s deliberately provocative title enhances this effect, leaving us to wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.
persona1_adapted: In this big painting, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of different ages seem to be going about their daily lives in a bright, colorful outdoor setting. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting mixes real details with imagination to create a dreamlike scene that doesn’t have an easy story to follow. The title, which is meant to make you think, adds to this effect. It leaves us wondering if the “you” in the title is someone in the painting or us, the viewers.

CuratorialText: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of travel. Whether people traveled alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture how and why we move through the world.
persona1_adapted: People have traveled for many different reasons throughout history—and they've used just as many ways to get around. Whether they walked, rode animals, joined caravans, sailed on ships, or took planes, trains, and cars, art can show us both how and why we move through the world.

CuratorialText: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant who spent about seventeen years in China—from around 1274 to 1292—during the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou with minimal destruction. Sometime after this, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.
persona1_adapted: **Marco Polo (1254–1324)** was a merchant from Venice, Italy. He spent about 17 years in China, from around 1274 to 1292. This was during the rule of **Khubilai Khan**, the Mongol emperor who started the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s army took over China’s capital city, Hangzhou, without causing much damage. Sometime after that, Marco Polo visited Hangzhou and its famous **West Lake**.

CuratorialText: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. From ancient times, the emperors of China maintained a network of roads and waterways that unified their vast realm and facilitated the movement of people and goods. Owing to this imperial association, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers connoted the benevolent rule of an enlightened sovereign and evoked an era of peace and prosperity. Rather than documenting the hardships or actual circumstances of any particular journey, images of anonymous characters traveling or working together capture some of the noteworthy sights and scenes a traveler might encounter along the way. Simultaneously, they promote the fundamental ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.
persona1_adapted: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. Long ago, China's emperors built a system of roads and waterways that connected their huge empire and helped people and goods move around. Because the emperor was in charge, pictures of everyday travelers on these roads and rivers suggested that the ruler was wise and kind. These images also reminded people of peaceful and prosperous times. Instead of showing the hard parts or real details of a trip, these paintings focus on interesting sights a traveler might see along the way. At the same time, they promote the idea of a peaceful society and a well-organized country.

CuratorialText: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.
persona1_adapted:
**Why People Travel for Religion**

One big reason people have traveled for thousands of years is their faith. Almost every major religion—like Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism—has a tradition called pilgrimage. A pilgrimage is a special journey to a place that is important to that religion.

For example, Christian pilgrims used to travel to the Holy Land to follow the path of Jesus. Others visited local places connected to saints or their holy objects. In Islam, making a pilgrimage (called hajj) to the city of Mecca is one of the most important duties for every believer. People also visited other holy sites regularly.

For centuries, hundreds of pilgrims from China, Japan, and Korea braved many dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Sometimes, pilgrims would bring gifts called votive offerings. These were donations they left at the holy site as a sign of respect or thanks.

CuratorialText: Trade was another common motivation for travel and occurred in many forms, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road, the overland trade routes that linked the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants joined Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants in a wealthy, worldly environment that offered a ready market for exotic imports, including silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.
persona1_adapted: People also traveled to trade. There were many ways to trade, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road. This was a network of land routes that connected China’s wealthy Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. Along the Silk Road, foreign merchants traveled with Buddhist missionaries, government messengers, translators, craftspeople, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants. Together, they created a rich and worldly culture where people wanted to buy exotic goods from far away—like silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.

CuratorialText: In the twentieth century, travel offered a key means for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), Theodore Roosevelt’s daughter, embarked on a tour of Asia accompanying William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission in 1905. This voyage is immortalized in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts that dignitaries, such as the emperor of Japan, bestowed on Roosevelt.
persona1_adapted: In the 1900s, traveling was an important way for countries to build friendships and share their cultures. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), the daughter of President Theodore Roosevelt, went on a trip to Asia in 1905. She joined a goodwill mission led by William Howard Taft. We know about this journey from photographs, but also from special gifts that leaders, like the emperor of Japan, gave to Roosevelt as a sign of friendship.

CuratorialText: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long been traversing the globe to conduct research and to explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel among Europeans and North Americans also became increasingly common. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to expand their horizons by venturing there.
persona1_adapted: For a long time, explorers, scientists, and historians have traveled the world to study and discover new places. In the early 1900s, more people from Europe and North America started traveling just for fun. They were curious about the art, history, and culture of Asia and the Middle East, so they began visiting those regions to see them for themselves.

CuratorialText: Artists who traveled played a pivotal role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists and the things they experienced on their journeys contributed to the dispersal of different artistic styles and motifs.
persona1_adapted: When artists traveled to faraway places, they drew and painted what they saw. Their pictures helped people back home learn about those distant lands. Along the way, these artists met other artists and saw new things. Those experiences changed how they made art—and helped spread new styles and ideas around the world.

CuratorialText: While travel has mostly positive connotations in popular culture today, it’s important to remember the darker sides of travel as well. Not all travel is undertaken by choice: people can be forced to travel as a result of political exile, as well as due to war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, and colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous, and this was particularly true in ages past. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and diseases, the natural elements were unpredictable and threatened modes of transportation, and there was an inherent unpredictability to being on the road far from home. The recognition of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.
persona1_adapted: We usually think of travel as a fun adventure, but it’s important to remember that not all travel is happy or by choice. Sometimes people are forced to leave their homes because of war, hunger, disease, money problems, natural disasters, or being ruled by another country. Even when people choose to travel, it can be risky—especially in the past. Travelers could get hurt or sick, face dangerous weather, or deal with the uncertainty of being far from home. These dangers showed up in the stories and beliefs people shared.

CuratorialText: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.
persona1_adapted:
Objects have their own stories to tell. Many of them have traveled across the world through trade, as gifts, or by being collected by different people. For conservators—the people who take care of and repair old objects—these journeys are written right into the object itself. You can see marks, scratches, repairs, and other changes that show how people have used and handled it over time.

Conservation is about putting together all these clues to understand an object’s past. But it’s not just about looking back. Conservators also think about how to keep the object safe for the future. Anthropologists (scientists who study human cultures) sometimes talk about objects having “lives” or “biographies,” just like people do. For conservators, this idea makes a lot of sense. Every time they clean, fix, or protect an object, they add another chapter to its story—both in what the object looks like and in the time it has spent on Earth.

CuratorialText: Individual travelers carried things with them that were essential for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suitable to traversing long distances sometimes set them apart from local residents, and travelers carried personal effects and accessories, such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets, to make the journey easier.
persona1_adapted: When people traveled alone, they brought along things they really needed for the trip. They wore clothes and carried gear that helped them cover long distances—sometimes making them look different from the locals. Travelers also packed personal items like small books, water flasks, and good luck charms to make their journey a little easier.

CuratorialText: Travelers brought objects acquired on their journeys home with them as well. Holy objects acquired by pilgrims allowed them to bring the blessings of their pilgrimage back from their destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for future reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip also offered travelers a way of commemorating a journey and gave them something tangible on which to reflect. The advent of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, accruing the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.
persona1_adapted:
Travelers often brought back souvenirs from their trips. Pilgrims collected holy objects to carry blessings home with them. People also made sketches, paintings, or photos to remember their journeys. Souvenirs gave travelers something physical to look at and reflect on later.

As global travel became more common, people started bringing back lots of photos, drawings, postcards, and other keepsakes. These items helped record both scientific discoveries and personal experiences. The most famous travel souvenir from the last 150 years is probably the picture postcard. People collected them as memories or mailed them to family and friends. A postcard with a foreign stamp and postmark was even more special.

CuratorialText: Most travel we think of involves physically moving between places, but virtual travel (colloquially, “armchair travel”) that takes place in the mind spans time and cultures and is facilitated by books and works of art. Through focused contemplation of a travel account, a map, a cherished souvenir, or images of far-off places, memory and imagination combine to transport someone mentally and to simulate the experience of travel.
persona1_adapted:
When we think of travel, we usually picture moving from one place to another. But there’s another kind of travel—virtual travel, sometimes called “armchair travel.” This kind of trip happens in your mind. It can take you across time and to different cultures, all without leaving your seat.

Books and works of art make this possible. By looking closely at a travel story, a map, a special souvenir, or pictures of faraway places, your memory and imagination work together. They can mentally carry you away and make you feel like you’ve really been there.

CuratorialText: While it may not be the first thing we think of when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped everything about it, from how it was made, to how it has been perceived and appreciated, to how it has been preserved for today’s audiences. Dive into the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from images of the natural world to recycling ancient jades—to see our collections in a whole new way.
persona1_adapted: When you look at a piece of art, you probably don’t think about the environment first. But the environment actually affects everything about it—how it was made, how people saw and valued it, and how it was saved for us to see today. In this exhibit, we’ll explore the surprising ways art and nature are connected. From paintings of the natural world to old jade objects that were reused and recycled, you’ll see our collection in a whole new light.

CuratorialText: While environmental conditions differ from place to place, contact with nature has been a universal human experience. It’s therefore not surprising that natural motifs—plants, animals, water, the sky—adorn works of art across all cultures and are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.
persona1_adapted: Nature is different everywhere you go, but one thing is the same: people everywhere have always spent time in the natural world. That’s why you’ll find plants, animals, water, and the sky in art from all over the world. These natural images have inspired artists for centuries and carry deep meaning.

CuratorialText: Depictions of landscapes can serve as valuable documentation of specific places, but they can also evoke strong emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of the beauty and power of nature, evoking feelings of tranquility, wonder, awe, and contemplation.
persona1_adapted: Landscape paintings do more than just show what a place looks like. They can also make you feel something. Pictures of nature can remind us how beautiful and powerful the world is, bringing up feelings like calmness, curiosity, amazement, and quiet thought.

CuratorialText: From the earliest Japanese poetry anthology, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of the Japanese landscape has been celebrated in literature and art. At first, landscapes were not an independent subject but were incorporated into the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Beginning around the thirteenth century, possibly through the influence of the introduction of new modes of ink painting from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in the full color mode, which effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are inextricably linked with natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China, which most Japanese artists understood through their study of imported paintings, and later, of painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists are imbued with a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation of the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Since the very first collection of Japanese poems was put together over 1,200 years ago, people in Japan have been celebrating the beauty of their country's landscapes in both art and writing. At first, artists didn't paint landscapes by themselves. Instead, they used them as backgrounds in Buddhist paintings, pictures of the four seasons, and scenes of famous or sacred places.

Around 800 years ago, something changed. New styles of ink painting came to Japan from China and Korea, and artists started making landscapes the main subject of their work. When Japanese artists painted scenes of Japan, they usually used bright, full colors. This helped them show the changing seasons, which are a big part of Japanese poetry and art.

For dramatic landscapes of China—places most Japanese artists had never visited—they often chose ink painting. They learned about these faraway scenes by studying paintings that were brought from China, and later by using painting manuals.

Whether they used rich, deep colors or soft, subtle shades of ink, Japanese artists created landscapes that feel personal and full of meaning. Their work shows a deep love and respect for nature's beauty, which is always changing and has a special spiritual importance.

CuratorialText: Travel to misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century on, accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute), in this exploration of nature as an inspiration for Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.
persona1_adapted:
Imagine walking through misty mountain valleys or standing on a foggy ocean beach. Now, picture seeing these beautiful places through the eyes of famous Japanese artists from the 1500s to today. As you explore, you'll hear the peaceful sounds of the shakuhachi, a traditional bamboo flute.

This journey is guided by two experts: Kurahashi Yodo II, a master musician, and curator Frank Feltens. Together, they'll show you how nature has inspired Japanese art and music for centuries.

CuratorialText: For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings not only provided aesthetic delight, but a landscape setting was often vested with meaning, which could range from providing a clue about the season of the painting to conveying more complex meanings. Seeing an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism. The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian notion that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was faced with serving a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers then, following the tradition of eremitism, a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least assumed the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman, to protest the corruption of moral order. Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs had poignant significance.
persona1_adapted:
For Chinese scholar-officials, looking at landscape paintings wasn't just about enjoying their beauty. These paintings often had hidden meanings. For example, a scene could hint at what season it was, or it could tell a deeper story.

One important idea in these paintings was **eremitism**—the choice to live alone, away from society. The scholar class, called the *literati*, believed that serving the government was a moral duty, based on Confucian teachings. But what if the emperor was corrupt? Or what if a scholar was asked to work for a dynasty started by foreign rulers who had taken over by force? In those cases, a true gentleman would refuse to serve. Instead, following the tradition of eremitism, he would become a mountain recluse—or at least act like a hermit or a simple fisherman. This was a way to protest the breakdown of moral order.

So, when you see a painting of a lonely figure in a remote landscape, it’s not just a pretty scene. It’s a powerful symbol of someone choosing honor over power.

CuratorialText: Careful observation of nature did not only translate to depictions of real places. Artists used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes as well.
persona1_adapted: Artists didn't just paint real places they had seen. They also used what they knew about nature to create imaginary landscapes.

CuratorialText: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes embracing the category of “scholar’s rocks,” alternatively called “spirit stones.”  Scholar’s rocks consist of wondrously shaped and textured rocks, often of limestone, that are collected for placement in a designed garden or are brought indoors for display in a scholar’s study. The size can range from twelve feet or more to tiny enough to be enclosed in a fist. Philosophic and religious texts as well as poetry have a long history of singling out mountains and rocks for special consideration. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists pictured mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs for infinite life. Placement of evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn a mundane space into a paradise, or at least into an environment conducive to cultivating personal longevity or to the pursuit of eternal life.
persona1_adapted:
**Scholar's Rocks: Nature's Art**

To really understand Chinese art, you need to know about "scholar's rocks" (also called "spirit stones"). These are amazing-looking rocks, often made of limestone, with weird and wonderful shapes and textures. People collect them to put in gardens or bring them inside to display in a scholar's study. They can be huge—over twelve feet tall—or small enough to fit in your hand.

For a very long time, Chinese philosophers, religious writers, and poets have thought mountains and rocks were special. A famous saying from Confucius (who lived around 551–479 BCE) goes: "The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains." Meanwhile, Daoists believed mountains were like paradise for immortal beings. They even used minerals from rocks in special potions meant to give eternal life.

By placing these interesting rocks in gardens and homes, people could turn an ordinary space into a kind of paradise. It was a way to create a peaceful place that helped them live longer or even seek eternal life.

CuratorialText: In Chinese tradition, the educated class delighted in rocks as tangible embodiments of the powers of creation and cosmic transformation, and they sometimes rhapsodized about them as eternally faithful “friends.” Gazing upon them could encourage imaginary travel deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all engaged in collecting rocks, and painters often studied the rocks they collected, treating them as both muse and model for their landscape paintings.
persona1_adapted: In Chinese tradition, educated people loved rocks because they saw them as small examples of nature's power and change. Some even called rocks their forever loyal "friends." Looking at a rock could make you feel like you were taking an imaginary trip deep into the wilderness. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and even emperors collected rocks. Painters often studied the rocks they owned, using them as both inspiration and a model for their paintings of mountains and landscapes.

CuratorialText: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.
persona1_adapted:
Gardens have been important in Asia for thousands of years. Some were small and private, while others were huge and grand, like temples, tombs, and pleasure gardens. The very first garden people know about is the Garden of Eden from the Bible. It might have been in West Asia, near the Tigris and Euphrates rivers in what is now Iraq.

The word "paradise" actually comes from an old Persian word, *pardis*, which meant a walled garden or hunting park. These enclosed gardens were a big part of Persian life for centuries. They often had small buildings called pavilions where people could sit in the shade and escape the hot sun.

In China, the earliest gardens date back to the Zhou period (around 1050–256 BCE). They were enclosed hunting grounds just for royalty. Chinese gardens were famous for having carefully placed rocks and ponds that were meant to look like a tiny version of nature.

Japanese gardens were more natural-looking. They had rolling hills and calm ponds to show harmony between people and the world around them.

In dry parts of West and South Asia, gardens often used a system of pools and water channels that connected to each other.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, art has served as a reflection of and response to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately brought about through human beings adapting to and altering their surrounding environment. Cultural landscapes—including the ways that humans have built on, altered, and imagined natural environments—play a significant role in how we understand the world we inhabit.
persona1_adapted: Art has always shown us how people react to changes in the world around them. Sometimes, people cause these changes on purpose as they adapt to their surroundings and reshape them. The places we build, change, and even imagine—called cultural landscapes—help us understand the world we live in.

CuratorialText: Works of art can bear witness to the environmental changes that have taken place throughout recorded history. Depictions of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist, for example, can tell us about migrations or extinctions.
persona1_adapted: Art can show us how the environment has changed over time. For example, if a painting shows animals that no longer live in that area, it might mean the animals moved away or died out.

CuratorialText: Humans have always had an impact on the environments where they live, but the scale of that impact dramatically increased with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation sprung up to create a range of consumer goods and ways to travel with new ease and speed. Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.
persona1_adapted: People have always changed the places where they live. But when factories and new ways to travel appeared, the changes got much bigger. In the U.S. and Europe, factories made lots of products, and trains and cars made travel faster and easier. Most of these machines ran on coal, which created a lot of pollution. Artists like Whistler tried to show this pollution in their paintings.

CuratorialText: Similarly, the infusion of Western technology into Japan from the beginning of the Meiji era (1868–1912) spurred widespread visual documentation of the new developments. Railroad transportation, telegraphic communication, gas-fueled lighting, steam-powered river and ocean vessels, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings were crammed into the Japanese landscape over two decades.
persona1_adapted: When Japan opened up to Western technology in the late 1800s, things changed fast. New inventions like trains, telegraphs, gas lights, and steamships appeared all over the country. Bridges and buildings were built with new materials and designs. Artists rushed to draw and paint these changes, capturing the modern world taking shape around them.

CuratorialText: Contemporary artists are increasingly exploring the impact of climate change on the natural world and human communities, whether their goal is to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change, to highlight the social and political issues that are bound up in environmental degradation, or to envision alternative futures and inspire hope and action.
persona1_adapted: Climate change is a big topic for many artists today. They create art to show how it affects nature and people. Some want to make us more aware of the problem. Others focus on the social and political issues tied to the environment. And some imagine new, hopeful futures to inspire us to take action.

CuratorialText: The creation of art itself impacts and is impacted by the environment. Stones and precious metals need to be mined. Wood needs to be harvested. Clay is extracted from the earth. Plants need to be gathered. Many works of art make use of animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel sources for melting, firing, and glazing. The gathering of raw materials and their transformation into art objects is not without impact on the environment and has at times come at the expense of the exploitation of natural resources and people.
persona1_adapted: Making art affects the environment, and the environment also affects the art we make. To create art, we need to get materials from nature. For example, stones and precious metals have to be mined from the ground. Wood comes from cutting down trees. Clay is dug up from the earth. Plants are gathered for dyes or other uses. Many artworks also use animal products like parchment (made from animal skin), feathers, horns, and ivory (from elephant tusks). Different ways of making art—like melting, firing, or glazing—require burning fuels for heat. Getting these raw materials and turning them into art can harm the environment. Sometimes, it has also meant using up natural resources or taking advantage of people.

CuratorialText: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.
persona1_adapted:
You might think recycling is a modern idea, but people have been reusing materials to make art for a very long time. Sometimes it was because materials were hard to find, and other times it was because an object was already valuable.

In ancient China, jade was a very precious material. Instead of throwing away broken or old-fashioned jade items, people turned them into new objects. This was especially common during the late Shang dynasty (around 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists digging at the Shang capital of Anyang found that, because jade was in high demand and short supply, people took even older Stone Age objects (from about 5000–1700 BCE) and carved them into jewelry and other items that were more meaningful to them.

The three pendants you see here were all made by cutting up a special type of Neolithic square tube called a *cong*. By the time of the Shang dynasty, the *cong* had lost its original religious meaning. What’s interesting is that these recycled *cong* came from different Stone Age cultures across northern and southern China.

CuratorialText: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.
persona1_adapted:
**How the Environment Affects Art (And What We Do About It)**

The world around us can change art in big ways. Things like temperature, humidity (how much water is in the air), light, and pollution can slowly damage paintings, sculptures, and other objects. Over time, these factors can make colors fade, materials crack, or even cause objects to fall apart.

On a bigger scale, changes in the climate—like hotter weather, stronger storms, or rising temperatures—can also threaten famous historic sites and museums all over the world.

That’s why conservators (art doctors!) work hard to protect the art in museums. They study how to keep objects safe from damage and help them last for many more years.

Want to know more? You can read about conservation and science projects at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution says climate change is one of the biggest challenges of our time. Learn more about what the Smithsonian is doing to help our planet.

CuratorialText: A color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration, red has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to take notice. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona1_adapted:
Red is a color full of life. It stands for energy, passion, good luck, and celebration. Red grabs your attention and makes you feel something. It can even make your heart beat a little faster.

People have made red from many different things—like shiny gems, reddish soil, tiny bugs, and special rocks. In this exhibit, you’ll see examples from all around the museum that show how people have created and used red throughout history.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Some metals—like iron, mercury, and lead—are used to make colored pigments that people have used for a long time. These pigments can be found in nature, dug up from the ground, or made by people.

For example, iron in the earth can create different colors. Red iron earth is easy to find or make. People have used it for many things, like painting pottery or making paint.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Mercury-based pigments are made by grinding up a red mineral called cinnabar. People have also made a synthetic (man-made) version for a long time, which is called vermilion. This deep red pigment is poisonous, but it was still used in burial ceremonies, added to lacquers, and used to decorate paintings and documents.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona1_adapted: Red lead is a bright red powder that people used to make paint. Even though it can be found in nature as a mineral called minium, it was usually made in a lab. To create it, workers heated lead-based chemicals very carefully. They controlled the heat and air to get the right red color and avoid making other unwanted colors.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Red copper oxide, also called cuprite, is a substance that forms on bronze objects over time. It happens when the copper in the bronze reacts with the air around it.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.
persona1_adapted:
**Reds from Nature: Plants and Bugs**

Did you know that some red colors in art come from plants and even bugs? It's true!

**From Plants**
Plants like madder root and safflower can be used to make red dye. People take the color out of the plant, clean it up, and get it ready to use on things like fabric or paper. The way the dye is made can change the final shade of red you get.

**From Bugs**
Believe it or not, some reds come from tiny insects called scale insects. Two examples are lac bugs and cochineal bugs. People would collect these bugs, then take the red color from their bodies to make paint or dye.

**Why It Matters**
When scientists figure out what kind of red was used in an old artwork, it can tell us something cool about history. For example, these bugs only lived in certain parts of the world.
