Perugino’s elegant figures, breathtaking command of technique, and efficient methods for reproducing designs left their mark on the younger artist.
persona1_adapted:
Raphael’s father noticed his son was incredibly talented at a young age. So he took him to study with a famous artist named Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), who he probably knew from working on local art projects. Perugino was a brilliant painter and drawer who had busy workshops in Perugia and Florence. He often worked with assistants to get all his projects done for customers across Italy.

Raphael quickly moved through the stages of being a student and apprentice. First, he became a helper to Perugino, and then he became an independent artist on his own. Perugino’s graceful figures, amazing skill, and smart ways of copying his designs had a big influence on the younger artist.

CuratorialText: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious institutions for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of patronage for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.
persona1_adapted:
**Young Raphael: An Ambitious Start**

Raphael was a hardworking and talented young artist. He wanted to make a name for himself, so he moved to a small, wealthy town called Città di Castello, near Perugia. There, he painted important altarpieces for churches.

In this area, many artists got work from **confraternities**. These were religious groups made up of regular people who did charity work. They often hired painters to create art for their churches.

In this room, you can see paintings and sketches made by Perugino (Raphael’s teacher) and by Raphael himself. One of these paintings is especially important: it’s the first painting that Raphael did completely on his own. We know this for sure because of recent cleaning and restoration work.

CuratorialText: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael endeavored to cultivate patrons by painting both monumental altarpieces and small-scale devotional works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. An iconic example is the large, multipart painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full ensemble is reunited here as a complete work for the first time since it was dismembered around 1663 and the individual paintings dispersed. Other notable projects include a mammoth altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and preliminary drawings, and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, represented here by studies and the painting that provided its base.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Reader:**

Between 1500 and 1507, a young artist named Raphael worked hard to get noticed by important people. He painted two kinds of art: huge altarpieces (big paintings for churches) and smaller, personal pieces for people to pray with at home. He did this in three regions of Italy: the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany.

A great example is the big, multi-part painting in the middle of this room. It was made for nuns in Perugia, Italy. Today, it's called the *Colonna Altarpiece* (named after a later owner). For the first time in over 350 years, all the pieces of this painting are back together in one place. They were taken apart around 1663 and sold separately.

Raphael also worked on other big projects. One was a huge altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello. That painting was destroyed in an earthquake, so only small pieces and early sketches survive. Another was a famous altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia. In this gallery, you can see the studies Raphael made for it, plus the painting that was used as its base.

CuratorialText: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the workshop practices that Raphael had absorbed during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Dazzling studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of media—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a virtuosic command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and undoubtedly impressed his patrons.
persona1_adapted: Raphael made full-size drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece. These drawings show the skills he learned while training and working with another artist named Perugino. Some of Raphael’s drawings are amazing to look at. He used different materials like black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on special paper. He was really good at using these tools. Drawings like these probably helped him get jobs. They definitely impressed the people who paid him to make art.

CuratorialText: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.
persona1_adapted:
**Why Raphael Went to Florence**

Raphael probably moved to Florence because other painters couldn't stop talking about two amazing mural designs there. These were huge drawings (called "cartoons") made by two famous artists: Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo. The designs were for giant wall paintings that never actually got finished.

In this gallery, you can see artworks that show how exciting and powerful those unfinished projects were. The artists' ideas were so new and bold that they blew people away.

While Raphael lived in Florence from 1504 to 1508, he studied Michelangelo's sculptures and Leonardo's paintings and drawings. He was a quick learner—he took parts of their styles and techniques and made them his own, blending them smoothly into his work.

CuratorialText: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.
persona1_adapted: Raphael also learned from other artists’ new and exciting ways to create compositions. Leonardo’s ideas about “sketching quickly,” while feeling inspired, changed how Raphael worked. Leonardo believed that spending too much time on finished drawings and small details could kill fresh ideas. Instead, he suggested working out ideas in “rough sketches,” like a poet writing a first draft with messy handwriting and crossed-out words. Before moving to Florence, Raphael used to build his compositions step by step, focusing on one detailed figure at a time, just as his teacher Perugino had taught him. But now, sketching freely helped him create more natural compositions full of movement and strong emotion.

CuratorialText: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.
persona1_adapted:
When Raphael saw the art of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, it changed how he painted. His work started to feel more spacious, the people in it looked more solid and powerful, and their emotions came through more strongly.

This didn't happen by accident. Raphael did tons of experiments on paper. He would start with quick sketches, then make more detailed drawings, and even build small models out of clay or wax to figure out how to arrange his figures.

Over time, Raphael got more and more organized in how he planned his art. For example, he liked to use a pyramid-shaped layout for his paintings (a trick he learned from Leonardo). But instead of letting that layout make the painting feel stiff or boring, he added movement by having the people in the painting look at each other and gesture in ways that made the whole scene feel alive and connected.

CuratorialText: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most significant work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s entombment for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.
persona1_adapted:
In 1507, a 24-year-old artist named Raphael painted one of his most important works before moving to Rome. It was an altarpiece—a painting made for a church—showing the burial of Christ. He created it for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia, Italy.

Raphael’s early sketches show he was already a master at telling a story through art. They reveal how he built a dramatic scene with a clear, exciting climax. By studying hard and learning from ancient Roman sculptures, he turned a still, calm group of figures into an emotional, action-packed moment. Raphael had also picked up a key idea from Leonardo da Vinci: that the way people move their bodies can show what they are thinking and feeling inside.

CuratorialText: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.
persona1_adapted: For centuries, people have admired Raphael’s paintings of Mary and baby Jesus because they show so much love and tenderness. Around the year 1300, Byzantine religious art from the Eastern Orthodox Church featured a type of Mary called the Virgin Eleousa (which means “compassionate” in Greek). In these images, Mary holds baby Jesus close as he presses his cheek against hers. This style became very popular and inspired many artists during the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael used this idea as a starting point, but he changed it to create something completely new.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
persona1_adapted: Raphael was amazing at painting happy, lively babies and mothers—even though life for real mothers and babies in his time was very hard. The old account book in this display shows the funeral costs for Raphael’s own mother, who died from problems after giving birth in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died as babies. Back then, doctors knew very little about women’s health and childbirth. Having a baby was extremely dangerous, and many babies didn’t survive. These sad facts, plus the fact that Renaissance artists wanted to make religious stories feel more human and relatable, help explain why paintings of Mary and baby Jesus were so popular. People saw them as comforting and hopeful images.

CuratorialText: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.
persona1_adapted: Raphael had big dreams, but he struggled to make a name for himself in Florence’s tough art world. His best chance for work came from the city’s rich merchants. They hired him to paint portraits and gentle religious scenes, mostly of Mary and baby Jesus. Even after moving to Rome in 1508, Raphael kept making this kind of art for private buyers. Many of his helpers and coworkers did the same.

CuratorialText: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.
persona1_adapted: Raphael wanted to paint the Virgin Mary as both beautiful and holy. Like other artists and poets of his time, he often showed her as an elegant woman with delicate features and golden hair. But he also wanted to capture the Christian idea that her beauty reflected her perfect spirit. To make her feel real, Raphael added human emotions through her gestures and reactions. His paintings show amazing skill with light, color, space, and shapes. He also painted babies more naturally than many other artists did, making their bodies, poses, and expressions look more lifelike.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.
persona1_adapted: Raphael’s portraits show deep understanding and care for the people he painted. He spent many years practicing his drawing skills to capture a quiet, thoughtful moment with each person. The famous pose of Leonardo da Vinci’s *Mona Lisa*, started in 1503, gave Raphael a new way to show a richly dressed person: a three-quarter view, with the head turned to the left, body straight, and hands resting gently at the waist. The graceful poses in Raphael’s paintings also show the polite manners and beauty standards admired in Italian Renaissance courts. Raphael was close friends with Baldassarre Castiglione, who wrote a book about how to behave at court. His book praised *sprezzatura*—a kind of cool, effortless style that makes things look easy even when they’re not.

CuratorialText: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
persona1_adapted: During the Renaissance, many portraits of women were painted when they got engaged or married. Back then, marriage was more about money and family connections than love. A woman’s fancy clothes and jewelry were meant to impress her future husband’s family while they worked out deals about money and property. Poets and artists also openly praised women’s looks, which helped create a culture where young women could become the “innamorate”—meaning either a platonic crush or an actual girlfriend—of rich, powerful men.

CuratorialText: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An intriguing question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to supersede an earlier generation of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal savvy were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also paved the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.
persona1_adapted:
In 1508, a young artist named Raphael arrived in Rome. He was only 25 years old, but he quickly became the favorite artist of two popes: Julius II and Leo X. 

How did such a young artist beat out older, more experienced painters to get the most important job in the Vatican? He was a genius, and he knew how to get along with powerful people. But that wasn't all. New research shows that Raphael also had help from his friends back home in Urbino. One of them was Donato Bramante, the pope's favorite architect. Thanks to these connections, Raphael got the chance to paint the famous rooms of the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing media. Rapidly sketched pen-and-ink sheets teem with the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear monumental despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.
persona1_adapted: Here are Raphael’s sketches for his famous Vatican paintings: *The School of Athens*, which shows a group of philosophers, and the *Disputa*, which represents Catholic beliefs. These drawings show an artist who really knew how to use different materials. Quick pen-and-ink sketches are full of energy, like ideas coming to life. Studies in black chalk look big and important, even though they’re small. In the next room, a video shows how powerful Raphael’s finished paintings are in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting accolades as the most visible and prolific painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo labored to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he concluded his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X ascended to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.
persona1_adapted: By around 1510, Raphael was getting a lot of praise as the most famous and busy painter working for the pope in Rome. At the same time, Michelangelo was working secretly in the Sistine Chapel on his ceiling paintings. These paintings would later have a big impact on Raphael, who snuck in to see them. The drawings in this room show projects Raphael worked on as he finished his first set of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and started his second set, in the Stanza di Eliodoro. These later paintings are bigger and more dramatic. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X became pope, some of the more complicated, learned images were replaced with simpler messages that showed the pope’s power more directly.

CuratorialText: Other drawings and prints illustrate Raphael’s collaboration with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their fruitful partnership, beginning in 1510, they created engravings of tremendous technical skill and artistic power. This medium enabled the reproduction of imagery in multiples and ensured a long afterlife for Raphael’s compositions. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and repurposed in other media, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.
persona1_adapted:
Raphael worked with a close friend named Marcantonio Raimondi, a printmaker from Bologna. Starting in 1510, they made engravings together—detailed prints carved into metal. These prints were incredibly skilled and powerful works of art.

Because they could be copied many times, Raphael’s designs lived on long after he made them. Artists kept copying these prints into the late 1800s, and you can even see them reused on ceramic plates in a nearby display case.

CuratorialText: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s monumental fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of scale and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the gargantuan oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).
persona1_adapted: In the next room, there’s a digital video showing Raphael’s giant wall paintings in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The video helps you see how big these paintings are and how they fit into the rooms. It also gives you a better idea of the drawings in this exhibit. Between 1508 and 1524, Raphael and his team painted these frescoes in four large rooms. The rooms are called the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, plus a huge, long room called the Sala di Costantino. Raphael died in 1520, so his helpers finished that last room.

CuratorialText: The durable but difficult fresco technique requires expertise, the use of full-scale drawings (cartoons), and a willingness to anticipate challenges. Water-based colors applied to moist surface plaster (intonaco) dry and carbonate in a chemical process, permanently bonding the colors to the wall. Executed primarily by Raphael between 1508 and 1512, the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura are the most famous and include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the following three rooms Raphael worked with a greater involvement of collaborators who followed his designs closely.
persona1_adapted:
Painting a fresco is a tough but long-lasting way to make art. It takes a lot of skill, big full-size drawings called cartoons, and careful planning to avoid problems. The artist uses water-based paint on wet plaster. As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction happens that locks the colors into the wall forever.

Raphael was the main artist behind the most famous frescoes, painted between 1508 and 1512 in a room called the Stanza della Segnatura. His works there include *The School of Athens*, *The Disputa*, a figure representing Justice, and *Parnassus*. In the next three rooms, Raphael worked with more helpers, but they still followed his designs very closely.

CuratorialText: The paintings here represent the breathtaking visual eloquence of Raphael’s later years. New harmonies of color are transformed by a dark chiaroscuro reminiscent of Leonardo. Sculptural forms seem to project from dense compositions. Charged gazes, poses, and gestures communicate a sense of impending drama.
persona1_adapted: These paintings show how Raphael’s later work became incredibly powerful and expressive. He used new color combinations, and added dark shadows and bright light—a technique he learned from Leonardo da Vinci. The people in the paintings look almost like statues, standing out from busy, detailed backgrounds. Their intense stares, poses, and hand gestures make you feel like something important is about to happen.

CuratorialText: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X rained project after project upon Raphael. He produced few paintings independently; using his drawings as a guide, skilled assistants painted most of the frescoes in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio. He was often disparaged for this practice by rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Executive help freed the artist to concentrate his creative energies on inventing new designs and exploring new forms. The involvement of workshop assistants in paintings and in certain types of drawings has inevitably led to debates about authorship. Labels in this exhibition attempt to indicate instances of their likely intervention, and a nearby display allows comparison of studies by Raphael and his fellow artists in an effort to characterize their individual styles.
persona1_adapted:
Between 1514 and 1520, Pope Leo X kept giving Raphael one big project after another. Raphael didn’t paint many pictures by himself anymore. Instead, he made detailed drawings, and his skilled assistants used them to paint most of the large wall paintings in a room called the Stanza dell’Incendio at the Vatican.

Some of Raphael’s rivals, especially his fierce enemy Michelangelo, made fun of him for this. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop got an amazing amount of work done in his last six years. Having helpers let Raphael focus his creative energy on coming up with new ideas and trying out new styles.

Because assistants worked on many paintings and drawings, it’s sometimes hard to know who did what. In this exhibition, labels try to show where assistants probably helped. A nearby display lets you compare drawings by Raphael and his assistants, so you can see the differences in their styles.

CuratorialText: The imperious Pope Leo X charged Raphael with the daunting task of designing a set of monumental tapestries to hang in the Sistine Chapel during special occasions—a project that put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo and his ceiling frescoes above. Starting in 1515, Raphael began drawing small preliminary studies. Aided by workshop assistants, he then painted in color gouaches on paper the enormous full-scale cartoons that would guide the weavers. Woven in Brussels using rich materials, the stratospherically expensive tapestries contributed to the bankruptcy of Leo’s papacy. With their inventive compositions, striking color harmonies, and monumental scale, they were soon the envy of monarchs across Europe. Kings commissioned second editions woven from Raphael’s cartoons, and the set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.
persona1_adapted:
In the early 1500s, Pope Leo X gave the famous artist Raphael a huge job: design giant tapestries for the Sistine Chapel. These tapestries would hang during special ceremonies, and they put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo, who had already painted the chapel’s famous ceiling.

Starting in 1515, Raphael made small sketches. Then, with help from his assistants, he created full-size, colorful paintings on paper called cartoons. These cartoons were sent to weavers in Brussels, Belgium, who used rich materials like silk and gold thread to turn them into tapestries. The tapestries were so expensive that they helped drain the pope’s money.

The tapestries were a huge hit. Their bold designs, bright colors, and huge size made kings all over Europe want their own copies. Some rulers ordered second editions made from Raphael’s original cartoons. The three tapestries you see here are from a set owned by King Philip II of Spain, who lived in the 1500s.

CuratorialText: As the tapestry designs progressed, the Raphael team largely took over the fresco work in the Vatican Palace, endeavoring to complete the Stanza dell’Incendio and assisting Raphael as he began to conceive decorations for the final space. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) acted as his studio foreman and went on to enjoy a brilliant independent career.
persona1_adapted: As the designs for the tapestries moved forward, Raphael’s team took over most of the fresco painting in the Vatican Palace. They worked to finish the Stanza dell’Incendio and helped Raphael plan decorations for the last room. One of his main assistants, Giulio Romano (about 1499–1546), served as the studio manager. He later became a famous artist on his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the enormous Transfiguration, commissioned by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (the future Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Spurred by competition with artistic rivals, Raphael produced a work markedly more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Although its execution was interrupted by his untimely death in 1520, the Transfiguration became Raphael’s artistic testament. The main event is Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, during which he transformed before the stunned apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal his divinity.
persona1_adapted: Raphael’s last and biggest altarpiece is the huge *Transfiguration*. It was ordered by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (who later became Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Because Raphael wanted to outdo his artist rivals, he made this painting much more dramatic, ambitious, and complicated than his earlier works. Even though Raphael died suddenly in 1520 before he could finish it, the *Transfiguration* became his artistic final statement. The main scene shows Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, where he changed form in front of the amazed apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal that he was divine.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s studies for the Transfiguration, displayed in the niche at left, represent the culmination of his career as a draftsman. They are reunited here to show the range of his design process. Raphael used red chalk primarily when drawing nude models from life. He reserved black chalk for the full-scale studies of apostles’ heads and hands, a type of drawing called an “auxiliary cartoon” because the design starts from a dotted chalk outline transferred from another sheet.
persona1_adapted:
Raphael’s drawings for the *Transfiguration*, shown in the niche on the left, are some of the best work he ever did as an artist. These sketches are brought together here to show how he planned his paintings step by step.

Raphael often used red chalk when drawing live models without clothes. He used black chalk for larger studies of the apostles’ heads and hands. These detailed drawings are called “auxiliary cartoons.” The name comes from the way the design begins: a dotted chalk outline is copied from another sheet of paper.

CuratorialText: Raphael rarely interrupted his projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X to make room for other commissions. The notable exception was his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met in about 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi became the richest banker in Europe, and his legendary wealth financed the popes’ cultural and military enterprises. Raphael’s work for Chigi included fresco paintings, as well as designs for his remodeled stables and the multimedia ensembles of his two chapels.
persona1_adapted: Raphael usually focused on his projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X. But he made an exception for his good friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met around 1510. Chigi was born in Siena and became the richest banker in Europe. His huge fortune helped pay for the popes’ art projects and military campaigns. For Chigi, Raphael painted wall frescoes, designed plans for his remodeled stables, and created the artwork for two of his chapels.

CuratorialText: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Michelangelo’s language of contorted poses and powerful muscularity is transformed with an effortless grace and the sensuality of living flesh. The drawings also allude to the legacy of Raphael’s approach to the human figure, which led to the style known as Mannerism after his death. Male and female forms here anticipate prototypical Mannerist poses—as with Raphael’s primeval personification of an earthquake in a Sistine Chapel tapestry design, which spawned the figural vocabulary that his former assistant Giulio Romano would exploit to spectacular effect in his later frescoes.
persona1_adapted:
Here are some drawings Raphael made for his rich client, Chigi. In these sketches of naked figures, you can see how Raphael was inspired by Michelangelo’s powerful, twisted poses. But Raphael made them look smooth, graceful, and almost alive.

These drawings also show how Raphael’s style would later turn into a new art movement called Mannerism. For example, one drawing shows a person representing an earthquake. That dramatic pose became a model that Raphael’s assistant, Giulio Romano, later used in his own famous wall paintings.

CuratorialText: Rome and its ancient monuments transformed Raphael’s art, and his work likewise changed the face of Rome. Following his arrival in the Eternal City in 1508, he threw himself into archaeological studies and made drawings of monuments that demonstrate a nearly scientific rigor. Pope Leo X tasked Raphael with creating an enormous drawing of ancient Rome, “making those members that are entirely ruined and are completely invisible correspond with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter to Leo, written on Raphael’s behalf, attests to the artist’s deep archaeological knowledge and technical skill, including a highly original method for using a magnetic compass in conducting surveys of ruins.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

When Raphael moved to Rome in 1508, the city’s old buildings and ruins had a huge impact on his art. In return, his work also changed how Rome looked.

Raphael became really interested in studying ancient Roman ruins. He made detailed drawings of them that were almost as careful and exact as a scientist’s work. 

Pope Leo X asked Raphael to create a giant drawing of what ancient Rome used to look like. The Pope wanted him to show parts of buildings that had completely crumbled away, matching them up with the parts that were still standing. 

A letter written for Raphael to the Pope shows just how much he knew about archaeology and how skilled he was as an artist. In the letter, he even described a clever new way to use a magnetic compass to measure and map ruins.

CuratorialText: Around 1503 Pope Julius II had charged Bramante, Raphael’s mentor and close friend from Urbino, to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica that would convey the majesty of the papacy in the aesthetic language of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints here illuminate Raphael’s career as an architect after he took over this massive project upon Bramante’s death. In 1517 Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, designed by Bramante and now destroyed but pictured in two works here, where he lived out his final years in Rome as the “prince of painters.”
persona1_adapted: In 1503, Pope Julius II asked Bramante—Raphael’s teacher and close friend from Urbino—to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica. The goal was to make the church look powerful and grand, using the style of ancient Roman buildings. The drawings and prints in this exhibit show how Raphael became an architect and took over this huge project after Bramante died. In 1517, Raphael bought a house called the Palazzo Caprini, which was designed by Bramante. That building no longer exists, but you can see it in two artworks here. Raphael spent his final years in Rome living in that house, known as the “prince of painters.”

CuratorialText: The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
persona1_adapted:
The Met got its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the museum has added over 2,000 works of painting and calligraphy. Now, it has one of the most complete collections in the world. The art spans 1,500 years and includes many different styles, techniques, and subjects. This collection is an important tool for studying Chinese painting and calligraphy.

This exhibition shows a wide range of works from the collection, mostly in the order they were made. The last room tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), who led the museum’s Asian Art department from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). In the 1940s, when Fong was a young calligraphy star in Shanghai, he studied brush art with Li. The display focuses on a group of fans that Li painted and wrote on. These fans were meant to pass down classic art styles from teacher to student. They came to the United States in 1949 and have been treasured by Fong’s family ever since. Now, they are being shown to the public for the first time.

CuratorialText: The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art. Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
persona1_adapted:
In the 1800s, the hand-held fan became a surprising source of inspiration for some of Europe’s most creative artists. Fans were super popular across all levels of society—not just as useful objects to keep cool, but also as stylish accessories and even a way to send secret messages. Famous French Impressionists like Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro didn’t just paint women holding fans; they also used the fan shape itself as a new, experimental way to make art.

This exhibition, called *Fanmania*, explores why these bold, forward-thinking artists were so into fans. It looks at ideas about gender, dating, shopping, and borrowing styles from other cultures. Artists loved the half-circle shape for many reasons: they were fascinated by fans from Asia and Spain, they wanted to sell their work to more people, and they were excited to try new techniques and forms.

With over 75 artworks from The Met’s collection, this multimedia show includes painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia, plus paintings of women using fans. It’s all about the craze that swept through the art world—fanmania!

CuratorialText: During the nineteenth century, handheld fans achieved unprecedented popularity throughout European society. A newfound artistic appreciation for these objects also emerged, fueled by cultural crazes for all things Japanese and Spanish as well as by exhibitions and publications devoted to fan making and its history. “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans,” wrote the author Octave Uzanne in 1882. For the fourth Impressionist exhibition of 1879, Edgar Degas envisioned an entire gallery devoted to fans. Though the fan-shaped paintings his colleagues submitted were ultimately dispersed throughout the show, they nevertheless elicited strong responses from the public, including bewilderment, as illustrated below. Today, these works continue to raise questions—mainly, why did ambitious European artists adopt this accessory as a format for their work? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, Fanmania explores the reasons artists were drawn to this semicircular form, including its commercial potential, its fashionability, and the opportunities it offered for formal and technical innovation. Although avant-garde fans were primarily designed for display rather than use, they retain many of the associations of functional fans. Themes of gender, courtship, appropriation, and experimentation unfold in this examination of fans as objects of adornment, decoration, and communication in the nineteenth century.
persona1_adapted:
In the 1800s, handheld fans became super popular all across Europe. People started to see them as art, not just useful items. This was partly because of crazes for Japanese and Spanish culture, and also because there were new books and exhibitions all about fans and how they were made.

A writer named Octave Uzanne said in 1882, “Almost all the great artists of the century have helped decorate the best fans.” For a big art show in 1879, the famous artist Edgar Degas even wanted a whole room just for fans. In the end, the fan-shaped paintings his friends made were spread around the show, but they still got strong reactions from people—including confusion, as you can see in the picture below.

Today, these works still make us wonder: why did serious European artists decide to make art on fans? This exhibit, *Fanmania*, brings together artworks from across The Met to explore the reasons. Artists were drawn to the fan’s half-circle shape because it could help them sell art, it was fashionable, and it let them try new techniques and ideas.

Even though these fancy fans were mostly made to be looked at, not used, they still remind us of real fans. As we look at them, we’ll see themes like gender, flirting, borrowing from other cultures, and trying new things. Fans were more than just pretty objects—they were a way to decorate, communicate, and show off in the 1800s.

CuratorialText: Handheld folding fans are multimedia objects that involve many types of artistry and artisanal skill. This show focuses primarily on the role of painters and printmakers in the production of the fan’s semicircular leaf. In Europe, prior to 1700, most of these designs were unsigned. In the nineteenth century, major artists grew increasingly interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did preeminent fan-making houses, which evolved out of earlier guilds. Some of the precedents for these practices are on display here.
persona1_adapted: Handheld folding fans are more than just accessories—they're works of art made by many different kinds of skilled artists and craftspeople. This exhibit focuses on the painters and printmakers who created the designs on the fan's semicircular leaf (the part that opens up). Before 1700 in Europe, most fan designs weren't signed, so we don't know who made them. But in the 1800s, famous artists started getting interested in making fans and began signing their work. So did the top fan-making companies, which grew out of older craft guilds. You can see some early examples of these signed and unsigned fans in this exhibit.

CuratorialText: Historically, European fans have been most closely associated with the eighteenth century, when their use became widespread. The examples here show the various roles fans played, including as tools for flirtation, amusement, education, propaganda, observation, and masquerade. Fan leaves communicated through their subjects and style, while users brought them to life through their gestures. Women were not only the consumers of fans but also among their producers, as fan making was considered an art form appropriate for female practitioners, whether amateur or professional.
persona1_adapted: Fans were super popular in Europe during the 1700s. People used them for all kinds of things—like flirting, having fun, learning, sharing political messages, people-watching, and wearing to costume parties. The pictures and designs on the fans sent messages, and the way people held or moved them brought those messages to life. Women didn’t just buy and use fans—they also made them. Back then, fan-making was seen as a proper art form for women, whether they did it as a hobby or a job.

CuratorialText: Handheld fans found perhaps their most prominent public display in the opera house. The atmosphere of spectatorship and performance—present both onstage and among the audience—heightened attention to fans in the hands of female theatergoers. The emphasis on seeing and being seen even gave rise to fan designs that incorporated opera glasses, or lorgnettes (from the French lorgner, “to ogle”), and small mirrors. A lorgnette fan is on view in the case nearby. Fan makers also developed a genre of fans devoted to the opera. These fans incorporated scores, lyrics, and homages to celebrated composers.
persona1_adapted: Handheld fans became a big deal at the opera house. Everyone was watching the show onstage—but also checking out the audience. Women with fans were part of the scene, too. Since seeing and being seen was so important, some fans even came with tiny binoculars (called lorgnettes, from a French word meaning “to ogle”) or small mirrors. You can see a lorgnette fan in the case nearby. Fan makers also created special opera-themed fans that showed sheet music, lyrics, and tributes to famous composers.

CuratorialText: As theatergoing grew more popular later in the century, Impressionist artists seized on the novel subject of spectators in the theater loge. The fashionability and ubiquity of fans at the opera had become fully apparent by the early 1880s, as evidenced by the cartoon below.
persona1_adapted: As more people started going to the theater in the late 1800s, Impressionist artists became interested in painting the audience sitting in theater boxes. By the early 1880s, it was clear that fans were a very common and fashionable accessory at the opera, as you can see in the cartoon below.

CuratorialText: Movement is an essential aspect of the handheld fan. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc wrote that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The expressive qualities of a fan’s motions are among the ways it can convey meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall (or be deliberately dropped) at a particularly opportune moment before a potential suitor.
persona1_adapted: A handheld fan is all about movement. In the 1800s, a critic named Charles Blanc said that a fan is really just an excuse to make graceful movements while pretending to cool yourself off. How you move a fan can say a lot about you. It can show your personality, your mood, or even what you're thinking. Fans can flutter, vibrate, gently wave, snap open or shut, rise up, or even fall—or be purposely dropped—at just the right moment in front of someone you like.

CuratorialText: Artists in the late nineteenth century understood the fan as a moving object and attempted to capture its dynamism in myriad ways. The fleeting and ephemeral nature of its movement surely made it an appealing subject for the Impressionists. Fan designs frequently incorporate metallic paints, gold leaf, feathers, spangles, and sequins, the visual impacts of which are heightened when in motion.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, artists saw fans as moving objects and tried to show their motion in many different ways. Because fans move quickly and briefly, they were a perfect subject for the Impressionists—artists who loved to capture fast, changing moments. Fan designs often include shiny materials like metallic paint, gold leaf, feathers, sparkles, and sequins. These details look even more exciting when the fan is in motion.

CuratorialText: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the passion for Japanese art and culture they observed among their contemporaries. Japanese goods became increasingly available in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. The 1867 and 1878 Universal Exhibitions in Paris were pivotal events in Europeans’ exposure to the arts of Japan. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected such works, including fans, acquiring them from newly established specialist dealers and shops. By the late 1880s, Japanese fans were imported in the millions to France, where they were used as women’s accessories and decoration.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th Grade Readers:**

In the 1870s, French critics started using the word “Japonisme” to describe how much people in Europe loved Japanese art and culture. This craze happened because, in 1853–54, the United States forced Japan to open its ports to trade with other countries. Suddenly, Japanese goods became much easier to find in Europe.

Two big world fairs in Paris—one in 1867 and another in 1878—helped introduce Europeans to Japanese art. Many Impressionist artists and their friends started collecting Japanese items, like fans, from new shops that specialized in them. By the late 1880s, millions of Japanese fans were being shipped to France, where women used them as fashion accessories and home decorations.

CuratorialText: European artists focused on the aesthetic value of these objects and borrowed from them liberally, often with little understanding of their original contexts. Today, some would call this cultural appropriation, though subtleties of interpretation varied. Certainly, the history of Japonisme is fraught, but the admiration for Japanese fans freed European artists to imagine new techniques and strategies for their art.
persona1_adapted: European artists liked how Japanese fans and other objects looked. They borrowed ideas from them freely, even though they often didn’t understand the original meaning or history behind these objects. Today, some people would call this “cultural appropriation,” though the way people saw it back then was more complicated. The story of Japonisme is not simple, but the love for Japanese fans helped European artists try new techniques and new ways of making art.

CuratorialText: In the nineteenth century, Spain became a center of fan production at the same time that the country’s tourism industry emerged and its culture was increasingly exported throughout Europe. Reporting from his travels to the Iberian Peninsula in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier claimed, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan’s prominent role in Spanish flamenco dance is likely another reason it became inextricably linked with Spain in the nineteenth-century imagination, and remains so today. In Paris at mid-century, Spanish culture was in vogue, a phenomenon known as Hispagnolisme. Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, exerted considerable influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance troupes and musicians performed often in the capital, and avant-garde artists, such as Edouard Manet, took them up as subjects. For some artists who made fans in this period, it was Spain that they had foremost in mind. Manet and Degas both chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.
persona1_adapted:
In the 1800s, Spain became a big center for making fans. Around the same time, tourism in Spain grew, and Spanish culture started spreading across Europe. A French writer named Théophile Gautier traveled to Spain in the early 1840s and wrote, “I haven’t seen a woman without a fan in this happy country.” Fans were also a key part of Spanish flamenco dancing, which is probably why people in the 1800s—and even today—strongly connect fans with Spain.

In Paris, Spanish culture became very popular in the mid-1800s. This trend was called *Hispanolisme*. The Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, had a big influence on fashion and style. Spanish dance groups and musicians often performed in Paris, and famous artists like Edouard Manet painted them. Some artists who made fans during this time were thinking mostly of Spain. Manet and another artist named Degas both used Spanish themes in their very first fan designs.

CuratorialText: The theme of the bourgeois woman with her fan in a domestic setting proliferated in art of the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book The Fan, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the role of fans to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or on ventures out, the handheld fan served as a companion, trusted protector, and elegant accoutrement.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, artists often painted wealthy women holding fans at home. In 1882, a French writer named Octave Uzanne wrote a book called *The Fan*. He told his female readers: "When you are alone or sad, your fan will be your secret friend." Whether at home or out and about, a handheld fan was more than just a pretty accessory—it was like a trusted companion and protector.

CuratorialText: As fashion accessories, fans signaled facets of their user’s identity through their materials and stylistic form as well as in the way they were handled. “One wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian,” remarked one female observer cited by Uzanne. A Japanese uchiwa fan in the hands of the Impressionist Berthe Morisot or a plumed fan held by one of Mary Cassatt’s sitters displayed their alignment with current taste and confirmed their social status.
persona1_adapted: Fans weren't just for keeping cool—they were fashion accessories that showed who you were. The materials, style, and even the way someone held a fan could send a message about their identity. One woman at the time said, “One wave of the fan is enough to tell the difference between a princess and a countess, or a marchioness and a regular person.” For example, when the artist Berthe Morisot held a Japanese uchiwa fan, or when a woman in a Mary Cassatt painting carried a feathered fan, it showed they were stylish and part of the upper class.

CuratorialText: Fans were among the first souvenirs, used since the seventeenth century to commemorate events and to remember visits to important sites. As travel increased in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, fans became popular as small, practical, and artistic objects that could serve as mementos. The Universal Exhibitions of the second half of the nineteenth century played a crucial role in promoting the French fan industry and exposing the European public to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were produced for each of these events, providing visitors with information and maps while also recording the remarkable buildings erected specifically for these occasions.
persona1_adapted:
Fans were some of the first souvenirs people ever bought. Starting in the 1600s, fans were used to remember special events or places people visited. As more people began to travel in the 1700s and 1800s, fans became popular because they were small, useful, and beautiful—perfect keepsakes.

In the late 1800s, big world fairs called Universal Exhibitions helped make the French fan industry famous. These fairs also introduced European visitors to fans from other cultures. Special souvenir fans were made for each fair. They gave visitors information and maps, and they often showed off the amazing new buildings built just for the event.

CuratorialText: This monumental work was one of a series of carved architectural supports designed for the exterior courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most renowned master sculptor in the history of Yoruba art, Olowe of Ise. The two-tiered composition embodies a formal dynamism that established Olowe's exceptional artistic reputation. Its principal personage, an equestrian warrior, is depicted frontally above a female caryatid with arms raised in three-quarter view. Through this contrast in their alignment, the figures at once reflect distinct attitudes while relating to each other fluidly. The compressed style of the upper half also contrasts with the greater degree of openness below. At the base two smaller figures radiate outward at oblique angles. In addition to the inventive dynamism of their overall design, works by Olowe are noted for the attention given to surface detail. The mounted warrior holds a spear and pistol in either hand, and his vest, saddle, and horse's headgear are articulated through deeply carved linear motifs. While innovative in its formal interpretation, the subject of this work is a classic emblem of regional leadership. In Yoruba art such equestrian figures identify their patrons with martial conquest achieved through physical might.
persona1_adapted:
This huge carving was part of a set of supports for the outdoor courtyard of a Yoruba palace. A king ordered it from Olowe of Ise, the most famous master sculptor in Yoruba art history. The two-level design shows a lively energy that made Olowe famous.

The main figure is a warrior on horseback, shown facing forward. Below him is a woman with her arms raised, shown at a slight angle. This contrast in their poses shows different attitudes, but they still connect smoothly. The top half is more compact, while the bottom half is more open. At the base, two smaller figures lean outward at angles.

Besides the creative design, Olowe’s work is known for careful surface details. The warrior holds a spear and pistol. His vest, saddle, and the horse’s headgear are carved with deep, straight patterns.

Even though the style is new, the subject is a classic symbol of leadership. In Yoruba art, horse-riding figures connect their owners to military victory through physical strength.

CuratorialText: This saltcellar is both an extraordinary example of skilled workmanship and an artifact that epitomizes a singularly important convergence of cultures. In the second half of the fifteenth century, Portuguese explorers and traders were impressed by the considerable talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. As a result, they were inspired to commission works of this kind for their patrons, which ingeniously combine both European and African aesthetics and forms. During this period, salt and pepper were costly commodities and elaborate receptacles were appropriate for their storage in princely homes. This work contains imagery relating to early Temne and Bullom worldviews. The four snakes, associated with mystical wealth, appear to confront four growling dogs. According to regional traditions, dogs are considered spiritually astute animals able to see spirits and ghosts that are invisible to humans. This depiction of the dogs, with teeth bared, hair bristling, and ears laid back, may relate to that ability. However, the level of animation in this scene could also derive from chivalric hunting scenes in European woodcuts, which were furnished to local African artists by their European patrons.
persona1_adapted:
This saltcellar is an amazing example of skilled craftsmanship and a special object that shows how two different cultures came together. In the late 1400s, Portuguese explorers and traders were really impressed by the talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. So, they asked these artists to make works like this one for their wealthy patrons back in Europe. The result is a mix of both European and African styles and ideas.

Back then, salt and pepper were very expensive, so rich families kept them in fancy containers like this. The carvings on this saltcellar tell us about the beliefs of the Temne and Bullom people. Four snakes, which stand for mystical wealth, seem to be facing off against four growling dogs. In local traditions, dogs are seen as spiritually aware animals that can see spirits and ghosts that people can’t. The dogs here have their teeth bared, fur standing up, and ears flattened—maybe showing that ability. But the lively action in this scene might also come from European hunting pictures, which European patrons gave to African artists as inspiration.

CuratorialText: By the early nineteenth century, Chokwe chiefs in the savanna of present-day Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola had become active trading partners with merchants from Europe and the New World. Profiting from commerce in ivory, rubber, wax, and African slaves, they emerged as important regional rulers whose prestige and power was reflected in the art they commissioned for their courts at this time. This figure depicts a Chokwe chief, or mwanangana, and epitomizes the balance of power and refinement that is characteristic of Chokwe court art. His large, spreading hands, muscular shoulders, and aggressive posture epitomize the strength and vigor of a ruler. A bulging, domelike forehead and large eyes suggest mental acuity and keen eyesight. The sweeping curves and swelling volumes of the distinctive headdress, rendered here in wood, depict an actual Chokwe crown made of cloth-covered basketry. Horns of the tiny duiker antelope, which bear associations with hunting and the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery, are placed on the sides of the headdress. The chief holds a lamellophone, or "thumb piano," an instrument whose music often accompanies recitations of oral histories and the praise songs of important individuals. Instrument in hand, the chief is cast as a repository and guardian of historical and cultural knowledge. While depicting a nineteenth-century Chokwe chief, this sculpture also evokes the culture hero Cibinda Ilunga, a legendary hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. Cibinda Ilunga is said to have introduced new principles of governance to the Chokwe peoples, and figures such as this one honor the productive union of these two cultures.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Readers:**

By the early 1800s, Chokwe chiefs in what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola were important trading partners with European and American merchants. They made money trading ivory, rubber, wax, and enslaved people. Because of this wealth, they became powerful rulers. The art they paid for in their courts showed off their status and strength.

This wooden figure shows a Chokwe chief, called a *mwanangana*. It perfectly captures the mix of power and elegance found in Chokwe court art. The chief’s big, open hands, strong shoulders, and bold pose show he is strong and full of energy. His large, dome-shaped forehead and big eyes suggest he is smart and sees things clearly.

The chief wears a special headdress. It’s carved from wood to look like a real Chokwe crown made of cloth-covered basket material. On the sides are horns from a tiny antelope called a duiker. These horns are linked to hunting and to dangerous, mysterious practices like medicine and sorcery.

In his hands, the chief holds a lamellophone, also called a “thumb piano.” This instrument is often used to play music that goes with storytelling and songs that praise important people. By holding the instrument, the chief is shown as someone who keeps and protects the history and culture of his people.

Even though this sculpture shows a 19th-century Chokwe chief, it also reminds viewers of a legendary hero named Cibinda Ilunga. He was a famous hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. According to stories, Cibinda Ilunga taught the Chokwe people new ways to rule. Figures like this one honor the connection between these two cultures.

CuratorialText: The Dogon live in the elevated rocky heights of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment. They rely on agriculture and manage to wrest subsistence crops from poor soil in an area that receives little rain. This monumental figure is one of the largest Dogon sculptures known. The region's dry climate has preserved Dogon works far longer than is usual for African wood sculpture, and radiocarbon testing of this work suggests that it may date as far back as the fifteenth century. Dogon sculpture is primarily concerned with the spirits responsible for the fertility of both land and people. These include a family's real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. While the exact functions and meanings of individual works often remain obscure, scholars agree that Dogon sculptures were created for shrines. The most distinctive subject rendered by Dogon sculptures is that of a single figure standing with raised arms. This posture has usually been interpreted as a gesture of prayer—an effort to link earth and heavens—and it has been suggested that it may represent an appeal for rain. This masterpiece of Dogon figurative art depicts a man with a well-modeled body in a naturalistic stance. The artist has carefully rendered the figure's musculature, anatomical detail, and body ornament. His stylized beard identifies him as an elder and an individual whose age and experience entitle him to participate in the most important religious, political, and social affairs of Dogon society. The figure wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that indicate his status, as well as a belt and neck pendants resembling leather talismans that also suggest his spiritual importance.
persona1_adapted:
The Dogon people live in the rocky highlands of Mali’s Bandiagara Escarpment. They farm the land, growing food in poor soil with very little rain. This large statue is one of the biggest Dogon sculptures ever found. Because the area is so dry, the wood has lasted much longer than most African wood carvings. Scientists used radiocarbon testing to figure out that this sculpture might be from the 1400s.

Dogon art is mostly about spirits that help the land and people be fertile and successful. These spirits include real and mythical family ancestors, the souls of women who died while giving birth, and water spirits. We don’t always know exactly what each sculpture was used for, but experts agree they were made for shrines. The most common type of Dogon sculpture shows a single person standing with their arms raised. This pose is usually seen as a prayer—a way to connect earth and sky—and some think it might be a request for rain.

This masterpiece shows a man with a well-shaped body standing in a natural way. The artist carefully carved his muscles, body details, and decorations. His styled beard tells us he is an elder—someone old and wise enough to take part in the most important religious, political, and social events of the Dogon community. He wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that show his high status, plus a belt and neck pendants that look like leather charms, hinting at his spiritual power.

CuratorialText: When works from Equatorial Africa in this refined style began to enter Western consciousness in the early twentieth century, they were a great enigma to art critics. Many speculated about the sources of their exotic aesthetic and even proposed possible Asian influence, though the art form was in fact indigenous to southern Gabon. Such masks were worn by virtuosic male performers of a stilt dance called "mukudj," which involved towering impressively while executing complex choreography and astonishing feats of acrobatics. The creator of a "mukudj" mask would attempt to capture the likeness of the most beautiful woman in his community. The subject of this particular idealized and stylized portrait was embellished in classic nineteenth-century fashion with a coiffure composed of a central lobe and two lateral tresses and with cicatrization motifs on the forehead and temples. Kaolin taken from riverbeds, which was associated with healing and with a spiritual, ancestral realm of existence, was applied to the surface of the face. By using this material, the artist both celebrated the beauty of a mortal woman and transformed her into a transcendent being.
persona1_adapted:
When artworks from Equatorial Africa first became known in Europe and America in the early 1900s, art critics were totally confused. They had no idea where these beautiful objects came from. Some guessed that Asian art had influenced them, but actually, these masks were made by artists in southern Gabon, a country in Africa.

These masks were used in a special dance called *mukudj*. Male dancers who were incredibly skilled would wear the masks while walking on tall stilts. They performed amazing acrobatic moves and complicated dance steps, towering above the crowd.

The artist who made a *mukudj* mask tried to create the face of the most beautiful woman in his village. This particular mask shows a woman with a classic 1800s hairstyle: a big section in the middle and two braids on the sides. You can also see scar patterns carved on her forehead and temples.

The mask’s face is covered with a white powder called kaolin, which comes from riverbeds. People believed kaolin had healing powers and was connected to the spirit world of ancestors. By using this special material, the artist honored the beauty of a real woman and also turned her into something magical and otherworldly.

CuratorialText: A lü me was typically commissioned by a man of standing in honor of a favorite wife. The subject’s distinguishing traits, including her facial features, cicatrices, and braided coiffure, would be captured by the artist through direct observation. The unveiling of a completed figure was celebrated with lavish feasting sponsored by its patron. Named for their subjects and stored in special baskets, such portraits were subsequently displayed on special occasions. This example was carved by celebrated early 20th century carver, Zlan of Belewale, and his wife, Sonzlanwan. Zlan’s calling was revealed to him as an adolescent through the miraculous appearance of his signature tool, an adze, falling from the branches of an oil palm. The sculptor honed his skills on soft tuber roots and soapstone before his mastery of wood earned him the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), meaning "creator" or "god." As the most sought-after Dan artist of the first half of the twentieth century, Zlan mentored scores of apprentices. Many of these trainees were sent by regional chiefs to acquire his expertise for their own communities. Through his efforts, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a sculptor—became an important carving center. Zlan’s works, including masks, ladles, and figurative portraits, have been collected globally. Defying the convention that woodcarving was a man’s vocation, Sonzlanwon (literally, "a snail, if god agrees") collaborated with her husband on many of his greatest works. She accompanied him on commissions for important patrons—the intense concentration required to execute these was such that they often worked side by side in seclusion. Sonzlanwon is credited with blocking out Zlan’s works’ initial form, using wood felled by his apprentices. Following his refinement of the contours, she handled final detailing, including the braided raffia-palm coiffures added to figures and ladles. As Zlan aged, it is believed that Sonzlanwon’s role in their collaboration expanded. Documentation of her contributions derives from interviews conducted by German anthropologist Hans Himmelheber shortly before Zlan’s death, as well as from remembrances of surviving relatives and acquaintances.
persona1_adapted:
A lü me was a special carved figure, usually made by a man of high status to honor his favorite wife. The artist would carefully study the woman to capture her unique features, like her face shape, scars, and braided hair. When the figure was finished, the person who paid for it would throw a big feast to celebrate. These portraits were named after the women they showed and were kept in special baskets, only brought out for important occasions.

This particular figure was carved by Zlan of Belewale, a famous artist from the early 1900s, and his wife, Sonzlanwan. Zlan discovered his talent as a teenager when his special carving tool, an adze, miraculously appeared falling from a palm tree. He practiced first on soft roots and soapstone before mastering wood, earning him the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), which means "creator" or "god." He became the most popular Dan artist in the first half of the 20th century and taught many students. Local chiefs even sent their people to learn from him so they could bring his skills back to their own villages. Thanks to Zlan, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a sculptor—became a major center for carving. His works, including masks, spoons, and figure portraits, are now collected all over the world.

Even though woodcarving was usually a man's job, Sonzlanwon (whose name means "a snail, if god agrees") worked with her husband on many of his greatest pieces. They would travel together for important jobs, often working side by side in private because the work required intense focus. Sonzlanwon is credited with carving the basic shape of Zlan's works using wood cut by his students. After he refined the details, she handled the finishing touches, including the braided raffia-palm hair on figures and spoons. As Zlan got older, her role in their partnership grew. We know about her contributions from interviews a German anthropologist did just before Zlan died, as well as from stories told by family and friends who were still alive.

CuratorialText: Central African power figures are among the ubiquitous genres identified with African art. Conceived to house specific mystical forces, they were collaborative creations of Kongo sculptors and ritual specialists. This example belongs to the most ambitious class of that tradition, attributed to the atelier of a master active along the coast of Congo and Angola at the end of the nineteenth century and identified with Mangaaka, the preeminent force of jurisprudence. That power was represented as a presiding authority and enforcing lord or king. Its crowning element is the distinctive mpu headdress worn by chiefs or priests. The figure's posture and gesture, leaning forward with hands placed akimbo on the hips, is the aggressive attitude of one who challenges fearlessly. Traces of a missing beard-a sign of seniority-survive in the form of nails along the contours of the chin. There are also vestiges of an abdominal cavity for medicinal matter that originally attracted the figure's defining force. The various metals embedded in the figure's expansive torso attest to its central role as witness and enforcer of affairs critical to its community. They document vows sealed, treaties signed, and efforts to eradicate evil. Ultimately, this work inspired reflection on the consequences of transgressing established codes of social conduct.
persona1_adapted:
**Power Figures: Art with a Purpose**

In Central Africa, artists created special statues called "power figures." These weren't just decorations—they were made to hold magical forces. Skilled sculptors and spiritual experts worked together to make them.

This particular statue is one of the most important of its kind. It was made by a master artist who lived along the coast of Congo and Angola in the late 1800s. The statue represents Mangaaka, the most powerful force of justice. Think of it like a judge or king who makes sure people follow the rules.

Look at the hat on top of its head. That's called an *mpu* headdress, and only chiefs or priests wore them. The statue leans forward with its hands on its hips—a bold, fearless pose that says, "I'm not backing down."

You might notice some nails around the chin area. Those are all that's left of a beard, which was a sign of wisdom and old age. There's also a hollow space in the belly where special medicines were placed to give the statue its power.

The metal pieces stuck into the statue's chest tell a story. They mark important moments for the community: promises made, peace treaties signed, and attempts to get rid of evil. This statue was a witness to it all.

In the end, this artwork made people think about what happens when you break the rules of society.
