CuratorialText: Throughout the twentieth century, members of Poro, a Senufo initiation association, wore small, finely carved face masks as insignia. The masks, known as kpeliye'e, feature delicate oval faces with geometric projections at the sides. Raised and incised scarification patterns ornament their smooth, glossy surfaces. Considered feminine, the masks honor deceased Senufo elders with their grace and beauty. They provide a complement to the aggressive Senufo helmet masks also sponsored by fraternal organizations in the region. The feathers and animal horns attached to this example are unusual, and may have reflected its owner's power to counteract negative forces in the community.
persona1_adapted: In the 1900s, members of a Senufo group called Poro wore small, finely carved face masks as badges of honor. These masks are called *kpeliye'e*. They have delicate, oval faces with geometric shapes sticking out from the sides. Raised and carved patterns on the smooth, shiny surface look like scarification marks. People think of these masks as feminine. They honor older Senufo leaders with their grace and beauty. The masks balance out the more aggressive helmet masks that other groups in the area also sponsored. The feathers and animal horns on this particular mask are unusual. They might have shown that the owner had the power to fight off bad things in the community.

CuratorialText: This cross was created in the northern Highlands, near the Red Sea, the birthplace of Ethiopia's earliest kingdom and of Christianity in Africa. A small corpus of Ethiopian Christian works from before the seventeenth century has survived. This exceptional creation, in which a highly unusual interplay of materials affords rich tonal contrasts, is a tour de force. The solidity of the carved-wood structure is permeated by finely applied metal inlays. The complexity of this work, with each side elaborately distinct, is unrivaled. It has been attributed to Ezra (ca. 1460-1522), a member of the Stephanite monastic order. The Stephanites came to be recognized as the foremost carvers of crosses and illuminators of manuscripts. During a period of the order's exile in Egypt, Ezra is reputed to have enhanced his training as a craftsman and learned the technique of marquetry. It has been suggested that he may have produced this work at the Gunda Gunde monastery, renowned for its manuscripts. In the Ethiopian church, the wooden cross is perceived as having been sanctified by Christ's blood, which conferred upon it infinite power to heal and to bless. Foliate and organic interlace designs, branching from the foot of the cross, are interpreted as symbolizing the Tree of Paradise, and thus a vibrant life-giving force. Commissioned by Ethiopian royalty, such works were presented to important monasteries to be carried in liturgical processions.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for an 8th-Grade Reader**

This cross was made in the northern Highlands of Ethiopia, near the Red Sea. That area is where Ethiopia’s first kingdom began, and where Christianity first came to Africa. Very few Ethiopian Christian artworks from before the 1600s have survived, so this one is special.

The artist used a mix of materials to create rich contrasts in color and texture. The main part is carved from solid wood, but it’s covered with thin metal inlays. Each side of the cross is different and very detailed—no other cross like it exists.

Experts think it was made by a man named Ezra (about 1460–1522). He was a member of the Stephanite monks, who were famous for carving crosses and decorating manuscripts. When the Stephanites were forced to leave Ethiopia for a while and live in Egypt, Ezra is said to have learned a new skill there: marquetry, which is a fancy way of inlaying pieces of material into wood.

Some believe Ezra made this cross at the Gunda Gunde monastery, which was known for its beautiful handwritten books.

In the Ethiopian church, wooden crosses are seen as holy because they were touched by Christ’s blood. That gave them the power to heal and bless. The designs on this cross—leaves and vines that twist together and grow from the bottom—are meant to remind people of the Tree of Paradise. They show life and energy.

This cross was made for Ethiopian royalty, who gave it to important monasteries to be carried in religious processions.

CuratorialText: This illuminated manuscript of the Four Gospels was created at a monastic center in northern Ethiopia. Twenty full-page paintings depict scenes from the life of Christ and four portraits of the evangelists introduce the respective Gospel texts. The New Testament was translated from Greek into Geez, the classical language of Ethiopia, in the sixth century. Both this text and its pictorial format draw upon Byzantine prototypes, which were transformed into a local idiom of expression. Stylistically consistent, the paintings reflect the hands of two distinct artists. The color scheme consists of red, yellow, green, and blue. A stylized uniformity is reflected in the abbreviated definition of facial features and the bold linear articulation of the human form in black and red. Figures' heads are depicted frontally, their bodies often in profile. Bodies are treated as columnar masses encased in textiles composed of striated fields juxtaposed against one another. This work is evidence of sub-Saharan Africa's historically complex interrelationships with Arabia, Egypt, and the eastern Mediterranean. Early complex societies in the Highlands developed primarily through local processes beginning in the late second millennium B.C.E. Those societies attracted trade from across the Red Sea, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean, which spurred migrations, religious exchanges, and greater concentrations of wealth. By the early first millennium C.E., the Kingdom of Aksum emerged as a powerful civilization that, at its height, extended from Ethiopia's Highlands in the South, to the Eastern Desert in the North, to southern Arabia in the East. Emperor Ezana (r. ca. 320s-360 C.E.) was the first monarch to convert to Christianity, and adopted it as the state religion. Subsequent kingdoms, including the Zagwe Dynasty (ca. 1137-1270 C.E.) and the Ethiopian Empire (1270-1974 C.E.) continued to embrace Christianity. Monasteries were founded as centers of learning responsible for disseminating knowledge and consolidating the power and influence of the ruling elite. In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the text of the Gospels was considered the most important holy writing; the miniatures at the beginning of this manuscript were intended to be viewed during liturgical processions. Such works were frequently presented to churches by distinguished patrons; they reflected both the prestige of royal benefactors and the erudition of the monastic scriptoria in which they were created. Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia's ruling elite may have commissioned this manuscript at Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery for presentation to his or her favored church or monastery. Brief notations indicate that the church in question was dedicated to the Archangel Michael.
persona1_adapted:
**A Very Old Book from Ethiopia**

This book is a handwritten copy of the Four Gospels, the stories of Jesus’ life from the Bible. It was made over 700 years ago by monks in a monastery in northern Ethiopia.

Inside, there are 20 full-page paintings showing scenes from Jesus’ life. There are also four portraits of the Gospel writers (Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John) at the start of each story. The text is written in Ge’ez, an ancient language of Ethiopia. The New Testament was first translated into Ge’ez from Greek around 1,500 years ago.

The art style was inspired by Byzantine (Eastern Roman) art, but the Ethiopian artists made it their own. Two different artists worked on the paintings, but they look very similar. The colors are bright: red, yellow, green, and blue. The faces are simple and look alike. The bodies are drawn with bold black and red lines. People’s heads face forward, but their bodies are often shown from the side. Their bodies look like solid columns wrapped in striped clothing.

This book shows how Ethiopia was connected to other parts of the world long ago. People in the Ethiopian Highlands had been trading with Arabia, Egypt, and the Mediterranean for thousands of years. By about 1,700 years ago, the Kingdom of Aksum had become a powerful civilization. It stretched from the Ethiopian Highlands to the Red Sea and even into southern Arabia.

King Ezana, who ruled around 1,700 years ago, was the first Ethiopian king to become Christian. He made Christianity the official religion of his kingdom. Later kingdoms, like the Zagwe Dynasty and the Ethiopian Empire, kept Christianity as their main faith. Monasteries became important centers of learning and helped the rulers stay powerful.

In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the Gospels were the most important holy book. The paintings at the start of this manuscript were meant to be seen during church processions. Rich and powerful people often gave books like this to churches as gifts. It showed how important they were and how skilled the monks who made the book were.

Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia’s royal family may have ordered this book from the Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery. They probably gave it to a church or monastery dedicated to the Archangel Michael.

CuratorialText: Phillips pursued a long and successful career over fifty years, taking to the road as an itinerant artist painting portraits of clusters of friends, relatives, and neighbors in New York State and New England. His stock in trade was the plain likeness, devoid of shadows or modeling. Although he depended on repetitious formulas to work efficiently as he traveled the countryside, he was nevertheless able to individualize his sitters imaginatively. In "Mrs. Mayer and Daughter," Phillips’s masterful design abilities are evident in the cleanly contained forms of the mother and child, enriched by the use of brilliant, saturated color and careful detail.
persona1_adapted: Phillips had a long and successful career that lasted over fifty years. He traveled from town to town as an artist, painting portraits of groups of friends, family members, and neighbors in New York State and New England. His specialty was creating simple, realistic portraits without shadows or shading. Even though he used repeated patterns to work quickly as he moved around the countryside, he still managed to make each person in his paintings look unique and interesting. In the painting *Mrs. Mayer and Daughter*, you can see Phillips’s strong design skills in the clean, simple shapes of the mother and child. The painting is made even more striking by the use of bright, bold colors and careful details.

CuratorialText: Madame Pierre Gautreau (the Louisiana-born Virginie Amélie Avegno; 1859–1915) was known in Paris for her artful appearance. Sargent hoped to enhance his reputation by painting and exhibiting her portrait. Working without a commission but with his sitter’s complicity, he emphasized her daring personal style, showing the right strap of her gown slipping from her shoulder. At the Salon of 1884, the portrait received more ridicule than praise. Sargent repainted the shoulder strap and kept the work for over thirty years. When, eventually, he sold it to the Metropolitan, he commented, “I suppose it is the best thing I have done,” but asked that the Museum disguise the sitter’s name.
persona1_adapted:
Madame Pierre Gautreau was born in Louisiana as Virginie Amélie Avegno. She lived in Paris and was famous for her stylish look. The artist John Singer Sargent wanted to become more well-known, so he decided to paint her portrait. He didn't get paid for it, but she agreed to pose for him.

Sargent showed off her bold fashion sense. He painted the strap of her dress slipping off her shoulder. When the painting was shown at a big art show in 1884, people made fun of it instead of praising it. Sargent later fixed the shoulder strap and kept the painting for more than thirty years.

When he finally sold it to the Metropolitan Museum, he said, "I suppose it is the best thing I have done." But he asked the museum to hide the woman's real name.

CuratorialText: Alexander, who lived in Paris during the 1890s, achieved international success with his figure paintings of gracefully posed women in elegant interiors. In this striking composition, the model’s provocative facial expression and supple curves speak to the contemporary French taste for sensuous images of women. The bold composition also suggests the undulating linear rhythms of the Art Nouveau style, examples of which were featured at the Paris Exposition. Alexander, known as the "the painter of the flowing line," was celebrated at home and abroad for these works of sinuous grace and lyricism.
persona1_adapted: Alexander lived in Paris in the 1890s and became famous around the world for his paintings of women. He showed them in graceful poses, often in beautiful rooms. In this striking painting, the model’s bold expression and soft curves show what French people at the time liked: images of women that felt elegant and full of life. The painting’s flowing shapes also remind us of the Art Nouveau style, which was popular at the Paris Exposition. Alexander was called "the painter of the flowing line," and people everywhere admired his work for its smooth, graceful, and poetic feel.

CuratorialText: In early 1859, Bierstadt accompanied a government survey expedition, headed by Frederick W. Lander, to the Nebraska Territory. By summer, the party had reached the Wind River Range of the Rocky Mountains in present-day Wyoming. Painted in New York after Bierstadt’s return, this work advertised the landscape as a frontier destined to be claimed by White settlers, according to the doctrine of Manifest Destiny. This belief that Americans were the divinely ordained "masters" of the continent systematically ignored—with dire consequences—the presence of Indigenous populations, such as the Shoshone peoples depicted in the foreground.
persona1_adapted: In early 1859, artist Albert Bierstadt joined a government survey team led by Frederick W. Lander to explore the Nebraska Territory. By summer, they reached the Wind River Range of the Rocky Mountains in what is now Wyoming. After returning to New York, Bierstadt painted this scene. The painting shows the landscape as a frontier that white settlers believed they were meant to claim, based on an idea called Manifest Destiny. This belief said that Americans were chosen by God to be the "masters" of the land. It ignored the fact that Native peoples, like the Shoshone shown in the front of the painting, already lived there. This had serious and harmful consequences.

CuratorialText: In the summer of 1845, Bingham returned to his St. Louis home from a winter stay in central Missouri, bringing with him several paintings and sketches. This was one of those works that he later sent to New York’s American Art-Union, a subscription-based organization that promoted American art nationally through exhibitions and the distribution of popular prints. Titled by the artist "French Trader & Half breed Son", the Art-Union changed it to the more generic and less controversial "Fur Traders Descending the Missouri". Bingham, who began his career as a portraitist, produced this distinctive genre painting with little precedent in his oeuvre. The tranquil scene, with its luminous atmosphere, idealized the American frontier for the benefit of an Eastern audience.
persona1_adapted:
In the summer of 1845, an artist named George Caleb Bingham came back to his home in St. Louis after spending the winter in central Missouri. He brought with him several paintings and sketches he had made. One of those works was this painting.

Bingham later sent this painting to the American Art-Union in New York. The Art-Union was a club where people paid a fee to join. In return, they got to see art shows and receive popular prints. The group’s goal was to promote American art all across the country.

Bingham originally called this painting *French Trader & Half breed Son*. But the Art-Union changed the title to *Fur Traders Descending the Missouri*. They wanted a name that was more general and less likely to cause arguments.

Bingham started his career painting portraits of people. This painting is different from most of his earlier work. It shows a calm, peaceful scene on the Missouri River. The light in the painting makes everything look soft and beautiful. Bingham painted this scene to make the American frontier seem like an ideal, perfect place—especially for people who lived back East and had never seen it.

CuratorialText: Fascinated by the winding Connecticut River, Cole made the U-shaped bend known as the “Oxbow” the subject of this painting. Beyond the tempestuous storm clouds and blasted tree lies a patchwork of farmland, sunlight illuminating private properties full of crops, hay bales, and sheep. Cole’s composition emphasizes the encroachment of Euro-American settlement into a supposed untamed “wilderness.” At the lower center, the artist depicted himself pausing from a sketch and gazing at the viewer. “I think that [Cole] is calling out the fact that our way of developing—our way of improving the land or making it profitable—was pushing nature out, Indigenous people out.” Xiye Bastida, climate activist, Audioguide 4026
persona1_adapted:
Thomas Cole was fascinated by the winding Connecticut River. He painted a famous U-shaped bend in the river called the “Oxbow.” In the painting, you can see dark storm clouds and a broken tree on one side. On the other side, there’s a patchwork of farmland, with sunlight shining on fields full of crops, hay bales, and sheep.

Cole’s painting shows how European settlers were moving into land that was often seen as “wild” or untouched. But that land wasn’t empty—it was already home to Indigenous people. At the bottom center of the painting, Cole painted himself taking a break from sketching and looking straight at you, the viewer.

Climate activist Xiye Bastida says, “I think that [Cole] is calling out the fact that our way of developing—our way of improving the land or making it profitable—was pushing nature out, Indigenous people out.”

CuratorialText: Returning to Philadelphia from Europe in 1870, Eakins began a series of representations of the sport of sculling, a subject with which he is uniquely identified. This is the first major work in that series of paintings and watercolors, and the one that launched his professional career. It is believed to commemorate the victory of Max Schmitt (1843–1900), a school friend, attorney, and skilled amateur rower, in an important race on the Schuylkill River in October 1870. Also an avid rower, Eakins depicted himself pulling the oars of a scull in the middle distance.
persona1_adapted:
In 1870, Thomas Eakins moved back to Philadelphia from Europe and started painting a sport called sculling (rowing a small, narrow boat). This painting is the first big work he did on that topic, and it helped launch his career as an artist.

The painting is believed to celebrate a victory by Max Schmitt, a friend from school who became a lawyer and a very good amateur rower. He won an important race on the Schuylkill River in October 1870. Eakins was also a big fan of rowing, and he painted himself in the background, rowing a scull.

CuratorialText: A man and his dog sit perched on the shore of Narragansett Bay, Rhode Island, sunlight at their backs as they witness darkening clouds and a thin bolt of lightning warning of the tempest to come. Heade vividly captures the anticipation—in the words of a critic of his day, the “ominous hush”—that prevailed under a blackening sky and the eerily illuminated terrain. This is one of the earliest works in a small series of coastal storm subjects painted by Heade, based on a sketch of an approaching storm he witnessed from Prudence Island around 1858.
persona1_adapted: A man and his dog sit on the shore of Narragansett Bay in Rhode Island. The sun is behind them, but ahead, dark clouds gather and a thin bolt of lightning flashes—a warning that a storm is coming. The artist, Heade, captures the tense, quiet moment just before the storm hits. A critic from his time called it the “ominous hush,” or the scary stillness under a dark sky and strange, glowing light. This painting is one of Heade’s earliest storm scenes by the coast. He based it on a sketch he made of a storm he saw coming from Prudence Island around 1858.

CuratorialText: Threatened by sharks and a waterspout, a Black man faces his demise aboard a damaged boat. The painting represents the culmination of Homer’s interest in depicting conflict between humans and nature. Sugarcane, the Caribbean commodity central to the economy of empire, and transatlantic slavery are linked by the titular Gulf Stream current. Homer interwove geopolitical themes into an epic saga that foregrounds human struggle—personified by a stoic survivor—against the relentless power of nature and history.
persona1_adapted: A Black man is stranded on a broken boat, facing danger from sharks and a waterspout. This painting is the peak of Homer’s work showing people fighting against nature. The title, *The Gulf Stream*, connects sugarcane—a key crop in the Caribbean that helped build empires—to the history of slavery across the Atlantic. Homer mixed big political ideas into a dramatic story about human struggle, with a calm survivor at the center, standing up to the unstoppable forces of nature and history.

CuratorialText: Cassatt and her family spent the summer of 1880 at Marly-le-Roi, outside of Paris, where the artist focused on depicting gardens and domestic life. Here, she has portrayed her older sister, Lydia, fashionably if incongruously overdressed and insulated by a walled garden landscape. She is absorbed in the sort of old-fashioned textile handwork that was increasingly prized by the affluent in these years of mechanized labor. Although Cassatt’s experiments with plein-air (outdoor) painting are few, in this dynamic composition she effectively captured the dazzling sunlight associated with her fellow French Impressionists.
persona1_adapted:
In the summer of 1880, Cassatt and her family stayed in a town near Paris called Marly-le-Roi. While there, she spent a lot of time painting gardens and scenes from everyday home life.

In this painting, Cassatt shows her older sister, Lydia. Lydia is dressed in fancy clothes that seem a bit out of place for being outdoors. She is sitting in a walled garden, completely focused on sewing or doing needlework by hand. At that time, many rich people valued handmade crafts more, even though machines were starting to do a lot of the work.

Cassatt didn’t paint outside very often, but here she did a great job capturing the bright, dazzling sunlight. This style was a key part of what made the French Impressionists famous.

CuratorialText: Spencer portrayed the unvarnished reality of male-female relationships in this painting, which chronicles the travails of early married life. Here, the unseen young wife is not in full command of her kitchen, having sent her young husband out to procure the groceries—a task for which he is evidently not qualified. Using her own husband as a model, Spencer depicted the man stumbling down the street and attempting to hold on to the items in his overflowing basket, attracting gazes from at least one onlooker and suffering a public humiliation. Spencer depicted in humorous terms a real social anxiety of the time: that of setting up a household and running it efficiently.
persona1_adapted: In this painting, Spencer shows the real, unglamorous side of relationships between men and women—specifically, the struggles of early married life. The young wife, who we can’t see, isn’t totally in charge of her kitchen. She sent her new husband out to buy groceries, but he’s clearly not great at the job. Spencer used her own husband as the model for this scene. The man stumbles down the street, trying not to drop the items overflowing from his basket. At least one person is staring at him, and he’s totally embarrassed. Spencer makes a joke out of a real worry people had back then: how to run a household and do it well.

CuratorialText: This sculpture is one of the most spectacular examples of ceramic figures created by Tolita-Tumaco artists. It depicts an elderly person, recognizable by facial wrinkles, baggy skin beneath the eyes, the few remaining teeth, and chin stubble. Its flattened head reveals cranial modification during childhood, a cultural practice that could have signaled ethnic identity or rank in antiquity. The gender of this effigy is not clear. Marked nipples, as in this figure’s chest, were more common in female than male representations in Tolita-Tumaco and Jama-Coaque ceramic traditions of coastal Ecuador. This statue, however, lacks the long skirts that are typically seen in female images. The artist(s) would have taken several days to complete the sculpture due to the complexity of the body and the weight of the moist clay. This figure was crafted using the gray clay paste that characterizes Tolita-Tumaco ceramics. The torso was shaped most likely using clay slabs that were gradually added until completing the final form. The limbs and the head were made separately and attached to the torso before drying and firing. A one-side mold was used for the face while the wrinkles and other details were incised when the clay paste was still soft. Today’s monochromatic and matte appearance of these sculptures is the result of wear. Apparently, many of these objects had a smooth and polished finish and were decorated with post-fired paints (see Valdez 1992:136; MMA 1991.436.7a, b), in a similar fashion to Jama-Coaque sculptures (MMA 1980.34.38a, MMA 1980.34.39). A careful examination of the lower back shows that this figure may have been seated on a stool (compare with MMA 1991.436.7a, b). Elaborate stone or wood seats were important symbols of authority in the ancient Americas, from Ecuador to Mesoamerica, including the Amazonian and the Caribbean areas (McEwan and Looper 2021). The relatively high rank or importance of this individual is also suggested by the collar and bracelets modeled in clay. Adornments, probably made of gold, originally hung from holes on its nose, ears, and nipples (see MMA 1979.206.1204 and 1979.206.1136). Tolita-Tumaco potters are known for their realistic renderings of humans, such as the examples in the collections of Casa de Alabado in Quito (Cruz Cevallos 1992:225) and Museo Chileno de Arte Precolombino in Santiago (MCHAP 0348). Some of their figures are depicted leaning forward, adopting an animated pose, with flexed legs, arched torsos, and faces staring ahead. Elders were a popular theme, and several examples can be found in the collections of the Museo del Banco Central de Ecuador in Quito (No. 12-53-69, LT-2-16-69, LT-3-11-70) and in Guayaquil (GA-1-2268-82, GA-5-1631-80). A common interpretation is that these elder figures were representations of religious specialists, or ancient "shamans" who had the responsibility to mediate relations between people and other beings in nature. The marked spine in the back may be revealing of more than a very lean body, as it bears a resemblance to iguanas (compare with a Bahia example of iguanas: MMA 1988.117.7). With dry and wrinkled skin, the comparison of Tolita-Tumaco revered elders with those reptiles could have been a way to evoke the human-animal connection that is a key element in shamanistic rituals. Tolita-Tumaco was a civilization that flourished on the tropical Pacific coast of northern Ecuador and southern Colombia. In a landscape shaped by the rivers, with swampy mangroves, and hills covered in dense forests, these communities settled in large villages organized around plazas and several massive earthen mounds, known as tolas, that were the residences and burials of the elite. Remains of these human and animal sculptures have been found in settlements and cemeteries, but their original use remains unknown. The Tolita-Tumaco tradition started around 600 BCE, but its distinctive style matured between 200 BCE and 400 CE. During these centuries, these communities produced one of the most refined metalwork traditions in the ancient Americas and established relationships with other communities on the Pacific coast, including those in Northern Peru.
persona1_adapted:
**An Elder Figure from Ancient Ecuador**

This sculpture is a famous example of ceramic art made by the Tolita-Tumaco people. It shows an elderly person. You can tell they are old because of the wrinkles on their face, the saggy skin under their eyes, their few remaining teeth, and the stubble on their chin.

The head is flat on top. This happened because the person’s skull was shaped when they were a baby. In ancient times, this was a cultural practice that might have shown what group they belonged to or their social status.

It’s not clear if this figure is a man or a woman. The chest has nipples, which was more common in female figures from this culture. But this figure is not wearing the long skirt that women usually wore in these artworks.

Making this sculpture was not quick. It probably took the artist several days because the body is complex and the wet clay was heavy. The artist used gray clay, which is typical for Tolita-Tumaco ceramics. They built the body by adding slabs of clay. The arms, legs, and head were made separately and then attached before the clay dried and was fired (baked hard in a kiln). The face was made using a one-sided mold, and the wrinkles and other details were carved into the soft clay.

Today, the sculpture looks plain and matte (not shiny). But originally, it was probably smooth and polished, and painted with bright colors after it was fired.

If you look at the lower back, you can see the figure might have been sitting on a stool. In ancient times, fancy stone or wooden seats were symbols of power and authority. This person was probably important. They are also wearing a collar and bracelets made of clay. Holes in the nose, ears, and nipples suggest that jewelry—probably made of gold—once hung there.

The Tolita-Tumaco artists were known for making very realistic human figures. Many of their sculptures show people leaning forward, with bent legs, curved backs, and faces looking straight ahead. Old people were a popular subject. Many experts think these elder figures represent religious leaders, or “shamans.” Shamans were people who acted as messengers between humans and nature spirits.

The figure’s spine is very visible on its back. This might not just mean the person was thin. It could also look like the back of an iguana. Since iguanas have dry, wrinkled skin, comparing an elder to an iguana might have been a way to show the connection between humans and animals—an important idea in shaman rituals.

**Who Were the Tolita-Tumaco?**
The Tolita-Tumaco people lived on the tropical Pacific coast of what is now Ecuador and Colombia. Their land had rivers, swampy mangrove forests, and hills covered in thick forests. They lived in large villages built around open plazas and huge earthen mounds called *tolas*. These mounds were homes and burial places for the rich and powerful.

Sculptures like this one have been found in both villages and cemeteries, but we don’t know exactly how they were used. The Tolita-Tumaco culture began around 600 BCE, but their most famous art style developed between 200 BCE and 400 CE. During that time, they also became known for making some of the finest metal objects in the ancient Americas. They traded with other communities along the Pacific coast, including people in northern Peru.

CuratorialText: This mask, made of hammered sheet gold alloy and covered in red pigment, once adorned the body of a deceased ruler on Peru’s north coast. Powerful dynasties arose in this region between the eighth and the fourteenth centuries CE and amassed great riches in gold and silver before they were conquered by the Inca Empire in the late fifteenth century. The lords of these dynasties were the patrons of vast workshops where finely crafted ornaments and ceremonial vessels were created (see 1991.419.62; 66.196.27). At death, the lords were buried deep in monumental mud-brick platform mounds along with large numbers of objects of precious metal, shell, and cloth. In addition to beakers, disks, and other ornaments, the burials included large masks made of sheet gold. As many as five masks were placed into one burial: one attached to the head of the textile-wrapped body, and the other four stacked at the feet of the deceased. This mask was made of an alloy of 74 percent gold, 20 percent silver, and 6 percent copper which was then hammered into a sheet and shaped into the form of a face. Interestingly, cinnabar, a red mineral pigment, covers much of the cheeks and forehead of this mask, obscuring the gold surface. The cinnabar may emulate the patterning of face paint worn by individuals of importance: according to early colonial accounts a courtier was charged with the task of maintaining such paints. Faint traces of the textile wrappings that once enveloped the mummy bundle can be detected in impressions on the red paint. Masks in museum collections are often missing such surface embellishment (see 1979.206.556), as the pigment would have been removed in modern times to highlight the golden substrate. The eyes of this mask have thin, skewer-like projections emerging from the pupils, perhaps suggesting powerful or even piercing vision. In other masks these projections include small beads of amber and emerald, which have led scholars to interpret the projections as tears. On the ears and ear ornaments can be seen restored examples of the silver-surfaced overlays (now corroded green) that would have also been originally present attached to the mask. Further surface additions include danglers on the U-shaped nose ornament. Such spangles would have caught the light of the bright sun and conveyed a sense of movement and life as a mummy bundle was conveyed to its final resting place. Masks such as these, with their characteristic ovoid eyes terminating in a point, are associated with the Lambayeque culture, named for a region near the modern city of Chiclayo on Peru’s north coast. This polity, also known as Sicán, erected great monumental centers such as Batán Grande, Chornancap, and other sites. Recent excavations at Chornancap by Carlos Wester and his team have revealed that such masks were part of the burial regalia of high-status women as well as men. According to myths on the north coast, gold was particularly associated with male rulers, silver with noble women, and copper with commoners. A mask from Chornancap, found in the tomb of an importance priestess, was made of silver. The pointed ovoid eyes on this mask and others, sometimes referred to as winged eyes, have been identified as defining features of a being known as the Sicán Deity. According to Izumi Shimada and colleagues, an individual interred with such a mask would have been thought to take on aspects of the Sicán Deity’s power, and would have been transformed into a venerated ancestor upon death.

**A Golden Mask for a Powerful Ruler**

This mask was made from hammered gold, silver, and copper, then painted with red pigment. It once covered the face of a dead king on the north coast of Peru. Between the years 700 and 1400, powerful families ruled this area. They became very rich in gold and silver before the Inca Empire took over in the late 1400s.

These rulers paid for huge workshops where artists made beautiful jewelry, cups, and other objects. When a ruler died, he was buried deep inside a giant mud-brick platform. He was surrounded by treasures made of precious metal, shell, and cloth. Along with cups, disks, and other ornaments, the burial included large gold masks.

Sometimes, up to five masks were placed in one grave. One mask was attached to the head of the wrapped body, and four more were stacked at the feet. This mask is made of 74% gold, 20% silver, and 6% copper. It was hammered into a thin sheet and shaped like a face.

What’s interesting is that most of the mask’s cheeks and forehead are covered with red paint made from a mineral called cinnabar. This hides the gold underneath. The red paint might copy the face paint that important people wore. According to old Spanish records, a special servant was in charge of keeping that paint fresh.

You can also see faint marks on the red paint. These are impressions from the cloth wrappings that once covered the mummy bundle. Many masks in museums today have had their paint removed so people can see the gold. But this one still has its original color.

The mask’s eyes have thin, stick-like shapes sticking out from the pupils. These might show that the ruler had powerful or piercing vision. In other masks, these shapes have small beads of amber and emerald, so some scholars think they represent tears.

On the ears and ear ornaments, you can see restored silver overlays (now turned green from age). There are also danglers hanging from the nose ornament. These little sparkles would have caught the sunlight and made the mummy bundle seem alive as it was carried to its final resting place.

Masks like this one, with their pointed oval eyes, come from a culture called Lambayeque (also known as Sicán). This culture built huge ceremonial centers like Batán Grande and Chornancap. Recent digs at Chornancap have shown that both high-status women and men were buried with these masks.

According to local myths, gold was linked to male rulers, silver to noble women, and copper to common people. A mask found in the tomb of an important priestess at Chornancap was made of silver.

The pointed oval eyes on this mask are sometimes called "winged eyes." They are a key feature of a being known as the Sicán Deity. Scholars believe that a person buried with such a mask was thought to take on the power of that deity and become a respected ancestor after death.

CuratorialText: The Olmec, whose heartland was located in present-day Mexico from 1200-400 BCE, excelled in creating fine greenstone sculptures. The almost flesh-like quality of the nose and parted lips belie the hardness of the stone from which this mask was made. Although it has been rendered in a naturalistic style, the face itself is not fully human. Rather, it is an idealized composite that alludes to the supernatural: the almond-shaped eyes, slightly downturned mouth, and wide, prominent nose are traits commonly found in depictions of Olmec otherworldly beings. One particular entity, the Olmec Maize God, is further evoked through the defined cleft at the center of the upper forehead, an element that represents the earth from which maize—the principal crop of many Mesoamerican peoples including the Olmec—sprouts and grows. The mask additionally features light incisions on both cheeks and etched scallop-like motifs on the upper rim. These shallow markings were possibly made through repeated scratching. The use of drilling is also evident in the mask, particularly at the corners of the mouth and eyes. Olmec artists may have carefully placed the drill marks as guidelines for the sculpture, to determine the placement of facial features and to demarcate the depth of the carving. Many of these holes may have been preserved in the finished sculpture for aesthetic effect. From the middle of the 11th century BCE to the Spanish Conquest in the 16th century, green and blue colored stones (broadly called greenstone, or “chalchihuitl” in Nahuatl) were esteemed across Mesoamerica for their exceptional luster and translucency. Greenstone, moreover, was thought to be water-retentive, capable of emitting vapor that bolstered the growth and sustenance of surrounding vegetation. The color of the stones was further tied to that of water and maize sprouts, closely linking greenstone to notions of fertility, abundance, and life-giving properties. The Olmec especially valued the bluish color of this jadeite mask. Jadeite, a rare variety of greenstone, occurs naturally in very few places around the world. The material for this mask likely originated from the Motagua River valley in present-day Guatemala, the only known source of jadeite in ancient Mesoamerica. The eroded corner on the mask’s proper right suggests that it was previously exposed to water, perhaps as a river cobble. Some of the earliest jadeite obtained from the region may have been stream-tumbled cobbles or boulders, rather than quarried stone. So special was the jadeite from which this mask was made that the artists seemed to have removed as little of it as possible from the reverse side. The shallowness of the circular, concave depression on the back of the mask may also be due to the toughness of the stone. Jadeite is an extremely dense rock with a relative hardness value equivalent to or even greater than that of steel. Artists in Mesoamerica generally used a combination of percussion and cutting—using lithic implements such as flint blades—to approximate the size of the jadeite, after which they grinded the surface of the stone with other coarse rocks for many hours to achieve the desired shape. Despite the concave depression, this mask was not made to be worn on the face, at least not by the living. Unlike the earlobes, which feature small holes, neither the eyes nor the mouth have been perforated for sight or breath. Holes along the edges of the mask, however, hint at its ritual usage. Masks appear in depictions of rulers or performers and are represented as belt ornaments, pectorals, or headdress components. The perforations additionally suggest that the mask may have been affixed to a textile, as part of a funerary bundle.

People loved their shine and see-through quality. They also believed greenstone could hold water and release a vapor that helped plants grow. The color of the stones was linked to water and young corn sprouts, so greenstone was connected to ideas of fertility, abundance, and life.

The Olmec especially liked the bluish color of this mask, which is made from jadeite. Jadeite is a rare type of greenstone found in very few places on Earth. The worn corner on the right side of the mask suggests it was once in water, maybe as a river rock. Some of the earliest jadeite might have been collected as smooth, tumbled stones from streams, not dug from a quarry.

Even though it has a hollow back, this mask wasn't made to be worn on a living person's face. The eyes and mouth don't have holes for seeing or breathing, unlike the earlobes, which have small holes. CuratorialText: The ceramic vessels of highland Tiwanaku are sturdy and clean lined, with a no-nonsense approach to shape that sets them apart from the sculptural ceramic traditions of the coastal regions to the north. Surface color too is restrained, with a pre-dominance of earth tones in a limited range of hue. This flaring-side vessel is a typical, but distinctive, Tiwanaku type. It has a large feline head on one side and a stumpy, erect tail on the other. The head is surrounded by a large flange on which remnants of a design appear. Winged profile felines are on each side of the vessel. These big-footed cats abundantly fill the space allotted to them. Their eyes are round and divided in half with one half white and the other black, a stylization that was widely used on feline images. Vessels such as these usually represent either large cats or llamas. A number are modeled on the shape of the animal itself, with the animal's body forming the vessel container. The insides of both modeled and abstract vessels are often found blackened and sooty. This has lead to the conclusion that they were censers in Precolumbian times. When in use, smoke would have come from the open mouth of the feline head of this example, which does have a soot-blackened interior.
persona1_adapted:
The pottery from the highlands of Tiwanaku is strong and simple in shape. Unlike the fancy, sculpted pottery from the northern coast, these pieces are straightforward and practical. They mostly use earthy colors like browns and tans.

This particular pot has a wide, flared rim. On one side, there’s a large cat head. On the opposite side, there’s a short, upright tail. Around the cat’s head is a flat ring with traces of a design. On each side of the pot, there are winged cats shown in profile. These big-footed cats fill up the space completely. Their eyes are round and split in half—one half white, the other black. This style was common for cat images.

Pots like this usually show either big cats or llamas. Some are shaped like the animal itself, with the animal’s body forming the pot. The insides of both shaped and plain pots are often blackened with soot. That’s why experts think these were used as incense burners in ancient times. When this pot was used, smoke would have come out of the open mouth of the cat head. The inside of this pot is still black from soot.

CuratorialText:
A master Maya potter modeled a vignette with three characters onto the body and lids of this ceremonial vessel. The main form of the vessel is composed of two identical cylindrical chambers connected by a central hollow bridge, each of which rests on two squarish supports. This style of ceramic, recognizable by a very fine reddish or gray paste fired with a reddish-black clay slip, is known from what is now northern Guatemala and Southern Campeche, Mexico. Artists who specialized in this highly polished blackware style in the 5th-6th centuries achieved great feats in gouged and incised designs, often on very thin walls or lids. Such a fine vessel would have been used on very special feasting or ritual occasion, perhaps later buried in the tomb of its owner by his or her descendants.

In this scene, a large supernatural bird known as the Principal Bird Deity (PBD) perches on one lid (which is actually a false lid, fused with the chamber), and faces a kneeling humanlike figure. A spherical chamber of a whistle is hidden inside the head of the bird, visible through an Rx radiograph image, and sounds when water is poured into the opposite chamber. The bird’s large beak and caruncle protrude from its open mouth, which connects to the interior chamber of the vessel. From the beak hangs a stylized double-headed serpent. The bird’s glittering eyes are square and marked with an L-shaped pupil, a convention that Maya artists used to depict deities with supernatural vision. The spray of feathers of the serpent-wings are modeled and incised, as are the scaley legs and talons. The bird is decked out in jade jewels: a diadem, circular earflares, and a large necklace with a central pendant.

A kneeling figure on the other removable lid presents the bird with a bundle in devotional tribute. He wears a loincloth, a feathered, jeweled headdress, nose ornaments, ear ornaments, and a necklace. He stares at the bird in reverence, stoic in his encounter with this resplendent being. The young man’s submissive position may be a trap laid to bring down the portentous bird. Despite his impending defeat, this majestic creature was revered as an avian manifestation (or a spiritual companion, way) of the god Itzamnaaj, the old celestial god. A feline or simian creature, also wearing a necklace, bridges the two chambers of the vessel and seems to climb up the side towards the two figures on top.

This vessel may be part of a series of three double-chambered blackware vessels featuring different scenes involving the PBD; one was excavated from a tomb at Calakmul, Mexico. Religious narratives from the late first millennium BCE until the colonial period feature this creature, which has a large hooked raptor beak grabbing a serpent and animate wings in the form of stylized serpents. Artists depicted the PBD descending from the sky, perching on trees, receiving offerings from humanlike figures, or even being shot by a blowgun.
persona1_adapted:
Here is the adapted version of the museum text, written for an 8th-grade reading level.

---

**A Maya Masterpiece: A Story in Clay**

A long time ago, a master artist from the Maya civilization created an amazing piece of art. It’s a special clay container, or vessel, that tells a story. The vessel is made of two round chambers connected by a bridge, and it rests on four square feet. This type of pottery is very fine and has a shiny, reddish-black color. It was made in what is now northern Guatemala and southern Mexico around 1,500 years ago. Artists who made this "blackware" were experts at carving detailed designs into the thin clay walls. This vessel was probably used for important feasts or ceremonies. Later, it might have been buried in the tomb of its owner.

**The Scene on the Vessel**

On top of the vessel, a huge supernatural bird is perched on one lid. This bird is called the Principal Bird Deity (or PBD). It faces a kneeling human-like figure. Inside the bird’s head is a hidden whistle. When water is poured into the other chamber, the whistle makes a sound! The bird’s big beak is open, and from it hangs a double-headed serpent. The bird’s eyes are square with an L-shaped pupil. Maya artists used this special eye to show that a creature had supernatural powers. The bird’s wings look like serpents, and its legs and claws are carved with scales. It is also wearing fancy jade jewelry: a crown, earrings, and a big necklace.

**The Kneeling Figure**

On the other lid, a young man is kneeling. He is holding a bundle as an offering to the bird. He wears a loincloth, a feathered headdress, and jewelry in his nose and ears. He stares at the bird with respect. But his kneeling position might be a trick! The story says that this humble pose is a trap to capture the powerful bird. Even though the bird will be defeated, it was still a very important creature. It was seen as a form of the god Itzamnaaj, the old sky god.

**A Final Detail**

A small animal—maybe a cat or a monkey—is climbing up the side of the vessel. It also wears a necklace and seems to be watching the scene.

**Why This Matters**

This vessel might be one of three that tell different stories about the Principal Bird Deity. In Maya stories, this bird is often shown grabbing a serpent with its hooked beak. Its wings look like snakes. Artists showed the bird coming down from the sky, sitting in trees, receiving gifts, or even being hunted with a blowgun. This artwork gives us a glimpse into the myths and beliefs of the ancient Maya people.

CuratorialText: Ceramic drums with central, bulging sounding chambers were made in southern Peru at the turn of the first millennium. Among the most elaborately finished are those of Nasca style. They were surfaced with the many rich colors commonly used on Nasca ceramic vessels. A favored form was one in which a fat-bodied figure was worked into the shape of the instrument, the rotund body spreading out equally on all sides and the legs drawn up in the front. The figure is depicted atop the wide mouth of the drum, over which a skin would have been stretched. The image is symbolically complex; a snake emerges from under the figure's chin and a killer whale outlines each eye. The killer whales are in profile and show the "two-tone" color differentiation normally given them in Nasca depictions. A headband is wound around the head and tied to form a hornlike projection on the forehead. In back, the figure's hair is shown as serpents with long tongues.
persona1_adapted:
**Drums from Ancient Peru**

Hundreds of years ago, in what is now southern Peru, people made special drums out of clay. These drums had a big, round middle part that helped make the sound loud and deep.

The most detailed and beautiful ones were made by the Nasca people. They painted them with lots of bright colors, just like they did on their other clay pots and bowls.

A popular style showed a chubby person sitting on top of the drum. The person’s round belly stuck out on all sides, and their legs were pulled up in front. On top of the drum, there was a wide opening where an animal skin would have been stretched tight to make the drumhead.

But this wasn’t just a simple drawing. The image had a lot of hidden meaning. A snake comes out from under the person’s chin. Around each eye, there’s a killer whale drawn from the side. These whales are shown in two colors, just like the Nasca people usually drew them. The person also wears a headband that is tied into a horn shape on their forehead. And in the back, their hair turns into snakes with long tongues.

CuratorialText: The three-dimensional figures of the Tolita-Tumaco area are among the most striking of Precolumbian gold objects. The present example is particularly distinguished by its fancy nose ornament, evidence that some form of headdress was once present, and by the position of the hands, which originally held objects now missing, perhaps an atlatl and darts. The feet too have been lost. Made of many pieces of high-quality sheet gold, the figure may have been clothed for special occasions. The Tolita-Tumaco style area crosses the Colombia-Ecuador border along the humid Pacific coast.
persona1_adapted: The gold figures from the Tolita-Tumaco region are some of the most eye-catching artworks from ancient America. This one stands out because of its fancy nose ring, signs that it once wore a headdress, and the way its hands are positioned—they probably held objects that are now missing, like a spear-thrower and darts. Its feet are also gone. The figure was made from many pieces of high-quality gold sheets, and it might have been dressed up for special events. The Tolita-Tumaco style comes from an area along the rainy Pacific coast, where Colombia and Ecuador meet.

CuratorialText: This cylindrical drinking cup is the magnum opus of the Maya vase painter known as the Metropolitan Master. It contains one of the finest extant deity portraits from the Classic Maya corpus. The young rain god, named Chahk, poses mid-stride, lifting off his left foot and extending the right leg in front of him, gracefully pointing his toes. The underside of each leg is marked with a scale pattern, evoking a shimmering and wet aquatic creature. He wears a complex loincloth that shows the typical knotted cotton of his costuming, and the rear panel of the loincloth contorts as if the artist was referencing a fish tail. The necklace of Chahk is quite unique: the collar consists of pendant extruded eyeballs and the pectoral is in the shape of an upside-down water jar marked with the hieroglyph for darkness, with what looks like a small serpent emerging from its mouth. His other jewels on the ankles and wrists may be jade or another precious material, and his headdress is a wild tangle of watery vegetation. The typical shell earrings accent the barbel extending from the nostril and projecting off of the god’s chin. His humanlike quality is emphasized by the artists’ brushstrokes in the distinguished profile of the face, the point of the elbow, the ankle joints, and finger and toenails. In his right hand, he grasps the wooden handle of a shining stone axe, and his left hand holds an animate stone. The rain god actively engages with a giant agnathus creature, likely the representation of a witz, the spirit of a mountain, as his leg crosses in front of the upper lip while his left arm passes behind. It is as if the rain personified needs to partake in a ritual combative dance with an animate mountain to set the actions presented here in motion. The mountain monster has a feathered eyelid present on crocodilians in Maya art, a jagged tooth, and liquid or vegetation spewing forth and spilling on the ground line at Chahk’s feet. The head of the mountain is covered with the grape bunch markings that signify that it is a stony place. Most significant about the zoomorphic mountain is the character reclining on top of it: the supernatural baby jaguar. The face of the jaguar baby character is clearly supernatural and contrasts sharply with Chahk’s more human-like visage. The square eye is a marker of godliness in Maya art, and the overbite with shark-like tooth also denotes that this reclining personage is a peer of Chahk and the sun god, both of which are shown with this protruding tooth at times. The ears, hands, feet, and tail are masterfully painted to portray those of a jaguar. In fact, all signs point to this creature as an infant jaguar, known from other portrayals of the same reclining infant, and the hieroglyphic logogram found in some royal names. The baby jaguar has a similar knotted hairstyle and vegetal headdress as Chahk and flails about as if fitfully searching for stability. Almost touching the baby jaguar is a frightening creature of the night, with a skeletal head marked with bone sutures and two extruded eyeballs, insect-like carapace, distended belly of a corpse, and spindly legs with knobby knees. This is likely a death god, a denizen of the Maya underworld who plays a role in this myth of the birth of the baby jaguar. He dances too, raising off his left foot and extending the right. His outstretched arms hold tense hands that seem to reach for the baby jaguar or the hieroglyphic caption hovering above him. The death god wears an elaborate backrack composed of textiles, bone elements, and extruded eyeballs. The death god has two creepy companions. Floating above the ground is a firefly. He appears as a skeletal cyclops, the central eye in the form of akbal, the hieroglyph for darkness. Three extruded eyeballs crown his head, and the artist delicately rendered his insect-like hind legs and abdomen. He holds a cigar or torch, a Maya artistic convention for signifying a creature of the night: the light of a cigar being smoked in the darkness mimics the natural flickering of mating bioluminescence of lightning bugs. Below the firefly is a mischievous dog with a spotted tail and ears. He pants in the rear of the dancing death god and raises up on his hind legs as if begging for a morsel of food or playing. The text comprising the eight glyph blocks that float above the baby jaguar is opaque in meaning. The owner is named as a "k’uhul chatan winik," a royal title in use during the Classic Period at certain places. The overarching theme of this vessel is the necessary interaction of life-giving rains and rotting death, the contrasts needed to produce life, represented in this case by the baby jaguar god. The death god and his companions are present as organic remains decay and when rains fall, life begins at the site of the fertilized space. All of this occurs on the top of a mythological mountain at the center of the Maya world. The wash used by the artist on the lower portion of the vessel perhaps represents the steamy breath that emerges from caves in order to create clouds and produce rain. Chahk in all his glory emerges from and interacts with the cave as he celebrates the birth of the baby jaguar. The death god’s pose seems to show that the baby jaguar was snatched from the clutches of death itself.

The young rain god, named Chahk, is caught mid-step, lifting his left foot and stretching his right leg forward, with his toes pointed gracefully. The underside of each leg has a scale pattern, like a shimmering, wet water creature. He wears a complex loincloth with knotted cotton, and the back panel twists as if the artist was hinting at a fish tail.

Chahk’s necklace is very unusual: it has dangling eyeballs, and the centerpiece is an upside-down water jar marked with the hieroglyph for darkness, with a small snake coming out of its mouth. His other jewelry on the ankles and wrists might be jade or another precious stone, and his headdress is a wild mess of water plants. Shell earrings accent the barbel (a whisker-like feature) coming from his nostril and chin. The artist made Chahk look human-like with careful brushstrokes on his face profile, elbow point, ankle joints, and even his fingernails and toenails.

In his right hand, Chahk holds the wooden handle of a shiny stone axe, and in his left hand, he grips a living stone. The rain god is actively fighting with a giant, jawless creature—likely a *witz*, or mountain spirit. Chahk’s leg crosses in front of the creature’s upper lip, while his left arm goes behind it. It’s as if the rain god needs to do a ritual dance-fight with a living mountain to start the action shown here.

The mountain monster has a feathered eyelid (like crocodiles in Maya art), a jagged tooth, and liquid or plants spilling out onto the ground at Chahk’s feet. The mountain’s head is covered with grape-like markings that mean it’s a rocky place. Most important is the character lying on top of the mountain: a supernatural baby jaguar. The baby jaguar’s face is clearly god-like and contrasts sharply with Chahk’s more human face. Its square eye is a sign of godliness in Maya art, and its overbite with a shark-like tooth shows that this baby is a peer of Chahk and the sun god, who sometimes have that same tooth. The ears, hands, feet, and tail are masterfully painted to look like a jaguar’s. Everything points to this being an infant jaguar, known from other images of the same reclining baby, and from a hieroglyph found in some royal names. The baby jaguar has a knotted hairstyle and plant headdress like Chahk, and it flails around as if searching for stability.

Almost touching the baby jaguar is a scary night creature with a skeletal head marked by bone seams, two dangling eyeballs, an insect-like shell, a swollen corpse belly, and spindly legs with knobby knees. This is likely a death god, a resident of the Maya underworld who plays a role in the myth of the baby jaguar’s birth. He also dances, lifting his left foot and stretching his right leg. His outstretched arms hold tense hands that seem to reach for the baby jaguar or the hieroglyphic caption floating above him. The death god wears an elaborate backpack made of textiles, bones, and dangling eyeballs.

The death god has two creepy companions. Floating above the ground is a firefly. It looks like a skeletal cyclops, with a central eye shaped like *akbal*, the hieroglyph for darkness. Three dangling eyeballs crown its head, and the artist carefully painted its insect-like hind legs and abdomen. It holds a cigar or torch—a Maya art convention for night creatures: the light of a cigar smoked in the dark mimics the natural flickering of fireflies’ mating glow. Below the firefly is a mischievous dog with a spotted tail and ears. It pants behind the dancing death god and raises up on its hind legs as if begging for food or playing.

The eight hieroglyph blocks floating above the baby jaguar are hard to understand. The owner is called a *k’uhul chatan winik*, a royal title used during the Classic Period in certain places. The main theme of this cup is the necessary interaction between life-giving rain and rotting death—the contrasts needed to create life, represented here by the baby jaguar god. The death god and his companions are present when organic matter decays, and when rain falls, life begins in that fertilized space. All of this happens on top of a mythical mountain at the center of the Maya world. The wash the artist used on the lower part of the cup might represent the steamy breath that comes out of caves to form clouds and make rain. Chahk, in all his glory, emerges from and interacts with the cave as he celebrates the baby jaguar’s birth. The death god’s pose seems to show that the baby jaguar was snatched from the clutches of death itself.

CuratorialText: Paired male and female figures are common subjects in the ceramics of the western Mexico peoples of Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit. Named for the states in which the works have been discovered in quantity in deeply buried, multichambered shaft tombs, the ceramic sculptures show aspects of daily and ritual life, from ballgame representations to feasting ceremonies to deceptively simple family scenes. While subject matter was relatively consistent throughout the entire region, style distinctions existed. The figures here are in Nayarit's Ixtlán del Río style, which is characterized by thin, pliant limbs, and stout, cylindrical torsos. Gender differences center on attire and activity, but both sexes wear numerous ornaments on ears and nose. Here the male figure wears a conical hat, a shoulder cloth, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in his right hand and raised drumstick in the other. His companion wears a shoulder cloth and skirt, and her right arm is draped around his shoulder.
persona1_adapted:
**Ancient Art from Western Mexico**

People from western Mexico—in the areas now called Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit—often made clay statues of men and women together. These statues were found deep underground in special tombs called "shaft tombs." The tombs had several rooms connected by a long, narrow shaft.

The statues show scenes from everyday life and special ceremonies. Some show ballgames, others show feasts, and some show simple family moments. Even though the same kinds of subjects appeared across the whole region, different areas had their own styles.

The statues you see here are from a style called Ixtlán del Río, from the Nayarit region. In this style, the figures have thin, bendable-looking arms and legs, and short, round bodies. Men and women are shown differently by what they wear and what they do, but both wear lots of jewelry on their ears and noses.

The man in this statue wears a cone-shaped hat, a cloth over his shoulder, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in one hand and a drumstick raised in the other. The woman wears a shoulder cloth and a skirt, and her arm is wrapped around his shoulder.

CuratorialText: This Wari bottle seamlessly merges the body of an anthropomorphized feline with that of a flask-like vessel. Combining in-the-round, low-relief, and painted elements, this ceramic is both visually and technically intricate. The depth of its earthy tones and its balance of volumes brilliantly illustrate the ability of Wari artists to fuse Andean mastery of color and sculpture in the art of pottery. Standing upright with its arms against its body, the feline’s posture de-emphasizes its animality. The figure stares defiantly at the viewer with its eyes wide-open, a threatening stance amplified by its upright ears, gaping fanged mouth, and dotted eyebrows. The animal’s paws are partially humanized, depicted with digits rather than claws and topped with a grey wristband. The clawed fingers grab a fantastical creature, commonly known as the "humped animal" in scholarship (Menzel 1964; Knobloch 1983), on each side (one side is damaged, perhaps from prolonged exposure to salts in the ground). The two humped animals were painted in brick red with a yellow outline on raised roundels. This color scheme is reversed on the two figures on the circumference of the bottle, whose elongated bodies fit closely the curvature of the vessel’s chamber. Geometric designs, such as dotted circles and rhombuses with appendages, complement the painted decoration of the vessel’s body. A row of orange, brick red, and grey chevrons crown the bottle’s spout, a feature common to Wari ceramics. The highly burnished orange slip (a suspension of clay mixed with water, often with added pigments) on this bottle provides a smooth and glossy background that accentuates the raised roundels and feline’s legs on the ceramic. The white highlights stand out on this surface, in particular the eyes, fangs, and claws of the multiple figures—lethal attributes that suggest that this object abounds with dangerous wildlife. By bringing together naturalistically-rendered animal features, hybrid supernatural figures, and abstract geometric motifs, this bottle invokes a complex microcosm of bodies and forms, which the viewer must examine from a distance as well as up-close. The imagery unfolds as the viewer handles, turns, and angles the vessel. This bottle comes from a looted elite burial from the Ingenio Valley in the Nasca drainage on the south coast of present-day Peru (Tello 1917; Menzel 1968). From around 600 to 1000 CE, the Wari ran an empire that expanded throughout most of the Central Andes, albeit with varying degrees of presence and control. Wari culture was born out of the interactions between the Huarpa, a local group of the central highlands in present-day Ayacucho, and the Nasca people on the South Coast. Its empire bridged multiple regions, cultures, and artistic practices. Indeed, this bottle features techniques and motifs inherited from the Nasca repertoire, from its polychromy to motifs such as the humped animal and the ventrally extended creature, but they are combined with elements taken from northern traditions, such as the modeled feline. An almost identical bottle was excavated at the site of Wilkawain, in the northern highlands of Peru (Paredes Olvera 2016: fig. 16). Archaeologists believe that it was imported there, possibly from the Wari heartland. Whether the bottles from Wilkawain and the Ingenio Valley were both made in the same workshop and then exported to different regions remains unknown. While other Wari bottles have a similarly flattened body, this model differs in that it has to be oriented sideways in order for the viewer to face the feline, adapting the form of the container to the figure. Although it is possible that this bottle was never used and only destined to be deposited in a burial, it could have once contained liquid to be poured on the ground or ingested, perhaps maize- or red peppercorn- beer (Sayre et al. 2012). The vessel’s small size suggests that it was meant for personal devotional practice. As one held this bottle featuring multiple powerful beings in one’s hands, the viewing experience would have evolved, especially as the bottle was tilted upside-down to pour liquid from the bottle’s spout. Would this physical manipulation have prompted in the user sensations of fear and respect for these entities, or instead a sense of control over them?
persona1_adapted:
**This Wari bottle is a mix of a cat-like creature and a flask.** It’s made of clay and combines 3D shapes, raised carvings, and painted details. The artist used earthy colors and balanced the bottle’s parts perfectly, showing off the Wari people’s skill at blending color and sculpture.

**The cat stands upright with its arms at its sides, which makes it look less like a wild animal.** But its wide-open eyes stare right at you, and its ears are perked up. Its mouth is open, showing big fangs, and it has dotted eyebrows. This makes it look threatening. Its paws are partly human-like—they have fingers instead of claws, and each paw wears a gray wristband. The clawed fingers grab a strange creature on each side (one side is damaged, maybe from being buried in salty ground for a long time). Scholars call this creature the "humped animal."

**The two humped animals are painted brick red with a yellow outline on raised circles.** The colors are swapped on two other figures that wrap around the bottle’s body. There are also geometric designs, like dotted circles and diamond shapes with extra parts. A row of orange, brick red, and gray zigzags decorates the bottle’s spout, which is common in Wari pottery.

**The bottle has a shiny orange slip** (a mix of clay and water, sometimes with color added). This gives it a smooth, glossy background that makes the raised circles and the cat’s legs stand out. White highlights pop on the surface, especially on the eyes, fangs, and claws of all the figures. These deadly details suggest the bottle is full of dangerous creatures.

**By mixing realistic animal features, imaginary supernatural beings, and abstract patterns, this bottle creates a tiny world of shapes and forms.** You have to look at it from far away and up close. The images change as you hold, turn, and tilt the bottle.

**This bottle came from a robbed elite burial in the Ingenio Valley, on the south coast of present-day Peru.** The Wari ruled an empire from about 600 to 1000 CE, spreading across most of the Central Andes. Their culture grew from the mixing of the Huarpa people (from the central highlands) and the Nasca people (from the south coast). The Wari empire connected many regions, cultures, and art styles. This bottle uses techniques and designs from the Nasca, like multiple colors and the humped animal, but also includes features from northern traditions, like the carved cat.

**A nearly identical bottle was found at a site called Wilkawain, in the northern highlands of Peru.** Archaeologists think it was brought there, maybe from the Wari heartland. We don’t know if both bottles were made in the same workshop and then sent to different places.

**Unlike other Wari bottles with flat bodies, this one has to be turned sideways so you can face the cat.** This changes the shape of the container to fit the figure. It’s possible the bottle was never used and was only meant to be buried. But it could have held liquid, like maize beer or red peppercorn beer, to be poured on the ground or drunk. The bottle is small, so it was probably used for personal prayer or ritual.

**As you held this bottle with its many powerful beings, the way you saw it would change—especially when you tilted it upside down to pour liquid out.** Would handling it make you feel fear and respect for these creatures, or would it make you feel like you were in control of them?

CuratorialText: The imagery on this plate represents the earliest known depiction of a well-known episode from the story of Bahram Gur or Bahram V, the Sasanian king (r. 420 –438). The story seems to have been popular for centuries—both in Sasanian times and under the Islamic rule that followed—but was only recorded in the Shahnamah, or Book of Kings, by Firdausi in the early eleventh century A.D., centuries after this plate was created. The Shahnamah recounts how Bahram Gur, challenged to feats of archery by his favorite musician, Azadeh, changed a male gazelle to a female and a female to a male and pinned the hind leg of an animal to its ear. This plate directly refers to the story of Bahram Gur because the horns of the male have been shot off with an arrow, transforming his appearance into that of a female, while the two arrows embedded in the head of the female make her appear to have horns like those of a male gazelle. This plate was likely hammered into shape and then decorated to highlight its varied surface contours and colors, making its human and animal forms stand out in relief. It was partially gilded using a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted onto the surface. Large numbers of gold and silver vessels made during Sasanian rule in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived, demonstrating an ancient Near Eastern tradition that spread westward to the Hellenistic and Roman world, where vessels made of precious metals also became symbols of power and prestige. These objects, presumably official state products, were presented as royal gifts to persons of importance within Sasanian Iran and beyond its borders.
persona1_adapted:
**The Story on a Plate: Bahram Gur the Archer**

This plate shows the oldest known picture of a famous story about King Bahram Gur (also called Bahram V), who ruled from 420 to 438 AD. People loved this tale for hundreds of years—both when the Sasanian Empire was in power and later under Islamic rule. But the story wasn't written down until around 1000 AD, in a book called the *Shahnamah* (the *Book of Kings*) by a poet named Firdausi. That's long after this plate was made!

Here's what happens in the story: Bahram Gur's favorite musician, Azadeh, challenges him to an archery contest. Bahram has to shoot arrows to change a male gazelle into a female, and a female into a male. He also has to pin a gazelle's back leg to its own ear.

Look closely at the plate. You can see the arrows at work. The male gazelle's horns have been shot off, so it looks like a female. And the female gazelle has two arrows stuck in its head, making it look like it has horns—like a male. That's how we know this plate is telling the Bahram Gur story.

**How the Plate Was Made**

The plate was probably hammered into shape first. Then artists decorated it to make the people and animals stand out. They used a mix of mercury and gold (called gilding) to paint parts of it, giving it shiny highlights.

**Why This Plate Matters**

During the Sasanian Empire (in modern-day Iran and Iraq), people made lots of gold and silver dishes like this one. This tradition of making fancy metal vessels spread west to the Greeks and Romans, who also used them as symbols of power and wealth. These plates were probably official royal gifts, given to important people inside and outside the empire.

CuratorialText: This solid-cast sculpture is one of a pair of nearly identical images of a hero or a demon wearing the upturned boots associated with highland regions, his power enhanced by the mighty horns of the ibex on his head and the body and wings of a bird of prey draped around his shoulders. It was created at the time the first cities emerged in ancient Sumer. A new world view conceived of human figures in realistic terms, through accurate proportions and highly modeled forms with distinctive features. The blending of human and animal forms to visualize the supernatural world and perhaps to express shamanistic beliefs, however, is more characteristic of the contemporary arts of Proto-Elamite Iran, where a remarkable tradition of metalworking developed during this period.
persona1_adapted:
This solid metal statue is one of two almost identical figures. It shows a hero or a demon wearing tall, turned-up boots, which were common in highland areas. The figure’s power is shown by the big horns of an ibex (a type of wild goat) on its head, and the body and wings of a bird of prey draped over its shoulders.

It was made when the first cities were being built in ancient Sumer (a region in what is now Iraq). At that time, people started to imagine human figures in a more realistic way—with correct body proportions, detailed shapes, and unique features.

But mixing human and animal forms to show the supernatural world—and maybe to express shamanistic beliefs—was more typical of the art in Proto-Elamite Iran (an area in what is now western Iran). That’s where a remarkable tradition of metalworking developed during this period.

CuratorialText: This fragmentary piece takes the form of the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storeroom at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. During the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C., the royal complexes at Nimrud were sacked and burned, and the lion was likely blackened through exposure to fire at this time. The flat back suggests that this piece may have originally been attached to a piece of wooden furniture such as the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and incised muzzle are detailed with incised dots and the eyes are deeply carved for now-lost semiprecious stone or colored glass inlays. A pattern of painted white triangles outlined with raised ribs is preserved on the right side at the back of the face, perhaps to represent a mane. Scenes of the Assyrian king hunting lions are depicted on royal reliefs from the Assyrian palaces. Because it shares the same subject matter, this piece has been attributed to an Assyrian style.
