Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.
persona1_adapted:
**The Lion's Face (A Fragment from Ancient Assyria)**

This broken piece is the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storeroom at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud (in modern-day Iraq). The fort was probably used to store treasure and gifts that the Assyrian army collected during their military campaigns.

When the Assyrian Empire was finally defeated around 600 B.C., the royal buildings at Nimrud were looted and burned. The lion's face was likely darkened by the fire at that time.

The back of this piece is flat, which suggests it was once attached to a wooden piece of furniture—maybe the arm of a chair. The lion's right cheek, eyebrows, and muzzle are decorated with tiny carved dots. Its eyes are deeply carved, probably to hold precious stones or colored glass that have since been lost. On the right side of the face, there is a pattern of white triangles outlined with raised lines. This might represent the lion's mane.

In Assyrian palaces, royal reliefs (stone carvings on walls) often showed the king hunting lions. Because this piece has the same subject—a lion—experts think it was made in the Assyrian style.

The palaces and storerooms at Nimrud were built by King Ashurnasirpal II. They held thousands of carved ivory objects. Most of these ivories were used as decorations on furniture or as small precious items like boxes. Some were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian wall reliefs. But most of them show styles and images from North Syria and the Phoenician city-states.

**Phoenician-style ivories** often use images from Egyptian art, like sphinxes and figures wearing Egyptian crowns. They also use fancy carving techniques like openwork (cut-out designs) and colored glass inlays.

**North Syrian-style ivories** tend to show thicker, more solid figures in active poses. They are carved as solid plaques with fewer extra decorations.

However, some pieces don't fit neatly into any of these three styles.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute (gifts from conquered peoples) or as booty (loot from battles). Some may have been made in workshops right at Nimrud. The ivory tusks used to make these objects almost certainly came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt. (Although elephants did live in some river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the 700s B.C.)

CuratorialText: Of the many legacies left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is paramount. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region, first as pictographs and then evolving into abstract forms called cuneiform. The pictographs, like the ones on this tablet, are called proto-cuneiform and were drawn in the clay with a pointed implement. Circular impressions alongside the pictographs represented numerical symbols. Cuneiform (meaning wedge-shaped) script was written by pressing a reed pen or stylus with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. Clay, when dried to a somewhat hardened state, made a fine surface for writing, and when fired the records written on it became permanent. Early writing was used primarily as a means of recording and storing economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, although the absence of verbs in early texts makes them difficult to interpret with certainty. In addition to the writing that appears on this tablet, the imagery of the cylinder seal, which was incompletely impressed on both faces and the edges of the tablet before it was inscribed, also records information. This seal apparently has not survived. The seal impression depicts a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding boars in a reed marsh. He is the so-called priest-king, a male figure who can be identified by his dress and pose. Here he appears in his role as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.
persona1_adapted:
**The Invention of Writing**

One of the most important things the ancient people of southern Mesopotamia gave us was writing. Around 3200 B.C., they started using written language. At first, they drew simple pictures called pictographs. Later, these turned into wedge-shaped symbols called cuneiform.

The marks on this clay tablet are an early form of writing called proto-cuneiform. People made them by pressing a pointed tool into soft clay. Next to the pictures, you can see circular marks that stood for numbers.

To write cuneiform, scribes used a reed pen with a wedge-shaped tip. They pressed it into clay tablets. When the clay dried, it became hard and the writing lasted a long time. If the tablet was fired (like pottery), the writing became permanent.

Back then, writing was mostly used to keep track of things like grain, animals, and money. This tablet probably shows grain given out by a large temple. But since early writing didn’t use verbs, it’s hard to know exactly what it says.

There’s also a picture on this tablet made by a cylinder seal. A cylinder seal is a small carved stone that you roll across clay to leave an image. This seal was pressed onto the tablet before it was written on. The original seal is now lost.

The seal shows a man leading two dogs on leashes. He is hunting or herding wild boars in a marsh. This man is called the “priest-king,” and you can tell who he is by his clothes and pose. In this scene, he acts like a good shepherd, protecting animals from wild predators.

CuratorialText:
In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled on a variety of objects made of clay. When seals were impressed on tablets or tablet cases the seal impressions served to identify the authority responsible for what was written in the documents, much as a signature does today. When seals were impressed on sealings — lumps of clay that were used to secure doors and the lids of storage jars— the seal impressions served to identify their owner and protect against unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made of durable materials, particularly stone, but also metal and fired clay. Perforated through the middle like a bead, seals were also believed to have apotropaic, or protective, functions and were worn as jewelry or pinned on garments.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene is composed of two basic groupings that form an overall continuous design unified by the stylized landscape setting. In one group, two tall trees flank a hunter grasping an ibex. Above the man a cuneiform inscription gives the name of the seal owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other and flanking three trees. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when the iconography used by the seal engraver expanded to include a variety of new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a relationship between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and secular ideas, fundamental to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were visualized in the miniature images carved on the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the forms were cut into the stone, to create the raised impression. The challenge of the seal carver was to create a design that would maintain its balance and clarity when rolled out only half its length on a small surface or twice its length on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression one can see how the rolled out design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in a fragmentary state, cylinder seals are in the unique position of appearing almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.
persona1_adapted:
In ancient Mesopotamia, people used special cylinder-shaped seals. They would roll these seals over soft clay objects, like tablets or jar lids. The marks left behind worked like a signature does today—they showed who was responsible for a document or who owned a sealed container. Some seals were also used to lock doors or storage jars, and the impression proved that no one had opened them without permission.

Many of these seals have survived because they were made from tough materials like stone, metal, or baked clay. They were often worn as jewelry, like beads on a necklace or pinned to clothing. People believed the seals had magical powers to protect them from harm.

The picture you see here is a modern copy of a seal’s design. It shows two main scenes that flow together. In one scene, two tall trees stand on either side of a hunter who is grabbing an ibex (a type of wild goat). Above the hunter, there’s writing in cuneiform (ancient script) that says the seal’s owner was a court official named Balu-ili, and his job was cupbearer. In the other scene, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other with three trees between them.

This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when artists started carving new kinds of stories and myths into seals. Here, the old idea of a hunt is shown in a clear landscape. If the pictures were meant to go with the writing, we don’t know what that connection was anymore.

The tiny carvings on seals showed both religious and everyday ideas that were important to ancient Mesopotamians. The artist used a technique called intaglio, where the design is cut into the stone so that when you roll it, the raised parts make the image. The challenge was to make a design that looked good whether you rolled it just halfway or all the way around. You can see on the left and right edges of the impression where the pattern starts to repeat.

Unlike many other ancient artworks that are broken or damaged, cylinder seals survive almost exactly as they looked when people used them thousands of years ago.

CuratorialText: This type of figurine known as an eye idol, made of stone and having incised eyes, has been excavated at Tell Brak, where thousands were found in a building now called the Eye Temple. They were probably dedicated there as offerings. Many are incised with multiple sets of eyes, others with jewelry, and still others with representations of "children"—smaller eyes and body carved on the body of the larger idol. Wide eyes demonstrate attentiveness to the gods in much of Mesopotamian art.
persona1_adapted:
These small stone figurines are called "eye idols." They were found at a place called Tell Brak, in a building that we now call the Eye Temple. Thousands of them were discovered there, and people probably left them as offerings.

Many of the eye idols have several sets of eyes carved into them. Some are decorated with jewelry, and others show what look like "children"—smaller eyes and bodies carved onto the larger idol. In ancient Mesopotamian art, wide eyes were a way of showing that a person was paying close attention to the gods.

CuratorialText: Some of the most elaborate representations of females in the art of the ancient Near East are images of divine and cult figures whose association with certain aspects of life made them essential to the welfare of humanity. Fertility, procreation, and the growth of crops and livestock were among the basic concepts identified with female divinities. Representations of nude females in clay, stone, and metal are the simplest and most obvious expression of these concepts, and such figures appear throughout antiquity in many regions. This striking example in clay from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran is hollow and probably served as a cult vessel as well as a sacred image.
persona1_adapted:
In the ancient Near East, some of the most detailed artworks of women showed goddesses and religious figures. These figures were important because they were linked to things that helped people survive, like farming, having children, and raising animals. Female gods were often connected to these basic needs.

Simple statues of naked women, made from clay, stone, or metal, were a clear way to show these ideas. People made these kinds of figures for a very long time in many different places. This special clay statue comes from the South Caspian region in northwestern Iran. It is hollow inside, so it was probably used both as a holy image and as a container for religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Distinctive clay female figures like this one were produced at sites belonging to the Halaf culture. This example displays a strong stylization with an emphasis on the sexual features. She sits with her large thighs extended, supporting her breasts with her arms; neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing; in other figures of this type when the head is intact, it is elongated into a large noselike projection but otherwise is featureless. Remains of paint may represent jewelry. The meaning of such representations is unknown but may be connected with fertility. The stylized depiction of the nude female form remained an artistic convention in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean for several millennia.
persona1_adapted: This clay figure of a woman was made by people from a culture called Halaf. She is very simply shaped, with a strong focus on her body. She sits with her large legs stretched out, and her arms are holding up her breasts. She has no hands or feet. Her head is missing, but in other similar figures that still have heads, the head is long and shaped like a big nose, with no other details. There are traces of paint left on the figure, which might show that she wore jewelry. We don’t know for sure what these figures meant, but they might have been connected to ideas about fertility (having children). This simple way of showing the naked female body became a common style in art across parts of the Middle East and Greece for thousands of years.

CuratorialText: Furniture inlaid with carved ivory plaques was highly prized by the Assyrian kings. During the ninth to seventh centuries B.C., vast quantities of luxury goods, often embellished with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles, accumulated in Assyrian palaces, much of it as booty or tribute. This object belongs to a group of plaques depicting animals and stylized plants. They were made by master carvers in a delicate openwork technique characteristic of Phoenician ivory carving. However, the style and subjects depicted have close parallels on stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf, in northern Syria, and a debate exists over which tradition produced these fine panels.
persona1_adapted:
Assyrian kings really loved fancy furniture decorated with carved ivory plaques. Between the 800s and 600s B.C., huge amounts of luxury goods—often decorated with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles—piled up in Assyrian palaces. Most of it was taken as loot or given as tribute.

This piece is part of a group of plaques showing animals and stylized plants. Master carvers made them using a delicate openwork technique that was typical of Phoenician ivory carving. But the style and subjects look a lot like stone reliefs from Tell Halaf in northern Syria. That’s why experts argue about which tradition actually created these fine panels.

CuratorialText: This statuette group, carved in the round, was found with five other statuettes with similar imagery. The six statuettes were excavated in and just below two arched niches built into the wall of a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. Those found just below the niches could have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during the the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory plinths and exhibited in the arched niches, perhaps arranged in a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king. A frontally facing male, striding to the right with his lower body in profile, grasps the horns of an oryx (a species of desert-dwelling antelope) that strides behind him. The male figure’s eyes, necklace, and armlet were carved to receive colored glass or semiprecious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt belted with a long sash, embroidered with decoration including two uraei (mythical, fire-spitting serpents), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is rendered with short incisions, sits erect on his left shoulder and grasps his short curly hair. Although his right arm does not survive, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.
persona1_adapted:
This small statue group, carved from ivory, was found with five others that look similar. They were discovered in and below two arched wall niches in a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud. The Assyrians probably used this building to store treasure and gifts they collected during military campaigns. The statues found below the niches may have fallen when the Assyrian Empire was finally defeated at the end of the 600s B.C.

Originally, these statues were attached to long ivory bases and displayed in the arched niches. They might have been arranged like a parade of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as gifts to the Assyrian king.

The statue shows a man facing forward, but his lower body is turned to the right as he walks. He holds the horns of an oryx (a type of desert antelope) that walks behind him. The man’s eyes, necklace, and armband were carved to hold colored glass or gemstone decorations. He wears a short skirt with a long sash, decorated with patterns like mythical fire-spitting snakes, zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles.

A monkey sits on his left shoulder, gripping his short curly hair. The monkey’s fur is shown with small carved lines. The man’s right arm is missing, but it was probably raised to hold a patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder.

King Ashurnasirpal II of Assyria built the palaces and storerooms at Nimrud, which held thousands of carved ivory pieces. Most of these ivories were used as decorations on furniture or as small precious objects like boxes. Some were carved in the same style as the large stone reliefs that lined the palace walls. But most show styles from North Syria and the Phoenician city-states.

Phoenician-style ivories often use images from Egyptian art, like sphinxes and figures wearing Egyptian crowns. They also use fancy carving techniques like openwork (cut-out designs) and colored glass inlays. North Syrian-style ivories tend to show stockier figures in more active poses, carved as solid pieces with fewer extra decorations. However, some pieces don’t fit neatly into any of these styles.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute (gifts from weaker states) or as loot from conquered enemies. Some may have been made in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt. Elephants did live in some river valleys in Syria, but they were hunted to extinction by the end of the 700s B.C.

CuratorialText:
This bronze monumental statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues of the third century preserved today. It is a reminder that many of the disembodied portrait heads displayed in these galleries were once attached to freestanding sculptures, whose original appearances were undoubtedly quite different. Portraits of the emperor served the highly important function in imperial propaganda of presenting the ruler to the public and of projecting the personality with which he wished to be perceived. Leadership and military strength are evoked in the heroic nudity of the figure and in the pose, which recalls the famous statue of Alexander the Great with the Lance by Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head represents the emperor with brutish realism. Trebonianus likely would have cradled a parazonium, or short sword, in his left arm, and held a spear in his upraised right hand.
The statue has undergone several campaigns of restoration since its discovery in the early nineteenth century and was examined in great detail as part of the conservation treatment in preparation for its display here. Visual examination inside and out, combined with x-radiography, made possible the clear identification of ancient and restored areas, which are indicated in the accompanying illustration. At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Despite the discrepancy in scale, the head belongs to the body. Although the mantle draped over his left shoulder is a modern restoration, cast edges beneath it confirm that the statue had a similar embellishment in antiquity. The left foot with its elaborately decorated open-fronted boot appears to be ancient but may not belong.
persona1_adapted:
**A Rare Look at a Roman Emperor**

This big bronze statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues from the 200s AD that still exists today. It reminds us that many of the heads you see in this gallery were once attached to full-body statues. Those statues probably looked very different when they were whole.

Statues of emperors were a key part of Roman propaganda. They showed the ruler to the public in a way he wanted to be seen. This statue shows the emperor as a strong leader and a military hero. He is naked (which was a way to show heroic strength in ancient art), and his pose is copied from a famous statue of Alexander the Great. But while his body looks perfect and ideal, his face is very realistic—almost ugly. The emperor Trebonianus likely held a short sword in his left arm and a spear in his raised right hand.

This statue has been fixed up many times since it was found in the early 1800s. Before it was put on display here, experts studied it very carefully. They looked inside and out, and used X-rays to tell which parts are ancient and which were added later. About three-quarters of the statue is original. The head belongs to the body, even though it’s a bit too big for it. The cloth over his left shoulder is a modern addition, but old marks underneath show that the statue had a similar cloth in ancient times. The left foot, with its fancy open-fronted boot, looks ancient but might not be the original one.

CuratorialText: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (Seasons), maidens closely associated with the fertility of the earth and the nurturing of children. The original bronze was erected in the Agora (marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. Rarely can an ancient monument be dated so exactly. We know from literary sources that the cult of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently found Panathenaic amphorae dated to 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and reported that it was by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was represented as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the major goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.
persona1_adapted:
**Eirene: The Goddess of Peace**

Eirene was the ancient Greek goddess of peace. She was the daughter of Zeus (the king of the gods) and Themis (the goddess of justice). Along with her two sisters, she was one of the Horai, or Seasons. These goddesses were linked to the earth’s fertility and the care of children.

A famous bronze statue of Eirene was set up in the marketplace (called the Agora) of Athens between 375 and 359 B.C. That’s over 2,300 years ago! It’s rare for historians to know the exact date of an ancient monument, but we can date this one thanks to old writings and special pottery. We know the cult (or worship) of Eirene began in Athens in 375/374 B.C. Also, six decorated jars called Panathenaic amphorae, made around 360/359 B.C., show a picture of the statue.

A Greek traveler named Pausanius saw the statue in the Agora around 150 A.D. and wrote that it was made by a sculptor named Kephisodotos.

In the statue, Eirene is shown as a beautiful young woman. She wears a long dress called a peplos and a cloak called a himation. In her right hand, she holds a scepter (a symbol of power). On her left arm, she carries a baby named Ploutos, who represents wealth, and a cornucopia (a horn-shaped basket overflowing with food and riches). This image is very similar to statues of Demeter, the goddess of farming and harvest, who was also Ploutos’s mother.

CuratorialText: Many private summer villas were located along the coast near Naples. One of the most sumptuous must have been the villa built by Agrippa, friend of the emperor Augustus and husband of his daughter, Julia. It stood overlooking the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. The villa was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905 after its accidental discovery during work on a railway. Wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms and the Archaeological Museum at Naples received the rest. Agrippa died in 12 B.C. and his son, Agrippa Postumus became the villa's proprietor in 11 B.C. as inscriptions found there indicate. The frescoes must have been painted during renovations begun at the time. Painted by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest existing examples of Roman wall painting. The so-called Black Room was one of a sequence of bedrooms facing south toward the downward slope of the mountain and the sea. The source of light was a wide doorway giving onto a terrace or promenade. This ambiguous and sophisticated decoration is a masterpiece of the so-called third style of Roman wall painting, which flourished during the reign of Augustus. The theme is a playful rendition of architectural motif. A low red dado serves as the base from which a skeleton of thin white columns appears to rise against a black background. There almost weightless columns support pavilions, candelabra, tripods, and a narrow cornice that runs around the room. They were embellished with jewel-like decorations. On the back wall tiny swans, the bird of Apollo, patron god of Augustus, perch improbably on threadlike spirals, and yellow panels with Egyptianizing motifs must have brought to mind the recent annexation of Egypt after the death of Cleopatra in 30 B.C. This architectural scheme creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind appear at once to be flat and to dissolve into limitless space. Tiny landscape vignettes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Burnished to a high polish, these walls must have appeared magical indeed when illuminated by lamps at night.
persona1_adapted:
**The Black Room: A Roman Masterpiece**

Long ago, rich Romans built fancy summer houses along the coast near Naples, Italy. One of the most amazing belonged to Agrippa, a close friend of Emperor Augustus and the husband of his daughter, Julia. This villa sat on a hill overlooking the Bay of Naples, near the modern town of Boscotrecase.

Workers accidentally discovered the villa in the early 1900s while building a railway. Between 1903 and 1905, archaeologists dug up parts of it. They found beautiful wall paintings in four bedrooms. The Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York got sections from three rooms, and the rest went to a museum in Naples.

Agrippa died in 12 B.C., and his son, Agrippa Postumus, took over the villa the next year. The paintings were probably made during renovations at that time. Artists who worked for the emperor created them, and today they are some of the finest examples of Roman wall painting.

The "Black Room" was one of the bedrooms. It faced south, toward the mountain and the sea. Light came through a wide doorway that opened onto a terrace or walkway. The room's decoration is a masterpiece of the "Third Style" of Roman wall painting, which was popular during Augustus's rule.

So what does the decoration look like? Imagine a low red baseboard. Above it, thin white columns seem to rise against a black background. These almost weightless columns hold up little pavilions, candelabras, tripods, and a narrow shelf that runs around the room. Everything is decorated with jewel-like details.

On the back wall, tiny swans (the bird of Apollo, Augustus's favorite god) sit on super-thin spirals. Yellow panels with Egyptian-style designs reminded viewers that Egypt had just been added to the Roman Empire after Cleopatra's death in 30 B.C.

The design doesn't try to look deep or 3D. The black walls seem both flat and like they go on forever. Tiny landscape scenes float like islands in the middle of the blackness. The walls were polished to a high shine, so they must have looked magical when lit by oil lamps at night.

CuratorialText:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)
Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda This fresco from the Imperial villa at Boscotrecase depicts two consecutive events from the myth of Perseus and Andromeda. Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the ketos, a snaky sea monster painted in a brilliant blue-green palette. The creature raises his head with gigantic open jaws and frightful teeth toward Andromeda, who stands with outstretched arms in the center of the panel. One hand appears to be chained to the crag; the other elegantly placed on the rocks. Perseus flies in from the left with his lyre in one hand, winged shoes on his feet, and a windblown cloak over his shoulder. In the upper right portion of the fresco, he is greeted by Andromeda's grateful father, a scene that alludes to the myth's happy endingâ€“the marriage of hero and princess. The fortunes of love and the ever-present sea are the themes that link this fresco and that of Polyphemus and Galatea (20.192.17) from the same cubiculum at Boscotrecase. The translucent blue-green background of both frescoes also unifies the disparate episodes combined in each painting, and must have brought a sense of coolness to the room.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th Grade Readers**

**A Hero, a Princess, and a Sea Monster**

This ancient Roman painting comes from a fancy villa in Boscotrecase, Italy. It tells the story of Perseus and Andromeda—a myth about a hero, a princess in danger, and a scary sea monster.

On the left side of the painting, you can see Perseus flying in. He has winged shoes on his feet, a cloak blowing behind him, and a lyre (a kind of harp) in his hand. He’s about to rescue Andromeda, who is chained to a rock in the middle of the picture. Her arms are stretched out, and one hand is tied to the cliff. A huge, snake-like sea monster called a *ketos* is right next to her. It’s painted in bright blue-green colors, with its mouth wide open and sharp teeth showing.

In the top right corner of the painting, you can see Perseus being greeted by Andromeda’s father. This part shows the happy ending of the story: the hero and the princess get married.

This painting is all about love and the sea. It was made to go with another painting in the same room—one about the myth of Polyphemus and Galatea. Both paintings have a cool, blue-green background that makes them feel fresh and calm, like being near the ocean.

CuratorialText: This noble image of a woman brings to mind the philosopher Aristotle's description of commonly held beliefs about the dead: "In addition to believing that those who have ended this life are blessed and happy, we also think that to say anything false or slanderous against them is impious, from our feeling that it is directed against those who have already become our betters and superiors" (Of the Soul, quoted in Plutarch, A Letter to Apollonius 27). Larger than life and seated on a thronelike chair, this figure assumes almost heroic proportions.
persona1_adapted:
This woman is shown as noble and powerful, like a hero from a story. She sits on a throne-like chair and seems larger than life. The artist wanted to make her seem important and respected.

Long ago, the Greek philosopher Aristotle wrote about how people thought of the dead. He said that people believed those who had died were blessed and happy. People also thought it was wrong to say bad things about them, because the dead were seen as better and greater than the living.

This artwork reflects that idea. The woman is shown as someone to look up to, almost like a hero.

CuratorialText: A stylized octopus flanked by fish covers each side of this stirrup jar, named for the shape of its handles. Mycenaean pottery often reflects Minoan-inspired themes and techniques that originated on the island of Crete. Animated marine motifs adopted from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels in about 1500 BCE and were initially rendered in a very naturalistic manner. Here, the symmetrical composition and abstract depiction of the sea creatures are characteristically Mycenaean. Such jars were commonly used to transport liquids.
persona1_adapted:
This stirrup jar has a picture of a stylized octopus with fish on both sides. It’s called a “stirrup jar” because its handles look like the stirrups on a saddle.

Mycenaean pottery was often inspired by the art of the Minoans, who lived on the island of Crete. Around 1500 BCE, Mycenaean artists started painting sea creatures on their pots. At first, these animals looked very realistic.

But in this jar, the octopus and fish are arranged in a balanced, symmetrical way and drawn in a more abstract style. That’s a typical Mycenaean touch. Jars like this were commonly used to carry liquids.

CuratorialText:
On the shoulder, women dancing between musicians playing flute and lyre
On the body, wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete known representation of an Attic wedding. The bridal couple with the best man behind them sit in a cart drawn by two donkeys. A mule cart with four guests follows. Other members of the procession are on foot. The woman in the lead holds two torches, indicating that the scene takes place at night. The procession heads toward the bridegroom's house where a woman, probably the mother of the groom, awaits. The architecture of the house is carefully indicated; the white columns of the porch may be painted wood.
persona1_adapted:
**On the shoulder:** Women are dancing between musicians playing a flute and a lyre (a stringed instrument like a small harp).

**On the body:** A wedding parade. This is the oldest and most complete picture we have of an ancient Greek wedding from Athens. The bride and groom sit in a cart pulled by two donkeys, with the best man behind them. A mule cart carrying four guests follows behind. Other people in the parade are walking. The woman at the front holds two torches, which tells us the scene happens at night. The parade is heading to the groom's house, where a woman—probably his mother—is waiting. The house's architecture is shown in detail; the white columns of the porch might be made of painted wood.

CuratorialText: This highly ornate and extremely well-preserved Roman marble sarcophagus came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort and was formerly displayed in their country seat, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unsightly back, the sides and front of the sarcophagus are decorated with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. The central figure is that of the god Dionysos seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures who represent the four Seasons (from left to right, Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). The figures are unusual in that the Seasons are usually portrayed as women, but here they are shown as sturdy youths. Around these five central figures are placed other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two other groups of large figures, similarly intermingled with lesser ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is portrayed reclining on the ground; she is accompanied by a satyr and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure, probably to be identified with the personification of a river-god, reclines in front of two winged youths, perhaps representing two additional Seasons. The sarcophagus is an exquisite example of Roman funerary art, displaying all the virtuosity of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person would have been able to commission and purchase such a sarcophagus, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. The subjects - the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons - are unlikely, however, to have had any special significance for the deceased, particularly as it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, although the treatment and carving of the figures is quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus the figures are carved in high relief and so endow the crowded scene with multiple areas of light and shade, allowing the eye to wander effortlessly from one figure to another. One must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gilding, making the whole composition a visual tour de force. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early eighteenth century and soon thereafter installed in Badminton Hall means that it has been preserved almost intact and only a few of the minor extremities are now missing.
persona1_adapted:
**A Fancy Roman Coffin (Sarcophagus)**

This amazing Roman marble coffin, called a sarcophagus, is super fancy and in great shape. It came to the Metropolitan Museum from a fancy English house called Badminton House. The back is rough and unfinished, but the front and sides are covered with 40 human and animal figures carved in high relief (that means they really stick out).

In the middle, you see the god Dionysos sitting on a panther. But he's kind of small compared to four bigger figures around him. These four figures represent the four Seasons: Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall. Usually, the Seasons are shown as women, but here they are strong young guys. Around them are smaller figures and objects related to the god Bacchus (another name for Dionysos).

On the left end of the coffin, Mother Earth is lying down with a satyr (a half-man, half-goat creature) and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded man (probably a river god) lies in front of two winged youths, who might be two more Seasons.

This sarcophagus is a top-notch example of Roman funeral art. The marble came from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome to be carved. Only a super rich and powerful person could have bought this, likely a member of an old Roman noble family. But the designs (Dionysos and the Seasons) probably didn't mean anything special to the dead person—they were just copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus in Germany has the same design, but the carving is different.

On this one, the figures are carved so they really stand out, making lots of light and shadow. This lets your eye move easily from one figure to another. We should also imagine that some details were painted in bright colors and even covered with gold. Very few Roman coffins this good have survived. Even though it's missing its lid, it was found in the early 1700s and put in Badminton House, so it's been kept almost perfect—only a few small parts are missing.

CuratorialText:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.
persona1_adapted:
**What’s on This Vase?**

On the neck of the vase, one side shows a battle between centaurs (half-man, half-horse creatures) and Lapiths (a group of people from Greek myth). The other side shows young men and women.

Around the main part of the vase, there’s a scene called an *Amazonomachy*—a fight between Greek warriors and Amazons.

**Why Did the Greeks Choose This Subject?**

The ancient Greeks almost never made art about real events or people from their own time. Even though they knew how huge their victory was in the Persian Wars (a major historical conflict), they didn’t paint or carve scenes of those battles. Instead, they preferred to show **mythological** fights—especially between Greeks and enemies from the east, like the Amazons.

**Who Were the Amazons?**

The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women. According to Greek stories, their homeland was far to the east and north.

**Where Did This Idea Come From?**

In Athens, during the first half of the 400s B.C., the most famous Amazon battle scenes were painted on the walls of two important buildings: the **Theseion** and the **Stoa Poikile** (the Painted Portico). Those huge paintings were very influential, and they inspired the scene you see on this vase—and on another nearby vase (06.286.86).

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 still life paintings. A still life is a type of art that shows objects that don’t move—like flowers, food, shells, books, or even a skull. These objects are carefully arranged, and sometimes there’s a person in the background. The name “still life” means that everything in the painting is frozen in time.

This kind of painting became popular in the 1500s, especially with artists from Flanders (a region in Europe). Over time, artists started to focus on different things. For example, Rachel Ruysch was famous for painting flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint food, plates, and cups.

There’s also a special type of still life called a *vanitas*. These paintings often include a skull to remind us that life is short and that material things don’t last forever.

CuratorialText: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.
persona1_adapted:
**Orange Above!**

In 1815, the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time. The new king was William I (1772–1843). He was the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier.

King William I focused on building up the country. He ordered the construction of canals and roads. He also invested in trade, shipping, and industry. But while he paid a lot of attention to the economy, he didn't focus as much on other parts of running the country.

This caused problems. People in the Southern Netherlands became unhappy. In 1831, they rebelled and broke away to form the country of Belgium. King William I tried to stop them, but he couldn't win them back. After years of struggle, he finally accepted that Belgium was independent in 1839.

Disappointed by this loss, William I gave up the throne a year later. His son, William II, became the new king.

CuratorialText: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. Many garments are custom-made for a special occasion, while others showcase the technological advancements of ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent: the collection features highlights of Dutch and international fashion. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has one of the oldest and biggest collections of clothing in the Netherlands. These clothes—for women, men, and kids—tell amazing stories. They show us what people liked to wear, how clothes were made, and how fashion was influenced by places near and far.

Some outfits were made just for special events, while others show how new technology changed the way clothes were mass-produced. From 17th-century royal Dutch fashion to famous designers like Yves Saint Laurent, this collection highlights the best of Dutch and international style. It’s a cool look at how fashion has changed over the centuries.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona1_adapted:
Around 1890, a new style of art called Art Nouveau (pronounced "art noo-VOH") appeared. Artists created it because they were tired of always copying old styles from past centuries. Instead, they wanted something fresh and exciting.

Art Nouveau is easy to spot. It uses lots of natural shapes like flowers, leaves, animals, and bugs. The designs have smooth, flowing lines, geometric patterns, and are often uneven or asymmetrical. Think of curvy vines, butterfly wings, and wavy hair.

This style became popular in big cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich. From there, it spread across Western Europe and even to the United States. Art Nouveau wasn't just for paintings—it showed up in everything from furniture and jewelry to buildings and posters. It lasted until World War I started in 1914.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona1_adapted:
**Hendrick Goltzius: An Artist Who Changed His Style**

Hendrick Goltzius was born in Germany in 1558. He started out as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, but later became a famous painter too.

Goltzius moved to Haarlem in the Netherlands with his teacher. There, he and two other artists introduced a new art style called **Mannerism**. You can spot Mannerism by its:
- Super muscular bodies
- Twisted, dramatic poses
- Small heads in busy, complicated scenes

In 1590-91, Goltzius traveled to Italy. He was inspired by the great Italian artists of the past. When he returned to the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind. Instead, he started using a more **classicist** style, which focused on balanced, natural-looking proportions.

Goltzius was especially famous for his engravings—detailed prints made by carving into metal. People all over Europe admired his skill. His work gives us a great look at how Dutch painting was changing around the year 1600.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.
persona1_adapted:
**Rembrandt van Rijn (1606–1669)** knew from a young age that he wanted to be a painter. He started by learning from a local artist in his hometown of Leiden, then moved to Amsterdam to train with another painter named Pieter Lastman.

Rembrandt didn’t like to follow the rules. He experimented freely with bold compositions, how he applied paint, and the way he showed different textures. He made his scenes more dramatic by using strong contrasts between light and dark.

His early paintings were very detailed and precise. As he got older, his brushstrokes became looser and more expressive.

Rembrandt received many important commissions. His most famous and largest painting is *The Night Watch*. Instead of a stiff, posed portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment where everyone is in action.

The Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam holds this masterpiece, plus 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and about 1,300 prints by Rembrandt.

CuratorialText: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.
persona1_adapted:
Johannes Vermeer (1632–1675) is one of the most famous painters in the world today, but we actually know very little about him. What we do know is that he lived in the Dutch city of Delft, worked as an art dealer, raised a big family, and, of course, painted.

His early paintings included scenes from the Bible and ancient myths. You can also see the influence of a group of artists called the Utrecht Caravaggists in his early work. Later, Vermeer focused on painting indoor scenes and “tronies”—which are like portrait studies of people.

His most famous paintings are quiet, everyday moments: a woman reading a letter, someone doing a household chore. These scenes feel calm and peaceful, and sometimes even a little mysterious. People admire Vermeer for his amazing use of light, bold colors, and incredible attention to detail.

During his lifetime, Vermeer was fairly well-known and could sell his paintings for high prices. But after he died, he was mostly forgotten. It wasn’t until the mid-1800s that people rediscovered his work and made him the famous artist we know today.

CuratorialText: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.
persona1_adapted:
Rachel Ruysch (1664–1750) was one of the most successful artists of her time. She painted flowers—roses, poppies, carnations, and irises—so detailed they looked almost real. Her work shows she knew a lot about nature.

Ruysch learned about plants and flowers at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father was a professor. She also studied his collection of dried flowers and insects. A still-life painter named Willem van Aelst taught her the tricks of the trade.

In 1701, Ruysch became the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she worked as a court painter for a German prince—a very important job. But he wasn't the only fan of her work. People all over Europe collected her flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.
persona1_adapted:
Thérèse Schwartze (1851–1918) grew up in an artistic family. Her father, Johann Georg Schwartze, was a painter, and she became a talented portrait artist, drawer, and printmaker. She learned to paint in her father’s studio and later studied art in Munich and Paris. When her father died suddenly, Thérèse was only 23 years old. She became the main earner for her family.

Her painting style was bold and energetic. She used quick brushstrokes and large areas of color. This made her portraits full of personality, but still flattering to the people she painted. People loved her work, and she became famous in the Netherlands and other countries.

Around the year 1900, Thérèse was the most successful portrait painter in the Netherlands. She even painted members of the Royal Family. She worked fast and created hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint. Today, the Rijksmuseum has many of her portraits of artists in its collection.

CuratorialText: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.
persona1_adapted: If you were walking through Amsterdam in the late 1800s, you might have run into George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). He spent days wandering his favorite city with a sketchbook. Breitner called himself "the painter of the people" and focused on drawing and painting everyday city folks. He also used a camera to capture street life. Then, he brought that photographic style into his paintings—sometimes cutting people off at the edge of the frame. His art used thick brushstrokes and smudges, which not everyone liked. Some journalists criticized his work, but fans loved it. Even today, Breitner is remembered as one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.

CuratorialText: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.
persona1_adapted:
Vincent van Gogh loved to draw. He got his first painting lessons from his cousin's husband, Anton Mauve, who was a famous artist. When he was young, van Gogh painted a lot of scenes of poor farmers, using dark colors.

After studying for a short time in Antwerp, he moved to Paris. There, he was inspired by new art styles like Impressionism and Pointillism. He started using short brushstrokes and bright colors that he didn't mix together, placing them side by side.

In 1888, he moved to sunny southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health problems and had to stay in the hospital. But even then, he kept painting. It wasn't until after he died in 1890 that people saw his work and he became famous all over the world.

CuratorialText: Born into a Utrecht family of silversmiths, the career path of Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was determined early on. Around 1590, Van Vianen must have completed his master's examination, as he worked independently from that moment on and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through lobe-like ornaments and forms. In 1614, Van Vianen received a commission from the Amsterdam guild to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had passed away shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this jug, the ornament plays the leading role for the first time.
persona1_adapted:
Adam van Vianen was born into a family of silversmiths in Utrecht, the Netherlands. That meant his future career was pretty much set from the start. Around 1590, when he was in his early twenties, he passed his master's exam. After that, he started working on his own and joined the blacksmiths' guild.

Van Vianen created imaginative objects that tell a story using curvy, lobe-shaped decorations and forms. In 1614, he got a special job from the Amsterdam guild. They asked him to make a jug in memory of his brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had recently died. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. For the first time, the ornament—not the shape of the object—was the main focus of the design.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona1_adapted:
**Hendrick Goltzius: An Artist Who Changed His Style**

Hendrick Goltzius was born in Germany in 1558. He learned to draw, engrave (carving designs into metal or wood), and paint on glass. Later, he became a famous painter.

Goltzius moved to Haarlem in the Netherlands with his teacher. There, he and two other artists introduced a new art style called **Mannerism** to the country. You can spot Mannerism by its:
- Very muscular bodies
- Twisted, dramatic poses
- Small heads in busy, complicated scenes

In 1590-91, Goltzius traveled to Italy. He was inspired by the great Italian artists of the past. When he returned to the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind. Instead, he started using a **classicist** style—more balanced, natural, and with realistic body proportions.

Goltzius was especially famous for his **engravings**. These were so detailed and technically perfect that people all over Europe admired them. His work shows us how Dutch painting was changing around the year 1600.

CuratorialText: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.
persona1_adapted:
In 2012, artist and photographer Viviane Sassen visited a small village called Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname. The people there live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. Even though the village is very remote, you can still see signs of the modern world. For example, people have mobile phones and plastic household items.

Sassen took a series of photos in Pikin Slee that show the beauty of everyday life in a creative, abstract way. Most of the photos are black and white, but some have bright splashes of color—something Sassen is known for. In 2013, the Rijksmuseum added this series to its collection. It became a modern addition to the museum’s photos of Suriname, which were mostly older, historical images before then.

CuratorialText: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.
persona1_adapted:
William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who became the leader of a rebellion and is remembered as the "father of the Netherlands." When he was just 11 years old, William inherited a small French territory called Orange and gained the title "Prince of Orange." His life was full of drama and big achievements.

King Philip II of Spain, the son of Emperor Charles V, chose William to be the governor of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht—the most important provinces in the Netherlands. But the two men didn't get along. Philip wanted a powerful empire with one ruler and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, however, believed in freedom of religion and the right for everyone to follow their own beliefs. This conflict eventually led to William's death. On July 10, 1584, he was shot and killed.

CuratorialText: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.
persona1_adapted:
Who is the girl in Simon Maris’s painting? We don’t know much about her. But we do know that around 1906, she posed for Maris in his studio in Amsterdam. She was about 12 years old at the time. We know this because Maris took photos of her there.

In Maris’s notes, he calls her “Isabella.” He also gave that name to other paintings he made of her. But we can’t be sure that was her real name. Maris might have made it up. Or maybe she used a fake name, which was common for models back then.

CuratorialText: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.
persona1_adapted: Amalia of Solms (1602–1675) helped make the House of Orange more powerful and put the Netherlands on the map in Europe. She was a trusted advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he got sick, she took over some of his duties and helped steer the country toward the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband died, her son also passed away. This started a period when the Netherlands had no stadtholder. To protect the future of the House of Orange, Amalia used her influence both at home and abroad. She used art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects was as impressive as those of other European royal courts. In this way, she helped make the Netherlands—led by the House of Orange—a major center for art and trade in Europe.

CuratorialText: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th Grade Readers:**

Between 1650 and 1672, the Netherlands had no single leader, called a Stadtholder. Instead, the most powerful citizens ran the country. Johan de Witt, the top official of Holland, became the most important politician. He was clever at playing the Republic's enemies, England and France, against each other. But in 1672, those countries, along with two German prince-bishoprics, attacked the Netherlands. Johan's brother, Cornelis de Witt, was in charge of the military as a representative of the government. As foreign armies advanced, more and more people demanded the return of a Stadtholder. Cornelis was arrested on suspicion of plotting against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visited him in prison, an angry mob dragged both brothers out and killed them. Even long after their deaths, people remember the de Witt brothers as defenders of "true freedom."

CuratorialText: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.
persona1_adapted:
In the early 1800s, a woman named Hortense de Beauharnais (1783–1837) and her husband Louis Napoleon became the first King and Queen of Holland. They were chosen for these roles by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte, who was a famous French leader.

The royal couple had three sons, including a boy who would later become Emperor Napoleon III. But even though they had children, their marriage was not a happy one.

Queen Hortense often went to parties and official events. She also visited local businesses, which made her very popular with the people of Holland. But her husband didn't like that she was so well-liked. He forced her to stay out of the spotlight.

After Napoleon was defeated, Hortense moved to Switzerland without her husband. She lived on a large estate called Arenenberg. Hortense was a talented musician and composer herself, and she hosted cultural gatherings there that people talked about for a long time.

CuratorialText: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.
persona1_adapted: On July 13, 1885, the Rijksmuseum officially opened after nearly ten years of construction. The building was designed by Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a well-known architect and a member of the Catholic Church. Cuypers included a portrait of himself on the outside of the museum, just like the builders of medieval cathedrals used to do. Over time, many of the original decorations inside the museum were lost. But during the last renovation, Cuypers' work was brought back to its original beauty. The Rijksmuseum wasn't Cuypers' only famous project. In 1889, his Amsterdam Central Station opened. Besides these big projects, Cuypers mainly designed churches and monuments.

CuratorialText: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.
persona1_adapted:
Willem Drees (1886–1988) was a Dutch politician who worked his way up from local government to become Prime Minister. He started as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party (which later became the PvdA) and held jobs like city councilor, alderman, and member of parliament.
