After World War II, Drees became the Minister of Social Affairs. He created the "Drees Emergency Act," which gave older people a steady income and helped them out of poverty. This made him very popular, and in 1948, he became Prime Minister.

His main job was to lead the rebuilding of the Netherlands after the war. Drees was the right person for this task. With a strong sense of teamwork and fairness, he helped build the modern Dutch welfare state—a system where the government supports people in need. He served as Prime Minister until 1958.

Even today, Willem Drees is remembered and respected for his hard work and dedication to the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.
persona1_adapted:
In the late 1500s, Italian artists really liked a style called Mannerism. It had complicated scenes with figures in twisted, dramatic poses. But around 1600, a painter named Caravaggio shook things up. He made art that looked much more real. His gods and saints had wrinkled faces, messy hair, and dirty feet—just like regular people.

Caravaggio loved using strong light and dark contrasts to create dramatic effects. His paintings were clear and easy to follow. His style quickly became popular all over Europe. Around 1620, a group of artists in Utrecht, Netherlands, became his followers. They were called the Caravaggists. Artists like Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen even traveled to Rome to study his work. And even though Rembrandt never went to Italy, he was also inspired by Caravaggio's dramatic lighting.

CuratorialText: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.
persona1_adapted:
**The Renaissance: A Time of "Rebirth"**

The 1400s and 1500s are known as the Renaissance, which means "rebirth." During this time in Europe, people became really excited about art, books, and science again. They looked back at the old ideas from ancient Greece and Rome for inspiration.

Artists and builders wanted things to look perfect and balanced. They studied old Roman buildings and started using round arches, columns, and triangular tops called pediments. In paintings and sculptures, artists tried to make people and nature look real. They learned how to show depth (called perspective) and studied the human body to get the details right.

Besides religious stories, artists also painted myths and everyday life scenes. The Renaissance started in Italy and spread across Europe. This happened partly because of a new invention: the printing press, which made it easier to share ideas and pictures.

CuratorialText: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.
persona1_adapted: Between World War I and World War II, a style called Art Deco became popular around the world. It mixed old traditions with new ideas from factories and machines. Art Deco grew out of an earlier style called Art Nouveau, but it also used simple, straight lines and geometric shapes. Artists were inspired by plants, flowers, and designs from Africa, East Asia, and Central America. They also looked at folk art and objects found in archaeological digs. The name "Art Deco" comes from a big 1925 fair in Paris called the *Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes*.

CuratorialText: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.
persona1_adapted:
**The Rise of Gothic Architecture and Art**

Around 1150, churches in and near Paris started getting taller and taller. The most famous example is Notre-Dame Cathedral. This was the beginning of a style called Gothic architecture. It spread quickly across Europe and lasted until about 1500.

What made Gothic churches different? They had tall, pointed arch windows filled with colorful stained glass. This let in lots of light, making the inside feel airy. The ceilings (called vaults) rested on thin columns and had visible ribs. Outside, the heavy stone walls were supported by flying buttresses—slender supports that looked like arms holding up the building.

Gothic style also changed sculpture. Artists carved people, animals, and plants with elegant poses and graceful, flowing clothes. The figures looked stylized, not exactly realistic.

This style didn't stay just in churches. It spread to other art forms like painting, book illustrations (called illuminated manuscripts), metalwork, and furniture.

The Rijksmuseum has many objects from this time, including painted altars, silver holders for the Eucharist (called monstrances), wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Reader:**

The term "Impressionism" means "snapshot." It was first used in the early 1870s as a negative label—basically an insult—for the work of a group of painters in Paris. Artists like Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot tried to show reality the way they saw it, not just copy it exactly.

These French Impressionists were inspired by the realistic style of an earlier French group called the Barbizon School, as well as by a slightly older Dutch artist named Johan Barthold Jongkind. They painted with bright colors and used short, loose brushstrokes. Instead of painting history or myths, they chose subjects from everyday modern life.

Because their style looked sketchy and unfinished, it sometimes seemed like they had painted their pictures really fast. That’s why many critics said their work wasn’t complete or good enough. But by the late 1880s, people in the Netherlands started to appreciate French Impressionism too.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona1_adapted:
In the late 1800s, a new style of art appeared. It was created by artists who were tired of always copying old styles from past centuries. They wanted to make something new and different. This fresh style was called Art Nouveau (which means "new art" in French).

Art Nouveau artists loved using shapes from nature—like flowers, leaves, animals, and bugs. Their work focused on smooth, flowing lines, geometric shapes, and designs that weren't perfectly balanced (asymmetrical). The style became popular in cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich, and soon spread across Western Europe and the United States.

Art Nouveau was big from around 1890 until World War I started in 1914. During that time, you could see it everywhere—in paintings, buildings, furniture, jewelry, and even everyday objects.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.
persona1_adapted: Rembrandt wasn't just a great painter—he was also an amazing draftsman and printmaker. The Rijksmuseum has about 1,300 of his prints, sometimes with multiple copies or different versions of the same image. His prints show all kinds of subjects: stories from the Bible and mythology, everyday life, landscapes, and portraits of himself and others. You can really see how his skills, style, and artistic ideas changed over time. His loose, sketchy drawing style and the way he used strong contrasts between light and dark are totally unique. He also liked to capture the most emotional moments in a scene, which was unusual for his time. That makes his work feel human and easy to connect with. Because of all this, Rembrandt had a huge influence on other artists during his life and for many years after.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.
persona1_adapted:
For a long time, Europeans had to buy expensive porcelain from Asia. This beautiful, delicate material was sometimes called "white gold" because it was so valuable. Only very rich people, like kings and nobles, could afford it.

Then, in 1708, something amazing happened. An alchemist named Johann Friedrich Böttger, working for a powerful German ruler named Augustus the Strong, figured out how to make porcelain in Europe for the first time. Soon after, the very first European porcelain factory opened in a town called Meissen.

Augustus the Strong had a big dream. He wanted to build a special palace, called the Japanese Palace, to show off his huge collection of both Asian and Meissen porcelain. The invention of European porcelain was a huge deal—it changed the history of art and design forever.

Today, the Rijksmuseum has one of the most important collections of early Meissen porcelain in the world. Some of these pieces originally belonged to a couple named Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since it opened in 1885. Most of the collection is Chinese (about 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (about 600 pieces), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. This makes sense because the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) was founded in 1602. Starting in the 1600s, thin, hard porcelain with blue Chinese decorations became super popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain became a big part of Dutch home decor.

Since 1952, the Rijksmuseum has also housed the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes different types of Asian art: Japanese tea ceramics, Chinese Song dynasty bowls and Tang dynasty funeral sculptures, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Starting in 1610, about 100,000 pieces of Chinese porcelain were shipped to the Netherlands every year. To compete with this popular Chinese pottery, Dutch potters had to improve their own work. They made their earthenware thinner, whiter, and shinier. They also painted blue designs on a white, shiny glaze—a look that reminded people of Asian porcelain.

This type of pottery was called "plateel" or "faience." But in the 1600s and 1700s, people started calling it "Hollants porceleyn" (Dutch porcelain). After 1650, the city of Delft became the main place for making this pottery, so it eventually became known as "Delft earthenware."

Around the same time, a civil war in China (from 1644 to 1684) stopped almost all trade in Chinese porcelain. This gave Delft potters a big chance. Their earthenware became very popular with rich people in Europe, and the style really took off.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a collection of small bronze sculptures from the Renaissance, mostly made in Italy. Some of these were created in the late 1400s in the university city of Padua. They are everyday objects inspired by ancient Greek and Roman art, like candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with figures from mythology.

Many small workshops made these fancy items in large numbers, but some unique bronze pieces were also created. A great example is the large candlestick designed by Verrocchio in 1469 for the city hall of Florence, called the Palazzo Vecchio.

Bronze casting in the style of ancient Greece and Rome also became popular in other Italian cities, like Rome and Venice. In the 1500s and 1600s, these small statues ended up in the studies of scholars, the art collections of wealthy people, and the "cabinets of curiosities" owned by princes and nobles.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Readers:**

At first, artists painted the sea to show important events and battles. They wanted people in the future to remember these heroic moments. One artist, Willem van de Velde I, was like a war reporter. He painted famous Dutch naval battles from what he actually saw.

His son, also named Willem, was really good at painting both calm and stormy seas. Later, in the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen liked to show ships in trouble in a dramatic way. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study the weather up close.

Thanks to painters like Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and the Schotel family (father and son), Dutch art became famous around the world for its seascapes.

Later, the artists of the Hague School focused more on light and cloudy skies. They also paid more attention to the hard lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.
persona1_adapted:
**Back in the 1600s, cities in the Netherlands had their own security. Instead of a police force, they had groups of citizens called the civic guard. These were regular people—mostly men—who had to join if they were between 18 and 60 years old. But there was a catch: you had to buy your own weapons!**

**To show off their group, the guards would pay for a big group portrait. Each member chipped in money, so these paintings were expensive. The earliest ones, from the 1500s, were pretty stiff and boring—everyone just stood still. But by the 1600s, artists started making them more lively and interesting. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's *Banquet of the Marksmen*.**

**And it wasn't just the guards who wanted group portraits. Other city groups, clubs, and even rich families also paid artists to paint them together.**

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 still life paintings. A still life is a type of art that shows objects that don’t move—like flowers, food, shells, books, or even a skull. These objects are carefully arranged, and sometimes a person appears in the background. The name “still life” means that life is paused for a moment.

This style became popular in the 1500s, starting with Flemish painters. Soon, artists began to focus on different subjects. For example, Rachel Ruysch was known for painting flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint food, glasses, and plates.

There’s also a special type called a vanitas still life. It often includes a skull to remind us that life is short and that material things don’t last forever.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a huge collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages all the way to today. These more than 1,700 portraits aren't just beautiful art—they also show us what people from the past looked like. You can see the clothes and jewelry they wore, their hairstyles, and sometimes even what their homes looked like.

Different time periods focused on different things. For example, during the Renaissance, portraits became more realistic. But in the 20th and 21st centuries, artists moved away from that realism and tried new styles.

People have ordered portraits for many reasons: to show off their job or social status, to celebrate a wedding, or to remember someone after they died. The museum also has many self-portraits by famous painters like Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.
persona1_adapted:
**Genre Paintings: Art of Everyday Life**

Genre paintings are pictures that show scenes from everyday life. You might see people cooking in a kitchen, hanging out in a living room, or shopping at a market. These paintings capture real moments: kids playing, a family gathered around a fire, or a merchant selling goods on the street.

The first genre paintings appeared in the 1500s in Flanders (a region in Europe). But in the Netherlands, this type of art didn't become popular until about 100 years later. Famous Dutch artists like Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster made genre paintings famous. Their works often had a hidden joke or a secret moral lesson.

The name "genre painting" wasn't used until the 1800s, when this style was at its most popular. The Rijksmuseum museum in Amsterdam has genre paintings from that time too, including works by Jozef Israëls. He painted lively scenes of farmers and fishermen going about their daily lives.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert depicted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo , the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, painters also chose Roman scenes as a metaphor for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, depicted by Ferdinand Bol in the painting Claudius Civilis, refers to the struggle against the Spaniards.
persona1_adapted:
**History Paintings: Stories from the Past**

A history painting tells a famous story from the past. These stories often come from books like the Bible, myths, or real historical events. You’ll usually see several people in the painting, acting out the story.

For example, around 1530, artist Jan Jansz. Mostaert painted a scene from the conquest of America. Later, in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman created *The Battle of Waterloo*—the biggest painting in the Rijksmuseum.

In the 1600s, Dutch painters sometimes used Roman history to talk about their own country’s politics. One example is a painting by Ferdinand Bol called *Claudius Civilis*. It shows a revolt by the Batavians against the Romans, but it was really a way to talk about the Dutch fight against the Spanish.

CuratorialText: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” By uttering these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared his country, Indonesia, independent on August 17, 1945. Former colonizer the Netherlands refuses to recognize that independence and attempts to reoccupy Indonesia. In defense of its independence, Indonesia wages war against the Netherlands on its own soil. From the beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from the international community. Under international pressure, the Netherlands is forced to recognize Indonesia's independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia becomes a member state of the United Nations, and the international confirmation of independence becomes a reality.
persona1_adapted: On August 17, 1945, a leader named Sukarno said, “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” With those words, he announced that Indonesia was now a free country. But the Netherlands, which had ruled Indonesia before, didn’t accept this. They tried to take control again. To protect its freedom, Indonesia fought a war against the Netherlands on its own land. From the start, other countries around the world supported Indonesia. Because of this international pressure, the Netherlands finally had to recognize Indonesia’s independence in 1949. The next year, in 1950, Indonesia joined the United Nations, which made its independence official worldwide.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.
persona1_adapted:
Water helped the Netherlands become a major trading nation. The country’s economy grew because raw materials came in by rivers and oceans. These included bulk goods like grain and wood from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from Spain.

The Dutch used these materials to make products such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for preserving herring), weapons, and ships. Many of these goods were then shipped out again, often by water.

Cities played a key role in this international trade. In the 1400s and 1500s, Antwerp was the biggest port city in the Netherlands. But after the Spanish took it back during the Dutch Revolt, Holland—especially Amsterdam—became the main trading hub. This international trade led to the rise of a financial market, lots of jobs, and fast growth of cities.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.
persona1_adapted:
**What Makes Money Valuable?**

Have you ever wondered why a coin that costs just a few cents to make can be worth 2 euros? It’s all because of the stamp on it. Whoever has the right to make coins gets to decide what they look like, how much metal they contain, and what they’re worth.

For hundreds of years, gold and silver coins traveled all over the world through trade. Coins that had very little gold or silver in them usually stayed close to home. Why? Because if you took them to another country, people cared more about the actual metal in the coin than what it said on the front.

The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was a major center for trading precious metals. Even during the Eighty Years’ War, a lot of the silver that Spain took from the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. And until the late 1700s, huge “silver fleets” of ships sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, that money traveled everywhere—even all the way to Asia with the Dutch East India Company (the VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.
persona1_adapted:
William of Orange is known as the "Father of the Fatherland" because he led the fight for Dutch independence. The House of Orange got its princely title from a small French city-state called Orange, located on the Rhône River. Even though it was tiny, the family became one of the great royal houses of Europe through its wealth and influence. The wives of the stadtholders (Dutch leaders) played a big role in building these connections.

But by the end of the 1700s, it looked like the House of Orange was finished. Stadtholder William V fled to England, and the Batavian Republic was declared. However, in 1813, his oldest son returned and became King William I. From then on, the Netherlands became a "constitutional monarchy," and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always been on the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.
persona1_adapted:
**Who owned this object before it came to the museum?**  
The people who owned it—their lives, relationships, and stories—can tell us a lot about why the object matters.  

By doing something called *provenance research* (which means tracing an object’s history), we can learn about the people who owned it before World War II. This reminds us that many of those people were persecuted (treated unfairly and cruelly) during the Holocaust.  

Even if an object wasn’t stolen, its history still gives us clues about the past. The Rijksmuseum’s collection mostly tells the stories of collectors from 1933 to 1945. But that doesn’t give us the full picture of everyone who suffered. Why? Because many everyday objects never ended up in museums, or they’re harder to track down.  

Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum help us remember how diverse (full of different kinds of people and cultures) Europe was before the war. Provenance research shows that the people the Nazis targeted were active members of Dutch culture and history.  

Today, these objects are silent witnesses. They can’t speak, but they still have stories to tell.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.
persona1_adapted:
Before Europeans arrived, people had already been trading across the Indian Ocean for hundreds of years. The Dutch East India Company (called the VOC) took over these trade routes by force and used places like the Moluccas and Sri Lanka to get spices. Cities like Batavia (now Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope became important centers for growing sugar, coffee, and rice.

In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants also traded sugar and coffee. This led to the creation of the Dutch West India Company (the WIC). Over time, almost every trading post used enslaved people and took part in the slave trade.

Even regular workers in the Netherlands could make money from colonial trade. For example, a craftsman might sell supplies for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. Shipping goods to Europe and turning them into finished products also created jobs and profits. In these ways, colonial trade helped grow the Dutch economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.
persona1_adapted:
For over 350 years, the Netherlands was a colonial power. In the 1600s, it set up trading posts and colonies all over the world—including in places like Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Sometimes the Dutch made deals to settle these areas, but they also used military force.

The Rijksmuseum has many objects from this time, including art and everyday items. Some were taken as war booty, and others were bought or traded. It’s important to be honest about where these objects came from and to think about the Netherlands’ role in its colonial past. By researching and working with the countries these objects originally came from, the Rijksmuseum hopes to share a fuller, more balanced story of this shared history.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.
persona1_adapted:
For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, thrives, and buzzes with activity. People from all over the world have helped make it the city it is today.

The first people to live in Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen who settled along the River Amstel. During the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city in the middle of peatlands.

Then, in the 1600s, everything changed. Because of big trading companies like the VOC and WIC, Amsterdam quickly became a global city with over 200,000 people. Almost everyone in Amsterdam was connected to colonial trade in some way—whether they were rope makers, mapmakers, lawyers, or candy makers.

To handle all the new people, the city made a plan to expand. That was the beginning of Amsterdam’s famous canal belt.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.
persona1_adapted: The tulip originally came from Turkey and was brought to Europe in the late 1500s. It grew especially well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coast. In the 1600s, traders who sold tulips and other rare flowers started making "Florilegia"—books filled with detailed pictures that worked like catalogs of what they had for sale. People who loved unusual flowers could also hire painters or artists to draw them. That’s how flower still lifes became a popular type of art. Real flowers wilt and die, but paintings last much longer. Plus, these flower paintings sometimes cost less than the actual flowers shown in them.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.
persona1_adapted:
Pets haven't always been the cuddly friends we know today. Long ago, most animals had jobs. Dogs helped with hunting or guarding, and cats were kept around to catch mice. In fact, cats had a bad reputation for a long time because people in the Middle Ages sometimes linked them to witches.

It wasn’t until around 500 to 1500 that rich nobles started keeping dogs just for company. By the 1600s, you can see dogs in paintings as beloved pets, not just workers. Cats took even longer to become popular as pets. That didn’t really happen until the Victorian era (the 1800s). Then, fancy purebred cats like British Shorthairs or Persian Longhairs became a status symbol for wealthy people.

By the 1800s, dogs, cats, and later birds and fish, became common pets for people of all social classes. One artist, Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909), became famous for painting cats. Rich families often hired her to paint portraits of their beloved pets.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.
persona1_adapted:
**Sculpture at the Rijksmuseum: A World in 3D**

Sculpture is all around us—from ancient statues to modern art. At the Rijksmuseum, the collection of European sculpture is huge and includes works made from many different materials, from marble to metal.

This online catalog is a brand-new guide to every single European sculpture in the museum’s collection. The last time a full catalog was published was way back in 1973. Since then, art historians have learned a lot more about these sculptures, and the museum has added many new pieces. So it was time for a big update!

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions, like the Royal Cabinet of Rarities) didn’t start collecting sculptures in a serious way until the late 1800s. Before that, there were only a few. One early example is a marble bust of a man named Andries de Graeff, made by the artist Artus Quellinus. The Dutch government bought it in 1817.

At first, the museum mostly wanted sculptures that were important to Dutch history. But in the 1900s, that changed. Two museum leaders—Adriaan Pit (director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (a curator who made the 1973 catalog)—worked hard to bring in sculptures from other countries. Today, about half of the collection comes from outside the Netherlands.

The international side of the collection got a big boost in 1952. That’s when the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of Italian and German sculptures that had belonged to a man named Fritz Mannheimer. These works were recovered from Germany after World War II. This addition helped the museum keep growing its collection of art from all over Europe.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has an amazing collection of Japanese art on paper, including woodblock prints, illustrated books, and drawings. Out of nearly 4,000 prints, over 800 are special, privately made prints called *surimono*. This online catalog shows 203 of these *surimono*, which were given to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by a collector named JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The information in this catalog comes from Matthi Forrer's 2013 book *Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam*.

**Poetry and Pictures Together**

*Surimono* (which means "printed things") are one of the most delicate types of Japanese printmaking. They are fascinating because they mix poetry with images and act like a picture puzzle. This gives viewers a special look into the smart and creative world of late 1700s and early 1800s Edo (now called Tokyo). Famous artists like Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei made pictures to go with poems written by members of poetry clubs.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of Maria Trip. She lived from 1619 to 1683. She was the sister of Jacobus Trip and later married Balthasar Coymans. In the portrait, she is standing and shown from the waist up. She faces to the left. Her left arm rests on a banister, and she holds a fan.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
persona1_adapted:
Here’s the adapted version for an 8th-grade reader:

---

A playful figure sits on a cloud resting on a round stand. He has wings spread wide. With one hand, he holds his index finger to his lips, like he’s saying “shh,” and he has a mischievous smile on his face. With his other hand, he reaches for an arrow in a quiver lying next to him. One of his legs is pulled up, and the other leg stretches out past the edge of the stand.

Beside him is a flowering rose branch. The quiver is decorated with carved details, including fluting (vertical grooves), a wreath of laurel leaves, and a pattern called godrons (curved, rib-like shapes). The stand is a short column with a decorated base, also featuring a laurel leaf wreath. The middle part has curved grooves and smooth sections, separated by a raised edge. On top, there’s a lid-like piece with an egg-and-dart pattern (oval shapes alternating with pointed ones). This top part can be turned using two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.
persona1_adapted: A mother is carefully picking lice out of her child's hair in a cozy indoor room. This scene is called "Mother's Duty." They are sitting in front of a box bed on the right side of the room. Next to them is a high chair. On the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor, looking out an open window in the room behind. Above the door hangs a small painting of a landscape.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
persona1_adapted: This portrait shows Alida Assink in her fanciest clothes, from head to toe. That’s pretty unusual, because the artist, Kruseman, usually painted full-body portraits only for kings and queens. Alida was 23 years old when her guardian—a rich landowner—asked for this painting. The countryside background, with a hunting dog and a garden vase, copies a style from England that Kruseman really liked.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona1_adapted: Aert Schouman was an artist from Dordrecht, not a scientist. But he really cared about painting birds that looked real and true to life. You can see how carefully he observed nature in this painting of a red-billed toucan. Notice how the bird grips the branch with two toes instead of three, like most other birds do. The background is painted in light, see-through greens and blues. It gives you a feeling of the toucan's home in Guyana and northern Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
persona1_adapted:
**Still Life with Roses**  
This painting shows a bunch of roses in a vase. Look closely—you’ll also spot a butterfly and two snails. The artist wanted to capture a quiet moment in nature, mixing beautiful flowers with tiny, everyday creatures.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.
persona1_adapted: In this painting, the Duke of Wellington is getting the news that Prussian soldiers are on their way to help him. Wellington was the leader of the British and Dutch troops, and he’s the main person in this group portrait of the key figures at the Battle of Waterloo. On the left side, lying on a stretcher and wounded, is the Dutch Crown Prince, who later became King William II. The painting was first meant for Wellington, but it ended up staying in the Netherlands because King William I bought it for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
persona1_adapted: William of Orange was one of the noblemen who fought against King Philip II of Spain, who ruled the Netherlands at the time. William became the main leader of the Dutch Revolt. He fought for his own interests, wanted more independence for the Netherlands, and supported the idea that people should be free to choose their own religion. In 1580, Philip II put a reward on William’s head, and four years later, he was killed.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.
persona1_adapted:
Look closely at this painting. You’ll see lots of tiny details—bright flowers, unusual shells, and even beautiful bugs. It’s like a celebration of all the amazing things in nature.

But look again. Some fruit has brown spots. Caterpillars and snails have nibbled holes in the leaves. Ants are dragging a dead fly. The artist is showing us that nothing lasts forever. Even the most beautiful things will fade and decay.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a bunch of amazing animals from all over the world. You can see two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa, and a purple-naped lory from Indonesia (that one is on a chain in the bottom left corner). The artist, Hondecoeter, mixed these creatures with other beautiful birds to create this scene. The painting was made for Het Loo, the palace of King William III, and it hung right above the door of his private room.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a wide, sunny river scene. The faraway parts look a little hazy, and the clouds are painted quickly, like a sketch. Two Dutch soldiers stop by the river to let their horses drink. Nearby, a shepherd is resting with his cows. Even though this looks like a peaceful Italian countryside, it’s actually a view of the Waal River, between the cities of Nijmegen and Cleves. The artist, Cuyp, made drawings of this area and brought them back to his studio in Dordrecht.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
persona1_adapted:
**Winter Fun on the Ice**

This painting shows a winter day in a village where the frozen water has turned into a giant playground. People are skating, playing games, and even bowling on the ice. But watch out—on the left side, two people have fallen through! In the background, you can see a drawbridge and a windmill.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
persona1_adapted: This dollhouse is incredibly lifelike. Everything inside is made from real materials, and all the sizes are perfectly to scale. The fancy cabinet, made from tortoiseshell and decorated with pewter, was built by a French cabinetmaker who worked in Amsterdam for a few years. The dollhouse belonged to Petronella Oortman, who was married to a wealthy Amsterdam merchant named Johannes Brandt.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
persona1_adapted: This little painting was made on a copper panel. Because copper is very smooth, the artist could paint every tiny detail with care. The artist, Van Kessel, made lots of these small copper paintings of bugs and other animals. He would then put them together to create a bigger scene. People might have also used these little paintings to decorate a special cabinet filled with cool and curious objects.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
persona1_adapted:
**June 18, 1648 – A Big Party in Amsterdam**

A big celebration was happening at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. Why? Because a peace treaty had just been signed, ending a long war with Spain. The leaders of the city’s guard shook hands to show there was peace. A special drinking horn was passed around for everyone to share. And a poem on a drum said how happy the soldiers were that they could finally put down their weapons.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona1_adapted:
**Fishing for Souls: A Story of Rivalry During a Time of Peace**

This painting is like a puzzle. It shows a scene from a time called the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621), when the Dutch Republic and Spain paused their long war. But even though they weren't fighting, there was still a lot of tension—especially about religion.

In the picture, you see a river with people on both sides. There are boats on the water, and a rainbow stretches across the sky. But this isn't just a pretty landscape. Every part of the painting has a hidden meaning.

**On the left side** are the **Protestants** (a branch of Christianity). They have powerful friends, like the Princes of Orange, the King of England, and the young King of France. In the big boat in front, Protestant fishermen are casting nets. Their nets have labels that say "Faith," "Hope," and "Love"—important ideas for them.

**On the right side** are the **Catholics** (another branch of Christianity). Their supporters include the rulers of the Spanish Netherlands, a famous general, and even the Pope (carried by church leaders). In their small boat, priests are fishing too, led by a bishop.

So what's the point? The "fishing" is a metaphor. Both sides are trying to "catch" people's souls—to win them over to their religion. The painting shows how jealous and competitive these groups were, even during a time of peace. It's a snapshot of a world divided by belief, but also a reminder that people have always argued about what's true.

CuratorialText: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, a thistle; on the right, ivy. On the right in the background, elegant couples are walking in a garden with a fountain at a country house.
persona1_adapted: This is a wedding portrait of Isaac Massa and Beatrix van der Laen. They got married in Haarlem, Netherlands, on April 25, 1622. In the painting, the couple is sitting on a raised platform under a tree. On the left side, you can see a thistle plant. On the right, there is ivy. In the background on the right, fancy couples are walking through a garden next to a country house with a fountain.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and the tips of the wings are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries lies beneath the right leg. The figure is unmarked.
persona1_adapted: This small statue is made of painted porcelain. It shows a parrot (a macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head, and a white body. Its tail and wing tips are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries sits under its right leg. The statue has no maker's mark.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona1_adapted:
This kimono was made for a young, unmarried woman. It’s called a *furisode*, which means “swinging sleeves.” The sleeves are extra long and loose.

The whole kimono is covered with over 200 flying cranes. In Japanese culture, cranes are a symbol of good luck and long life.

The kimono is made of black silk crepe, a fabric with a bumpy texture. The white, red, and gray crane designs were created using a special dyeing technique called *yuzen*. Tiny bits of shiny gold foil add sparkle.

The inside is lined with red silk. On the back, there is a single bamboo crest, which was the family symbol of the woman who owned it.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers. A bouquet of flowers in a metal vase standing in a niche. The bouquet contains roses, irises, poppies, a wheat stalk, tulips, and other flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and over the niche. Pendant of SK-A-337.
persona1_adapted:
**Still Life with Flowers**

This painting shows a big bunch of flowers in a metal vase, sitting inside a small, window-like space called a niche. The bouquet is packed with different kinds of blooms—roses, irises, poppies, tulips, and even a stalk of wheat. But look closely! Among the petals and leaves, you’ll spot a snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawling around. This artwork is a matching piece to another painting in the museum (SK-A-337).

CuratorialText: Mary Magdalene sitting under a tree with the ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rugged rock formations.
persona1_adapted: Mary Magdalene sits beneath a tree, holding a jar of ointment in her lap. She is surrounded by a landscape of mountains and rocky cliffs.

CuratorialText: Portrait of the twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing full-length on a checkered tile floor in an undefined space. He is dressed in an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters, wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows Marten Soolmans when he was twenty years old. He stands on a floor with black-and-white checkered tiles, but the room around him is not clearly shown. Marten wears a fancy black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters. He also has a black hat and shoes decorated with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.

CuratorialText: Portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing full-length on a tiled floor in an otherwise undefined space. She is dressed in an expensive black dress, lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan of black ostrich feathers.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows Oopjen Coppit when she was 23 years old. She stands on a tiled floor, but there’s no background—just an empty space. She’s wearing a fancy black dress with lace around her collar and cuffs, plus a black veil. She also has expensive jewelry on. In her right hand, she holds a fan made of black ostrich feathers.

CuratorialText: Still life with a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver holder on which stands a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters and a roll.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a fancy table setting. You can see a golden cup, a silver stand holding a Chinese plate, and lots of food: an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters, and a bread roll.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, in Turkish costume. Full-length, seated on a divan. Her head resting on her right hand. On the divan lie a book and a basket containing a mirror, comb, and bonnet. On the floor are an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations
persona1_adapted: This painting shows Marie Fargues, the artist’s wife, dressed in a Turkish costume. She is sitting on a couch, resting her head on her right hand. Next to her on the couch are a book and a basket holding a mirror, a comb, and a bonnet. On the floor, there’s an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations.

CuratorialText: A cheerful violinist holding a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask filled with wine; in his left hand, a violin with a bow. On his head, a feathered hat.
persona1_adapted: A happy violinist holds a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window that's covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask full of wine. In his left hand, he holds a violin and a bow. On his head, he wears a hat with a feather.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona1_adapted:
**A View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil**

This painting shows the Brazilian town of Olinda when the Dutch controlled it. On the left, you can see a hill with a group of people standing near the ruins of a church. In the front of the painting, the artist included lots of tropical plants, fruits, and animals you’d find in the region—like an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard.

CuratorialText: Winter landscape. View of a wide, snow-covered lane leading past a forest. In the foreground, three boys with sleds. Further back, a wagon drawn by two horses stands on the road in front of a farm.
persona1_adapted: This is a winter scene. A wide, snowy road stretches through a forest. In the front, three boys are pulling sleds. Farther down the road, a horse-drawn wagon is stopped in front of a farm.

CuratorialText: Italian landscape with draftsman. A road through the landscape with a bridge over a mountain stream. On the road, a man with a mule. In the foreground, some seated figures.
persona1_adapted:
**Italian Landscape with a Draftsman**

Imagine walking down a dusty road in the Italian countryside. You pass a stone bridge that crosses a rushing mountain stream. A man leads a mule along the path, and in the front of the scene, a few people are sitting down, maybe resting or sketching the view.

This painting shows a peaceful, everyday moment in nature.

CuratorialText: Still life with turkey pie. Table with a white damask tablecloth on which stand pewter plates with a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and olives (?). Also a pewter pitcher, roemer, knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Wan Li bowl with fruit. In the background stands a large pie on which a turkey has been placed.
persona1_adapted:
**Still Life with Turkey Pie**

Imagine a fancy dinner table from hundreds of years ago. This painting shows a table covered with a crisp white cloth. On it, you can see:

- Pewter plates (like old-fashioned metal dishes) holding a pie, oysters, lemon, salt, pepper, and maybe some olives  
- A pewter pitcher and a special wine glass called a roemer  
- A knife, nuts, bread rolls, and a fancy cup made from a nautilus shell  
- A beautiful bowl from China (a Wan Li bowl) filled with fruit  

In the back of the scene, there’s a big pie with a whole turkey sitting on top. Everything is arranged to show off the wealth and taste of the person who owned these items.

CuratorialText: The stone bridge. Landscape with a stone bridge over a river or canal. On the water are two small boats with figures. Various people are walking on the road, a wagon on the left. In the middle, a hay barn between trees, a church tower in the distance on the right.
persona1_adapted:
**The Stone Bridge**

This painting shows a peaceful countryside scene. A stone bridge crosses a river or canal. Two small boats with people float on the water. On the road, several people are walking, and there’s a wagon on the left side. In the middle of the picture, you can see a hay barn hidden between trees. Far away on the right, a church tower rises in the distance.

CuratorialText: A stone bust of Flora in an ornamental cartouche surrounded by a festoon of various types of flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.
persona1_adapted: This stone carving shows the Roman goddess of flowers, Flora. Her portrait sits inside a fancy frame called a cartouche. All around her is a beautiful garland made of different flowers, like tulips, roses, and ivy.

CuratorialText: The overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, knocks over a vase containing a bouquet of flowers. In the bouquet: tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying among the flowers are butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails.
persona1_adapted:
**The Overturned Bouquet**

A cat is playing with a mousetrap. It bumps into a vase full of flowers, and the whole thing tips over. The bouquet has tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying around the flowers are butterflies, a dragonfly, a spider, and even some snails.

CuratorialText: The Fall of Man. Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Surrounding the figures are various animals: monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, Adam and Eve are warned by God not to eat from the tree.
persona1_adapted:
**The Fall of Man**

Adam and Eve stand in front of the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Around them are many animals—a monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, God warns Adam and Eve not to eat fruit from the tree.

CuratorialText: View of an orchard with plum blossoms, seen through the branches of a plum tree in the foreground; picnicking visitors in the background; part of the entrance gate on the left in the foreground; against a bright red sky.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a view of an orchard full of plum trees in bloom. In the front, you see the branches of a plum tree up close. In the background, people are having a picnic. On the left side, part of the entrance gate is visible. The sky above is a bright red.
