CuratorialText: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
persona1_adapted: This painting uses a technique called pointillism, where the artist creates the image with tiny dots of color instead of brushstrokes. A woman sits on the right side of the painting, facing us as she reads a book by the light of a kerosene lamp. She wears a pink gown—a style known as "reform clothing," which was a simpler, more comfortable type of dress for women at the time. Her hair is pulled back in a neat updo. On the left, you can see part of the kerosene lamp on a small table covered with a floral cloth. Next to the lamp is a tiny vase of roses. In the background, a large vase of flowers blends into the pattern of the wallpaper, making it hard to tell where the vase ends and the wall design begins.

CuratorialText: Kitten game. A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. There is also an ashtray with a cigar on the table.
persona1_adapted:
**A Cat Playing with Dominoes**

A tiny kitten is having fun on a table, batting around some dominoes. There's also an ashtray with a cigar sitting nearby.

CuratorialText: Interior in which a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas. In the center, a girl holding a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy in her arms. On the left, a crying boy who has received a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother with outstretched arms stands a laughing boy with a club. On the floor is a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a family celebrating Saint Nicholas Day, a fun holiday where kids get gifts and treats. In the middle, a girl holds a doll of John the Baptist and a bucket of candy. On the left, a boy is crying because he got a rod (a switch) in his shoe instead of a present. Next to their mom, who has her arms out, a laughing boy holds a club. On the floor, there’s a basket of cakes and rolls. By the fireplace, some kids are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman are watching.

CuratorialText: Jeremiah mourning over the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on the rocky slope of a mountain, his head resting on his left hand. On a stone beside him lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books, sanctuaries from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers can be seen.
persona1_adapted: Jeremiah is sad, looking at the ruined city of Jerusalem. He sits on a rocky hillside, resting his head on his hand. Next to him on a stone are a golden bowl, a pitcher, and some books—things saved from the destroyed temple. Down below on the left, you can see the city’s buildings on fire and soldiers walking around.

CuratorialText: Still life with fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. On a stone table, a silver plate with oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an inverted roemer, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. Also snails and insects.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a fancy snack table. On a stone tabletop, there's a silver plate piled with oysters and fruit. Next to it sits a delicate porcelain bowl, an upside-down wine glass, and a small, glowing candle wick. A bunch of grapes rests on a wooden box. Look closely—you can also spot snails and tiny insects crawling around.

CuratorialText: Painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug'. River landscape with the Trekvliet near Rijswijk, with De Binckhorst Castle in the distance on the left and the Laakmolen on the right. A woman with children stands on the road to the farm, and boats are sailing on the water.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting is called *View at the Geestbrug*. It shows a river scene near Rijswijk, in the Netherlands. On the left, you can see De Binckhorst Castle far away in the background. On the right is the Laakmolen, a windmill. A woman and her children are standing on a road that leads to a farm. Meanwhile, boats are sailing on the water.

CuratorialText: The well-stocked kitchen, with Jesus with Martha and Mary in the background. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, the other is skewering a chicken. To the left, others are sitting by the hearth. In the foreground, all kinds of game and poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. Among these are also various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a busy kitchen full of food and activity. In the background, you can see a scene from the Bible: Jesus visiting with Martha and Mary. Up close, two maids are hard at work. One is plucking feathers from a chicken, and the other is putting a chicken on a skewer to cook. On the left side, more people are sitting by the fireplace. In the front of the painting, there’s a huge spread of food—different kinds of meat, birds, vegetables, and fruits. You can also spot glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates mixed in with all the ingredients.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers and fruit. On a plinth stands a stone vase with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the plinth is a glass, and lying are a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket handing a child a flower wreath.
persona1_adapted: This painting is called *Still Life with Flowers and Fruit*. It shows a stone vase sitting on a shelf, filled with colorful flowers, fruit, and twisting vines. Next to the vase, there’s a glass, and on the shelf you can see a pineapple, some peaches, grapes, and a single rose. In the background, a painting on the wall shows a woman with a flower basket, reaching down to give a flower wreath to a child.

CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
persona1_adapted: Six warships sit on a calm, wide sea. Sailors are raising the sails and lifting the anchors. On the front-right ship, the back is decorated with Amsterdam's coat of arms, held up by two lions standing on their hind legs. This scene probably shows a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat carrying important people—likely from the Admiralty—sails past the ships while trumpets play and cannons fire in salute. A little farther away, the last supplies and crew members are still being loaded. The artist paid close attention to every tiny detail, like the ropes, sails, and cannons, making them look incredibly real.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a fancy bronze vase holding a colorful bouquet of tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. But look closely at the table. There’s also a dead frog, a small bunch of berries, and a caterpillar resting nearby.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
persona1_adapted:
**Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516)**  
This is a portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo, an architect who lived in Italy about 500 years ago. He was the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo.  

In the painting, Giuliano is shown from the chest up, facing to the right. Behind him, you can see a landscape. At the bottom, on a small shelf, there are two tools: a compass and a pen. These tools tell us that he was a designer and builder.  

This painting is one half of a two-part artwork called a diptych. The other half is labeled SK-C-1367.

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows an old woman reading a large book on her lap. It’s probably Rembrandt’s mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck, who died in 1640. She might be dressed up as the prophetess Anna, a wise woman from the Bible.

CuratorialText: Allegorical musical group. On the left, a man in Oriental clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head looks on. In the foreground lie musical instruments (lute, violin) and stacks of books. On the wall is a painting of Lot's Flight from Sodom.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a group of people making music together, but it’s also meant to tell a bigger story. On the left, a man wearing a turban and fancy clothes from faraway lands plays a viola da gamba—an old-fashioned string instrument like a cello. Next to him, a young man holds a harp. A young woman sits nearby with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an older woman with a cloth over her head watches quietly. In the front of the painting, you can see a lute and a violin lying on the floor, along with stacks of books. On the wall behind them hangs a painting that shows a famous Bible story: Lot and his family fleeing the city of Sodom.

CuratorialText: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to prevent Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's quiver hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.
persona1_adapted:
**Venus and Adonis**

In this painting, the goddess Venus and her son Cupid are trying to stop a young man named Adonis from going hunting. Venus is only half-dressed, showing she’s trying to get his attention. Cupid, the little god of love, is also there to help convince him to stay.

In the bottom right corner, you can see two white doves, which are symbols of love. Adonis’s quiver (the bag that holds his arrows) is hanging from a tree branch. He’s ready to go, and his two hunting dogs are right there with him, waiting to join the hunt.

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
persona1_adapted:
**The Cannon Shot**

A warship sits quietly on calm water, its sails hanging loose. Suddenly, it fires a cannon! Two small boats float nearby, one on each side. In the distance, you can see another warship with its sails rolled up. This painting is a companion piece to another one called SK-A-1848.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona1_adapted: When you hear the word "Sufi," you might picture Turkish whirling dervishes spinning in a beautiful, trance-like dance. But outside of Islamic mysticism, most people don't know much about the beliefs and traditions behind these ceremonies—or about the communities that practice them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona1_adapted:
This display explores Sufi life and art through objects from Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement that began in the Islamic world around the 700s AD. It is a path for people to have a personal experience with God.

The objects on display come from the British Museum and include Islamic artifacts with Sufi meanings. Some items, like alms bowls, are clearly linked to dervishes (Sufi followers who often live simply). Others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphy, miniature paintings, and wall tiles.

Sufis come from all kinds of backgrounds—different classes, ethnicities, and regions. This display looks at themes like mystical love, poverty, and self-denial, as well as how Sufism has influenced the larger societies around them.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
persona1_adapted:
**Hawaiʻi: A Kingdom Crossing Oceans**

This exhibition is a stunning look at the art and history of Hawaiʻi. You’ll see amazing objects—like feather cloaks worn by chiefs, finely carved statues of gods, powerful weapons made with shark teeth, and bold new works by Native Hawaiian artists (called Kānaka ʻŌiwi).

In 1824, two young royals—King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu—traveled across the ocean on a trip that would change their kingdom forever. This exhibition marks over 200 years since that fateful royal visit. It explores the deep and complicated relationship between Hawaiʻi and Britain.

The show was created with the help of Native Hawaiian artists, experts, and scholars. It puts Indigenous knowledge at the center, telling fresh stories about incredible objects. You’ll see rare items on loan from around the world, plus treasures from the British Museum’s own collection—one of the largest outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This exhibit features prints by Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006), a Scottish poet, writer, artist, and gardener. His work explores the idea of revolution and how it connects to modern society.

Finlay first became known for his poetry in the late 1950s. But by the 1960s, he was also making prints and three-dimensional artworks out of stone, metal, neon, and glass. He often worked with skilled craftspeople to create these pieces.

Finlay was also a dedicated gardener. He built a special "conceptual garden" at his remote home near the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. Many of his artworks were placed in the garden. He named it **Little Sparta**, after the ancient Greek city that was a military rival to Athens. The name shows his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
persona1_adapted: In the 1980s, artist Ian Hamilton Finlay became really interested in the French Revolution. He was fascinated by big changes and sudden breaks in history, and how good ideas could sometimes lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints you see here were made between 1984 and 1992. They are part of Finlay's work about the French Revolution. These prints come from a group of 26 that were donated in 2025 by Professor Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written a lot about Finlay and was his friend for many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona1_adapted: Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) was an artist whose career spanned from the 1760s to the 1830s. During that time, he lived through some of the most dramatic events in French history. Wille studied under the famous painter Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805). He became known by copying the kinds of everyday life scenes that made Greuze popular in the 1760s art shows. Wille had a gentle, delicate style that fit perfectly with the tastes of the French royal court before the revolution.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
persona1_adapted:
Johann Georg Wille supported the French Revolution. He even joined the republican army and spent his time writing about the famous people of his day. But in the end, the Revolution turned out to be more of a problem than a good thing for him.

When he was young, Wille tried many different art styles. As he got older, he had a hard time changing to the simpler, more serious taste that became popular in France under Napoleon. By the time he died, he had been poor for a long time.

During his life and after, Wille was better known for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been collecting his work since the 1800s, and its collection recently doubled in size thanks to a gift from the art dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020).

This gift included some of Wille's best finished drawings. One of the most powerful is a portrait that is said to show Charlotte Corday, the "Angel of Assassination." She was drawn in her prison cell just days or even hours before she was executed.

This celebration of Clark's gift shows Wille's world through his drawings and those of his friends. It reveals that he was a very talented artist whose works give us a fascinating look at French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
persona1_adapted:
**The Asante Ewer: A Jug with Many Lives**

This fancy jug, called the Asante Ewer, was made in England a long time ago, between 1340 and 1405. But it didn't stay in England. It traveled all the way to West Africa.

In the 1880s, someone took a photo of it sitting in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asante king (called the Asantehene) in Kumasi, which is in present-day Ghana. The jug was placed in the middle of the courtyard, near what looks like a shrine, with its base tangled in the roots of sacred trees. This suggests it was a very important object for ceremonies or rituals.

Then, in 1896, during a war between the British and the Asante people (the Anglo-Asante War), British soldiers stole the jug. It was later bought by the British Museum. Another matching jug, also stolen in that same war and shown in the same photo, was given to a British army regiment.

Now, for the first time in over a hundred years, these two jugs are back together in this display.

A big new research project is studying the Asante Ewer, which is one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze work. Researchers want to learn more about how it got to Ghana, what it meant to the people there, and why it was so special.

The Asante Ewer has had a wild journey. It started as a fancy English jug, then became a sacred West African vessel, and finally ended up as war loot taken by British troops. It's amazing that it survived the long trip from Europe to Africa and back again.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
persona1_adapted: This display is part of a three-year research project called *The Age of Copper, Ivory and Gold*. The project was supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation. It looks at the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and the Early Modern period. To make this display possible, we worked closely with partners at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, the York Army Museum, and many other groups in Ghana, the US, and the UK.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona1_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries (an area that includes modern-day Belgium and the Netherlands) were a major center for new ideas in art. During this time, drawings changed a lot. At first, artists only used them to plan bigger paintings. But soon, people started seeing drawings as art all by themselves.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition has about 120 drawings by famous artists like Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder, and Hendrick Goltzius. It shows how drawing developed in the Low Countries—what we now call Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands—before the year 1600.

The British Museum has a huge and amazing collection of these drawings. That’s why we can show you such a complete picture of art from this region.

Early Netherlandish drawings are very rare. Why? Because back then, artists used drawings as tools in their workshops. They weren’t meant to be kept or collected. This was different from Italy or German-speaking areas, where people started collecting drawings in the 1500s. That’s why more drawings survived there.

Drawings were super important for making all kinds of art: tapestries, paintings, stained glass, and prints. The ones that survive today help us see how creative and collaborative Netherlandish artists really were.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is based on a research project funded by the International Music and Art Foundation. It shows new discoveries made by combining art history, conservation, and science. For example, experts studied drawings that were once only linked to the artist Rogier van der Weyden and his followers. New research now shows that these drawings actually came from Rogier’s own workshop. They were likely made by his students as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
persona1_adapted: **Be one of the first to see the plans for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.**

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
persona1_adapted:
A new memorial for Queen Elizabeth II is coming to St. James’ Park in London. It will feature a 10-foot-tall bronze statue of her as a young queen, standing on a 14-foot base. The memorial honors her lifelong dedication to public service and is expected to be finished in 2028.

The statue is being created by famous sculptor Martin Jennings. It will show the Queen early in her reign, wearing the special robes of the Order of the Garter. Jennings is using a well-known painting by Pietro Annigoni as his inspiration.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
persona1_adapted:
Mr. Jennings will also make a statue of Prince Philip, the husband of Queen Elizabeth II who served as her partner longer than anyone else in that role. The statue will show him at a similar age, wearing his Admiral-of-the-Fleet uniform. It will stand near Queen Elizabeth's statue to honor the support he gave her during her 70 years as queen.

Other parts of the memorial were designed by Foster + Partners, working with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. At the British Museum, you can see small models of the statues and a scale model of the whole memorial park.

The King, Queen, other royal family members, and the Prime Minister saw the designs at the Museum on April 21, which was the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth.

The permanent memorial will also include:
- A new bridge made of cast glass, inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara (the tiara Queen Elizabeth wore on her wedding day)
- A bust (sculpture of the head and shoulders) of the Queen in her later years, made by artist Karen Newman
- The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract artwork by Yinka Shonibare

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
persona1_adapted: The memorial also has gardens for the Commonwealth and the nations of the UK, giving people quiet places to relax and think. It was created with help from the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee, after a design contest last year. Besides the park, the committee suggested starting a charity to improve community spaces across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial where people can share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona1_adapted:
**What Was Life and Death Like in Ancient Egypt?**

Step into the world of ancient Egypt and discover how people lived, died, and what they hoped for after death. This tour will show you amazing objects like painted coffins, jewelry called amulets, small statues called shabtis, and models of daily life. You’ll also see giant wall paintings and ancient scrolls called papyri.

We’ll explore what tombs, bodies, and burial items can teach us about the lives of ancient Egyptians—and what they believed happened after they died. You’ll learn how they prepared a body for burial, including the process of mummification. You’ll also see how they filled tombs with things people might need for a peaceful, eternal rest, and how they used magic to help guide and protect the dead on their dangerous journey to the afterlife.

This tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum treats these remains with care, respect, and dignity. If you want to learn more about how we handle and study human remains, you can ask for more information.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
persona1_adapted:
**Step into Ancient Egypt!**

Come explore one of the coolest rooms in the Museum before anyone else gets in. In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you'll see giant statues of kings and gods, huge tomb buildings, and ancient carvings from tombs that are over 3,000 years old.

Check out the massive bust of Ramesses II—it's a giant stone head of a famous pharaoh. You'll also see the Gayer-Anderson Cat, a sleek statue of a cat goddess. On this special guided tour, you'll learn why ancient Egyptians made such big sculptures for their temples and tombs—and what they tell us about life and death in ancient Egypt.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
persona1_adapted:
The British Museum opened in 1759, just six years after it was founded in 1753. It tells the story of human culture through many different collections of objects.

This room celebrates some of the collectors who helped shape the Museum over the past 400 years. It also honors people and groups who are still helping to shape its future today.

The rooms next door also explore collecting. **Room 1: Enlightenment** shows how, in the Museum’s early days, people gathered and sorted objects and knowledge. **Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest** displays a collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces. These were left to the Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild when he died in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona1_adapted:
The British Museum raised £3.5 million to save a special piece of jewelry called the Tudor Heart Pendant. Now, it will stay on display for everyone to see for many years to come.

In December 2019, someone using a metal detector in Warwickshire found this amazing heart-shaped jewel buried underground. It had been resting there for hundreds of years. The pendant is linked to King Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon.

This pendant is one of a kind—there’s nothing like it in the British Museum or anywhere else in the UK. It’s a beautifully made jewel with colorful enamel designs. It hangs from a 75-link gold chain, and the clasp is shaped like a hand coming out of a cloud.

Very few objects from Henry and Katherine’s life together still exist. That’s because their marriage was ended (annulled) in 1533. Katherine was a devoted Catholic, and Henry wanted to marry someone else. Still, their marriage was Henry’s longest—it lasted 24 years and shaped the first 30 years of his time as king.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
persona1_adapted:
Back in the 1700s, the museum’s collection was split into two groups: “Natural Rarities” (things from nature) and “Artificial Rarities” (things made by people). At first, only a few rooms had human-made objects. Most of the space was filled with natural items from Sir Hans Sloane’s collection, plus his giant library—which included 265 volumes of pressed plants called a herbarium.

In 1735, a Swedish scientist named Carl Linnaeus changed the way people named and sorted plants, animals, and other natural objects. One of his students, Daniel Solander, worked at the museum. He used Linnaeus’s system to organize Sloane’s collection, as well as the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on Captain Cook’s first voyage. Many of those original specimens—and a bunch of fossils—are still in the exhibition today.

By 1880, there were so many natural history specimens that they needed their own building. That’s how the Natural History Museum in South Kensington was born.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona1_adapted:
**What Were Antiquaries?**  

Back in the 1600s and 1700s, people called *antiquaries* were like history detectives. They collected old books, handwritten papers, drawings, and objects from the past. They also traveled around the country to study ruins—especially from Roman times and even earlier.  

It was pretty easy to learn about the Middle Ages (medieval times) because a lot of stuff from that period had survived. Things like religious objects, armor, books, and buildings were still around.  

But antiquaries didn’t stop there. They started to study even older places, like Stonehenge, in a more scientific way. They made maps and surveys of Roman and prehistoric sites. This helped them learn more about the very first people who lived in Britain.  

At the same time, scientists were studying rock layers (called *strata*). When you put together what the antiquaries found and what the rock layers showed, some big questions came up. For example, many people at the time believed the world was created in 4004 BC—based on the Bible. But the new evidence made archaeologists wonder if that date was really correct.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona1_adapted:
In 1824, the British Museum received a famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures. They had been collected by a man named Charles Townley during his travels in Italy. These sculptures joined other ancient objects already at the museum, including prints, drawings, coins, vases, and bronzes. These items came from other collectors, like Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight.

These collectors studied each other’s collections to understand how art had developed over time. They believed that art started out simple and “primitive” in early civilizations, and then reached its peak in the sculpture and architecture of ancient Greece. For them, Greek art was the best art ever made.

This idea became the standard for judging all art. It led to a revival of classical styles in British buildings and decorative arts in the late 1700s.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona1_adapted:
**How to Make Sense of a Giant Collection**

In the 1700s, people collected tons of stuff—from coins to vases to weird tools. But if you just piled it all up, it wouldn’t make much sense. So collectors had to organize everything.

**King George III’s Library**
King George III had a huge library. Later, his son, George IV, gave it to the nation. It was kept in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (the books themselves are now in the British Library). The coins and medals from the king’s collection were some of the first things to arrive at the museum. They were sorted by country and ruler, so you could see history come alive.

**George III’s Other Interests**
The king was also really into the navy, navigation, and science. He collected instruments and working models of ships and tools.

**Sir William Hamilton’s Vases**
A man named Sir William Hamilton found ancient vases near Naples, Italy. He arranged them by the style of painting on them. This helped him figure out which ones were older and which were newer. Later, he published pictures of them in fancy books.

**Sir Hans Sloane’s Cabinets**
Earlier in the 1700s, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious manmade objects from around the world. Some of these came from enslaved people in Jamaica. He cataloged and displayed them in different ways—by type, material, date, or what they were used for.

**The Big Idea**
All this organizing helped people understand the past and the world around them.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona1_adapted: During the Enlightenment, people became really curious about ancient writing. They wanted to understand mysterious symbols like Egyptian hieroglyphs, the wedge-shaped writing called cuneiform from the Bible lands, and early forms of Sanskrit from India. In the 1800s, scholars finally "cracked the codes" and unlocked thousands of years of history. At the same time, experts figured out runes and other old writing systems, revealing more about our own past. This was also a time when many ancient objects—gathered from collectors, diplomats, and digs—came to the British Museum.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona1_adapted:
For a long time, many Christians were suspicious of other religions. They saw things like rituals, magic, and statues as superstition or idol worship. This made people ignore or misunderstand other faiths.

Later, during a period called the Enlightenment, scholars tried to change that. They began studying both ancient and modern religions from around the world. They looked at different cultures’ rituals and beliefs, collected religious objects, and grouped gods into families. They also studied what each god did and what they stood for.

Their goal was to find similarities between religions. They hoped this would help explain where the world’s religions came from. But it’s important to know that much of this research happened while European countries were taking over other lands, a time known as colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona1_adapted:
During the time of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats collected objects from different cultures around the world. These objects included both everyday items and special ceremonial pieces. People thought these items showed what life and customs were like in other countries.

Many of these collectors wrote books and made drawings about the people and cultures they saw. But they described everything from a European point of view, not from the perspective of the people they were writing about.

The British Museum has objects from Sir Hans Sloane's collection. It also holds items brought back by Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks from their voyages to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands.

Many of the objects in the museum came from people who worked for the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company. These companies became wealthy by using enslaved people and by taking advantage of other people and their resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona1_adapted:
**Alexander the Great and the Hellenistic Age**

Alexander the Great was a famous leader who ruled from 336 to 323 BC. During that time, he conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. He also founded more than 70 cities. His empire stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles—that’s huge!

After Alexander died in 323 BC, his empire was split into smaller kingdoms ruled by his generals. This time period, called the **Hellenistic period**, lasted until 31 BC. That’s when Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony were defeated at a battle called Actium.

The new kingdoms were led by powerful families called dynasties. They built big buildings and supported art and culture. In Room 22 of the museum, you can see some amazing objects from this time. For example, there’s a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos. That temple was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World! You can also see a statue called the Demeter from Knidos in Turkey, and Greek bronzes.

Smaller objects on display show how skilled ancient artists were. You’ll find gold jewelry, metalwork, pottery, clay figurines, and carved gems. These items were made by goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, and gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is a stunning look at the art and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together incredible objects—from feather cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved statues, to powerful weapons made with shark teeth and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu sailed across the ocean on a trip that would change the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition honors over 200 years since that royal visit and explores Hawaiʻi's history and culture by looking at the deep and lasting connection between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition is a stunning celebration of the art and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together amazing objects—like feather cloaks worn by chiefs, finely carved statues of gods, powerful weapons made with shark teeth, and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean on a journey that would change the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition honors over 200 years since that royal visit—a trip that ended in tragedy—and explores Hawaiʻi’s history and culture by looking at the deep, complicated relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona1_adapted:
This fashion show features modern clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great Pacific Ocean). It was organized in the UK by the Interisland Collective and put together by Lyall Hakaraia, a Māori designer. Lyall created the collection with help from respected experts across the Pacific. These experts are called *tohunga*—people who build and share Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides and try to be "good ancestors" through their daily actions, strong intentions, and vision. Their goal is to create a strong future for *mokopuna* (grandchildren) and generations to come.

The show will be a special gathering for the Pacific diaspora community. It will open a *VĀ* (a portal) to their ancestors. The show is a gift to those ancestors whose spirits remain at the British Museum, represented by Pacific objects in the collection. The hope is to "warm" them as they rest. During the show, the group will sing, chant, and offer prayers to them with *Aroha* (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona1_adapted: This gallery shows the history of China, from 5000 BC all the way to today. You’ll see famous blue-and-white porcelain from the Ming dynasty, delicate handscrolls, amazing Tang dynasty tomb figurines, and even modern art. The displays are full of beautiful objects from China’s past and present, including paintings, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer, and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona1_adapted: This part of the gallery shows the long history of South Asia, organized by time and place—from the first humans who lived there all the way to today. Some of the coolest things to see are ancient seals from the Indus civilization, amazing sculptures of the god Shiva from southern India, and one of the best statues of the goddess Tara ever made in Sri Lanka. You’ll also find fancy paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors, plus 20th-century paintings by famous artists, including Rabindranath Tagore, who won a Nobel Prize.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona1_adapted:
China is one of the oldest civilizations in the world. Today, it covers a huge area about the size of Europe and is home to about one-fourth of all people on Earth. China has a very unique culture, and people there have been making beautiful objects in large numbers since ancient times.

Room 33 looks at 7,000 years of Chinese history. It explores topics like writing systems, religious rituals, beliefs, war, and international trade.

Room 33 also looks at South Asia’s history, from 1.5 million years ago up to today. It examines themes like trade, exchange, migration, and political power. South Asia includes countries like India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. People in this region speak many ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. Religions like Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, and Islam have inspired amazing buildings, sculptures, paintings, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona1_adapted:
**Africa at the Museum**

The British Museum has lots of amazing objects from Africa. You can find them in Room 25 (the Africa gallery), the Sainsbury Galleries, and in other parts of the museum too. These objects show Africa’s rich history and cultures.

Want to learn more? Join one of our free tours! These tours were created with help from people in the community. Each tour guide picks their own favorite objects to show you. The objects come from ancient times all the way to today.

You can choose from these tour themes:

- **Out of Africa** – Stories about how people, materials, and myths moved across the world
- **The Igbo World** – Artifacts from the Igbo people of Nigeria, now at the British Museum
- **African Metal Art** – How metal objects were made, traded, and used in tradition
- **Ancient African Kingdoms** – Objects from powerful kingdoms long ago

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona1_adapted:
**Step into Spring Fun!**

Imagine stepping into the month of May with a super creative play session made just for kids ages five and under.

You’ll meet characters like May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures in this fun, open-ended activity. You get to lead the way! Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with different art supplies, and make crowns and masks to wear for spring celebrations.

We’ll work at your child’s pace, exploring textures, colors, and scents together using all kinds of cool materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona1_adapted:
Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704 to 681 BC. He was famous for his huge building projects. His palace in Nineveh, called the Southwest Palace, was decorated with detailed stone carvings. Many of these carvings are on display in Room 9.

The carvings show different scenes. One scene shows workers moving giant stone statues of human-headed winged bulls, called *lamassu*. These statues weighed up to 30 tons and were placed at the main entrances to the palace. The carvings give us a look at how ancient people cut and moved heavy stone. They also show how much Sennacherib cared about his building projects.

Other carvings in the room show the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.
persona1_adapted:
These three rooms show what life was like in ancient Assyria. You'll see stone carvings of lion hunts, a battle scene from the city of Lachish, and pictures of life inside the palace of King Sargon II.

When you walk into Room 10a, you'll be greeted by two giant stone bulls with wings and human heads. These creatures were meant to be magical guards. They stood watch at King Sargon's palace to scare away any evil spirits or bad luck.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona1_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was a sport only for kings. It showed that the king had the job of protecting and fighting for his people. The stone carvings in Room 10a tell the story of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (who ruled from 668 to 631 BC). These carvings were made for his palace in Nineveh, which is in modern-day northern Iraq. The hunting scenes are full of action and look very real. They are some of the best examples of Assyrian art. The carvings show the lions being let out, the chase, and then the kill.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Lachish was one of the most important cities in the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the ancient Middle East. In 701 BC, it was captured by the Assyrian king Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC). The Assyrian Empire was based in what is now northern Iraq.

The attack happened because Lachish refused to pay tribute—a kind of tax or payment—to the Assyrians. This event is even mentioned in the Bible.

In Room 10b, you can see many stone carvings (called reliefs) that show the capture of the city. There are also weapons and other items used during the siege. A special clay object called a "prism," which has an Assyrian story about the campaign written on it, is also on display.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona1_adapted:
**The Palace of King Sargon II**

In ancient times, a powerful king named Sargon II ruled the Assyrian Empire. He built a huge city and palace at a place called Khorsabad, which is in modern-day northern Iraq. This was around 2,700 years ago.

To protect his palace, Sargon placed giant statues at the entrances. These were massive stone creatures that looked like bulls but had the heads of humans and wings on their backs. They were meant to be magical guardians, watching over the palace and keeping evil away.

Today, you can see two of these amazing statues in Room 10c. They are very tall and impressive! Around them are stone carvings that show the king, his son (the crown prince), royal servants, and scenes of hunting.

Nearby, there are also some old stone writings (called inscriptions) that came from a similar winged bull statue. This one was from the palace of another Assyrian king, Sennacherib, who ruled after Sargon. His palace was in a city called Nineveh. These inscriptions are badly burned because the city was destroyed in a war around 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona1_adapted: Rooms 57–59 show objects from a region that was home to many different people over thousands of years. The story starts with the first farmers in the area, around 8,000 BC, and ends when the Neo-Babylonian Empire fell in 539 BC. The items on display help us understand the everyday life and traditions of the Canaanites, who lived in what is now Israel, Palestine, Lebanon, and Syria. These objects also show that both the Israelites and the Phoenicians came from the same Canaanite roots. In these rooms, you can compare the Canaanite way of life with that of other nearby groups, like the Amorites and Aramaeans from central Syria.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona1_adapted:
**Ancient Anatolia and Urartu: A Bridge Between Worlds**

Long ago, the lands of Anatolia and Urartu connected Europe and Asia. Today, these areas are part of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwest Iran.

In the Early Bronze Age (thousands of years ago), skilled artists made beautiful objects. You can see a silver bull and a silver cup from this time.

Later, during the Middle Bronze Age, merchants kept records of their business deals. These old documents show us how people traded between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq).

During the Hittite period, artists created delicate gold jewelry and small statues.

From the Iron Age, the Urartu people made amazing furniture decorations. Look for winged bulls and griffins—mythical creatures that were part animal, part bird.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition looked back more than 2,000 years to show how Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist sacred art began. It started with ancient nature spirits in India that people believed were very powerful. The show also followed how this art spread beyond India to other parts of the world.

It was one of the first big exhibitions to look at early Indian religious art from many faiths, modern times, and a global view. The exhibition highlighted what inspired the now-familiar images of gods and enlightened teachers in these world religions. It also showed how these ideas traveled across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is full of color, sound, and feeling. It was created with help from a group of people who practice Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism today. These religions and their sacred art are a big part of daily life for nearly two billion people around the world, including in the UK. Special objects loaned by our community partners helped us share this modern story.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition had over 180 objects—like sculptures, paintings, drawings, and old handwritten books. They came from the British Museum’s South Asian collection, plus special loans from museums in other countries. The show focused on where each object came from and how it ended up in a museum, telling the story of each piece from the time it was made.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This exhibition tells the ancient stories behind three living religions: Buddhism, Hinduism, and Jainism. You'll see how artists used symbols—like footprints to represent the Buddha before they showed him as a person. You'll also discover cosmic serpents in Hindu art and nature spirits that attend Jain holy teachers.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Reader:**
