This was the first big Hiroshige show at the British Museum in over 25 years. It gave a beautiful look at a country that was about to change forever.

Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) was born during a tough time in Japan’s history. But he became one of the country’s most talented, hardworking, and popular artists. As Japan started to deal with the outside world, Hiroshige’s calm art helped people feel safe and connected—no matter who they were.

His work showed all kinds of life in Japan back then: stylish people, busy city scenes, faraway landscapes, and nature. His bird-and-flower prints show a poetic love for nature. His landscapes capture the growing excitement about traveling across Japan.

Hiroshige painted his world sometimes exactly as it was, but often the way he dreamed it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona1_adapted: Hiroshige was an incredibly talented artist. He was great at using color and drawing, and he really cared about all kinds of people. Unlike most other printmakers of his time, he came from a samurai family, but he didn't stick to just one social group. Instead, he chose to show everyday life and customs of regular people. His art was also affordable. Besides his famous landscape prints, he made hundreds of hand-held fans that anyone could buy and use.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition has prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum. It also includes a special gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major U.S. collector of Hiroshige’s work, plus other important loans.

The show explores Hiroshige’s amazing art. It also looks at how his work influenced artists around the world—from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to Vincent van Gogh and modern artists like Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.
persona1_adapted:
This stone carving shows the Hindu gods Shiva and Parvati sitting together. Shiva has Parvati on his knee. This type of statue is also called "Umamahesvaramurti."

Around them, you can see a lion and a bull named Nandi. Many helpers float in the air or stand nearby. Some are musicians, and others carry flower garlands.

At the bottom, there is a row of special items used in ceremonies: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona1_adapted: These are a pair of special slippers called *tarkasin*, made for a bride to wear. They are made from red-dyed leather and decorated with colorful silk thread and metal studs. The front part of each slipper is cut into the shape of a *khamsa* (which means “five” in Arabic), also known as the “Hand of Fatima.” This shape is a symbol that is believed to bring good luck and protect the wearer.

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona1_adapted:
This is a giant statue of Ramses II, made from pink and gray granite. It was one of a pair that once stood in front of a temple called the Ramesseum. The statue shows the king’s head and upper body. He wears a royal headdress called a *nemes* and a crown of cobras (called *uraei*), though about half of the cobras are now missing.

The sculptor used the two colors of the stone on purpose. The gray part is the face, and the pink part is the body. This helps the face stand out. On the back pillar, there are rows of hieroglyphs carved into the stone. They tell the king’s name and titles, and part of a prayer to the god Amun-Ra.

In 1817, people noticed that there were still traces of paint on the statue. That means it was probably painted red long ago.

CuratorialText: Head from monumental red granite statue of Amenhotep III(?).
persona1_adapted: This is a giant head, carved from red granite. It might be a statue of the pharaoh Amenhotep III.

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona1_adapted:
**The Coffin of Hornedjitef**

This is the inner coffin of a man named Hornedjitef. He was a priest in ancient Egypt, which meant he had many important jobs in the temple. His father's name was Nekhthorheb.

The coffin is shaped like a person. The face, wig, and jewelry are painted in bright colors and covered with gold. On the chest, there's a winged scarab beetle—a symbol of protection and rebirth. The rest of the body is covered with rows of painted hieroglyphs (picture writing) and images of gods standing on either side.

The inside of the lid has a picture of Nut, the sky goddess, surrounded by stars and constellations. The bottom of the coffin is also decorated inside.

This coffin was meant to help Hornedjitef on his journey to the afterlife.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona1_adapted:
**A Royal Head from Long Ago**

This is a brass head, made using a special technique called "lost wax" (or *cire perdue* in French). It's a little smaller than a real human head, and it's made to look very natural and lifelike.

The head wears a fancy crown that has three main layers:

- **Top layer:** Four flat, round beads, each topped with a red-painted tube-shaped bead and a tassel.
- **Middle layer:** A row of vertical rectangles that look like tube-shaped beads with tassels.
- **Bottom layer:** A row of rosettes painted red.

Around the forehead, the crown has a curved part made of small tube-shaped beads, edged with red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The center of this cover has eighteen vertical lines that look like braided hair, with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides of the neck cover have a row of red-painted rosettes.

On top of the crown, at the front, there's a crest. It has a cone-shaped roundel with a central bump, surrounded by seven rings (probably beads). Behind the roundel, a braided element rises up and ends in a pointed oval tip. Both parts have traces of black paint.

The face is a bit long and has vertical lines (striations) carved into it. There are two lines of holes between the ears and the jaw, and another double line of holes across the upper lip. The eyes are almond-shaped and small compared to the head, with a sharp eyebrow ridge. The lips are smooth (no lines). There are grooves around the front of the neck that look like skin creases. There are also holes pierced through the neck at the front and both sides. Finally, there's a large, uneven hole on the right side of the jaw.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona1_adapted:
**Goddess Tara**  
This is a small statue of a goddess named Tara. She was made from bronze and covered with a thin layer of gold.  

Tara has a tall, tube-shaped hairdo held in place with a fancy medallion. On either side of the medallion are *makaras*—mythical water creatures. The medallion and Tara’s eyes were probably decorated with jewels at one time.  

Her earlobes are long and stretched. She wears a tight cloth tied at her hips, but her upper body is bare.  

Her right hand is held in a special pose called *varadamudra*, which means she is offering a gift or blessing. Her left hand is in *katakahastamudra*—the tips of her fingers touch her thumb to form a ring, like she’s holding a flower.  

Some parts of the statue are missing: the two middle fingers on her right hand and some toes from both feet.

CuratorialText:
The Virgin and child: cartoon; the Virgin half-length, Christ standing with his arms around his mother
Black chalk and/or charcoal, touches of white chalk, pricked and indented for transfer, on two conjoined sheets; framed
persona1_adapted:
**The Virgin and Child (A Drawing)**

This is a drawing of Mary and baby Jesus. Mary is shown from the waist up, and Jesus is standing with his arms around her.

The artist used black chalk and charcoal, with a little bit of white chalk for highlights. The drawing was made on two pieces of paper joined together. Tiny holes were pricked along the outlines so the image could be transferred to another surface (like a painting or a wall). It's now displayed in a frame.

CuratorialText: Marble torso of Nike, originally winged, wearing a sleeveless chiton; probably part of an akroterion.
persona1_adapted: This marble statue is the torso, or upper body, of Nike, the Greek goddess of victory. She originally had wings and wore a sleeveless tunic called a chiton. This piece was probably part of an **akroterion**—a decorative statue placed at the top or corner of a temple roof.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona1_adapted:
**A Marble Corner Piece with a Bird's Foot**

This is a small piece of a marble decoration that was once part of a larger building. It came from the corner of a fancy ornament called a "palmetto" (which looks like a palm leaf) that sat above a spiral-shaped part called a "volute."

On both sides of the middle leaf (which sticks out from the corner), you can see three gently curved, hollow-shaped leaves. Their edges have a raised line running alongside them. The rounded tips of the leaves were made to stand out with little bumps on the outside. Between the leaves, there are pointed shapes called "darts."

On the left side of this fragment, you can still see a bit of a curved molding from the spiral part. The top and back of the piece have a small, five-sided block with a tunnel-like hole that sticks out at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with a rounded back seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, you can see two claws with bird legs at the edges.

On the darts (the pointed shapes), traces of red paint were found on the top one and the third one down. There are also traces of red and blue paint in other spots.

The marble is light and sparkly, and its surface is fairly smooth, but the bottom is rough and broken.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona1_adapted:
This chess piece is a king carved from walrus ivory. He has a beard and wears a crown decorated with flowers. He is dressed in a tunic and a cloak, held together by a brooch. The king sits on a tall chair with a high back, holding a sword in its sheath.

The back of the chair has two sections separated by a zigzag band. The top section shows a row of connected round arches. The bottom section has matching leaf-shaped decorations. The sides of the chair are covered in woven, interlaced patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona1_adapted:
**A Tiny Altar from Ancient Egypt**

This small clay object was once part of a miniature altar from ancient Egypt. It’s decorated with a half-palmette design—a fan-shaped pattern that looks a bit like a palm leaf.

The altar was made using a mold, probably in two pieces. You can still see traces of a dull red coating (called a slip) on the inside and outside. There are also signs of burning inside, so it was likely used in some kind of ritual or ceremony.

The clay itself is brown Nile silt, with a light-brown center. It contains lots of tiny, shiny gold mica flakes and some white bits mixed in.

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona1_adapted:
This cloth is a type of fabric called **adire eleko**, which is a special kind of indigo-dyed cloth from Nigeria. The designs are made using a "resist" method—meaning parts of the cloth are protected from the dye to create patterns.

The fabric is made from cotton that was bought from a factory, and two large pieces have been sewn together by machine. Each piece is divided into a grid of 4 rows and 7 columns, making 28 squares in total.

The patterns were painted by hand. In two of the squares, you can see the pillars of **Mapo Hall**, a famous building in the city of Ibadan, Nigeria. Between those pillars, there are four shapes that look like pestles (tools used for grinding) or spoons.

CuratorialText:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, with image of a creature behind the ticket, with a bat's wing, claw and bone rib cage. 1888
Wood-engraving and letterpress
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a ticket from 1888. It was sent to a man named A. W. Franks. The ticket lets him see a painting called *Nightmare* by the artist Falero. The painting was shown at the Gainsborough Gallery.

On the back of the ticket, there’s a creepy picture. It shows a monster with a bat’s wing, a claw, and a rib cage made of bones. The ticket was made using a wood-engraving and a printing press.

CuratorialText:
Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station', with central image of a steam train arriving at the station with figures; empty space at the top to add name. 1865
Lithograph
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a ticket to see a famous painting called *The Railway Station* by William Frith. The picture shows a steam train pulling into a station, with lots of people around. There’s an empty space at the top where you could write your name. This ticket is from 1865 and was made using a printing method called lithography.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photographs of, left to right, Klimt painting, the Leopold Museum and Shiele painting; inscription in black above: multicolour print on white background.
Inscription, in black print on white background.
persona1_adapted: This is an old paper ticket for a museum. It shows three pictures: a painting by Klimt, the Leopold Museum building, and a painting by Schiele. The words on the ticket are printed in black on a white background, with some colorful printing above them.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photograph, view of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul showing columns and arched roof; inscription to right in black: multicolour print on pink and white background.
Unidentified historical painting showing forces gathered, tree to left: multicolour print on white background.
persona1_adapted:
Here's the adapted text for an 8th-grade reader:

**Paper Ticket**
This is an old paper ticket for an event or place. It shows a photo of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul—a huge underground room with rows of columns and a curved ceiling. Next to the photo, there's writing in black. The ticket is printed in multiple colors on a pink and white background.

**Old Painting of a Battle**
This is a painting from long ago, but we don't know the artist or the exact battle. It shows a group of soldiers gathered together, with a tree on the left side. The image is printed in multiple colors on a white background.

CuratorialText:
View of the interior of a tent with visitors gathered before Copley's huge history painting (Guildhall Art Gallery), elaborately framed with pillars and swags, including two young men at left flirting with young women, and two older men at right seated with papers, figures at a desk behind at left; facsimile of the admission ticket to the exhibition, from an engraving by Bartolozzi. c.1850s-60s
Photomechincal reproduction
persona1_adapted:
**Inside the Exhibition Tent**

This picture shows the inside of a big tent where people have come to see a famous painting by the artist Copley. The painting is huge and has a fancy frame with pillars and fabric decorations.

Look closely at the people in the tent. On the left side, two young men are talking and laughing with two young women. On the right side, two older men are sitting down with papers in their hands. Behind them on the left, someone is working at a desk.

At the bottom of the picture, there's a copy of the ticket people used to get into the exhibition. This image was made from an engraving by an artist named Bartolozzi, sometime in the 1850s or 1860s. It's a photomechanical reproduction, which means it was printed using a special early photographic process.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona1_adapted: This ticket was for a show put on by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left, you see the painter Richard Wilson. He’s bringing a painting to a pawn shop—probably because he needed money. On the right is the writer Daniel Defoe, who wrote *Robinson Crusoe*. He’s leaving a publisher’s office with a sad look on his face. His worried wife and daughter are waiting for him. On the ground in front, there are books and a paint palette with brushes. This picture was made after an artwork by someone named Ward, in 1851. The artist used a mix of techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving, on a special paper called chine collé.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona1_adapted:
This ticket is for a show put on by a group called the Guild of Literature and Art. The picture on the ticket tells two stories about struggling artists.

On the left side, you see the painter Richard Wilson. He’s carrying a painting into a pawn shop to sell it for money.

On the right side, the writer Daniel Defoe (author of *Robinson Crusoe*) is leaving a publisher’s office. He looks sad. His worried wife and daughter are waiting for him. In his pocket, you can see his manuscript for *Robinson Crusoe*.

In the front of the picture, there are books and a painter’s palette with brushes lying on the ground.

This artwork was made in 1851 using a mix of techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It was printed on a special type of paper called chine collé.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona1_adapted:
This ticket was for a show put on by a group called the Guild of Literature and Art. The picture on it tells two stories about struggling artists.

On the left, you see a painter named Richard Wilson. He’s carrying a painting to a pawn shop to sell it for money.

On the right, you see the writer Daniel Defoe, who wrote *Robinson Crusoe*. He’s leaving a publisher’s office with a sad look on his face. His worried wife and daughter are waiting for him. In his pocket is the manuscript (the handwritten copy) of his famous book.

In the front of the picture, you can see books and a painter’s palette with brushes lying on the ground.

This print was made in 1851 using a mix of techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It was printed on a special paper called chine collé.

CuratorialText:
Ticket to a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, with signature by James Beck; am emblem on the top left with the letters VR and a crown above. 1862
Woodcut and letterpress
persona1_adapted: This is a ticket for a special exhibit at the South Kensington Museum. It has a signature from James Beck. In the top left corner, there is a symbol with the letters "VR" and a crown above them. The ticket is from 1862. It was made using woodcut and letterpress printing.

CuratorialText:
Advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel"; Princess Alexandra, full length, seated to right, painting a stool with Aspinall's Enamel; illustration to "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" (3 December, 1887).
Wood engraving
persona1_adapted: This is an old advertisement for a paint called "Aspinall's Enamel." It shows Princess Alexandra sitting down and painting a stool with the enamel. The picture was made for a women's newspaper called *The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper* in December 1887. The image is a wood engraving, which is a type of print made by carving a picture into a block of wood.

CuratorialText: Large glazed and framed oil painting on canvas depicting a swirling abstract composition dominated by crimson on a dark background. The central bright design has areas of thickly applied paint while in the black background the contracting of the paint surface has produced a textured effect.
persona1_adapted: This is a big painting on canvas, covered in glass and framed. It shows a swirling, abstract design. The main color is a bright, deep red, set against a dark background. In the center, the artist used thick layers of paint. On the black parts, the paint has dried and cracked, creating a bumpy, textured look.

CuratorialText: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne which suggests an aniconic reverence to Viṣṇu, also suggested by the large conch positioned centrally in the foreground. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a special ceremony called the "Adoration of the Śālagrāma." In the middle of the scene, you can see people worshiping a sacred stone. On the left, there's a young figure of Krishna holding a butter ball. On the right, a woman holds a parasol (like an umbrella) over an empty throne. This is a way of showing respect to the god Vishnu without actually showing him. You can also see a large conch shell in the front, plus other items used in Hindu worship, like a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; oil painting on canvas in a carved and gilded wood frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca Capello, that Layards' house in Venice, overlooking the Grand Canal. She wears a closely-fitting belted red jacket and a black skirt with an enormously long train; she holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony balustrade. Open French doors set with glass panels give onto an interior with potted plants; in the distance is a view across the Grand Canal.
persona1_adapted:
**Portrait of Lady Layard**

This painting shows Lady Layard, the wife of Henry Layard. She is sitting on the balcony of Ca’ Capello, the Layards’ home in Venice, Italy. From there, she can look out over the Grand Canal, a famous waterway in the city.

Lady Layard wears a fitted red jacket with a belt and a long black skirt that trails far behind her. In her right hand, she holds a fan. She leans against the balcony railing. Behind her, glass-paneled French doors open into a room with potted plants. In the distance, you can see the view across the Grand Canal.

CuratorialText: Painting in mixed media (?oil pencil) of a bearded head and shoulders of a male figure. This is one from the series 'Telingana Faces'. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. Signed and dated by the artist. Mounted
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a man’s head and shoulders. He has a beard and wears a square amulet around his neck. The artist used mixed media—possibly oil pencil—to create this piece. It’s part of a series called *Telingana Faces*. The artist signed and dated the work, and it’s mounted for display.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Voltaire (1694-1778), T.Q.L., standing, body nearly in profile to r., head turned half r., and eyes looking full, wearing dark green coat trimmed with gold lace, and holding book in his l. hand.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows Voltaire, a famous writer and thinker who lived from 1694 to 1778. He is standing up, facing mostly to the right. His head is turned a little toward us, and he looks directly at the viewer. He wears a dark green coat with gold trim and holds a book in his left hand.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk a Dakota Sans Arc Chief, sitting. Wearing three feathers in his hair, ear and neck -ornaments, decorated shirt with feathers on upper sleeves and blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows a man named Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk. He was a chief of the Dakota Sans Arc people. In the portrait, he is sitting down. He wears three feathers in his hair, along with ear and neck ornaments. His shirt is decorated, with feathers on the upper sleeves, and he is wrapped in a blanket. In his hands, he holds a Pipe-tomahawk—a tool that could be used as both a pipe for smoking and a weapon. This artwork was made with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398-1468), H.L., turning half-r., the eyes looking full, wearing a cap, holding a wood block with the letters of the alphabet A-I.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398–1468)**  
This painting shows Johann Gutenberg, the inventor of the printing press. He is turned slightly to the right, but his eyes look straight at you. He wears a cap and holds a wooden block carved with the letters A through I.  
*Oil paint on canvas*

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566-1633), Infanta of Spain, half-length turned slightly to left, wearing white dress trimmed with red rosettes etc., large ruff, and jewelled headdress; after Frans Pourbus II
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria, who was a princess of Spain. She lived from 1566 to 1633. In the portrait, she is shown from the waist up, turned a little to the left. She wears a white dress decorated with red rosettes, a big ruff (a fancy, stiff collar), and a jeweled headdress. This artwork is a copy of a painting by Frans Pourbus II, made with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Augustus II ('The Strong') (1670-1733), King of Poland and Elector of Saxony, three-quarter length, standing, body turned half-right, head half-left, wearing armour and ermine-trimmed cloak, and holding sceptre.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**Augustus the Strong: King and Show-Off**

This painting shows Augustus II, who was the King of Poland and a powerful ruler in Germany. He was nicknamed "the Strong" because he was famous for his physical strength—legend says he could bend horseshoes with his bare hands!

In the portrait, Augustus stands in a proud pose. He's wearing shiny armor and a fancy cloak trimmed with soft, white ermine fur (a symbol of royalty). In his hand, he holds a scepter, which is like a royal stick that shows he's in charge.

The artist wanted everyone to see Augustus as powerful, wealthy, and important. This type of painting is called a "portrait," and it was made with oil paint on canvas.

**Medium:** Oil painting on canvas

CuratorialText:
Portrait said to be of Mary Tudor (1516-58) as Princess; Head and shoulders, 3/4 face, eyes looking full. Wearing ruff, earring, embroidered dress, ?chain, headdress.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted:
This painting is a portrait, which is a picture of a person. It is believed to show Mary Tudor when she was a princess, before she became queen. Mary lived from 1516 to 1558.

In the painting, we see her from the chest up. Her body is turned slightly, but she looks straight at us. She is wearing fancy clothes: a ruff (a stiff, pleated collar), an earring, a dress with embroidery (decorative stitching), and a headdress. She might also be wearing a chain.

The artist used oil paint on a canvas.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Jacques (aka Jean, John, James) Rousseau (1630-93), Oval, head and shoulders turned half-r., eyes looking full, wearing dark coat and lace cravat. Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a man named Jacques Rousseau. He also went by the names Jean, John, and James. He lived from 1630 to 1693. In the portrait, we see his head and shoulders. His body is turned slightly to the right, but his eyes look straight at us. He is wearing a dark coat and a fancy lace collar. The painting is oval-shaped and made with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Dr Robert G W Anderson. Head, profile, facing to sitter's right
Oil painting on panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio at Talfourd Road, Peckham. Superimposed on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe. The tie is a version of the Great Court ties sold at the British Museum around the end of 2000, to mark the opening of the Great Court.
persona1_adapted:
This is a painting of Dr. Robert G. W. Anderson. We see his head from the side, looking to the right. He is looking out of a window in the artist’s studio in London.

A part of an old scientific tool called an astrolabe is drawn over the window. The astrolabe is the Sloane astrolabe, which is a famous object from history.

Dr. Anderson’s tie is special. It is a version of the “Great Court” tie that was sold at the British Museum around the year 2000 to celebrate the opening of the museum’s new Great Court.

CuratorialText: Oil painting on canvas stretched and glued to back of stained wooden frame. Painting shows scene of an imaginary beach side village with huts and trees in the foreground, ocean and sky with full moon behind. Painting is signed.
persona1_adapted: This painting is made with oil paint on canvas. The canvas is stretched and glued to the back of a stained wooden frame. The scene shows an imaginary beachside village. In the front, there are huts and trees. Behind them, you can see the ocean and the sky with a full moon. The painting is signed by the artist.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; commercial sign-board made of plywood, used to advertise barbers' shop hairstyles. Six individual heads are depicted in profile, each numbered and displaying a different haircut. The heads face in different directions and are painted in bold black outlines on a white background; each head has a blue collar.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on a plywood sign that was once used to advertise haircuts at a barbershop. It shows six different hairstyles, each on a separate head drawn from the side. Each head is numbered and faces a different direction. The heads are painted with thick black outlines on a white background, and every head has a blue collar.

CuratorialText: A large oil painting on canvas depicting the slaying of the dragon by St George who is the central figure of the painting, mounted on a white horse and carrying a circular shield in one hand, a spear in the other. The dragon, with striped skin and small spotted wings, lies on its back beneath the horse's hooves, together with a little blue vanquished devil, his face in profile. To the right of the painting Biruwit is depicted in the branches of a tree; to the left stand a group of soldiers armed with spears and shields.
persona1_adapted:
**St. George and the Dragon**

This big painting shows the famous story of St. George slaying a dragon. St. George is right in the middle, riding a white horse. He holds a round shield in one hand and a spear in the other.

The dragon has striped skin and tiny spotted wings. It lies on its back under the horse's hooves. Next to the dragon is a small blue devil, looking defeated, with his face turned to the side.

On the right side of the painting, a man named Biruwit is hiding in the branches of a tree. On the left, a group of soldiers stand with spears and shields, watching the action.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; wooden frame painted brown; matte of white mesh; at centre is miniature oil painting on white background with brown matte around it; painting shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. Written on back: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985"
persona1_adapted: This is a small oil painting inside a brown wooden frame. The painting has a white background with a brown border around it. In the center, you can see a matador waving a red cape at a bull. On the back of the frame, there is some handwritten text that says: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985."

CuratorialText:
Painting; pouches made from the skin of small animals; used for carrying tobacco, smoking barks and weeds. plate 13.
Oil on cardboard
persona1_adapted: This painting shows small pouches made from animal skin. People used them to carry tobacco, smoking bark, and weeds. It is painted with oil on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Painting; three Javanese men; wearing head-dress, shirt and sarong. Man at the front has a kris or sword, secured to his back with a waist wrap. House and trees are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows three Javanese men. They are wearing head coverings, shirts, and sarongs (a type of long cloth wrapped around the waist). The man in front has a kris—a special sword—tucked into a cloth belt on his back. In the background, you can see a house and some trees. The artist used oil paint on canvas to make this picture.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait a Javanese woman; wearing head, neck, arm ornament and designed costume. People, trees and two thatched buildings are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows a Javanese woman from head to toe. She is wearing fancy jewelry on her head, neck, and arms, along with a beautifully designed outfit. In the background, you can see people, trees, and two buildings with thatched roofs. The artist used oil paint on canvas to create this portrait.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait of a Javanese man holding a spear; wearing a helmet, striped shirt and leggings and sarong with waist wrap. He carries a kris either side of his waist wrap and a sword hanging on his left side. Trees and buildings, one with a large flag are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**A Javanese Warrior**  
This painting shows a man from Java (an island in Indonesia) standing tall and holding a spear. He’s dressed for action: a helmet on his head, a striped shirt and leggings, and a sarong (a wrapped skirt) with an extra cloth tied around his waist. On each side of his waist, he carries a *kris*—a special wavy-bladed knife. A sword also hangs from his left side.  

In the background, you can see trees and buildings. One building has a large flag flying from it.  

*Made with oil paint on canvas.*

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr; was principal Chief of the Sacs & Fox Nation; wearing headdress with feathers, ear-rings, necklaces, one made of bear claws, shirt and a decorated blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk and fan made of feathers.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a man named Wunagisa, who was also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr. He was the principal chief of the Sac and Fox Nation. In the portrait, he wears a feathered headdress, earrings, and several necklaces—one of them is made from bear claws. He also has on a shirt and a decorated blanket wrapped around him. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk and a fan made of feathers. The painting is made with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; from a collection of seventeen. An Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria; wearing head-band and possum skin cloak.
Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This painting is part of a set of seventeen. It shows an Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, in Victoria. She is wearing a headband and a cloak made from possum skin. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of a Native North American Chief, name unknown; wearing two feathers in his hair, neck ornament, decorated frill shirt, blanket wrap and holding a Pipe-tomahawk and tobacco pouch.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a Native American chief. We don't know his name. He wears two feathers in his hair and a decorated shirt with a frill. A blanket is wrapped around him. He holds a pipe-tomahawk (a tool that works as both a pipe and a weapon) and a tobacco pouch. This artwork is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; side view of a Javanese soldier holding a kris and circular shield; wearing head-dress, top garment and serong, with a kris-sheath secure to his back with his waist wrap. People, trees and the top of a building are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**A Javanese Soldier**  
This painting shows a soldier from Java (an island in Indonesia) standing sideways. He holds a *kris* (a special wavy-bladed dagger) and a round shield. He wears a headdress, a top garment, and a *serong* (a cloth worn over the shoulder). A sheath for his kris is tucked into the cloth wrapped around his waist, keeping it secure on his back. In the background, you can see people, trees, and the top of a building.  
The artist used oil paint on canvas to create this scene.

CuratorialText:
Painting; five decorated pipe bowls featuring men, bears, and a woman; below is a decorated tomahawk pipe. plate 8.
Oil on cardboard
persona1_adapted: This painting shows five decorated pipe bowls. They are carved with images of men, bears, and a woman. Below them is a decorated tomahawk pipe. This artwork was made with oil paint on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Framed painting; portrait of Sina Kavaliku, seated on the ground, wearing a western-style dress, her hair in plaits and a flower in her hair. Tonga. 1951.
Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This painting is a portrait of a woman named Sina Kavaliku. She is sitting on the ground and wearing a Western-style dress. Her hair is in braids, and she has a flower tucked into it. The artist painted this in Tonga in 1951 using oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona1_adapted:
This small, round pot is called an **alabastron** (say: al-uh-BASS-tron). It was made in ancient Greece, in a region called Protocorinthia. People used it to hold perfumed oil.

The pot was shaped on a potter’s wheel. It has a tiny mouth with a slanted rim, and a little handle shaped like a button on top. The clay is very fine and smooth, and the artist painted black decorations on it. They also added purple paint on the animals’ bodies and carved fine lines to show details like fur and feathers.

The main picture around the pot shows two **griffins** (mythical creatures with the head and wings of an eagle and the body of a lion). Between them is a tree-like design. A **lion** stands in the space between the two griffins. Small extra shapes fill the empty areas.

Above the main scene is a band of flower-like patterns. Below that are simple dots separated by S-shaped lines. The bottom of the pot has simple rays (like sunbeams), and the rim has tongue-shaped decorations.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.
persona1_adapted: The Imperial War Museum has about 11 million photos showing wars from World War I all the way up to today.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.
persona1_adapted: This collection shows what British and Commonwealth forces did during wars, along with views from other countries around the world. You'll see photos taken by the military, news reporters, and regular people—like soldiers, nurses, and civilians who lived through the fighting. These pictures help us understand how war affects real human lives. The collection also features famous photographers like Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.
persona1_adapted: You can look at the photos next to the original documents. These documents help us understand how and why the photos were taken.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia
An important and international collection of flags and uniforms, the latter representing all of the armed services and institutions of not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but Allies as well as former enemies along with their associated badges and insignia.
persona1_adapted:
**Uniforms, Flags, Badges, and Insignia**

This collection is full of flags and uniforms from all over the world. You'll see uniforms from the army, navy, and other military branches—not just from Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but also from our allies and even former enemies. Each uniform comes with its own badges and insignia, which are special symbols that show a person's rank or group.

CuratorialText:
Mementoes and Miscellanea
A diverse collection containing objects of personal significance, including escape and evasion items, prisoner of war and civilian internee items, trench art, battlefield souvenirs, commemorative objects, foodstuffs, currency, tobacciana, toys and games, Kindertransport and Holocaust related items, home-front and peace-time items.
persona1_adapted:
**Mementos and Miscellanea**

This collection is full of all kinds of different objects that were important to people during wartime. You’ll find things like:

- Items used to help soldiers escape or avoid capture
- Objects made or kept by prisoners of war and civilians held in camps
- “Trench art” (things soldiers made from leftover war materials)
- Souvenirs picked up on battlefields
- Commemorative objects (things made to remember a person or event)
- Food and money from different times and places
- Tobacco-related items, toys, and games
- Objects connected to the Kindertransport (a rescue mission that saved children) and the Holocaust
- Items from life at home during the war and from peacetime after the war ended

CuratorialText:
Orders, Medals and Decorations
An important collection comprising orders, medal and decorations issued to service personnel and civilians.
persona1_adapted:
**Ribbons, Medals, and Awards**

This collection is full of special awards given to people in the military and to regular citizens. These medals and pins were given to honor brave acts, hard work, or important service.

CuratorialText:
Equipment
An extensive collection of equipment used in twentieth and twenty-first-century warfare, including radios, cipher machines, radars, cameras, medical equipment and even pigeon baskets.
persona1_adapted:
**Gear and Gadgets**  
This room is packed with tools used in wars from the 1900s to today. You’ll see radios, secret code machines, radar systems, cameras, medical kits, and even baskets that once carried messenger pigeons!

CuratorialText:
Weapons and Ammunition
A major reference collection that includes firearms, edged weapons, trench clubs or concussive weapons, ordnance and munitions.
persona1_adapted:
**Weapons and Ammunition**

This collection has lots of different kinds of weapons and ammo. You'll find guns, knives and swords, clubs used in trenches, and other big weapons and explosives.

CuratorialText:
Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships
An internationally renowned collection including models, battle tanks, maritime equipment and aircraft components. Most of the collection of artillery and large military equipment is on show at IWM London or IWM Duxford.
persona1_adapted:
**Vehicles, Planes, and Ships**

This collection is famous around the world. It includes model tanks, boats, plane parts, and other military gear. You can see most of the big guns and large military vehicles at IWM London or IWM Duxford.

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona1_adapted: The IWM Sound Collection has more than 35,000 recordings about conflicts since 1914. It’s the biggest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with over 60,000 hours of material and interviews in more than 15 different languages. Besides interviews, it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio shows, and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of everyday people—like soldiers, activists, factory workers, and journalists. It also features famous figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and singer Vera Lynn. These unique oral history interviews have been used in important academic research and appear in bestselling books, movies, TV and radio shows, and podcasts. You can listen to thousands of these audio recordings on the IWM website, which adds new ones regularly.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
persona1_adapted:
For over 100 years, IWM has kept a collection of films. From the very start, IWM knew that film is an important way to understand history. Its huge collection shows the causes, events, and results of conflicts from World War I to today. It’s a great resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our collection keeps growing and now has over 25,000 hours of moving images. It includes a wide range of materials, such as public information films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and home movies. We also have two large collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. We are working to digitize these films so people can watch them online for free, and we add new digital copies as soon as they are ready.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona1_adapted:
**Private Papers**

The museum has more than 20,000 personal collections. These include private diaries, letters, and memoirs—mostly written by British and Commonwealth soldiers and nurses since 1914. There are also writings from civilians during World War I and World War II. In addition, we keep important documents from people who survived or were harmed by the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona1_adapted:
**Foreign and Related Documents**

We also take care of a big collection of documents from other countries. This includes the official British records from the major war crime trials that took place in Nuremberg and Tokyo. We also have related papers from World War II that mostly talk about the German and Japanese war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.
persona1_adapted:
This collection is one of the most important displays of 20th-century British art in the world. It includes many great artworks from the British government's war art programs during World War I and World War II. These programs hired the best artists of the time, including leaders of the avant-garde (artists who tried new, bold styles). Some of these artists were Paul Nash, C. R. W. Nevinson, John Singer Sargent, and Sir William Orpen.

IWM also has the unique War Artists Archive. It shows how the war art programs worked day to day and gives us a fascinating look at what the artists experienced.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.
persona1_adapted: Our library has more than 150,000 items about wars from the 1900s to today that involved Britain and other Commonwealth countries. You'll find books about military groups and battles, as well as biographies, manuals, and materials about how war affects money, society, and culture. We also have magazines, newspapers, posters, propaganda, and online resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona1_adapted:
**The Sinking of HMS Laurentic**

This painting shows the moment a ship called HMS Laurentic was sinking. You can see half of the ship underwater, with its front end sticking up into the air. In the front of the painting, a lifeboat full of sailors is lit up by a small light in the dark. Another lifeboat is on the right, and tiny lights in the background show even more lifeboats. On the left, you can just see the top of a German submarine (called a U-boat).

HMS Laurentic started out as a passenger ship for a company called P&O. In 1939, it was turned into a warship by adding big guns. This was the first ship that the artist, Frank Worsley, served on. He stayed with it until it was sunk off the coast of Ulster (in Ireland) by a German U-boat captain named Otto Kretschmer on the night of November 3, 1940.

Worsley painted the first version of this picture right after the sinking, while he was on leave. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought the painting. In August 1942, they sent it on a tour to South America. But the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed and sank! So Worsley painted this replacement version at the WAAC’s request.

CuratorialText:
image: a canopy of camouflage nets suspended over a road that leads to a cluster of industrial buildings painted with a camouflage patters. A group of figures gather in the background.
History note
War Artists Advisory Committee purchase

**Image:** A road leads to some factory buildings. The buildings and a big net above the road are painted in a camouflage pattern. A group of people stand in the background.

**History Note:** The British government bought this artwork through a special committee called the War Artists Advisory Committee.

CuratorialText:
image: a coastal radar station with the radar equipment and masts in the centre background. In the foreground are several buildings with personnel in dark uniforms milling around outside. The sea is visible in the background to the left.

War Artists Advisory Committee purchase
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Reader:**

This photo shows a coastal radar station during wartime. In the background, you can see the radar equipment and tall masts. Closer to the front, there are several buildings, and people in dark uniforms are standing or walking around outside. To the left, you can see the sea in the distance.

**How we got this image:**  
The British government’s War Artists Advisory Committee bought this picture to help record and remember what life was like during the war.

CuratorialText:
image: The burnt-out wreck of a fire engine lying in an empty landscape. There is a heavy sky above, and several buildings visible on the horizon in the far right of the composition.

War Artists Advisory Committee purchase

image: a half-length portrait of Andrew Nures Nabarro wearing his fireman's uniform with his hat under his arm.

This is a painting of Andrew Nures Nabarro. He’s a firefighter, and you can see him in his uniform. He holds his hat under his arm.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Two warships moored at Plymouth dock with hundreds of people standing on the dockside in the foreground. There are two cranes on either side of the composition and other vessels are visible on the water in the background.
History note
War Artists Advisory Committee commission
persona1_adapted:
**Object Description**  
This picture shows two warships parked at a dock in Plymouth. In the front, you can see lots of people standing on the dock. There are two cranes, one on each side of the scene. In the background, more boats are floating on the water.

This artwork was created as part of a special program called the War Artists Advisory Committee. The committee hired artists to make art about World War II.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: A view looking down at men sitting in rows, listening to the proceedings at the Nuremberg trials through headphones. Behind them is a row of soldiers.
History note
War Artists Advisory Committee commission
persona1_adapted:
**Object Description**  
This photo shows a group of men sitting in rows, looking down at them from above. They are listening to what’s happening at the Nuremberg trials through headphones. Behind them, a row of soldiers stands watch.

This picture was made as part of a special project called the War Artists Advisory Committee.

CuratorialText:
Object description
Image: A view of a pond from a boat. On the pond are floating supports, suspending a large camouflage net over the water. To the right is another boat containing three men inspecting the nets.
Physical description
Framed and mounted watercolour painting.

**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Reader:**

This painting shows a pond seen from a boat. Floating platforms hold up a big camouflage net over the water. On the right, another boat has three men who are checking the nets.

**Physical description:** This is a watercolor painting, framed and mounted.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: A reconstruction of the sinking of a number of merchant ships by German U-boats in the North Atlantic. Merchant sailors and debris from a destroyed ship are in the water in the foreground. To the right a merchant ship is in the process of sinking, with the stern end submerged and the bow end pointing skywards. In the background, other merchant vessels are being hit by enemy fire.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a terrifying scene from World War II: a German submarine, called a U-boat, attacking merchant ships in the North Atlantic. In the front of the picture, you can see sailors from a destroyed ship struggling in the freezing water, surrounded by wreckage. To the right, another ship is sinking fast—its back end is already underwater, and its front end is pointing straight up into the sky. In the background, more ships are being hit by enemy fire.

CuratorialText: A rough for a furniture mural. whole: text is split between left, middle and right side, with separate images associated with each. image: (left) blueprint illustrating furniture with aircraft, tanks and ships in background. (middle) various types of furniture arranged over a blueprint of a house with factories in the background. (right) birds eye view of a port, mainly in yellow, with modern buildings and furniture floating above the buildings. text: FROM WAR PRODUCTION THROUGH AUSTERITY TO BETTER TIMES
persona1_adapted:
This is a sketch for a big painting that would go on a piece of furniture. The sketch is split into three parts, left, middle, and right, each with its own picture.

*   **Left side:** A blueprint shows furniture. In the background, there are airplanes, tanks, and ships.
*   **Middle:** Different types of furniture are arranged on top of a house blueprint. Factories are in the background.
*   **Right side:** A bird's-eye view of a port, mostly in yellow. There are modern buildings, and furniture is floating above them.

The text on the sketch reads: "FROM WAR PRODUCTION THROUGH AUSTERITY TO BETTER TIMES." This means the artist is showing how things changed from making things for war, to a time of being careful with resources, and finally to happier, more comfortable times.

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.
persona1_adapted:
**A Drawing That Changed Everything**

In the summer of 1983, artist Eric Rimmington was drawing an old train station in London. He planned to make ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. But while working on the second drawing, he made a shocking discovery.

The train tracks he was drawing were used several times a week to carry nuclear waste from a power plant called Sizewell to another facility called Sellafield. This fact changed everything for Rimmington. For the next six weeks, he couldn't stop thinking about it. Every mark he made in his drawings was connected to that scary truth.

**A Time of Fear and Protest**

Rimmington created these drawings during a period when many people were protesting against nuclear weapons and nuclear power. In 1979, the British government agreed to let American Cruise Missiles be placed in the UK. This decision sparked huge marches and protests. One famous protest was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which started in 1981 and was at its peak in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived.

That same year, 1979, a serious accident happened at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA. This accident made people even more afraid of nuclear power.

**Still a Concern Today**

Nuclear waste—and how it is transported and stored—remains a big worry for many people. In fact, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly asked for a special report on the safety of nuclear waste trains. So the issues Rimmington drew about are still with us today.

War Artists Advisory Committee purchase
persona1_adapted:
**Object Description**  
This photo shows the inside of a reporting room. On the wall are two big circular maps of East Anglia. Women wearing dark blue uniforms stand around the maps. In the background, you can see information boards on the wall. In the front of the photo, more women are working.

**History Note**  
This artwork was bought by the War Artists Advisory Committee.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Standard steel transmitter towers in the foreground with wooden receiver towers in the background. In the foreground are piles of tires, some vegetation and a line of barbed wire fencing.
History note
War Artists Advisory Committee purchase
persona1_adapted:
**What You're Looking At:**
In the front of the picture, you see tall steel towers that send signals. Behind them are wooden towers that receive those signals. On the ground nearby, there are piles of tires, some bushes and grass, and a line of barbed wire fence.

**How This Object Got Here:**
The British government's War Artists Advisory Committee bought this artwork during World War II.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona1_adapted:
**Object Description**  
In this photo, five kids are sitting around a table in a room lit by a big window. An older woman is in the middle, holding the youngest child on her lap. On the table, there’s a large “Spotted-Dick” pudding (a type of British dessert) with bowls and forks around it. The woman is serving a boy on the far right, who holds out his bowl for a portion.

**Label**  
This is Mrs. Norris from Cookham, with five of the seven kids from London who were staying with her during World War II. By January 1941, they had been living with her for sixteen months. Mrs. Norris is the perfect foster parent—and there were many other people like her. Over a million children had been moved from cities to safer areas in school groups. With so many kids, finding homes for them mostly meant placing them with local families. Thanks to Mrs. Norris’s kindness and care, city children quickly got used to their new surroundings.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.
persona1_adapted:
**Portrait of a Factory Worker**

This painting shows a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a large machine called a lathe. She was only 21 years old and had never worked in a factory before. But she was so good at her job that the War Artist's Advisory Committee called her "an outstanding factory worker."

The artist, Laura Knight, first planned to paint Loftus in a studio. But the Ministry of Supply asked her to paint Loftus at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport, Wales. There, Loftus was making a part for a Bofors gun called a breech ring. This was the most difficult job in the factory—it usually took eight or nine years of training to learn. Loftus mastered it in a very short time.

Some people think Loftus was put in front of the machine just for the painting, to make a good impression. But either way, her skill was real. At the time, it was rare for a woman to do such complex factory work, and her success was a big deal for the war effort.

Laura Knight had never painted industrial machinery before. But she got the details right, and critics praised her accuracy. Like Loftus, Knight was proving that women could succeed in jobs that were usually done by men.

Knight was also known for painting circus performers and dancers. In this portrait, you can see that same focus on balance and posture—how Loftus stands and moves as she works the machine.

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona1_adapted: This photo shows a busy scene at Charing Cross train station during wartime. In the bottom left corner, wounded soldiers lie on stretchers on the ground. A nurse and men from the Order of St John (wearing uniforms) are helping them. Some are lifting stretchers off the train. In the middle, a group of soldiers in coats stand together, many with bandaged arms or legs. The top half of the picture is filled with steam and smoke from the train, rising up to the station roof.

CuratorialText:
image: A line of Indian lancers charge at Turkish infantry across a valley, with high ground visible in the background. In the foreground two Turkish infantrymen lie on the ground as they are speared by two Indian lancers.
Physical description
Framed and glazed oil painting.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows a dramatic battle scene. Indian soldiers on horseback, called lancers, charge toward Turkish soldiers on foot. The fight takes place in a valley, with hills in the background. In the front, two Turkish soldiers have been knocked down and are being attacked by the lancers' long spears.

**What it is:**  
A framed oil painting with glass over it.

CuratorialText:
image: a troop of Indian Lancers on horseback advance towards the viewer along a dusty road, passing the derelict inn. The road runs through a valley and there are further hills in the background.

Wellington House commission, transferred to Dept and Ministry of Information and then to the Imperial War Museum
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

In this painting, a group of Indian soldiers on horseback rides toward you along a dusty road. They pass by an old, empty inn. The road goes through a valley, and you can see more hills far away in the background.

This painting was first ordered for Wellington House. Later, it was moved to the Department and Ministry of Information. Finally, it came to the Imperial War Museum.

CuratorialText:

Imperial War Museum commission
persona1_adapted:
In this photo, you can see Indian soldiers wearing Sikh turbans. They are moving wood and supplies from two military trucks to a train waiting in the background. In the front, three soldiers carry planks of wood toward the viewer. This scene took place near Pozières on the Somme in northern France, just behind the front line of battle.

CuratorialText:
image: a group of Indian infantrymen, all wearing distinctive Sikh turbans, dismantle a strongpoint near Pozières on the Western Front. The strongpoint consists of earth, brick, sandbags and corrugated iron and is the prominent feature in a flat, largely desolate and battle scarred landscape.
History note
Imperial War Museum commission
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**
