This photo shows a group of Indian soldiers, all wearing traditional Sikh turbans, taking apart a small fort near Pozières on the Western Front during World War I. The fort is made of dirt, bricks, sandbags, and metal sheets. It stands out in a flat, empty, and war-torn landscape.

*This photo was made for the Imperial War Museum.*

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona1_adapted: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left, several wounded soldiers lie on stretchers on the ground. A nurse helps them, and people from the Order of St John in uniform stand on the platform, lifting stretchers off the train. In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats have bandaged arms or legs. The top half of the picture shows the station roof filled with steam and smoke from the train below.

CuratorialText: image: A half length portrait of a British officer wearing a red dress uniform jacket with seven medals on his chest, including the DSO and OBE. He also wears the insignia of the Order of the Indian Empire around his neck.
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a British military officer. He is shown from the chest up. He wears a red dress uniform jacket. On his chest, there are seven medals. Two of them are the DSO and the OBE. He also wears a special badge around his neck. It is the insignia of the Order of the Indian Empire.

CuratorialText:
image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.
persona1_adapted:
**Image Description:**
A skeleton, representing Death, drives a bulldozer through a war zone. The ground is covered with rubble, bricks, metal beams, and tires. On the right side, something is on fire.

**Adapted Label:**

This artwork imagines total destruction, with Death itself driving a bulldozer through the mess. Only flags are still standing, but they cross over each other and make the scene even more confusing. The only buildings left are piles of bricks and metal beams.

The artist, Michael Sandle, is mostly a sculptor who has made many public monuments. War is a big theme in his work. This comes from his own experiences—he lived through air raids in Plymouth as a child and later served in the military. This print is one of six in a series called *The Driver*.

CuratorialText: image: a view through a few trees across the large field of crosses of the British military cemetery at Etaples on the French coast. The coastline is bordered by trees and a sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.
persona1_adapted: You're looking at a photo of a huge field of white crosses. This is a British military cemetery in Etaples, France, right near the coast. You can see a few trees in the foreground, and beyond the crosses, the shoreline is lined with more trees. Out in the English Channel, there's a small sailboat.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona1_adapted:
**A Ship Sinks in the Night**

This painting shows the British destroyer HMS *Eclipse* sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship has broken in two, with only the front and back ends sticking out of the water. The front part is on fire, sending a bright cloud of flames and smoke into the night sky. Another ship is in the background, and two Greek islands can be seen on the left and right.

**What Happened**

On October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—were sailing along the coast of Turkey. Their destination was the Greek island of Leros, which was held by British soldiers. On board the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking officer on his way to inspect the island's defenses. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., disaster struck. The *Eclipse* hit a mine (an underwater bomb) between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The ship exploded and sank in just two minutes. In addition to its crew of about 200 men, the ship was carrying another 200 soldiers. Only about 100 people survived. They were rescued by a small RAF boat and by boats from the *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the cold water.

Brigadier-General Davy was one of the survivors. He later became the commander of land forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He recorded this terrifying event 24 years after it happened.

CuratorialText: image: two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent with their heads resting on khaki bags. Both are wearing dessert gear. The soldier furthest away is reading, but the man nearest the artist has fallen asleep with his book resting on his chest.
persona1_adapted: Two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent. Their heads rest on khaki bags, and they’re both wearing desert gear. The soldier farther away is reading a book. The one closer to the artist has fallen asleep, with his book resting on his chest.

CuratorialText:
image: A view across the river Spree with the sun rising over the city in the background. There are fortifications and an observation post on the opposite bank with a vessel moored below, and a British Military policeman with a vehicle on the near side.
History note
Artistic Records Committee Commission, December 1980
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows a scene along the River Spree in Berlin. The sun is rising over the city in the background. On the far side of the river, you can see old fortifications and a lookout post. A boat is tied up below them. On the near side, a British military police officer stands next to a vehicle.

**History Note:** This artwork was created in December 1980 as part of a special commission from the Artistic Records Committee.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This drawing shows a jumble of twisted metal beams of all different sizes. It’s not a picture of a building—it’s what’s left after bombs hit. The artist, Paule Vézelay, wanted to show how explosions twist and break structures. You can almost feel the force of the blast in the way the beams bend and curve.

Vézelay was born in Bristol, England. She spent more than 10 years living in Paris as an artist, but when World War II started, she moved back home. Because of her political views (she was left-wing) and because her art was abstract (not realistic), she never got an official job as a war artist. But with help from the Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she got permission to draw the bombed-out ruins in her hometown.

CuratorialText: image: In the foreground two sailors sit with their backs to the viewer on the deck of a ship. A third sailor stands behind them leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun protrudes out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead of them and clouds of smoke rising from the hills in the distance. The sky is dotted with barrage balloons. The artist has also made several other sketches of ships and sailors in the upper right corner of the image.
persona1_adapted: In the front of the picture, two sailors are sitting on a ship’s deck with their backs to us. A third sailor stands behind them, leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun sticks out above them. They are watching two ships anchored in the water ahead, and smoke rising from the hills far away. The sky is filled with small, floating barrage balloons. The artist also drew a few extra sketches of ships and sailors in the top right corner of the image.

CuratorialText: image: British soldiers are in action on a German street, crouching and firing their guns among the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers are lying on a Nazi flag bearing a swastika, the left of the two in the process of firing a Bren Gun. Four other infantrymen stand directly behind them to the left, two armed with rifles and one with a Bren Gun. Other British soldiers are visible in the middle distance.
persona1_adapted: British soldiers are fighting on a street in Germany. They crouch and shoot their guns among the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers lie on a Nazi flag with a swastika. The soldier on the left is firing a Bren Gun. Four other soldiers stand behind them to the left. Two carry rifles, and one has another Bren Gun. More British soldiers can be seen in the distance.

CuratorialText: image: two British Royal Army Medical Corps stretcher-bearers stand with their stretchers in a shallow trench in the foreground. The trench leads up to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station, marked with an ADS sign, which is housed in the bomb damaged remains of a large house in a ruined village. Other heavily bomb damaged buildings are visible in the background.
persona1_adapted: Two British Army medics stand in a shallow trench, holding stretchers. The trench leads to an Advanced Dressing Station, or ADS, set up inside a bombed-out house in a ruined village. More destroyed buildings can be seen in the background.

CuratorialText:
image: the interior of the designers' room in the Old Vic Theatre, Waterloo, London, after suffering the effects of an air raid. The windows have been blown out and internal walls damaged, with the room littered with disturbed papers, designs and a fallen mannequin. The window looks out roughly south towards Lambeth, with the roofs of buildings visible.
Label
Short text of working notes. Inscription verso reads: 'Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941'.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th Grade Reader:**

This photo shows the designers' room at the Old Vic Theatre in London after a bombing attack during World War II. The windows are blown out, the walls are damaged, and the room is a mess—papers and designs are scattered everywhere, and a mannequin has fallen over. Through the broken window, you can see the rooftops of buildings in Lambeth, a neighborhood south of the theater.

The short note on the back of the photo says: "Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre, September 1941."

CuratorialText: image: Against a gloomy sunset and upon a grassy mound stands a smoking but unmanned French 75mm field gun, facing left. Below running the length of the composition lies the detritus of battle: a broken limber, four German corpses and two dead mules.
persona1_adapted: In this painting, a French 75mm field gun sits on a grassy hill. It’s smoking, but no one is operating it. The gun faces left, and the sunset behind it looks dark and gloomy. Below the hill, across the whole scene, you can see the wreckage of battle: a broken cart, four dead German soldiers, and two dead mules.

CuratorialText: image: A convoy at sea being bombarded by enemy aircraft. In the centre of the composition is a large tanker, which has black smoke emanating from it. Other ships can be seen to the left and right with aircraft visible above. Large splashes of water in the sea show several near-misses.
persona1_adapted: A convoy of ships is under attack at sea. Enemy planes are dropping bombs from above. In the middle of the scene, a large tanker is hit and black smoke pours out of it. Other ships are on the left and right, with aircraft flying overhead. Big splashes in the water show that some bombs just missed their targets.

CuratorialText: Powder paint painting, hinged in corners onto a single sheet of white board. Bottom left hinge has previously been cut to allow access to object number. Neatly torn along top and right edges, rather than cut. Previously folded in half vertically.
persona1_adapted: This painting is made with powder paint. It's attached to a white board with hinges at the corners. The bottom left hinge was cut so you can see the object's number. The top and right edges were neatly torn, not cut. The painting was also folded in half, top to bottom, at some point.

CuratorialText: image: eight internees stand behind a barbed wire fence at Hutchinson camp near Douglas on the Isle of Man. The word 'Parachutists' is on the wall behind them. Four of the men wear glasses and all but one of them appear to be wearing kippot, indicating their Jewish faith. The other man wears a bowler-style hat.
persona1_adapted: In this photo, eight people are standing behind a barbed wire fence at a camp called Hutchinson, near Douglas on the Isle of Man. On the wall behind them, the word “Parachutists” is written. Four of the men are wearing glasses, and most of them are wearing kippot, which shows they are Jewish. One man is wearing a bowler-style hat instead.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.
persona1_adapted:
J.M.W. Turner (1775–1851) is considered one of the greatest landscape painters ever. He knew Oxford really well. As a kid, he made lots of drawings and watercolors of the streets, colleges, and pretty views around the city. His famous oil painting of Oxford's High Street is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery.

To celebrate the 250th anniversary of Turner's birth on April 23, 1775, check out this collection of his Oxford pictures from the Ashmolean's collections. The museum's Western Art Print Room has over 100 watercolors and drawings by Turner, plus many landscape etchings. You'll find lively studies of animals, birds, and fish, as well as beautiful views of places he visited in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Around 4,000 years ago, the island of Crete was home to a group of people called the Minoans. They lived long before the Greeks took over.

In 1900, an archaeologist named Sir Arthur Evans started digging at a place called Knossos on Crete. As he uncovered old buildings and objects, he noticed they reminded him of ancient stories from Crete. He named the main building the “Palace of Minos,” after a mythical king.

According to the legend, King Minos ordered a giant maze called the Labyrinth to be built. Inside, he trapped a terrifying monster called the Minotaur. This creature had the head of a bull and the body of a man. The hero Theseus, who was part god, went into the maze, killed the Minotaur, and escaped. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and founded the city of Athens.

Even after the palace was destroyed, the people who lived in Knossos—first Greeks and later Romans—kept telling these stories for many generations.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona1_adapted:
Landscape painting has been a big part of East Asian art for a very long time. But in Japan, it wasn't until the early 1800s that landscapes became popular in woodblock prints. Around that time, more Japanese people started going on outdoor trips—like visiting temples or taking boat rides—and they wanted souvenirs to remember their adventures. In the competitive world of publishing, companies were always looking for new ideas. Landscape prints turned out to be a fresh and exciting product.

New technology also helped make landscape prints possible. A key change was the arrival of a bright blue pigment called Prussian Blue, which was imported from Europe. Unlike older Japanese blues that faded quickly, this new color stayed vivid. It gave artists more freedom to show off the sky and water in their work.

In this gallery, you'll see some of the best landscape prints by the master Utagawa Hiroshige, along with selected works by two other famous artists, Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.
persona1_adapted:
Our collection of ancient objects includes world-famous archaeological finds from many different places and time periods. You'll see artifacts from prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, the Minoan and Mycenaean civilizations, Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, and Medieval and later Europe. We also have the museum's original Founding Collection and historic archives.

In addition, we have one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of plaster casts of Greek and Roman statues in the whole United Kingdom.

CuratorialText: In our Eastern Art collections you will find sculpture, textiles, ceramics and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India and the Himalayas.
persona1_adapted: In our Eastern Art collection, you'll find sculptures, fabrics, pottery, and paintings from many places. These include the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India, and the Himalayas.

CuratorialText: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the leading international coin cabinets with particular strengths in the fields of Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic and Chinese coinages. It also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons and commemorative art medals.
persona1_adapted: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the world’s top collections of coins. It has especially strong holdings in Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic, and Chinese coins. It also includes paper money, tokens, and special medals.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.
persona1_adapted: The Western Art collection features amazing European art and decorative objects, from the Renaissance period up to today. You'll find paintings, drawings, watercolors, and prints by famous old masters and later artists. There are also English and European ceramics, sculptures, silver items, watches, rings, and musical instruments. The Ashmolean has a huge print collection with about 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints. Many of these can be seen in our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
persona1_adapted:
This painting started as a special job. A publisher wanted two watercolors to use in a yearly calendar called the *Oxford Almanack*. The artist, J.M.W. Turner, did such a good job that he ended up making ten watercolors for them between 1798 and 1804.

Later, a local art dealer named James Wyatt asked Turner to turn this watercolor and another one into oil paintings. Turner even asked Wyatt for advice on the details of the buildings in the scene. Turner also added people to the painting—university members, church leaders, and some women—just to make the colors look better.

We know a lot about this painting because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him. Those letters were sold after Wyatt died. The original plan was to make an engraving (a printed copy) of the design. But instead, Wyatt decided to buy an oil painting. It was half the size of Turner’s usual paintings, and it cost 100 guineas (a lot of money back then).

The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. Years later, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing scenes of Oxford for his most famous series of watercolors. He didn’t pick the High Street again. He felt that in this painting, he had already created a perfect view—one so technically amazing that he couldn’t do it again.

CuratorialText: Together with a St Francis (Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (National Gallery of Wales), WA1850.13 and WA1850.13.1 come from a lost altar-piece by Granacci, painted around 1520. The four panels may have been placed in the pilasters. Granacci was a follower of Ghirlandaio who was particularly interested in the art of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.
persona1_adapted:
This painting, along with three others, was once part of a larger artwork called an altarpiece. An altarpiece is a big painting or sculpture placed behind a church altar. These pieces were painted around 1520 by an artist named Granacci.

Granacci was a student of another famous artist, Ghirlandaio. But Granacci was especially inspired by the work of two other artists: Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.

CuratorialText: These are pinnacle panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece, which may have been crowned with a central pinnacle panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was Florentine, working in a late Gothic manner and an attribution to Bartolomeo di Fruosino has recently been suggested. A painter and miniaturist, Bartolomeo was a pupil of Agnolo Gaddi.
persona1_adapted: These tall, pointed panels were once part of the same unknown altarpiece. It may have had a central panel showing Jesus on the cross. The artist was from Florence, Italy, and painted in a late Gothic style. Experts recently suggested the artist might be Bartolomeo di Fruosino. He was a painter and book illustrator who studied under Agnolo Gaddi.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona1_adapted:
**The Hunt in the Forest: A Nighttime Adventure**

Imagine a dark forest at night, filled with hunters, dogs, and deer. That’s the scene in *The Hunt in the Forest*, a painting by Paolo di Dono—better known as Uccello (which means “Bird” in Italian). This artwork is special because it shows a hunt happening at night, which was unusual for its time. Hunting was a fancy hobby for rich people, but a nighttime hunt? That’s playful and might be more about imagination than real life.

Uccello was a master of making things look three-dimensional on a flat surface. To do this, he first drew a grid on the wooden panel and placed a single point in the center to pull your eyes deep into the scene. He added clever details—like the hunters’ spears, cut branches, logs, and water in the front—to guide your gaze toward the people and animals fading into the distance.

The painting was meant to be seen by candlelight. Tiny gold flakes in the leaves would have sparkled and seemed to move as the candles flickered, making the dark forest come alive. Uccello was famous for his skill with perspective, animals, and landscapes. He also worked on mosaics and stained glass, and you can see all those talents combined in this masterpiece.

*The Hunt in the Forest* is a type of painting called a *spalliera* (say: spah-lee-AIR-ah). It was hung at shoulder height, either on the back of a fancy chest or set into wooden wall panels. We don’t know who paid for it, but it was definitely made for a grand Renaissance palace—maybe in Urbino, where Uccello worked in 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The portrait corresponds generally to a number of paintings and prints of Louix XIII of France on a prancing horse. From the type and handling of the paint, it seems to be either by or after a Flemish painter. The appearance of the king suggests a date of about 1622.
persona1_adapted: This painting looks a lot like other portraits of King Louis XIII of France riding a prancing horse. Based on the style and how the paint was applied, it was probably made by a Flemish artist (or someone copying that artist’s work). The way the king looks in the picture suggests it was painted around 1622.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.
persona1_adapted:
You can tell this is Mary Magdalene because she’s holding a jar of ointment. That’s a clue from a Bible story where a woman washed Jesus’ feet with perfume and dried them with her hair (Luke 7:36–50).  

This small painting might have been part of a bigger picture at first. Later, someone added extra wood around it and painted more of her body to make it larger. The artist probably worked in Parma, Italy, in the early 1600s, and was part of a group of painters influenced by Bartolomeo Schedoni.

CuratorialText: The landscape is reminiscent of Éragny. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium. In: Jon Whiteley, Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum, (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum 2022)
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a landscape that looks like the countryside around Éragny, a village in France. It was probably painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, just before the artist’s son, Félix Pissarro, moved away to Belgium.

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting is a copy of part of a famous ceiling fresco in Rome. The original was made by Raphael and his team in 1518–1519. It shows scenes from the myth of Cupid and Psyche. The part copied here might have been painted by Raphael's top assistant, Giulio Romano. The copy was made in the late 1600s or early 1700s. The artist who made this copy changed the shape from a triangle to a square.

CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona1_adapted: Some of the objects in this painting show up in other works by Renoir from his later years. For example, the silver cup appears in a small still life that was supposedly painted in 1910, both with and without a knife. The bronze-colored child statue on the column was included in a small study from around 1905. The double salt holder made of faience—a type of glazed pottery that was mass-produced in the 1800s in places like Desvres, France, and Quimper, Brittany—shows up in at least four other sketches. You can also see a ghostly image of the same child statue from behind, floating in the upper right corner. This is probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir started his career painting on ceramics, so he likely owned several pieces of this popular faience pottery and used different colorful ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and has affinities with the work of Andrea in the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome c.1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.
persona1_adapted: This painting is a portrait of a woman we don't know the name of. Even though it's damaged, you can tell it's very well made. It looks a lot like the work of an artist named Andrea from the 1520s. Andrea spent most of his career in the Italian cities of Siena and Florence, except for a short trip to Rome around 1516. He often worked with his brother, Raffaello Piccinelli.

CuratorialText: After 1739, Ruysch painted mainly small-scale paintings of bouquets and posies, inscribed with her age, as here. The arrangement of curving stems, full, rounded roses and a number of smaller flowers looks back to the work of Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered
persona1_adapted: After 1739, Ruysch mostly painted small bouquets and flower arrangements. She often wrote her age on the paintings, just like in this one. The way the stems curve, the big round roses, and the smaller flowers are inspired by another artist named Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has never been found.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona1_adapted:
This panel was once part of a larger artwork called an altarpiece, made between 1433 and 1435 for a church in Florence, Italy. The altarpiece was broken up over time, and its pieces are now in different museums around the world.

The main part of the altarpiece showed Mary holding baby Jesus, surrounded by four angels. On either side were pairs of saints, including Saint Benedict, Saint Nicholas, John the Baptist, and another saint (probably Saint Matthew). 

The bottom section, called the predella, told stories from the life of Saint Nicholas. Five of these story panels still exist today. They show:
- The birth of Saint Nicholas
- Pilgrims visiting his shrine
- Saint Nicholas bringing three young people back to life
- Saint Nicholas giving money to three poor sisters (this is why he is often shown with three gold balls)
- Saint Nicholas calming a storm

The panels probably had a four-leaf clover shape around them. The artist, Bicci, based his design on an earlier altarpiece by another famous artist, Gentile da Fabriano.

CuratorialText: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind. He holds a terracotta figure of a saint, perhaps the Virgin Mary. The painting has been tentatively attributed to the circle of the de Critz family.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of a professional sculptor. He’s in his studio, and you can see some of his artwork on the shelf behind him. He’s holding a small clay figure of a saint—possibly the Virgin Mary. Experts think this painting was made by someone in the de Critz family’s workshop, though they’re not completely sure.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.
persona1_adapted: These calligraphy pages were all written by the same person. Most of them are *kanshi*—Chinese poems. The artist signed them using different versions of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems have dates: 1878 and 1880. A few others mention where the poem came from or where it was written. If the calligraphy was made at the same time as the paintings, then this album is an early work by Sobun—made before he started showing his art in big national and international exhibitions.

CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
persona1_adapted:
This painting started out in 1857 with the title *The Mother's Grave*. At first, it only showed a sad boy standing by his mother's grave. We know this from an early sketch that's now in the Ashmolean Museum.

The artist, Arthur Hughes, began painting the outdoor scene in the summer of 1856 in an old churchyard in Chingford, Essex. About five years later, around 1862, he changed the background and added the boy's sister. His wife, Tryphena, posed as the model for the sister.

Hughes filled the painting with small details that make you feel the sadness of loss. Look closely at the spiderwebs, dewdrops, wild roses, and dandelion seeds. All these things are delicate and don't last long—just like life itself. Even the lamb on the other side of the tomb is separated from its mother, reminding us of the boy's own loss.

CuratorialText: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) succeeded his father as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace and further enriched the family collection of specimens of natural and man-made curiosity. He is shown here with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer (for which Zythepsa is the Greek) from Lambeth, who may have contributed the shells to Tradescant's museum.
persona1_adapted: John Tradescant the Younger (1608–1662) took over his father’s job as the keeper of the king’s gardens at Oatlands Palace. He added even more cool and unusual items to the family’s collection of natural and man-made objects. In this painting, he’s shown with his friend Roger Friend, a brewer from Lambeth. (The Greek word for brewing is “Zythepsa.”) Roger might have given the shells that were part of Tradescant’s museum.

CuratorialText: As one of the leading members of post-war generation of St Ives artists, Lanyon was primarily interested in the representation of landscape. This work epitomises the tension between abstraction and figuration in an otherwise carefully descriptive work.
persona1_adapted: Peter Lanyon was a key artist in the generation that came after World War II in St Ives, England. He mainly focused on painting landscapes. This artwork shows a push and pull between abstract shapes and realistic details, even though the scene is carefully described.

CuratorialText: Bundi, Rajasthan, c.1760-70. Gouache with gold on paper. Attended by a drowsy maid, a young noble couple sleep in close embrace on a palace terrace, sheltered by a shamiana or awning. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie close to hand.
persona1_adapted:
This painting is from Bundi, a place in Rajasthan, India, and was made around 1760–1770. The artist used a type of paint called gouache, along with real gold, on paper.

In the scene, a young prince and princess are sleeping closely together on a palace terrace. They are under a special canopy called a *shamiana* (an awning). A sleepy maid is nearby, watching over them. Even though the couple is resting, the prince’s sword and arrows are lying right next to him, ready to be used.

CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.
persona1_adapted:
**A Portrait, Not a Nun**

This painting was once called *A Nun Playing a Piano* when it was sold in 1919 and again in 1936. Why? Because the woman in the painting is wearing a long veil, which makes her look like a nun.

But there’s no other reason to think she’s a nun. In fact, it’s almost certainly a portrait of the artist’s daughter, Desirée-Marie Ribot (known as Louise). The artist often used her as a model. She was born in 1857, and her young face helps us guess when the painting was made—probably in the late 1860s or early 1870s. That’s before the artist’s painting style became thicker and more textured.

The dark background and the simple, homey scene remind us of the work of two other artists: François Bonvin and Alphonse Legros. Bonvin was an early supporter of Ribot, and both Bonvin and Legros were interested in Spanish art.

CuratorialText: The inscription on this painting reads ‘Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors, the proud [chrysanthemum] are falling but their beauty remains. In fading moonlight I [stayed] with these flowers while they cooled, versing through this cool evening in autumn.’
persona1_adapted: The writing on this painting says: “A breeze from the west blows through the curtains on the doors. The proud chrysanthemums are falling, but they are still beautiful. In the fading moonlight, I stayed with these flowers as they grew cold, writing poems on this cool autumn evening.”

CuratorialText: Sinnott is well known for his complex figurative compositions. He often deals with the human figure in a context. Although the same fluid brush-strokes can be seen in the background and in the figures, the two wrestling men can be distinguished by a lighter touch and a slight variation in the use of paint. The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.
persona1_adapted: Sinnott is known for making detailed paintings of people. He often shows the human body in a specific setting. You can see the same smooth brushstrokes in both the background and the people. But the two men wrestling stand out because the artist used a lighter touch and a slightly different way of applying paint. In some spots, the first layer of paint shows through the top layers, which makes the painting look like it was made very quickly.

CuratorialText: The only securely documented painting by the artist is an altar-piece of 1447 now in the Museo Civico, Padua. A follower of Michele Giambono, Francesco may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Together with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. St Catherine, of royal birth, wears a crown and carries her martyr's palm; the wheel refers to earlier attempts at her martyrdom.
persona1_adapted:
Here is the adapted text for an 8th-grade reader:

We know for sure that this artist painted only one thing: an altar-piece from 1447, which is now in a museum in Padua, Italy. The artist was a follower of Michele Giambono, and he may have worked with another artist named Antonio Vivarini in Venice. This panel, along with another one in the museum, was probably part of a larger altar-piece for a church in Padua or Venice. The person in the painting is Saint Catherine. She was born into a royal family, so she wears a crown and carries a palm branch, which is a symbol of her martyrdom. The wheel in the painting is a clue to how people tried to kill her before she was finally executed.

CuratorialText: The grief-stricken figures of the Virgin and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful devotional image; it may have been the right wing of a portable diptych, although no hinge-marks are now visible. The anonymous artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (to whom WA1921.3 has also been attributed) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in mid 14th-century Florence
persona1_adapted:
This small painting shows the Virgin Mary and St. John the Evangelist filled with grief. It was likely meant to help people pray and reflect on their faith. The panel might have been the right side of a small, two-part folding artwork called a diptych, though we can’t see any hinge marks today.

The artist is unknown, but their style is very similar to Jacopo di Cione (who also made another painting in this museum). This artist probably trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, who was the most famous artist in Florence during the mid-1300s.

CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona1_adapted:
In 1573, the artist Veronese painted a famous version of *The Last Supper* for a church in Venice. But the religious authorities, called the Inquisition, accused him of being disrespectful (blasphemy) because of some of the details he included. To avoid trouble, Veronese simply changed the title of the painting to *The Feast in the House of Levi*—a story from the Bible that allowed for more colorful and lively scenes.

The original painting is very large and now hangs in a museum in Venice. Many artists made copies of it. The painting in this museum (WA1855.159) is a smaller, high-quality copy made by a Venetian artist around the mid-1700s, most likely Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: A companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1800.
persona1_adapted: This painting is a companion piece to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764–1815), which is also in the museum's collection (WA1934.422). Both paintings were shown at the Royal Academy in 1800.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.
persona1_adapted:
**A Father and Son Paint the Same View**

This painting was made in 1886, when Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien were both using a technique called pointillism. That’s where you paint with tiny dots of color instead of brushstrokes.

The scene shows a bend in a path near Éragny, a village in France. In the middle of the painting, you can see the spire (the tall, pointy top) of the Saint-Martin church. On the right is a brick manor house from the 1500s. A river called the Epte runs behind the line of trees that cuts across the picture.

In 1887, Lucien painted almost the same view, but in watercolor. If you compare the two paintings, you’ll notice something interesting: Lucien made the church and manor look much bigger than they really were. He also left out some farm buildings that stood in front of the manor. Today, trees hide most of these buildings from that spot on the path.

Lucien probably knew that his father had painted the exact same view from the same place in 1884. But Camille’s version used a looser, more spontaneous style—the way he painted in the early 1880s. So even though father and son stood in the same spot, they saw the scene in very different ways.

CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
persona1_adapted:
This painting shows a faraway view of the village of Lauris in southern France. On the left, you can see a castle and garden terrace built in the 1700s. In the background are the mountains of the Petit Luberon.

The artist, Paul Guigou, painted this scene during the dry season. He stood or sat on one of the many sandbanks in the Durance River, which flows between the towns of Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron.

Like most of Guigou’s landscapes from this region, the air looks clear and the sunlight is strong. He may have been inspired by other artists like Corot or Daubigny, who also liked wide, panoramic views. But Guigou had his own special style. He painted the sky, sand, and river with thin layers of paint, then added small, bright dabs and quick, short brushstrokes to make the scene feel lively.

CuratorialText: This painting, which is a cosmic landscape with Daoist undertones, was produced in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003) who wished for something with a predominantly blue colour. The inscription reads ‘To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.’
persona1_adapted: This painting is a space-like landscape with hints of Daoist ideas. It was made in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919–2003), who asked for something mostly blue. The writing on it says, “To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.”

CuratorialText: One of Lucien's most extrovert works, painted in June 1925 and illustrating the broom in full flower at Le Brusq.
persona1_adapted: This painting is one of Lucien’s most lively and outgoing works. He made it in June 1925, and it shows a broom plant in full bloom at a place called Le Brusq.

CuratorialText: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) was educated at Oriel College, Oxford, before entering the church. He was Headmaster of Winchester College between 1766 and 1793 and an accomplished poet. This portrait was painted and exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1777, the year in which Warton was also elected to 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, the Poet Laureate, whose portrait Reynolds also painted (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).
persona1_adapted: Joseph Warton (1722–1800) went to Oriel College at Oxford University before becoming a church leader. He was the head of Winchester College from 1766 to 1793 and also a talented poet. This portrait was painted and shown at the Royal Academy in 1777, the same year Warton was chosen to join a famous group called "The Club." He was the brother of Thomas Warton, who was the Poet Laureate (the official poet of the country). The same artist, Reynolds, also painted Thomas’s portrait.

CuratorialText: The influence of Vuillard is strongly marked. There is a less finished version of the same composition on the back
persona1_adapted: You can really see Vuillard's influence here. On the back of the painting, there's a rougher, less finished version of the same scene.

CuratorialText: Bollongier was one of the few painters of the first half of the 17th century in Haarlem who specialised in painting flowers. His work seems to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school but with an added naturalism in the arrangement of the flowers. The glass container in this painting was a favourite accessory. It appears first in a work of 1627 and for the last time in a flower piece of 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).
persona1_adapted: In the early 1600s, a painter named Bollongier was one of the few artists in Haarlem who focused on painting flowers. His style was inspired by another group of painters called the Bosschaert school, but he made his flowers look more natural and less stiff. In this painting, you can see a glass vase that Bollongier really liked to use. He first painted it in 1627 and used it for the last time in a flower painting from 1639, which is now in the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.
persona1_adapted:
**The Story of Baby Moses (Exodus 2:2-4)**

Long ago, the Pharaoh of Egypt ordered that all newborn Hebrew baby boys be killed. To save her son, Moses’s mother placed him in a small basket made of reeds (called an “ark of bulrushes”) and set it in the Nile River. Moses’s sister stayed nearby to watch over him. Soon, Pharaoh’s daughter came to the river with her servants, found the baby, and decided to take care of him.

**About This Painting (WA1950.169)**

This painting is special because it shows a very emotional moment—Moses’s parents are heartbroken and grieving. Most artists painted the happy scene where Moses is found, but this sad moment is much rarer. The artist, Nicolas Poussin, painted it in 1654 for a man named Jacques Stella. People at the time loved it because Poussin was very learned, painted reflections beautifully, and created a lovely landscape.

**Fun Details in the Painting**

- The city in the background is based on an artist’s reconstruction of famous buildings from ancient Rome.
- A sphinx (a mythical creature with a lion’s body and human head) is shown with a river-god and a cornucopia (a horn of plenty) to represent the Nile River.
- There are pipes of Pan hanging from a tree behind the group. Scholars have different ideas about what they mean, based on Poussin’s love of ancient history and art.

**Copies and Versions**

- Two painted copies were made in the 1600s.
- Other copies and variations exist, including a tapestry version of the right side.
- Three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was an eclectic artist who painted numerous works for domestic settings. This type of decorative, ceremonial subject is often found on cassone panels. Although the picture is much abraded, it has sufficient affinities with the mature work of Lo Scheggia to make the attribution convincing.
persona1_adapted: Lo Scheggia, the brother of the famous artist Masaccio, was a versatile painter who created many artworks for people's homes. This kind of fancy, ceremonial scene was commonly painted on wedding chests called *cassoni*. Even though this painting is worn and damaged, it still looks a lot like Lo Scheggia's later work, which is why experts believe he painted it.

CuratorialText: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris with Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He exhibited at the Royal Academy from 1880 and came into contact with Giovanni Costa and his circle of Etruscans in Italy. This slight sketch from nature probably dates from the last years of the 19th century.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

Lemon studied art in Rome and Paris with a famous teacher named Carolus-Duran. But he grew up in California. Starting in 1880, he showed his work at the Royal Academy in London. In Italy, he met an artist named Giovanni Costa and his group, the Etruscans. This small, quick sketch from real life was probably made in the late 1890s.

CuratorialText: The subject, the flat and dry handling and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to c.1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.
persona1_adapted: The person in the painting, the simple and flat way it's painted, and the large, powerful feel of the figures all show how Shannon was influenced by another artist named Puvis de Chavannes. This painting was made around 1910 and is a different version of one of Shannon's favorite subjects.

CuratorialText: Views in and around the village of Berck in northern France appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This view across the dunes is precisely dated. The unbroken horizon and solitary figure in the foreground emphasize the windswept emptiness of the site.
persona1_adapted: The artist painted many scenes of Berck, a village in northern France, from 1870 until he died in 1898. This painting shows a view across the sand dunes, and we know exactly when it was made. The flat horizon and the single person in the front make the place feel empty and windy.

CuratorialText: Traditionally but wrongly said to be a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, A863a is a portrait of a musician or instrument-maker of the late 16th century and identifications of Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) and Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609) have been proposed.
persona1_adapted: People used to think this painting was a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, but that's not true. It actually shows a musician or instrument maker from the late 1500s. Some experts have guessed it might be Claudio Monteverdi (1567–1643) or Gasparo da Salò (1540–1609).

CuratorialText: During his later years, Calvert produced many small works in thin oil colours on paper, the restrained colours and muted harmonies evocative of a dream world. They are generally peopled with figures from Greek mythology. Painted on a pink ground.
persona1_adapted: In his later years, Calvert made many small paintings on paper. He used thin oil paints and soft, quiet colors that feel like a dream. Most of the paintings show characters from Greek myths. He painted them on a pink background.

CuratorialText: Traditionally attributed to Guido Reni as the Head of a Sibyl, WA1926.9 was recently identified as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive head appears in his altar-piece of The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes painted for San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now in the convent there.
persona1_adapted:
For a long time, people thought this painting was by an artist named Guido Reni. They called it *Head of a Sibyl*. But recently, experts figured out it’s actually a copy of a work by another artist, Cerrini.

The same face appears in one of Cerrini’s religious paintings. That painting shows the Holy Family with two saints. It was made for a church in Rome in 1646 and is still kept in the convent there.

CuratorialText: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union for the purposes of engraving in 1838. It was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with the 'Ancients' at Shoreham. Samuel Palmer was the model for the disciple on the right. The source is Luke 24: 13-35.
persona1_adapted: This painting is one of the artist Linnell’s most important religious works. It was shown at the Royal Academy in 1835 and later sold in 1838 to the Art Union, which made engravings (printed copies) of it. Linnell based the painting on sketches he made in 1829, when he was part of a group of artists called the “Ancients” in Shoreham. The disciple on the right side of the painting is actually a portrait of Samuel Palmer, another artist in the group. The story comes from the Bible, Luke 24: 13-35.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Readers:**

Design is something that connects people everywhere, no matter when or where they lived. This exhibit brings together two types of art that are usually shown separately: fashion and textiles (like clothing and fabric) with decorative and industrial arts (like furniture, glassware, and everyday objects). The goal is to help us understand why we design things and what drives us to be creative.

You’ll see outfits from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection. Iris Apfel is a famous fashion icon known for mixing and matching styles in bold, fun ways. Nearby, you’ll find groups of unique and important works of design, fashion, and textiles that show different kinds of creative thinking.

A special part of the gallery features 38 colorful and flashy outfits from the collection of Yolanda Cellucci, a fashion icon and business owner from Boston. Her shop, Yolanda’s, was in Waltham, Massachusetts. It was famous for fancy evening gowns and wedding dresses. In the late 1900s, the shop became a household name in the area—a symbol of glitz and glamour. In 2021, Cellucci generously donated 57 fashion pieces and accessories to the museum, along with old photos and materials that tell the story of her bridal shop.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.
persona1_adapted:
Edmonia Lewis was born in Greenbush, New York, in 1844. She became the first sculptor with both Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) heritage to be famous around the world. She started her career in Boston in 1863, and by 1866 she moved to Rome, Italy, to join the top American sculptors of her time. Along the way, she broke through barriers of race, gender, and nationality.

In 1878, Lewis said, “Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way. That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.”

After she died in 1907, Lewis’s legacy lived on in Black communities, but her work wasn’t fully recognized for a long time. Some of her greatest sculptures were rediscovered decades later, while others are still missing today. Her stone carvings are vivid and lifelike. They show abolitionists and social reformers, explore her Indigenous identity, and give new life to religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography. The boreal forest is critical to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land biome, is a critical water reservoir and holds two to three times as much carbon as tropical forests.
persona1_adapted: The boreal forest is one of the biggest wild places left on Earth. It stretches across the top of the world, just below the Arctic Circle. This huge forest is home to 3.7 million people, 85 kinds of animals, 32,000 types of insects, and 2 billion migrating birds. But it’s not just big—it’s super important for the whole planet. The boreal forest helps control Earth’s climate, stores two to three times more carbon than tropical forests, and acts as a giant water tank. Through real stories, special objects, hands-on activities, and amazing photos and videos, you can explore why this northern forest matters so much.

CuratorialText: Salem’s connections to the Declaration of Independence center around Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and disseminating the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition coincides with the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution as well as the 400th anniversary of the European settlement of Salem. Works on view in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest broadside editions of the Declaration of Independence alongside Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets and broadsides that showcase the nation’s founding values of democracy: freedom, liberty and equality.
persona1_adapted:
**Salem and the Declaration of Independence**

Did you know that Salem helped spread the news of America’s independence? A local printer named Ezekiel Russell was a key player in getting the Declaration of Independence printed and shared with the public in 1776.

This exhibition celebrates two big anniversaries: 250 years since the American Revolution and 400 years since European settlers first came to Salem. In the small James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery, you’ll see some of the very first printed copies of the Declaration of Independence. You’ll also find letters, newspapers, pamphlets, and posters from the Revolutionary era. Together, they show the core ideas that America was built on: freedom, liberty, and equality.

CuratorialText: These films honor the lesser-known stories of Salem and offer a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Produced over the course of more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera and many other filmmakers for presentation at the Salem Film Fest, these short films span topics from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime Salem residents, these stories might spark nostalgia; for new residents and tourists, they illuminate Salem’s vibrant communities, bygone businesses and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to record and preserve. Beyond the Broom showcases the vibrant community of Salem, one with an identity that extends beyond its witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which commemorates the quadricentennial of the city’s settlement while honoring the preexisting Indigenous community. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and ensure a vibrant and sustainable city for generations to come.
persona1_adapted:
These short films shine a light on the lesser-known stories of Salem and show what makes the city so special. Filmmakers Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera, and many others worked on them for over seven years. They were made for the Salem Film Fest. The films cover everything from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows.

If you’ve lived in Salem for a long time, these stories might bring back memories. If you’re new or just visiting, they give you a look at Salem’s lively communities, old businesses, and traditions that local filmmakers have worked hard to save and share.

*Beyond the Broom* celebrates Salem’s real community—one that’s about much more than just the witch trials. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which marks 400 years since the city was settled, while also honoring the Indigenous people who lived here long before. The goal is to bring the Salem community together and help make sure the city stays vibrant and strong for years to come.

CuratorialText: Explore several hundred fascinating objects inside the historic hall, representing a sampling of the many collections that were displayed in the museum during its earliest years. This installation gives voice to early collectors, trading partners and museum visitors about these objects — with some accounts extracted from records in the museum’s vast archive — and shares perspectives from a range of people living today. These captivating stories and works that reflect local and global history, as well as the impact of maritime trade, recenter East India Marine Hall as the heart of the museum. From its inception, this space and the objects in it have elicited empathy, curiosity and wonder and transported visitors to places around the world. This project has been made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support is provided by Mr. Ulf B. Heide, Tim and Joanie Ingraham, Alex Ingraham, and Sam Ingraham. Generous support has been provided by Haven Trust, Mr. and Mrs. James B. Hawkes, Mr. and Mrs. Howard B. Hodgson Jr., Angus and Leslie Littlejohn, Chip and Susan Robie, Mr. and Mrs. Stuart W. Pratt, Katie Huddleson and Alan Kraning, Edward A. Studzinski and Anne G. Studzinski, the Salem Marine Society, and Henry Birdseye Weil and Ann Uppington. Additional support has been provided by The Lee and Juliet Folger Fund, Jonathan B. Loring, Walter C. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.
persona1_adapted:
**Step into the Past: A Hall Full of Stories**

Step inside this historic hall and discover hundreds of amazing objects. They’re a sample of the very first things people saw when the museum opened, many years ago.

This exhibit lets you hear from the people who collected these objects, the traders who brought them here, and the early visitors who saw them. Some of their stories come from old records kept in the museum’s archives. You’ll also hear from people today, sharing their own thoughts and ideas.

These objects and their stories show us local and world history, and how trade by sea changed everything. They remind us that this hall has always been the heart of the museum. From the very beginning, this space has made people feel curious, amazed, and connected to places far away.

**Thank you to our supporters:**

This project was made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities. Major support comes from Mr. Ulf B. Heide, Tim and Joanie Ingraham, Alex Ingraham, and Sam Ingraham. Generous support from Haven Trust, Mr. and Mrs. James B. Hawkes, Mr. and Mrs. Howard B. Hodgson Jr., Angus and Leslie Littlejohn, Chip and Susan Robie, Mr. and Mrs. Stuart W. Pratt, Katie Huddleson and Alan Kraning, Edward A. Studzinski and Anne G. Studzinski, the Salem Marine Society, and Henry Birdseye Weil and Ann Uppington. Additional support from The Lee and Juliet Folger Fund, Jonathan B. Loring, Walter C. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.

CuratorialText: Renowned for its expansive holdings of decorative arts and paintings, PEM’s American art collection spans four centuries of creative expression in the United States. Rooted in our historic holdings that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, the collection has continued to grow to embrace a broader representation of the art of this country. The artists represented in this Seamans gallery use a variety of materials — wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber and more — to convey their creative ambitions. Many of the artistic practices and decorative inspirations behind these works have long histories, with their origins traceable to Europe, Asia and the Americas.
