These examples of formal perfection, domestic utility and luxury, and innovative twists on cultural traditions tell a larger story of our global connectedness. Together, these works tell compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between the people of the region, the country and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
PEM’s American art collection is famous for its huge selection of decorative arts and paintings. It shows over 400 years of creative expression in the United States. The collection started with pieces that celebrate the rich and diverse art and culture of Essex County, Massachusetts. Over time, it has grown to include art from all over the country.

In this Seamans gallery, artists use many different materials—like wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, and fiber—to share their creative ideas. Many of the techniques and designs behind these works have long histories, with roots in Europe, Asia, and the Americas. Some pieces are perfect examples of fine art, others are useful household items or luxury goods, and some put a new spin on old traditions. Together, they tell a bigger story about how people around the world are connected.

These works show us what life was like in America and how people from this region, the country, and the wider world have shared ideas and culture over time. You can find this gallery in the Seamans Gallery.

CuratorialText: Renowned for its expansive holdings of decorative arts and paintings, PEM’s American art collection spans four centuries of creative expression in the United States. Rooted in our historic holdings that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, the collection has continued to grow to embrace a broader representation of the art of this country. The artists represented in this Seamans gallery use a variety of materials — wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber and more — to convey their creative ambitions. Many of the artistic practices and decorative inspirations behind these works have long histories, with their origins traceable to Europe, Asia and the Americas. These examples of formal perfection, domestic utility and luxury, and innovative twists on cultural traditions tell a larger story of our global connectedness. Together, these works tell compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between the people of the region, the country and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.
persona1_adapted: PEM’s American art collection shows how people in the United States have expressed themselves creatively over the past 400 years. It started with artworks from Essex County, Massachusetts, but has grown to include art from all over the country. In this Seamans Gallery, artists use many different materials—like wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, and fiber—to bring their ideas to life. Many of the techniques and designs in these works come from long traditions that began in Europe, Asia, and the Americas. Some pieces are beautiful and perfect in form, some are useful everyday objects, and others put a fresh spin on old cultural traditions. Together, they tell a bigger story about how people in America are connected to each other and to the rest of the world. You can find this gallery in the Seamans Gallery.

CuratorialText: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles and miniature paintings inspire the precise, stylized floral forms that compose Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural chamber of light and shadow. This luminous installation located in the Wheatland Family gallery provides an opportunity to contemplate the differences and commonalities that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the US, Agha is acutely attuned to the social codes that inform the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her effort to create a sense of how women can reclaim and safely open up private space to welcome others. Located in the Wheatland Family Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
This artwork is a room filled with light and shadow, covered in beautiful, repeating flower patterns. The artist, Anila Quayyum Agha, was inspired by designs from Persian and Turkish art—like those found in buildings, fabrics, and tiny paintings.

Agha grew up in Lahore, Pakistan, and now lives in the United States. She understands the unwritten rules that shape the lives of Muslim women and people who move to a new country. She created this glowing space to show how women can take a private area and make it feel safe and welcoming for others.

You can find this artwork in the Wheatland Family Gallery.

CuratorialText:
Merchants across the globe went to great lengths to acquire these spectacular commodities. Now known as Asian export art, these objects connected societies and created a complex global economy that continues to shape our world to this day.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, foremost in the world, explores cross-cultural exchange as a catalyst for creativity and celebrates the interplay of commerce and creative expression. The gallery features more than 200 works of art made in diverse media by artists in China, Japan, and South Asia. These transcultural objects demonstrate the beauty and ingenuity that can be created through blending artistic traditions, materials, and technologies. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the focus of intensive trade activity between Asia and the rest of the world. This installation in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also addresses the uncomfortable truth that many of these works of art were originally purchased with profits derived from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of Indian and Chinese lives were devastated by opium, a foreshadowing of today’s opioid crisis.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Readers**

Long ago, merchants all over the world worked hard to get amazing goods from Asia. Today, we call these items "Asian export art." They connected different cultures and helped create a global economy that still affects us today.

PEM has the world’s best collection of Asian export art. It shows how people from different places shared ideas and made beautiful things together. The gallery has more than 200 artworks from China, Japan, and South Asia. Artists used many materials like clay, silk, silver, and ivory. These objects mix different art styles, materials, and techniques to create something new and clever.

Porcelain (fine china), textiles (fabrics), tea, ivory, and silver were some of the most traded items between Asia and the rest of the world. But this gallery also tells a harder truth: many of these artworks were bought with money from the illegal opium trade. In the 1800s, opium destroyed the lives of millions of people in India and China. This was an early version of the opioid crisis we face today.

CuratorialText: This exhibition explores America as a place where disparate viewpoints are brought together: America as both longed for and greatly revered. Previously on view in the context of PEM's 2019 Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle exhibition, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal presents a solo exhibition of Collins’ art as an immersive experience. Explore 100 different versions of the popular patriotic anthem “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 18th and 20th centuries in support of various American causes. Versions of the lyrics are bound together chronologically in Collins’ artist book, where she burns away the musical notes with a laser — chronicling an idea of America through the history of one song’s varied verses. A range of these powerful voices and expressions are heard throughout the gallery in a sound piece, what Collins calls, “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The gallery is wallpapered with a newly commissioned work of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower contains a meaning as the artist uses floriography, the symbolic language of flowers, to relay messages of hope, longing and loss to further illustrate the evolution and complexity of American experiences. In addition to the artwork on view, a study center offers deeper dives into Collins' artistic approaches and historical sources. Through interactives, PEM collection objects and media components, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal inspires us to activate our own civic mindset. Located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th Grade Reader:**

This exhibition looks at America as a place where different ideas come together—a country that people both dream about and deeply respect. It was first shown as part of PEM's 2019 *Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle* exhibition. Now, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* is a solo show that surrounds you with art.

You can explore 100 different versions of the famous patriotic song “My Country ‘Tis of Thee.” People rewrote these lyrics between the 1700s and 1900s to support different American causes. Collins put all the lyrics together in a book, but she used a laser to burn away the musical notes. This shows how one song’s changing words tell a story about America.

In the gallery, you’ll hear a sound piece that Collins calls “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” It mixes many different voices and ideas.

The walls are covered with a new artwork made of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower has a special meaning. Collins uses floriography—the language of flowers—to share messages of hope, longing, and loss. This helps show how American experiences have changed and grown more complex over time.

There’s also a study center where you can learn more about Collins’ art and the history behind it. With hands-on activities, objects from PEM’s collection, and media, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* encourages you to think about your own role as a citizen.

**Location:** Jurrien Timmer Gallery

CuratorialText: Encompassing everyday objects and the fine arts, the museum’s Japanese collection is distinguished for its range, from paintings and sculpture to decorative arts and costumes and textiles. Spanning Hokkaido in the North to Okinawa in the South, and prehistory to the present, these objects illuminate the varied artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. Toward the end of the 19th century, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a precursor to PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. Morse was highly influential in encouraging American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped create this remarkable Japanese collection.
persona1_adapted:
The museum’s Japanese collection has all kinds of objects, from everyday items to fine art. It includes paintings, sculptures, costumes, and textiles. The collection covers the whole country of Japan, from Hokkaido in the north to Okinawa in the south, and spans from ancient times to today. These objects show the many different art and cultural traditions of Japan.

In the late 1800s, a man named Edward Sylvester Morse visited Japan. He was the director of the Peabody Academy of Science, which later became part of PEM. Morse was one of the first Western scholars to visit Japan after it opened up to travelers. He helped spark American interest in Japanese art and culture, and he played a big role in building this amazing collection.

CuratorialText: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today, PEM’s exceptional collection explores art, culture, and life in Korea during a time of significant transition and global connection. PEM’s early Korean collection was primarily formed by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a predecessor organization to PEM), who proactively pursued Korean acquisitions during his tenure from 1880 to 1914. Among his most significant acquisitions from this period is a set of eight Korean musical instruments played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition. PEM’s connection with Korea was also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856-1914), the reformist scholar and politician who came to the U.S. as a member of the first Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, presented alongside the museum’s renowned Korean textile collection, exquisite 19th-century paintings — including the Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan screen — and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.
persona1_adapted:
**Korea: Art, Culture, and Change**

PEM’s Korean collection shows how art, culture, and daily life in Korea changed from the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today. This was a time of big transitions and new connections with the world.

The museum’s early Korean collection was mostly built by Edward Sylvester Morse, who was the director of the Peabody Academy of Science (one of PEM’s earlier names) from 1880 to 1914. He actively collected Korean objects. One of his most important finds was a set of eight Korean musical instruments that were played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago.

PEM’s link to Korea also grew thanks to Yu Kil-Chun (1856–1914), a reform-minded scholar and politician. He came to the U.S. as part of the first official Korean delegation and lived near Salem, Massachusetts, for almost two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many important and meaningful works to the museum.

Today, the Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture at PEM brings these early stories to life. The gallery features the museum’s famous Korean textile collection, beautiful 19th-century paintings (including the *Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan* screen), and modern works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.

CuratorialText: PEM’s Maritime art collection, which is the finest in the country, frames the sea as an enduring source of opportunity as well as peril, a force that inspires creativity and innovation, and encourages engagement with the wider world. This installation located in the Byrne Family gallery offers a global perspective on our creative response to the sea, from a 17th-century brass Pakistani astrolabe to Valerie Hagerty’s monumental Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles (2012) and artworks that reflect Salem’s rich history of maritime trade and exploration. Immersive digital media amplify compelling stories that lay behind unassuming objects, like a calendar stick from 1803 whose carved notches record the long days that Rhode Island native, James Drown, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a remote speck island in the South Atlantic.
persona1_adapted:
The sea has always been a place of both danger and opportunity. It inspires people to create, explore, and connect with the world. PEM’s Maritime art collection—the best in the country—shows how people have responded to the ocean over time.

In the Byrne Family gallery, you’ll find art from around the world. There’s a brass astrolabe from 1600s Pakistan, which sailors used to navigate the stars. There’s also Valerie Hagerty’s huge *Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles* (2012), a piece that shows what happens when a shipwreck meets the sea. Other artworks tell the story of Salem’s history in trade and exploration.

Digital displays bring hidden stories to life. For example, a simple wooden calendar stick from 1803 has carved notches. Those notches record the long days that James Drown, a sailor from Rhode Island, spent stranded on a tiny island in the South Atlantic after his shipwreck.

CuratorialText: Bringing two extraordinary collections of Native American and American art together for the first time in our institution's history, this long-term installation celebrates artistic achievements across time, space, and worldviews. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the 250 artworks on display span in time from 10,000 years ago to the present, and demonstrate a range of voices, modes of expression, cultures, and media including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and costumes, furniture, decorative arts, works on paper, installations, video, and a re-envisioned period room. The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most iconic dramas in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem region to death. The new installation will be responsive to these urgent times by giving our public an opportunity to grapple with the links, continuities, and disjunctions of our complex histories in America in order to shape a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might envision a better future together.
persona1_adapted:
**Two Amazing Art Collections, Together for the First Time**

For the first time ever, our museum is showing two incredible collections side by side: Native American art and American art. This new, long-term exhibit celebrates art from many different times, places, and ways of seeing the world.

You’ll find 250 artworks in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and the Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery. These pieces span from 10,000 years ago all the way to today. They include sculptures, paintings, textiles, costumes, furniture, decorative objects, drawings, videos, and even a reimagined historical room. There are also original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials—one of the most famous and tragic events in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people to death in the Salem area.

This exhibit is designed for our times. It gives you a chance to think about the connections, differences, and surprises in America’s complex history. By looking at these artworks and stories, we can learn to understand each other better and imagine a more connected, caring future. The big question the exhibit asks is: How can we work together to create a better tomorrow?

CuratorialText: These figures, from disparate cultures around the world and across time, embody power as both a fundamental social dynamic and a part of our innate wiring to respond to figures and faces. Curved niches encourage visitors to engage with each artwork, decluttering the field of view and focusing attention. Poetically concise labels heighten the emotional tenor of the experience. Located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.
persona1_adapted: These sculptures come from different cultures and time periods around the world. They show power in two ways: as a basic part of how societies work, and as something we are naturally drawn to in faces and figures. Curved display spaces help you focus on each artwork without distractions. Short, poetic labels make the experience more emotional. This gallery is named after Louise Du Pont Crowninshield.

CuratorialText: The exhibition starts at “A is for Always Indigenous” to acknowledge the Native communities who have lived for millennia on the land where the museum now sits. It ends with Z is for Zoology and coincides with the return to the galleries of a leatherback turtle specimen captured in 1885, a favorite of longtime visitors. Salem is a city with many stories of local, national and international significance. Alexander Graham Bell completed the first successful long-distance telephone call from Salem in 1877. Parker Brothers produced Monopoly here. And in 2013, President Obama signed legislation recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Using selections from PEM’s vast collection, Salem Stories features more than 100 works, including paintings, sculpture, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A–Z structure creates an accessible and entertaining way to engage with Salem’s history, from past to present day. This is an evolving story, one that will continue to unfold throughout the life of the exhibition. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
**Salem Stories: From A to Z**

This exhibition starts with **A for “Always Indigenous”** to honor the Native people who have lived on this land for thousands of years. It ends with **Z for Zoology**, and it also brings back a favorite old exhibit: a leatherback turtle specimen caught in 1885.

Salem is a city full of big stories. Did you know that Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful long-distance phone call from Salem in 1877? Or that the board game Monopoly was made here? In 2013, President Obama even signed a law saying Salem is the birthplace of the U.S. National Guard.

Using items from PEM’s huge collection, *Salem Stories* shows over 100 objects. You’ll see paintings, sculptures, clothes, furniture, photos, animals, old letters, posters, books, firsthand accounts, and even a murder weapon.

The A–Z setup makes it fun and easy to explore Salem’s history, from the past all the way to today. And this story is still growing—new things will be added over time.

**Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.**

CuratorialText: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, mica paintings and Kalighat paintings, the gallery considers and questions the timeless tropes of India that persist even today.
persona1_adapted:
**The Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery**  
This gallery mixes myths with real history. It shows how India’s greatest 20th-century artists helped tell the story of building a new nation and finding their own identity. After India gained independence from British rule in 1947, artists wanted to create a style of art that was truly Indian. They used this new style to share their experiences, struggles, hopes, and dreams.

**The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery**  
This gallery shows objects from PEM’s large collection of historical Indian art. It focuses mostly on the 1800s. The gallery looks at India’s long history of being ruled by foreign powers and how that affected the way Indian people were shown in art. Some of the oldest objects in PEM’s collection are here, including unfired clay sculptures, mica paintings, and Kalighat paintings. The gallery asks us to think about the old, repeated ideas about India that still exist today.

CuratorialText: This diverse selection of contemporary glass represents the work of more than 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 20th century to today. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection is remarkable for its range of forms, scale and techniques in glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift significantly expands PEM’s noted historic glass collection while celebrating the extraordinary aesthetic possibilities that arise from the combination of sand and fire.
persona1_adapted: This collection of modern glass art shows the work of over 40 of the most important glass artists from the 1900s to today. It was given to PEM in 2022 by Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, collectors from New York. What makes this collection special is the wide variety of shapes, sizes, and glass-making methods you can see. The Pforzheimers’ gift makes PEM’s glass collection much bigger and better, while also celebrating the amazing beauty that can come from mixing sand and fire.

CuratorialText: The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, which investigates our place in nature through contemporary art, memorable objects and interactive experiences. Designed for visitors of all ages, the family-friendly center features installations, changing exhibitions, hands-on displays and art-making spaces. Grow your awareness of nature, try on new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline modeled on a live tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move as different animals and embody local habitats. And create your own imaginary creature inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the globe. What creative sense will be sparked in you on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod, describes her idea of a pod and what you’ll encounter when you visit our new multisensory exhibition space. There is something for everyone here, ranging from fabulous, nature-inspired contemporary and historic artworks to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone which includes activity bins, a light table and picture books just for them
persona1_adapted:
**The Pod: A Place to Explore Art and Nature**

The Pod is part of PEM’s Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center. It’s a space where you can think about how people fit into the natural world. You’ll see cool modern art, interesting objects, and get to try hands-on activities. It’s designed for all ages, so families, teens, and kids can all enjoy it.

Here, you can:
- See a life-size wire tree made by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline. It’s based on a real tree from PEM’s campus.
- Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move like different animals and show what local habitats feel like.
- Create your own imaginary creature, inspired by real animals and mythical beasts from around the world.

What will spark your creativity when you visit The Pod?

**What’s a “Pod,” Anyway?**
When you hear the word “pod,” what comes to mind? A pea pod? An iPod? Or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the director of the Art & Nature Center, explains her idea of a pod and what you’ll find in this new, multi-sensory space.

There’s something for everyone here. You can check out amazing nature-inspired art (both old and new), explore nature up close at different stations, or make your own art. For families with young kids, there’s the **Wonder Zone** with activity bins, a light table, and picture books just for them.

CuratorialText: This extraordinary series of events between June 1692 and March 1693 led to the deaths of 25 innocent women, men and children who were wrongfully convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, the personal tragedies and grievous wrongs that occurred still provoke us to reflect and reckon with the experiences of those involved. Learn the true story of this tragedy as told through the voices and with the possessions of those directly involved. This ongoing installation tells this story through court documents and authentic historic objects presented as tangible fragments directly tied to people in Salem and nearby communities in the late 17th century. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick — each illuminates an aspect of individuals who lived through Salem’s witch trials and serves as a reminder of the real people impacted by these harrowing events. The Salem Witch Trials 1692 is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.
persona1_adapted:
Between June 1692 and March 1693, a series of terrible events led to the deaths of 25 innocent people—women, men, and children—who were wrongly convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, these personal tragedies still make us think about what happened and the people who lived through it.

Learn the true story of the Salem witch trials through the voices and belongings of the people who were there. This ongoing exhibit uses court documents and real historic objects from the late 1600s. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick—each item tells us something about the people who lived through the witch trials and reminds us that these were real people affected by these frightening events.

*The Salem Witch Trials 1692* is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.

CuratorialText: The artist researched the museum’s vast and varied holdings, diving deep to find unexpected connections across time, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned her findings into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with X-Acto blades. After applying the stencils to the walls, she and her assistant used a variety of spray-paint and graffiti techniques, including drop shadows, high contrast, and layering, to apply color to the walls and give dimensionality and life to her forms. Organic forms and curvilinear lines emerged as unifying design motifs that speak to the natural world. As you move deeper into these galleries, floral forms give way to enchanting fauna and larger-than-life presentations of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’ labor-intensive contemporary approach to painting connects to the artistry and skill embedded within the historical objects in PEM’s collection, creating a fascinating interplay between artistic practice then and now. What discoveries await? Located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.
persona1_adapted: The artist explored the museum’s huge and varied collection, searching for surprising connections between different times, cultures, and materials. She turned what she found into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with an X-Acto knife. After placing the stencils on the walls, she and her assistant used spray paint and graffiti techniques like drop shadows, high contrast, and layering to add color and make the shapes look three-dimensional and alive. Curving lines and nature-inspired shapes became the main design themes. As you walk deeper into these galleries, flower shapes give way to amazing animals and larger-than-life versions of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’s detailed, hands-on modern painting style connects to the skill and artistry in the historical objects at PEM, creating a cool back-and-forth between art from the past and art today. What will you discover? Find it in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.

CuratorialText: As one of the 20th century’s most critically acclaimed and genre-blurring artists, Noguchi created a remarkable design legacy encompassing sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics and designs for public spaces such as gardens, playgrounds and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer" shines a light on the artist’s limitless creativity and social consciousness. Discover the evolution of Noguchi’s design work, particularly the way he created environments through theatrical set design, furniture and inclusive public spaces — all of which were intentionally designed to improve the way people lived. Many of his designs remain incredibly popular, such as the celebrated coffee table and Akari light sculptures that you may recognize in stores today. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also excelled at working collaboratively and forming close relationships with other creative professionals, particularly with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful creative relationships are also highlighted within the exhibition.
persona1_adapted:
Isamu Noguchi was one of the most famous and creative artists of the 1900s. He didn't stick to just one type of art—he made sculptures, lights, furniture, ceramics, and even designed parks, playgrounds, and public squares. This exhibition, organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, is called *Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer."* It shows how Noguchi’s imagination and care for people shaped his work.

You’ll see how Noguchi’s designs changed over time, especially how he created whole environments—like theater sets, furniture, and public spaces that everyone could enjoy. He wanted his art to make people’s lives better. Many of his designs are still popular today, like his famous coffee table and the Akari light sculptures you might recognize in stores.

Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” He also loved working with other creative people, like dancer Martha Graham, architect R. Buckminster Fuller, and sculptor Constantin Brâncuși. The exhibition highlights these important collaborations, too.

CuratorialText: In the nearly two decades since PEM featured the crowd-pleasing exhibition Origami Now!, contemporary origami has continued to expand into exciting new styles of expression and invention. This multigenerational exhibition in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center highlights origami as not just an exciting art form, but also an important influence on new innovations in science, engineering, math and more. See works from leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. In addition to showcasing masterworks of origami, PEM will highlight what is emerging in contemporary origami and design, ranging from incredibly complex artistic models and folding nano structures, to origami-inspired emergency shelters. Investigate origami-inspired breakthroughs in robotics, medicine and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials will give visitors of all ages hands-on opportunities to investigate and participate in the artistic process of origami.
persona1_adapted:
**Origami: More Than Just Paper Folding**

For almost 20 years, origami has been blowing people’s minds. Back then, the Peabody Essex Museum (PEM) had a super popular show called *Origami Now!* Since then, origami has grown into something even bigger and cooler.

This new exhibit, in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, shows that origami isn’t just an awesome art form. It’s also changing science, engineering, math, and more. You’ll see work from top origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the best in the world—and he lives right here in Massachusetts!

But this isn’t just about pretty paper animals. You’ll also discover what’s happening in origami right now. Think super complex models, tiny folding structures (like nano-sized!), and even emergency shelters that fold up just like origami. Scientists and engineers are using origami ideas to create new robots, medical tools, and ways to help the environment.

Want to try it yourself? No problem. There are hands-on stations and video tutorials where you can learn to fold your own creations. Come see how a simple piece of paper can lead to amazing art and real-world inventions.

CuratorialText: View iconic contemporary artworks that reach back into millennia-old art practices. The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, representing the incredible breadth and depth of Contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition charts watershed moments in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vivid history of creativity that predates the arrival of Europeans by many thousands of years. The exhibition features nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists, among them several undisputed masterworks from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these significant works have never before left the country. The Stars We Do Not See is curated especially to introduce North American audiences to customary forms and styles, such as the conceptual map paintings of the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes colloquially referred to as “dot paintings”), ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia and ambitious experimental weavings. The exhibition also presents powerful works in neon, video and photography that acknowledge and innovate upon tradition.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Readers:**

Check out amazing modern artworks that connect to traditions thousands of years old. *The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art* is the biggest show of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever in North America. It shows the huge range and depth of today's Indigenous Australian art.

The exhibition highlights key moments in Australian Indigenous art history. It celebrates a creative tradition that started many thousands of years before Europeans arrived. You'll see nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists. Some are famous masterpieces from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these important works have never left Australia before.

*The Stars We Do Not See* was specially designed to introduce North American audiences to traditional art styles. You'll see:
- Map-like paintings from the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes called “dot paintings”)
- Ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia
- Bold, experimental weavings

The exhibition also includes powerful works in neon, video, and photography that honor and build on tradition.

CuratorialText: Come and experience the liveliness of a drinking party, the opulence of a royal wedding and poetic evocation of spring on a delicate dish. Discover plants and animals, myths and symbols and decipher the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." With more than 30 highlights from the museum's wide-ranging Chinese collection spanning 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China's artistic achievements crystallized in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies and celebrations. Located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
**Step into a party, a wedding, and a spring day—all on one tiny dish!**  
See animals, plants, and secret symbols. Find the Chinese character for "Double Happiness."  

This exhibit shows over 30 amazing objects from China’s 3,000-year history. Each one celebrates festivals, ceremonies, and special events.  

**Where:** Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery

CuratorialText:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only Philadelphians painted by John Singleton Copley, the greatest artist in the American colonies prior to the Revolution. Mifflin was an ardent patriot and by the time this portrait was made, had established himself as a successful merchant; later he rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army, and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States achieved independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was an accomplished, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a portable loom, which may symbolize the couple's endorsement of the colonists' boycott of English goods.
Famous as one of Copley's finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the height of his powers. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley was self-taught. He developed a highly finished style that rendered the features, costumes, and settings of his subjects with remarkable accuracy. Copley always kept a keen eye on his competition, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin on a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale, who had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family that was admired for its portrayal of family unity and affection. Here, Copley depicts not only the features and costumes of his sitters with his famed skill, but creates an image of marriage as an affectionate, equal partnership--an innovative concept in American portraiture at the time.
persona1_adapted:
**Thomas and Sarah Mifflin: A Portrait of Partnership**

Thomas Mifflin and his wife, Sarah, were the only people from Philadelphia who got their portrait painted by John Singleton Copley. Copley was the most famous artist in the American colonies before the Revolutionary War.

Thomas was a strong supporter of American independence. By the time this painting was made, he was already a successful businessman. Later, he became a major general in the Continental Army and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States won its freedom.

Sarah married Thomas in 1767. She was smart, funny, and a great partner. In the painting, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a small, portable loom. This might show that the couple supported the colonists' boycott of British goods.

This portrait was famous right away as one of Copley's best works. It shows the artist at the top of his game. Copley was born in Boston in 1738 to a poor immigrant family. He taught himself how to paint. He developed a very detailed style that captured people's faces, clothes, and surroundings with amazing accuracy.

Copley always watched what other artists were doing. He may have been inspired to paint Thomas and Sarah together in one picture by another artist named Charles Willson Peale. Peale had just painted a family portrait that people loved because it showed family closeness and love.

In this painting, Copley does more than just show what Thomas and Sarah looked like. He creates an image of marriage as a loving, equal partnership. That was a new and bold idea for American portraits at the time.

CuratorialText: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was the most prolific and accomplished silversmith in late 1700s Boston. His shop produced domestic and ecclesiastical objects for clients belonging to the same civic, military, political, and religious organizations as Revere. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and engraved ornament of C-scrolls, foliage, and shells epitomizes the Rococo style of his early career. Equally renowned as an engraver of bookplates, trade cards, magazine illustrations, and political prints, Revere probably executed this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) depicts the silversmith with engraving tools, holding a teapot with the same low, domed cover and slightly pointed, pinecone finial as this example. Could they be one and the same?
persona1_adapted:
You probably know Paul Revere as the guy who rode his horse to warn, "The British are coming!" during the American Revolution. But did you know he was also one of the best silversmiths in Boston in the late 1700s?

Revere’s shop made fancy silver objects for people he knew from church, the military, politics, and local groups. This teapot is a great example of his early work. It has an upside-down pear shape and is decorated with curvy C-scrolls, leaves, and shells. That style is called Rococo, which was popular at the time.

Revere was also famous for engraving—carving designs into metal or wood for bookplates, magazine pictures, and political cartoons. He probably did the engraving on this teapot himself.

There’s a famous 1768 painting of Paul Revere by John Singleton Copley (you can see it at the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston). In the painting, Revere is holding a teapot that looks a lot like this one—with the same low, domed lid and a little pinecone-shaped knob on top. So, could this be the very same teapot from the painting?

CuratorialText: Human rights and social issues often figure in Pippin’s work, but this painting is particularly overt in its treatment of racism. Clouds hover over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a burly white man holding a noose. The two menacing figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, here painted brown instead of green. At the center, Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, segregating white and black machinists and servicemen. Pippin includes himself as one of the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.
persona1_adapted: Horace Pippin often painted about human rights and social issues. But this painting is especially direct about racism. Dark clouds hang over a hooded Ku Klux Klan member and a large white man holding a noose. These two threatening figures stand across from the Statue of Liberty, which Pippin painted brown instead of green. In the center, a character named Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a V-for-victory symbol, splitting apart Black and white workers and soldiers. Pippin painted himself as one of the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.

CuratorialText: While the bed chambers of chic early Philadelphia houses often featured a high chest (for storing textiles; 1957-129-1) and dressing table (for cosmetics and hair accessories; 2012-59-1), this pairing represents the pinnacle of design, cabinetmaking, and carved ornament. The raised space underneath the high chest distinguishes it as a confection of North America since the British had abandoned that design by the 1730s. While most high chests and dressing tables fit within well-defined groups, the proportions and shaping of the rails and the legs of this pairing relate to only one other dressing table, making it difficult to identify the maker. The choice mahogany, with an even surface and swirling figure, signifies the status of the commission. And a highly skilled carver ornamented it profusely—most conspicuously with a narrative from Aesop’s fable of “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. Aesop’s moralistic tales were the only fiction Quakers were allowed to read.
persona1_adapted:
In fancy early Philadelphia homes, bedrooms often had a tall chest (for storing clothes) and a dressing table (for makeup and hair stuff). But this particular pair is special—it's one of the best examples of furniture design, woodworking, and carving ever made.

The empty space under the tall chest shows it was made in North America. That's because British furniture makers had stopped using that style by the 1730s. Most tall chests and dressing tables fit into certain categories, but the shape and curves of this pair are so unique that they only match one other dressing table. That makes it hard to know who made them.

The furniture is made from high-quality mahogany wood, which has a smooth surface and swirly pattern. This shows the people who ordered it were wealthy and important. A very skilled carver added lots of decoration, including a story from Aesop's fable "The Fox and the Grapes" on the bottom drawers. Back then, Quakers (a religious group) were only allowed to read Aesop's moral tales as fiction—so this was a meaningful choice.

CuratorialText: Pennsylvania-made Kleiderschranken (clothes presses) functioned, as did the earlier models in northern Europe, to hold hanging clothes and store cherished textiles. This schrank is notable for its extraordinary decoration—bold architectural paneling, carving, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, and the date it was made, 1779, only a year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Rather than a lightwood inlay, the lettering and floral designs were made by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved in the walnut. Swiss and German designs inspired the decorative motifs, many of which are commonly found on other Pennsylvania German furniture as well as ceramics and fraktur, and helped to connect the owners and makers to their cultural roots. The wardrobe’s monumental size, bold composition, and profuse ornament demonstrate not only Huber’s aesthetic taste and wealth but also the skills and sensibilities of the Germanic artisans who made it.
persona1_adapted:
This big wooden wardrobe, called a *Kleiderschrank* (clothes press), was made in Pennsylvania. Like earlier versions from northern Europe, it was used to store hanging clothes and keep precious fabrics safe.

What makes this wardrobe special is its amazing decoration. It has bold, carved panels that look like parts of a building, plus detailed lettering. The lettering tells us the owner’s name—Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania—and the year it was made: 1779. That was just one year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer.

Instead of using light-colored wood for the designs, the maker poured hot sulfur into grooves carved into the dark walnut wood. This created the letters and flower patterns you see. The decorations were inspired by Swiss and German designs. You can find similar patterns on other furniture, pottery, and even handwritten documents made by Pennsylvania Germans. These designs helped remind people of their cultural roots back in Europe.

The wardrobe is huge, with a bold look and lots of decoration. It shows that Georg Huber had good taste and was wealthy. It also shows the skill and style of the German craftspeople who built it.

CuratorialText: Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish. German-speaking immigrants to Pennsylvania maintained the artistic traditions they brought from Europe, often retaining art forms and styles over several generations. This dish features decoration made using the centuries-old sgraffito technique, achieved by cutting through a slip coating to expose the red clay underneath. The verse in German around the border, extolling the skill of the potter, also shows an adherence to tradition: language provided an immediate means of holding onto ethnic identity. With such rich ornamentation, this dish was clearly not intended for use.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This dish was made in 1786 by a potter named G. H. It was owned by a woman named Cadarina Raeder. The dish is decorated with a special technique called sgraffito (skrah-FEE-toh). To do this, the potter covered the dish with a thin layer of light-colored clay, then scratched a design through it to show the red clay underneath.

German-speaking immigrants who moved to Pennsylvania brought their art traditions with them. They often kept these styles alive for many generations. The words around the edge of the dish are in German. They praise the potter’s skill. Using their native language helped these immigrants hold onto their culture and identity.

Because this dish is so richly decorated, it was probably not meant to be used for eating. Instead, it was likely made to be displayed and admired.

CuratorialText: In 1795 the Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this illusionistic portrait to showcase his artistic skills. At the same time, he wished to represent the roles played by two of his sons at Peale’s Museum, the first American museum created to educate and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale, who crafted natural science displays, gestures at the top of the stairs as his older brother, Raphaelle, an artist with his palette in hand, strides upward just above a real step attached to the base of the canvas. The life-size portrait is known to have startled and delighted visitors, reputedly even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was dismantled and finally sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the picture continues to engage and welcome visitors.
persona1_adapted: In 1795, a Philadelphia artist named Charles Willson Peale painted this tricky, lifelike portrait to show off his amazing skills. He also wanted to highlight the jobs of two of his sons at Peale’s Museum—the first American museum built to both teach and entertain people. In the painting, his son Titian Ramsay Peale, who made displays about nature and science, points from the top of the stairs. His older brother, Raphaelle, an artist holding his paint palette, walks up just above a real step attached to the bottom of the canvas. This life-size portrait amazed and even scared visitors a little—it’s said that even George Washington was startled by it! The painting stayed in the Peale Museum until the collection was broken up and sold in 1854. The Philadelphia Museum of Art bought it in 1945, and it still welcomes and surprises visitors today.

CuratorialText: A rare early representation of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, and an outstanding example of Peale’s late naturalistic style, the picture is distinguished by the direct and sympathetic encounter between the artist and his subject and a skilled rendering of details. Yarrow’s knit cap suggests a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to assert their religion or African identity, but Peale artfully employed its yellow band to highlight his sitter’s steady gaze with its glint of humor and wisdom. Seventy-seven years old when he created this portrait, Peale was seeking to record the traits that he believed supported a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale celebrated making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive attitude. He perceived Yarrow’s perseverance in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, industriousness, and sobriety.
persona1_adapted:
This painting is special for two big reasons. First, it’s an early example of art showing the different ethnic and religious backgrounds of people in the United States. Second, it’s a great example of how the artist, Charles Willson Peale, painted in a realistic style later in his life.

What makes the portrait stand out is the honest and kind way Peale painted his subject, a man named Yarrow. You can see Peale paid close attention to small details. For example, Yarrow is wearing a knit cap that looks like a *kufi*—a hat worn by African Muslim men to show their faith or African roots. Peale cleverly used the yellow band on the hat to draw your eye to Yarrow’s steady, wise, and slightly humorous gaze.

Peale was 77 years old when he painted this. He wanted to capture the qualities he believed helped people live a long life. In his own writings and museum exhibits, Peale talked about making smart choices to stay healthy and keep a positive attitude. He saw Yarrow’s strength and ability to keep going despite racism and slavery as a perfect example of being resourceful, hardworking, and level-headed.

CuratorialText: This chair is part of the elegantly painted furniture Latrobe designed for the Walns' drawing room. Decorated in rich red, yellow, and gilt on a black ground and incorporating classical motifs and symbols, the set of drawing room furniture consisted of a pier table, Grecian sofas, a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. Its legs that curved inwards make the chair the earliest American example of a Klismos chair, a forerunner of the classical style that enjoyed widespread popularity in the late 1810s and 1820s. In addition to this chair from the Waln set, the Museum's collection includes six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.
persona1_adapted: This chair is part of a fancy set of furniture designed by an architect named Latrobe for the Waln family’s living room. The set was painted in rich red, yellow, and gold on a black background. It included a table for the wall, Greek-style couches, two card tables, window benches, and chairs. The chair’s legs curve inward, making it the earliest known American example of a Klismos chair. That style became very popular in the late 1810s and 1820s. Besides this chair, the museum also has six more chairs, a Greek-style couch, a card table, and a wall table with a mirror on the back.

CuratorialText:
The Gross Clinic is recognized as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. The young and little-known Eakins created it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, intending to showcase his talents as an artist and to honor the scientific achievements of his native Philadelphia.
Choosing the city’s world-famous surgeon and teacher Dr. Samuel Gross as his subject, Eakins sets the scene in Jefferson Medical College’s surgical amphitheater. Dr. Gross is shown leading a clinic of five doctors operating on the left thigh of a patient. At the same time Gross is demonstrating to students the relatively new surgical procedure he had developed to treat bone infections. In contrast to the recoiling woman to the left—traditionally identified as the patient’s mother—Gross embodies the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Casting himself as a witness, Eakins can be seen seated to the right of the tunnel railing, sketching or writing.
persona1_adapted:
**The Gross Clinic** is one of the most famous American paintings ever created. A young and not-yet-famous artist named Thomas Eakins painted it for a big event in Philadelphia in 1876 called the Centennial Exhibition. He wanted to show off his skills as an artist and also honor the scientific achievements of his hometown, Philadelphia.

Eakins chose to paint Dr. Samuel Gross, a world-famous surgeon and teacher from Philadelphia. The scene takes place in a surgical amphitheater at Jefferson Medical College. Dr. Gross is leading a team of five doctors as they operate on a patient’s left thigh. At the same time, he is teaching students about a new surgery he developed to treat bone infections.

On the left side of the painting, a woman (often thought to be the patient’s mother) recoils in horror. In contrast, Dr. Gross looks calm and confident because of his knowledge and experience. Eakins even painted himself into the scene—you can see him sitting on the right side of the railing, sketching or writing, like a witness to the operation.

CuratorialText: The dramatic rescue from a foundering ship shown here was made possible by a recent innovation in lifesaving technology, the breeches buoy. Secured firmly to ship and shore, the device permitted the transfer of stranded passengers to safety by means of a pulley that was hauled back and forth by crews at either end. Cropped down to its essentials, Homer's composition thrusts us into the midst of the action with massive waves rolling past, drenching the semiconscious woman and her anonymous savior. The Life Line was immediately recognized by critics as a major contribution to American art, portraying a heroic, contemporary subject with both painterly virtuosity and detailed observation.
persona1_adapted:
**The Life Line: A Daring Rescue at Sea**

This painting shows a dramatic rescue from a sinking ship. The rescue was possible because of a new invention called the "breeches buoy." This device was a kind of pulley system. It was tied tightly to both the ship and the shore. Crews on each end would pull the pulley back and forth, moving stranded passengers to safety.

The artist, Winslow Homer, zoomed in on the most important part of the action. Huge waves crash past the scene, soaking the barely conscious woman and the man saving her. By cutting out everything else, Homer makes us feel like we are right there in the middle of the storm.

When *The Life Line* was first shown, critics immediately saw it as a major work of American art. It shows a heroic, real-life event with both amazing painting skill and careful attention to detail.

CuratorialText: Mary Cassatt’s flair for digging into an ordinary moment and distilling its emotions stands out in this portrayal of three individuals lulled by a carriage ride into silent reverie. Once titled simply Driving, the painting depicts an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver of the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, sister of the artist, and the girl seated primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is unidentified. Usually associated with domestic interiors and the private lives of women and children, here Cassatt explored an outdoor setting and a degree of autonomy enjoyed by upper-class women. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters enjoyed, particularly after 1879, when the family acquired a small carriage and Bichette, the horse partly depicted here
persona1_adapted:
Mary Cassatt had a special talent for capturing everyday moments and the feelings hidden inside them. In this painting, three people are riding in a carriage, lost in their own quiet thoughts. The painting was once called *Driving*, and it shows a trip through the Bois de Boulogne, a large park on the edge of Paris.

The person driving the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, the artist’s sister. Sitting neatly beside her is Odile Fèvre, the niece of the famous painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking servant in the back is not named.

Cassatt is best known for painting scenes inside homes, focusing on the private lives of women and children. But here, she shows an outdoor setting and the freedom that upper-class women could enjoy. Going for a drive in the park was something the Cassatt sisters loved to do, especially after 1879, when their family bought a small carriage and a horse named Bichette—you can see part of her in the painting.

CuratorialText: Tanner painted The Annunciation soon after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner specialized in religious subjects, and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the Son of God. Mary is shown as an adolescent dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other holy attributes. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899, making it his first work to enter an American museum.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text for 8th-Grade Readers:**

In 1897, artist Henry Ossawa Tanner traveled to Egypt and Palestine. He wanted to see the places where the Bible stories happened. His father was a minister, and Tanner often painted religious scenes. He wanted to get the people, culture, buildings, and light of the Holy Land just right.

What he saw inspired him to paint *The Annunciation* in a new way. The painting shows the moment when the angel Gabriel tells Mary she will give birth to the Son of God. But Tanner didn’t paint Mary with a halo or fancy robes. Instead, she looks like a regular teenager in wrinkled, simple Middle Eastern clothes. And Gabriel isn’t shown as a person with wings—he appears only as a beam of light.

Tanner entered this painting in the famous Paris Salon exhibition in 1898. The next year, the Philadelphia Museum of Art bought it. It was the first of Tanner’s works to enter an American museum.

CuratorialText: A father’s love radiates from this iridescent painting of sunlight and youth. Shaped by the realist tradition of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, Daniel Garber adopted Impressionist color and handling during a two-year sojourn in Europe. Returning in 1907, he settled into a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he harmoniously merged nature, art, and domestic life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio renders her smock translucent, while the composition’s serene geometry captures the shimmer of an endless summer afternoon.
persona1_adapted:
This painting glows with sunlight and a father's love. It shows the artist’s daughter, Tanis, sitting in a garden. The light shines through her smock, making it look almost see-through.

The artist, Daniel Garber, learned to paint realistic scenes from a famous teacher named Thomas Eakins. But when Garber traveled to Europe for two years, he discovered a new style called Impressionism. Impressionist painters used bright colors and loose brushstrokes to capture the feeling of a moment.

When Garber returned to the U.S. in 1907, he moved to a farm in Pennsylvania. There, he blended nature, art, and family life. In this painting, the calm, balanced composition makes you feel like you are stuck in a perfect, endless summer afternoon.

CuratorialText: Georgia O’Keeffe once remarked, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is an extraordinary example of her floral compositions, made of sweeping, broad waves of subtly blended hues. The white petals, highlighted in green, are arranged against a pink backdrop, and from each one emerges a bright yellow pistil. Many have interpreted O’Keeffe’s depictions of floral anatomy in relation to sexuality and gender, but the artist always resisted these interpretations, considering them too specific and limiting.
persona1_adapted: Georgia O’Keeffe once said, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is a great example of her flower art. She used big, flowing brushstrokes and soft, blended colors. The white petals have green highlights and are set against a pink background. Each flower has a bright yellow center. Many people have seen O’Keeffe’s flowers as symbols of sexuality and gender, but the artist always disagreed with that idea. She thought those interpretations were too narrow and didn’t capture what her art was really about.

CuratorialText: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors are among his most important early sculptural work. Inspired by the angular shapes and intersecting lines of the Cubist movement, the artist incorporated the natural patterns and grains of assorted woods into his pieces to emphasize their strong geometric and organic forms. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908–10, and discovered wood as a medium for artistic expression when he began to make carved frames for his canvases. By the mid-1920s he was fully committed to sculpture and extended his definition of the medium to the carving of furniture and architectural interiors. His work is often seen as a forerunner to the contemporary studio furniture movement.
persona1_adapted:
Wharton Esherick created some of his most important early sculptures by carving the insides of rooms. He was inspired by Cubism, an art style that uses sharp angles and lines that cross each other. Esherick also let the natural patterns and grain of different types of wood show through in his work, which made his pieces feel both geometric and organic (like something from nature).

Esherick was born in Philadelphia in 1887. He studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908 to 1910. He first started working with wood when he made carved frames for his paintings. By the mid-1920s, he had decided to focus only on sculpture. He also expanded his idea of what sculpture could be—he started carving furniture and even the inside of buildings. Many people see his work as an early example of the modern studio furniture movement.

CuratorialText: Andrew Wyeth shared the realist approach of many mid-twentieth-century painters of the American scene, although his process of simplification and synthesis often led to haunting, surrealist effects. His picture of his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s light-filled kitchen holds an air of something missing. Wyeth explained that the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The snarling log outside the window, like the knife on the table, connected in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experience, his violent dog, and his strong hand on the entire farm. Said Wyeth, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”
persona1_adapted:
Andrew Wyeth was a realist painter—he liked to show things as they really looked. But he also simplified and combined details in ways that could feel strange or dreamlike, almost like a surrealist painting.

This painting shows the bright kitchen of his neighbor, Karl Kuerner. At first, it looks calm and peaceful. But something feels missing. Wyeth said the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” In other words, the quiet scene hides something darker.

Look at the snarling log outside the window and the knife on the table. For Wyeth, these objects connected to Kuerner’s wartime experiences, his violent dog, and the strong control he had over his farm. Wyeth explained, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.” So even though Karl isn’t in the picture, his presence—and his hidden anger—fills the room.

CuratorialText: In November 1943 this painting was included in Jackson Pollock's stunning solo exhibition at Art of This Century, the innovative New York art gallery run by legendary art patron Peggy Guggenheim. As its title suggests, the work depicts a male figure, embodied in the black columnar form at right, with its mysterious arithmetic graffiti, and a curvy female figure at left, with marvelous catlike eyelashes. Standing on tiny triangular feet, both figures are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint that enhances the sense of energy inherent in the two vertical forms, while also foreshadowing the famous "drip" technique that Pollock would develop later that decade.
persona1_adapted:
In November 1943, this painting was part of a big solo show by Jackson Pollock at a cool New York gallery called Art of This Century. The gallery was run by Peggy Guggenheim, a famous art lover and supporter. 

As the title suggests, the painting shows a male figure on the right—a tall, black, column-like shape covered in mysterious, math-like scribbles. On the left is a curvy female figure with amazing catlike eyelashes. Both figures stand on tiny triangle feet. 

Around them, the paint is splashed and smeared, which makes the two vertical shapes feel full of energy. This style also hints at the famous “drip” technique Pollock would become known for later in the 1940s.

CuratorialText: Here you will find Benjamin Franklin as you’ve never seen him before. Philadelphia-born artist Roberto Lugo riffs on the traditional representations of Franklin found throughout the city—ones that celebrate him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one such stately portrayal of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384. Lugo places Franklin within a constellation of songs, symbols, and rappers’ names—ones that shaped his childhood and adult life. The artist also includes his own self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined dialogue between himself and Franklin around the themes of money, history, violence, and success.
persona1_adapted:
**Benjamin Franklin, Remixed**

This is Benjamin Franklin like you've never seen him before. The artist, Roberto Lugo, was born in Philadelphia. He takes the usual portraits of Franklin—the ones that show him as a famous Founding Father—and gives them a totally new twist. (By the way, you can see a classic statue of Franklin right behind you in gallery 384.)

Lugo decorated this vase with songs, symbols, and the names of rappers. These are things that mattered to him as a kid and still matter to him as an adult. On the other side of the vase, he painted a self-portrait. By putting himself and Franklin together, Lugo creates an imaginary conversation between them about money, history, violence, and what it means to be successful.

CuratorialText: Florine Stettheimer set this scene like a stage, framed by a grand red staircase at left and a long red drape mimicking a theater curtain at right. The depicted customers of Bendel’s, the famous Manhattan department store, lack the decorum and manners expected of women in this period. Instead, they frantically dive for clearance items, change clothes, inspect garments, and examine their mirrored reflections. Stettheimer lets us see "behind the curtain" to observe these women during a moment of unabashed liberation. The action-packed canvas contains an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs, including the Pekingese at the forefront who serves as a surrogate for the artist, as indicated by the "FS"-monogrammed sweater. Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, stands in the left foreground. Opposite is an androgynous salesperson, whose appearance and pose—slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and delicate hand gesture—align with how white gay men often presented themselves at the time.
persona1_adapted:
Florine Stettheimer painted this scene like a play. On the left, there’s a big red staircase. On the right, a long red curtain looks like one you’d see in a theater. The shoppers in this famous New York department store, Bendel’s, don’t act polite or proper like women were expected to back then. Instead, they dive for sale items, change clothes, check out clothes, and stare at themselves in mirrors. Stettheimer lets us see “behind the curtain” to watch these women having a moment of total freedom.

The busy painting has 32 people and two dogs. One dog, a Pekingese in the front, wears a sweater with the letters “FS” on it. That’s a clue that this dog stands in for the artist herself. Henri Bendel, the store’s owner (who was gay), stands on the left. On the other side is a salesperson who looks androgynous—not clearly male or female. With slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and a delicate hand gesture, this person’s style matches how many white gay men presented themselves at the time.

CuratorialText: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) of Daoist lore, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure at the far left holds a lingzhi fungus (thought to bestow longevity if ingested) and a basket of plants is draped over her arm. The focus of the painting is on a large health-benefiting ginseng root displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs depicted include leaves of banana and palm tree carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The representation of Feathered Women exhibiting precious medicinal items functions as a special talisman, protecting the owner from illness.
persona1_adapted:
Three immortal women wear cloaks and skirts made of grass and leaves. Their hairy bodies show they are the Feathered Women (called *mao nü* in Chinese stories). These women live deep in the mountains, searching for healing plants. In this painting, they are showing each other what they have found.

The woman on the far left holds a *lingzhi* mushroom—people believed eating it could give you a long life. A basket full of plants hangs from her arm. In the middle, the main figure holds a large ginseng root, which was thought to be very good for your health. The women also carry banana and palm leaves in baskets on their backs.

The painting of Feathered Women showing off these special plants was thought to work like a lucky charm, protecting the owner from getting sick.

CuratorialText:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. Candles placed within it would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice designs, which required great skill to achieve due to the extremely thin clay. It was not until the early 1700s that artists developed this piercing technique.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers within the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—convey peace, unity, and longevity.
persona1_adapted:
**A Light as a Feather**

This porcelain lantern is so thin and delicate that it feels almost weightless when you hold it. If you put a candle inside, the light would have flickered and glowed through the tiny holes in its design. Making those holes was very hard because the clay was so thin. Artists didn't figure out how to do this until the early 1700s.

**Good Luck Symbols**

On each of the six sides, there are four flowers in the middle: a camellia, a rose, a peony, and a lily. These flowers stand for peace, togetherness, and a long life.

CuratorialText:
Various deva, or guardian deities, are depicted on this fragment from the outer section of a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. Murals representing such figures, richly dressed in costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasties (1368–1644), when the ruling elite embraced a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. Although there is some loss of color, we can identify several of the deities:
(Far left) The guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, Weituo (Skanda in Sanskrit), wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and Buddhism’s enemies.
(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and stupa/pagoda.
(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one frontal and two in profile—and multiple arms.
persona1_adapted:
This broken piece of a giant wall painting (called a mural) once decorated the main hall of a Buddhist temple. It shows several **devas**, which are like guardian spirits or protectors in Buddhism.

Murals like this one, with richly dressed figures wearing fancy jewelry, were common in northern Chinese temples built between the late **Yuan dynasty** (1271–1368) and the early **Ming dynasty** (1368–1644). During this time, the rulers mixed Chinese and Tibetan Buddhist traditions.

Even though some of the paint has faded, we can still tell who these figures are:

- **Far left:** **Weituo** (also called Skanda) is the protector of Buddhist temples and the faith. He wears a winged helmet and carries a diamond sword. He uses it to fight demons and enemies of Buddhism.

- **Center:** Two **Heavenly Kings** appear here. The **Heavenly King of the West** holds a snake. The **Heavenly King of the North** carries an umbrella and a small model of a temple (called a stupa or pagoda).
